topics.title: Corrosion Rate Expressions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Corrosion Rate Expressions # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Corrosion Rate (CR)** is defined as the *amount of metal lost per unit area per unit time* due to electrochemical interaction with the environment. Mathematically, it quantifies the velocity at which a metal undergoes anodic dissolution and is fundamentally a **measure of mass-transport-free metal loss kinetics** at the metal–electrolyte interface. In the KTU 2024 scheme, corrosion rate is treated as a **dimensional engineering quantity** that can be expressed in any of the following standard units, depending on the design application: - **mm/yr** — millimeters per year (SI recommended, used in design codes) - **mpy** — mils per year (1 mil = 0.001 inch; widely used in US/API standards) - **ipy** — inches per year (used for thick sections and pipelines) - **gmd** — grams per square meter per day (used in chemical process industries) - **mdd** — milligrams per square decimeter per day (used in cooling water and refinery service) > [!NOTE] > **Key physical constants used throughout this module:** > - **Faraday's Constant** $F = 96{,}487 \text{ C/mol}$ (≈ 96,500 C/mol) > - **1 year** = 8760 hours = 31,536,000 seconds (using 365 days) > - **1 inch** = 2.54 cm = 25.4 mm > - **1 mil** = 0.001 inch = 0.0254 mm ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are checking how fast an **ice cube melts** in a glass of water. You could measure it three different ways: 1. **By the weight lost per hour** (analogous to *gmd* or *mdd*) 2. **By the thickness of ice that disappeared per day** (analogous to *mm/yr* or *mpy*) 3. **By the electric current you'd need to "un-melt" it** (analogous to *i_corr* from Faraday's law) > All three methods describe **the same physical process** — they are just different **units of the same underlying rate**. Corrosion rate expressions work the same way: every formula simply converts the *same electrochemical reality* into a different engineering unit. ### 1.3 Why Engineers Need Corrosion Rate Corrosion rate answers the practical questions: - *"How long until this pipe wall thins below its allowable limit?"* → requires **mm/yr** or **ipy** - *"Will my chemical tank survive the next maintenance cycle?"* → requires **mpy** - *"What inhibitor dosage is needed?"* → requires **i_corr** in $\mu A/cm^2$ ## 1.4 Categorisation of Measurement Methods Corrosion rate expressions fall into two broad families: | Family | Basis | Typical Output | |---|---|---| | **Weight-Loss (Gravimetric)** | Direct mass measurement over exposure time | mm/yr, mpy, ipy, gmd, mdd | | **Electrochemical (Faradaic)** | Converts measured current to equivalent metal loss | mm/yr, mpy (via $i_{corr}$) | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Faraday's Law of Electrolysis The entire family of corrosion rate expressions is built on **Michael Faraday's two laws of electrolysis (1834)**, which connect *electric charge passed* to *mass of metal dissolved*. ### 2.1.1 First Law (Mass–Charge Proportionality) The mass $W$ of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the charge $Q$ passed: $$W \propto Q \quad \text{where} \quad Q = I \cdot t$$ ### 2.1.2 Second Law (Equivalent Mass) The mass liberated per unit charge is the **chemical equivalent weight** $\dfrac{M}{n}$: $$W = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Combined Faraday's Equation (used everywhere in corrosion):** > $$\boxed{\,W = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}\,}$$ > where $W$ = mass of metal dissolved (g), $I$ = anodic current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = atomic weight (g/mol), $n$ = number of electrons transferred (valence), $F$ = Faraday's constant = 96,487 C/mol. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Conversion Step | Step | Physical Reasoning | |---|---| | Mass → Volume | Divide mass by density $\rho$ to get corroded volume: $V = W/\rho$ | | Volume → Thickness | Divide by exposed area $A$ to get penetration depth: $L = V/A$ | | Thickness → Rate | Divide by exposure time $t$ to get rate: $CR = L/t$ | | Current → Mass | Use Faraday's law to convert measurable $i_{corr}$ to equivalent mass loss | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Summary) > [!NOTE] > All formulas below assume: $W$ in **mg**, $A$ in **cm²** (or **in²** where noted), $t$ in **hours**, $\rho$ (or $D$) in **g/cm³**, $i_{corr}$ in **$\mu A/cm^2$**, $M$ in **g/mol**. ### Table 2.1 — Weight-Loss (Gravimetric) Corrosion Rate Expressions | Expression | Formula | Notes | |---|---|---| | CR in **mm/yr** | $CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | $W$ in mg; $A$ in cm²; $t$ in hr; **SI preferred** | | CR in **mpy** | $CR = \dfrac{534 \times W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | $W$ in mg; $A$ in **in²**; $t$ in hr; US/API standard | | CR in **mpy** (with cm²) | $CR = \dfrac{3444 \times W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | $W$ in mg; $A$ in cm²; $t$ in hr | | CR in **ipy** | $CR = \dfrac{0.534 \times W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | $W$ in mg; $A$ in in²; $t$ in hr | | CR in **gmd** | $CR = \dfrac{240 \times W}{A \cdot t}$ | $W$ in mg; $A$ in cm²; $t$ in hr; $1 \text{ gmd} = 10 \text{ mdd}$ | | CR in **mdd** | $CR = \dfrac{2400 \times W}{A \cdot t}$ | $W$ in mg; $A$ in cm²; $t$ in hr | ### Table 2.2 — Electrochemical (Faradaic) Corrosion Rate Expressions | Expression | Formula | Notes | |---|---|---| | CR in **mm/yr** | $CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot D}$ | $i_{corr}$ in $\mu A/cm^2$ | | CR in **mpy** | $CR = \dfrac{0.1287 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot D}$ | $i_{corr}$ in $\mu A/cm^2$ | | CR in **gmd** | $CR = \dfrac{0.0895 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M}{n}$ | $i_{corr}$ in $\mu A/cm^2$ | | **Stern–Geary Eqn** | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{B}{R_p}$ | $B = \dfrac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | ### Table 2.3 — Conversion Factors (Always Remember!) | From | To | Multiply by | |---|---|---| | 1 mm/yr | mpy | 39.37 | | 1 mpy | mm/yr | 0.0254 | | 1 ipy | mm/yr | 25.4 | | 1 gmd | mdd | 10 | | 1 cm² | in² | 0.1550 | | 1 in² | cm² | 6.4516 | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pipeline Design (API 570 / ASME B31.3):** Uses **mpy** to set inspection intervals. - **Refinery Overhead Systems:** Uses **mdd** for monitoring overhead corrosion. - **Marine / Offshore Cathodic Protection Design (DNV-RP-B401):** Uses **$\mu A/cm^2$** of $i_{corr}$ for protection criteria. - **Inhibitor Qualification (NACE TM0169 / TM0170):** Reports **mpy** for ranking inhibitor performance. - **Heat Exchanger Tube Life Prediction:** Uses **mm/yr** converted to remaining life in years. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Faraday's Law to CR (mm/yr) Using Weight Loss We start from the **fundamental Faraday expression** and convert it step by step to a corrosion rate in **mm/year**. **Starting Equation (Faraday's Law):** $$W = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ **Step 1:** Express the anodic current $I$ in terms of corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ and exposed area $A$: $$I = i_{corr} \cdot A$$ Substituting back: $$W = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ **Step 2:** Convert the mass $W$ into volume $V$ of metal dissolved by dividing by density $\rho$: $$V = \frac{W}{\rho} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$ **Step 3:** Volume divided by area gives thickness loss (penetration depth) $L$: $$L = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$ **Step 4:** Thickness loss per unit time is the corrosion rate. Convert units to **mm/yr** using $i_{corr}$ in $\mu A/cm^2 = 10^{-6} \, A/cm^2$, mm = 10 × cm, year = 31,536,000 s: $$CR \left[\frac{mm}{yr}\right] = \frac{i_{corr}\left[\frac{\mu A}{cm^2}\right] \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot M \cdot 10 \cdot 31{,}536{,}000}{n \cdot 96{,}487 \cdot \rho}$$ **Step 5:** Simplify the numerical constant: $$CR = \frac{3.1536 \times 10^{8} \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot 10}{96{,}487} \cdot \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho} = 0.00327 \cdot \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot D}$$ $$\boxed{\,CR \left[\frac{mm}{yr}\right] = \frac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot D}\,} \quad \text{(with } i_{corr} \text{ in } \mu A/cm^2\text{)}$$ > **Engineering interpretation:** Higher current density (faster anodic reaction) and lower density (less mass per unit volume) → faster metal loss. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Weight-Loss Form of CR (mm/yr) Starting again from: $$CR \left[\frac{cm}{s}\right] = \frac{W [g]}{A [cm^2] \cdot t [s] \cdot \rho [g/cm^3]}$$ This is the "penetration rate" because mass/area/density gives thickness, divided by time gives rate. Now do the unit conversion to **mm/yr** with $W$ in mg, $A$ in cm², $t$ in hours, $D = \rho$ in g/cm³: **Step 1:** Convert $W[g]$ to $W[mg]$: $W[g] = W[mg]/1000$ **Step 2:** Convert $t[s]$ to $t[hr]$: $t[s] = t[hr] \cdot 3600$ **Step 3:** Convert cm to mm: multiply by 10 **Step 4:** Convert seconds to years: $1 \text{ s} = \dfrac{1}{31{,}536{,}000} \text{ yr}$ Combining: $$CR \left[\frac{mm}{yr}\right] = \frac{W[mg]/1000}{A[cm^2] \cdot t[hr] \cdot 3600 \cdot D} \cdot 10 \cdot 31{,}536{,}000$$ $$CR = \frac{10 \cdot 31{,}536{,}000}{1000 \cdot 3600} \cdot \frac{W}{A \cdot t \cdot D} = \frac{87.6 \cdot W}{A \cdot t \cdot D}$$ $$\boxed{\,CR \left[\frac{mm}{yr}\right] = \frac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}\,} \quad \text{(with } W \text{ in mg, } A \text{ in cm}^2\text{, } t \text{ in hr)}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Conversion of CR (mm/yr) to mpy The conversion rests on a single length equivalence: $$1 \text{ mil} = 0.001 \text{ inch} = 0.0254 \text{ mm}$$ Therefore: $$1 \frac{mm}{yr} = \frac{1}{0.0254} \frac{mil}{yr} = 39.37 \frac{mil}{yr}$$ Multiplying our mm/yr expression: $$CR \left[\frac{mpy}{}\right] = 39.37 \times \frac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A[cm^2] \cdot t}$$ $$CR = \frac{3448.8 \cdot W}{D \cdot A[cm^2] \cdot t} \approx \frac{3444 \cdot W}{D \cdot A[cm^2] \cdot t}$$ To express with area in **in²** (using $1 \text{ in}^2 = 6.4516 \text{ cm}^2$): $$CR = \frac{3444 \cdot W}{D \cdot 6.4516 \cdot A[in^2] \cdot t}$$ $$\boxed{\,CR \left[\frac{mpy}{}\right] = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A[in^2] \cdot t}\,} \quad \text{(with } W \text{ in mg, } t \text{ in hr)}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for Problem Solving > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Golden Triangle" of Corrosion Rate Conversions:** > > Given any ONE of the three primary inputs ($W$, $i_{corr}$, or $CR$ in any unit), you can compute the other two. | Given Input | Target Output | Procedure | |---|---|---| | $W$ (mg) | CR (mm/yr) | Use $CR = 87.6W/(DAT)$ | | $W$ (mg) | CR (mpy) | Use $CR = 534W/(DAT)$ with $A$ in **in²** | | $i_{corr}$ ($\mu A/cm^2$) | CR (mm/yr) | Use $CR = 0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M/(nD)$ | | $i_{corr}$ ($\mu A/cm^2$) | CR (mpy) | Use $CR = 0.1287 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M/(nD)$ | | CR (mm/yr) | CR (mpy) | Multiply by 39.37 | | CR (mpy) | CR (mm/yr) | Multiply by 0.0254 | | $R_p$ ($\Omega \cdot cm^2$) | $i_{corr}$ | Use Stern–Geary: $i_{corr} = B/R_p$ | | $i_{corr}$ | $R_p$ | Rearrange: $R_p = B/i_{corr}$ | ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Fully Solved) > **Problem:** A carbon steel specimen (atomic weight $M = 55.85$, density $D = 7.87$ g/cm³, valence $n = 2$) of dimensions $5 \text{ cm} \times 2 \text{ cm} \times 0.3 \text{ cm}$ is immersed in seawater for 240 hours. The weight loss is found to be 188 mg. Calculate: > (a) The corrosion rate in **mm/yr** > (b) The corrosion rate in **mpy** > (c) The equivalent corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ in **$\mu A/cm^2$** ### Solution **Exposed Area Calculation** (assume 5 faces exposed): $$A = 2 \cdot (5 \times 2) + 2 \cdot (5 \times 0.3) + 1 \cdot (2 \times 0.3) = 20 + 3 + 0.6 = 23.6 \text{ cm}^2$$ **Part (a) — CR in mm/yr:** $$CR = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \cdot A \cdot t} = \frac{87.6 \times 188}{7.87 \times 23.6 \times 240}$$ Numerator: $87.6 \times 188 = 16{,}468.8$ Denominator: $7.87 \times 23.6 \times 240 = 7.87 \times 5664 = 44{,}575.7$ $$CR = \frac{16{,}468.8}{44{,}575.7} = 0.3695 \text{ mm/yr}$$ $$\boxed{CR \approx 0.370 \text{ mm/yr}}$$ **Part (b) — CR in mpy:** $$CR = 0.370 \times 39.37 = 14.57 \text{ mpy}$$ $$\boxed{CR \approx 14.6 \text{ mpy}}$$ **Part (c) — Corrosion current density:** Using the inverse Faraday relation $W = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$, solve for $i_{corr}$: $$i_{corr} = \frac{W \cdot n \cdot F}{A \cdot t \cdot M}$$ Convert units: $W = 188 \text{ mg} = 0.188 \text{ g}$, $A = 23.6 \text{ cm}^2$, $t = 240 \text{ hr} = 864{,}000 \text{ s}$ $$i_{corr} = \frac{0.188 \times 2 \times 96{,}487}{23.6 \times 864{,}000 \times 55.85}$$ Numerator: $0.188 \times 2 \times 96{,}487 = 36{,}279.1$ Denominator: $23.6 \times 864{,}000 \times 55.85 = 1{,}138{,}734{,}240$ $$i_{corr} = \frac{36{,}279.1}{1{,}138{,}734{,}240} = 3.186 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A/cm}^2$$ $$\boxed{i_{corr} \approx 31.9 \, \mu A/cm^2}$$ **Cross-check** using $CR = 0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M/(nD)$: $$CR = \frac{0.00327 \times 31.9 \times 55.85}{2 \times 7.87} = \frac{5.826}{15.74} = 0.370 \text{ mm/yr} \checkmark$$ # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Corrosion Rate Expressions The following block diagram maps the *logical flow* of converting a single underlying physical process (electrochemical mass loss) into the five standard engineering units used across the corrosion industry. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[CORROSION PROCESS
at metal-electrolyte interface] --> B{Measurement Approach} B -->|Weight Loss Method| C[Gravimetric Input
W in mg, A, t] B -->|Electrochemical Method| D[Faradaic Input
icorr from Tafel or LPR] C --> C1[Convert W to Volume
V = W over D] C1 --> C2[Volume to Thickness
L = V over A] C2 --> C3[Thickness to Rate
CR = L over t] C3 --> E1[CR in mm per yr
87.6 W over DAt] C3 --> E2[CR in mpy
534 W over DAt where A is in2] C3 --> E3[CR in gmd
240 W over At] C3 --> E4[CR in mdd
2400 W over At] D --> D1[Apply Faraday Law
W = I t M over nF] D1 --> D2[Use Stern-Geary Eqn
icorr = B over Rp] D2 --> D3[Convert icorr to mm per yr
0.00327 icorr M over nD] D3 --> F1[CR in mm per yr] D3 --> F2[CR in mpy
0.1287 icorr M over nD] D3 --> F3[CR in gmd
0.0895 icorr M over n] E1 --> G[Final Engineering Output] E2 --> G E3 --> G E4 --> G F1 --> G F2 --> G F3 --> G G --> H[Design Decision
Selection of material, inhibitor dose, or inspection interval] ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow for Solving a Numerical Corrosion Rate Problem ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[STEP 1
Identify given data
W, A, t, M, n, D] --> P2[STEP 2
Check unit consistency
mg, cm2, hr, g per cm3] P2 --> P3[STEP 3
Choose target unit
mm per yr, mpy, gmd] P3 --> P4[STEP 4
Apply correct formula
from cheat sheet] P4 --> P5[STEP 5
Substitute and solve
show all intermediate steps] P5 --> P6[STEP 6
Convert to required unit
if asked in different system] P6 --> P7[STEP 7
Sanity check
order of magnitude] ``` > [!TIP] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb for Quick Sanity Checks:** > - Steel in seawater typically corrodes at **5–25 mpy** (≈ 0.13–0.65 mm/yr) > - Aluminum alloys in marine atmosphere: **0.5–5 mpy** > - Stainless steel in mild environment: **< 0.1 mpy** > - Carbon steel in strong acid: **> 100 mpy** (extreme) # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define corrosion rate. List any four units commonly used to express corrosion rate.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Corrosion rate is defined as the rate at which a metal deteriorates due to electrochemical or chemical attack, expressed as the **amount of metal lost per unit area per unit time**. It is a measure of the severity of corrosive attack on a material exposed to a given environment. **Four Standard Units (1.5 marks):** 1. **mm/yr** — millimeters per year (SI recommended) 2. **mpy** — mils per year (1 mil = 0.001 inch) 3. **ipy** — inches per year 4. **gmd** — grams per square meter per day (or equivalently mdd — mg/dm²/day) --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]` **State Faraday's law of electrolysis. How is it used in the determination of corrosion rate?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement (1.5 marks):** Faraday's law states that the mass $W$ of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the total electric charge $Q$ passed through the electrolyte. Mathematically: $$W = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ where $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = atomic weight (g/mol), $n$ = valence (electrons transferred per atom), and $F$ = Faraday's constant (96,487 C/mol). **Application in Corrosion Rate (1.5 marks):** Since corrosion is an **electrochemical process**, the anodic current $I$ (or current density $i_{corr}$) can be related to the mass of metal lost. By measuring the corrosion current (using Tafel extrapolation, linear polarization, or impedance), the equivalent mass loss and consequently the corrosion rate in mm/yr, mpy, or gmd can be calculated. This forms the basis of all **electrochemical corrosion rate measurement techniques**. --- ## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 typically offers an **internal choice** between a derivation-oriented question and a numerical/applied question. Both are provided below. --- ### **Question B1 — CHOICE A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a)** Derive the expression for corrosion rate in **mm/yr** from Faraday's law of electrolysis. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A specimen of zinc ($M = 65.38$ g/mol, $D = 7.13$ g/cm³, $n = 2$) with an exposed area of $20 \text{ cm}^2$ is exposed to an industrial atmosphere for 30 days. The weight loss observed is 425 mg. Calculate: **(i)** corrosion rate in mm/yr, **(ii)** corrosion rate in mpy, and **(iii)** equivalent corrosion current density in $\mu A/cm^2$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Faraday's Law (1 mark):** $$W = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ **Step 2 — Replace current with current density times area (1 mark):** $$W = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ **Step 3 — Convert mass to volume via density (1 mark):** $$V = \frac{W}{\rho} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$ **Step 4 — Volume per unit area gives thickness loss (1 mark):** $$L = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$ **Step 5 — Rate = thickness / time (1 mark):** $$CR \left[\frac{cm}{s}\right] = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$ **Step 6 — Unit conversion to mm/yr (1 mark):** Convert $i_{corr}$ from A/cm² to $\mu A/cm^2$ (× 10⁶), cm to mm (× 10), s to yr (× 31,536,000): $$CR \left[\frac{mm}{yr}\right] = \frac{i_{corr}[\mu A/cm^2] \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot M \cdot 10 \cdot 31{,}536{,}000}{n \cdot 96{,}487 \cdot \rho}$$ **Step 7 — Final result (1 mark):** $$\boxed{CR \left[\frac{mm}{yr}\right] = \frac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot D}}$$ --- #### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $M = 65.38$ g/mol, $D = 7.13$ g/cm³, $n = 2$, $A = 20$ cm², $t = 30$ days = 720 hr, $W = 425$ mg. **(i) CR in mm/yr (3 marks):** $$CR = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \cdot A \cdot t} = \frac{87.6 \times 425}{7.13 \times 20 \times 720}$$ Numerator: $87.6 \times 425 = 37{,}230$ Denominator: $7.13 \times 20 \times 720 = 102{,}672$ $$CR = \frac{37{,}230}{102{,}672} = 0.3626 \text{ mm/yr}$$ $$\boxed{CR \approx 0.363 \text{ mm/yr}}$$ **(ii) CR in mpy (2 marks):** $$CR = 0.3626 \times 39.37 = 14.28 \text{ mpy}$$ $$\boxed{CR \approx 14.3 \text{ mpy}}$$ **(iii) Corrosion current density (2 marks):** Convert: $W = 425$ mg = 0.425 g, $t = 720$ hr = 2,592,000 s $$i_{corr} = \frac{W \cdot n \cdot F}{A \cdot t \cdot M} = \frac{0.425 \times 2 \times 96{,}487}{20 \times 2{,}592{,}000 \times 65.38}$$ Numerator: $0.425 \times 2 \times 96{,}487 = 82{,}013.95$ Denominator: $20 \times 2{,}592{,}000 \times 65.38 = 3{,}389{,}299{,}200$ $$i_{corr} = \frac{82{,}013.95}{3{,}389{,}299{,}200} = 2.42 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A/cm}^2$$ $$\boxed{i_{corr} \approx 24.2 \, \mu A/cm^2}$$ --- ### **Question B1 — CHOICE B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2022]` **(a)** Explain with neat expressions the different ways of expressing corrosion rate. Discuss the relationship between corrosion current density and corrosion rate. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The Tafel slopes for a corroding system are $\beta_a = 60$ mV/decade and $\beta_c = 120$ mV/decade. The linear polarization resistance is measured to be $R_p = 1250 \, \Omega \cdot cm^2$. Calculate: **(i)** the Stern–Geary constant $B$, **(ii)** the corrosion current density $i_{corr}$, and **(iii)** the corrosion rate in mpy (assume iron: $M = 55.85$, $D = 7.87$ g/cm³, $n = 2$). **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks **Five Methods of Expressing Corrosion Rate (5 marks):** **(1) mm/yr (millimeters per year):** SI unit, used in design codes. Formula: $$CR = \frac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$$ **(2) mpy (mils per year):** Used in US/API standards. Formula (with $A$ in in²): $$CR = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$$ **(3) ipy (inches per year):** Used for thick sections. Formula (with $A$ in in²): $$CR = \frac{0.534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$$ **(4) gmd (grams per m² per day):** Used in process industries. Formula: $$CR = \frac{240 \cdot W}{A \cdot t}$$ **(5) mdd (milligrams per dm² per day):** Used in cooling water systems. Formula: $$CR = \frac{2400 \cdot W}{A \cdot t}$$ **Relationship between $i_{corr}$ and Corrosion Rate (2 marks):** The electrochemical corrosion rate is directly related to $i_{corr}$ through Faraday's law. The corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ represents the **rate of electron flow** corresponding to the metal dissolution reaction. By applying Faraday's law, the equivalent mass loss and hence the corrosion rate in any desired unit can be calculated: $$CR \left[\frac{mm}{yr}\right] = \frac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot D}$$ $$CR \left[\frac{mpy}{}\right] = \frac{0.1287 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot D}$$ This relationship is the foundation of **non-destructive electrochemical corrosion monitoring** (Tafel, LPR, EIS). --- #### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $\beta_a = 60$ mV/decade, $\beta_c = 120$ mV/decade, $R_p = 1250 \, \Omega \cdot cm^2$, $M = 55.85$, $D = 7.87$, $n = 2$. **(i) Stern–Geary constant B (2 marks):** $$B = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot (\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{60 \times 120}{2.303 \times (60 + 120)} = \frac{7200}{2.303 \times 180}$$ $$B = \frac{7200}{414.54} = 17.37 \text{ mV}$$ $$\boxed{B \approx 17.37 \text{ mV}}$$ **(ii) Corrosion current density (2 marks):** $$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{17.37 \text{ mV}}{1250 \, \Omega \cdot cm^2} = \frac{0.01737 \text{ V}}{1250 \, \Omega \cdot cm^2}$$ $$i_{corr} = 1.39 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A/cm}^2$$ $$\boxed{i_{corr} \approx 13.9 \, \mu A/cm^2}$$ **(iii) Corrosion rate in mpy (3 marks):** $$CR = \frac{0.1287 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot D} = \frac{0.1287 \times 13.9 \times 55.85}{2 \times 7.87}$$ Numerator: $0.1287 \times 13.9 \times 55.85 = 99.91$ Denominator: $2 \times 7.87 = 15.74$ $$CR = \frac{99.91}{15.74} = 6.35 \text{ mpy}$$ $$\boxed{CR \approx 6.35 \text{ mpy}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit mismatch in area (most common, –2 to –3 marks):** The constant 534 is for $A$ in **in²**, NOT cm². Using it with $A$ in cm² will give an answer off by a factor of 6.45. Always check: "*Is 534 or 3444 the right constant for my area unit?*" > 2. **Time unit mix-up:** Weight-loss formulas require time in **hours**, but electrochemical formulas require time in **seconds**. Mixing these up is a classic blunder. > 3. **Forgetting the factor $10^{-6}$:** When $i_{corr}$ is given in $\mu A/cm^2$, the formula $CR = 0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M/(nD)$ already includes the conversion. Do not multiply by $10^{-6}$ again. > 4. **Wrong valence $n$:** Iron corrodes as Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻, so $n = 2$, NOT 3. Aluminium corrodes as Al → Al³⁺ + 3e⁻, so $n = 3$. Always confirm from the half-reaction. > 5. **Density $D$ in g/cm³** (NOT kg/m³). A common conversion error: $7870 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 7.87 \text{ g/cm}^3$ — students often write 7870 directly into the formula. --- ## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Corrosion Rate Expressions)** - **Corrosion Rate = Mass loss per unit area per unit time**; can be measured gravimetrically or electrochemically. - **Faraday's Law** ($W = I t M / nF$) is the *single most important equation* in this module — every formula is a derivative of it. - **Five standard units to remember:** mm/yr, mpy, ipy, gmd, mdd. - **Key constants to memorize:** - $F = 96{,}487$ C/mol - 1 mm/yr = 39.37 mpy = 0.03937 ipy - 1 gmd = 10 mdd - 1 in² = 6.4516 cm² - **Weight-loss formula set (with $W$ in mg, $t$ in hr, $D$ in g/cm³):** - $CR = 87.6 W / (D A t)$ with $A$ in cm² → **mm/yr** - $CR = 534 W / (D A t)$ with $A$ in in² → **mpy** - $CR = 240 W / (A t)$ with $A$ in cm² → **gmd** - **Electrochemical formula set (with $i_{corr}$ in $\mu A/cm^2$):** - $CR = 0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M / (nD)$ → **mm/yr** - $CR = 0.1287 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M / (nD)$ → **mpy** - $CR = 0.0895 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M / n$ → **gmd** - **Stern–Geary Equation:** $i_{corr} = B / R_p$, with $B = \beta_a \beta_c / [2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)]$ — basis of LPR technique. - **For iron specifically:** 1 $\mu A/cm^2$ ≈ 0.0116 mm/yr ≈ 0.457 mpy (memorize this for quick checks). - **Always cross-check** using two formulas when possible — this is the fastest way to catch arithmetic errors in the KTU exam. - **Engineering rule of thumb:** Corrosion rate > 20 mpy is considered "high"; < 1 mpy is "excellent" for most industrial alloys.

topics.title: Electrochemical Aspects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electrochemical Aspects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrochemical Corrosion** is defined as the destructive attack of a metal through **electrochemical reactions** occurring at the metal–environment interface, involving the simultaneous occurrence of two or more **half-cell reactions** — an **anodic reaction** (metal dissolution / oxidation) and one or more **cathodic reactions** (reduction of species from the electrolyte), accompanied by the flow of **electrons** through the metal and **ions** through the electrolyte.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, electrochemical corrosion is governed by the principles of:

1. **Galvanic Cell Action** — Difference in electrode potential drives electron flow.
2. **Electrolytic Conduction** — Ionic transport through the corrosive medium (electrolyte).
3. **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis** — Quantitative relationship between current and mass of metal dissolved.
4. **Nernst Equation** — Thermodynamic basis for the calculation of single electrode potentials under non-standard conditions.

The general electrochemical cell reaction can be represented as:

$$
\text{Anode (Oxidation):} \quad M \longrightarrow M^{n+} + n e^{-}
$$

$$
\text{Cathode (Reduction):} \quad \text{Oxidizer} + n e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Reductant}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term *"electrochemical aspect"* explicitly expects the student to connect the **thermodynamic driving force** (electrode potential / EMF) and the **kinetic outcome** (corrosion current / rate). A simple statement of the half-cell reactions is **incomplete**; the student must also express why the reaction proceeds in the observed direction (free-energy / potential criterion).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> **Real-World Analogy — The Leaking Battery Analogy**
>
> Imagine a brand-new **AA battery** dropped into a glass of salt water. The battery's terminals are made of two different metals, internally separated by an electrolyte paste. Once submerged, the salt water acts as an *external* electrolyte, connecting the two terminals through ions. Electrons now begin to flow **from the more active metal (anode) to the less active metal (cathode)** through the external metallic path, while ions shuffle through the salt water. Inside the original battery, the same chemistry was *useful* — it lit a torch. Outside, with no useful load, the very same reactions are *destructive* — the active metal slowly **dissolves** into the solution.
>
> **Corrosion IS exactly this phenomenon:** a metal that *wants* to return to its native oxide/ore form (its lowest energy state) uses any available dissimilar metal contact, surface defect, or impurity as a "cathode partner," and a thin film of moisture/electrolyte as the "external wire." The result: metal loss, pitting, rust, and eventual failure.

**Key Insight for the Student:**
> [!NOTE]
> - A metal does **not** corrode uniformly because of a single reaction. It corrodes because **microscopic anodes and cathodes** form spontaneously on the surface whenever there is a potential difference.
> - Even on a *single, pure* metal, **local heterogeneities** (grain boundaries, inclusions, differential aeration, stress, temperature gradients) generate the potential difference required for corrosion.
> - The standard reduction potential **$E^\circ$** (volts) is the **thermodynamic yardstick**; the corrosion current **$I_{corr}$** (amperes) is the **kinetic yardstick**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Quick Reference)

| Quantity | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Faraday's Constant | $F$ | **96,485 C mol⁻¹** (≈ 96,500 C mol⁻¹) |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** |
| Standard Temperature | $T$ | **298.15 K** (25 °C) |
| Electronic Charge | $e$ | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** |
| Avogadro's Number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹** |
| Standard Cell Potential | $E^\circ_{cell}$ | **Volts (V)** |
| Corrosion Current Density | $i_{corr}$ | **A cm⁻²** or **µA cm⁻²** |
| Corrosion Rate | $CR$ | **mm/yr** or **mpy** |
| Equivalent Weight | $EW$ | **g/equivalent** (dimensionless when in ratio) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrochemical Cell — Architecture of Corrosion

Corrosion, in its fundamental form, **IS** a **short-circuited galvanic cell**. To analyse any corrosion problem, the student must identify the following four essential components:

1. **Anode** — The site of metal dissolution (oxidation). Electrons are *liberated* here.
2. **Cathode** — The site of reduction. Electrons are *consumed* here. The cathode is *not* consumed.
3. **Electrolyte** — The ion-conducting medium (water with dissolved salts, acids, oxygen, etc.).
4. **Metallic Path** — The internal electronic conductor (the metal itself or a coupled dissimilar metal) connecting anode and cathode.

> **KTU Logic Step (Why and How):**
> - **Why** must both anode and cathode exist? → Because an oxidation half-reaction (loss of electrons) MUST be balanced by a reduction half-reaction (gain of electrons). Charge is conserved; electrons cannot accumulate.
> - **How** are they identified on a real surface? → The anode is the site where you observe *metal loss* (pits, etching); the cathode is the site where you observe *deposits, gas evolution, or no visible loss*.

## 2.2 The Four Most Common Cathodic Reactions (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In neutral/alkaline aerated water, the most common cathodic reaction is **oxygen reduction**. In acidic environments, it is **hydrogen evolution**. The student must memorize these:

### Cathode Reaction 1 — Hydrogen Evolution (Acidic, deaerated)
$$
2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow H_{2}(g) \quad E^\circ = 0.000\ \text{V vs SHE}
$$

### Cathode Reaction 2 — Oxygen Reduction in Acid Solution
$$
O_{2} + 4H^{+} + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 2H_{2}O \quad E^\circ = +1.229\ \text{V vs SHE}
$$

### Cathode Reaction 3 — Oxygen Reduction in Neutral / Alkaline Solution
$$
O_{2} + 2H_{2}O + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4OH^{-} \quad E^\circ = +0.401\ \text{V vs SHE}
$$

### Cathode Reaction 4 — Metal Ion Reduction (e.g., Copper deposition)
$$
Cu^{2+} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow Cu \quad E^\circ = +0.342\ \text{V vs SHE}
$$

> **KTU Reasoning:** The *more positive* the reduction potential of the cathodic reaction, the *larger* the driving force (ΔE) for corrosion. This is why aerated (oxygen-rich) conditions are *more corrosive* than deaerated ones — the $E^\circ$ is +1.229 V vs 0.000 V.

## 2.3 The Nernst Equation — Thermodynamic Foundation

The Nernst equation is the **single most important formula** for Module 1 of Corrosion Engineering. It allows the student to compute the actual electrode potential $E$ of a half-cell when the conditions deviate from the standard state ($25\ ^\circ\text{C}$, 1 M activity, 1 atm pressure).

For a general half-cell reaction:
$$
aA + ne^{-} \rightleftharpoons bB
$$

The Nernst equation is:

$$
E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{a_B^{\,b}}{a_A^{\,a}}
$$

Where:
- $E$ = actual electrode potential (V)
- $E^\circ$ = standard electrode potential (V)
- $R$ = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred
- $F$ = 96,485 C mol⁻¹
- $a_A,\ a_B$ = activities (≈ concentrations for dilute solutions) of oxidized and reduced species.

### 2.3.1 The Common Base-10 Form (Memorise This for KTU)

At $T = 298.15$ K, substituting constants:

$$
E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log \frac{[\text{Reductant}]}{[\text{Oxidant}]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** The Nernst equation is *always* written with the **oxidized form in the numerator** of the log term when the half-reaction is written as a **reduction** (the standard convention). The student who flips this will lose marks.

## 2.4 Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis — Quantitative Basis of Corrosion Rate

### 2.4.1 Faraday's First Law
> The mass $m$ of a substance liberated or dissolved at an electrode is **directly proportional** to the total electric charge $Q$ passed through the electrolyte.

$$
m = \frac{EW \cdot I \cdot t}{F}
$$

### 2.4.2 Faraday's Second Law
> The mass $m$ of a substance liberated is **directly proportional** to its **equivalent weight** $EW$ when the same quantity of electricity is passed.

$$
m = \frac{EW}{F} \cdot Q
$$

Where the **Equivalent Weight (EW)** is defined as:

$$
EW = \frac{\text{Atomic Weight (AW)}}{n}
$$

with $n$ = number of electrons transferred per atom/molecule.

### 2.4.3 Corrosion Rate from Faraday's Law

Combining Faraday's law with the definition of corrosion current density $i_{corr} = I/A$, the **engineering corrosion rate** in mm per year is:

$$
CR\ (\text{mm/yr}) = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}
$$

Where $\rho$ is the density of the metal (g cm⁻³), $i_{corr}$ is in µA cm⁻², and $EW$ in g/eq.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Anchor:** The constant **3.27 × 10⁻³** comes from: $(M)/(nF\rho) \times$ (conversion from seconds to year and from cm to mm). The student does NOT need to derive it but MUST cite the formula correctly.

## 2.5 EMF Series vs. Galvanic Series — KTU Frequently Confused

> [!WARNING]
> **The biggest conceptual trap in Module 1.** Students routinely treat the EMF Series and the Galvanic Series as interchangeable. They are NOT.

| Feature | EMF Series | Galvanic Series |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Pure thermodynamic (standard reduction potentials of pure elements in their standard states, in a specific electrolyte, usually 1 M). | Empirical, observed corrosion behaviour of commercial alloys in a **specific environment** (typically seawater). |
| **Nature** | Lists individual elements (Zn, Fe, Cu, Ag, Au…). | Lists commercial alloys grouped (active end → noble end). |
| **Environment** | Fixed, idealised, $25\ ^\circ$C, 1 M ions. | Real environment (e.g., flowing seawater, 25 °C). |
| **Reproduction** | Theoretical / tabulated. | Experimental. |
| **Practical Use** | Predicts thermodynamic tendency, used in Nernst equation. | Predicts actual galvanic corrosion behaviour when two commercial alloys are coupled. |
| **Size of Table** | ~70 entries. | ~30–50 entries, ranked in 4–5 groups. |

> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** Use **EMF Series** when calculating *whether* corrosion is thermodynamically possible. Use **Galvanic Series** when predicting *which alloy will corrode* in a real engineering couple.

## 2.6 Reference Electrodes (KTU High-Yield)

All potentials must be measured **relative to a reference**. The **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** is the universal primary reference ($E^\circ = 0.000$ V by definition).

| Reference Electrode | Half-Reaction | $E$ (V vs SHE) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SHE** | $H^+/H_2$ | **0.000 V** | Primary standard, lab use. |
| **Saturated Calomel (SCE)** | $Hg_2Cl_2/Hg$ | **+0.244 V** | Most common laboratory reference. |
| **Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl)** | $AgCl/Ag$ | **+0.222 V** (sat. KCl) | Seawater, field measurements. |
| **Copper/Copper Sulfate (CSE)** | $Cu/CuSO_4$ | **+0.318 V** | Cathodic-protection field surveys. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** When a potential is reported "vs SCE", the student must convert to "vs SHE" by **adding 0.244 V** before using it in Nernst-based thermodynamic calculations. A common mark-loser is to use the value as-is.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (One-Glance Master Table)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Typical Use in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nernst Equation (general) | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{a_{\text{red}}}{a_{\text{ox}}}$ | Calculate actual electrode potential. |
| 2 | Nernst Equation (base-10, 25 °C) | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log \dfrac{[\text{Reductant}]}{[\text{Oxidant}]}$ | KTU numerical problems. |
| 3 | Cell EMF (galvanic couple) | $E^\circ_{cell} = E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode}$ | Predict direction of corrosion. |
| 4 | Free Energy of Corrosion | $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$ | If $\Delta G < 0$, corrosion is spontaneous. |
| 5 | Faraday's First Law | $m = \dfrac{EW \cdot I \cdot t}{F}$ | Mass of metal dissolved. |
| 6 | Equivalent Weight | $EW = \dfrac{AW}{n}$ | Used in corrosion rate formula. |
| 7 | Corrosion Rate (mm/yr) | $CR = \dfrac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | Engineering design parameter. |
| 8 | Corrosion Current Density | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{I_{corr}}{A}$ | From Tafel or polarisation curves. |
| 9 | Reference Electrode Conversion | $E_{SHE} = E_{SCE} + 0.244$ | Field/lab data harmonisation. |
| 10 | Polarisation (overpotential) | $\eta = E - E_{eq}$ | Deviation from equilibrium potential. |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The electrochemical aspect underpins **all** corrosion control strategies taught later in the syllabus:
> - **Cathodic Protection (Sacrificial Anode Method):** Couples a more active metal (Zn, Mg, Al) to the structure (Fe), forcing the structure to be the cathode. The Zn *sacrifices* itself.
> - **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP):** Uses an external DC source + inert anode to force protective current onto the structure.
> - **Anodic Protection:** Maintains the metal in the passive region using a potentiostat.
> - **Coating Selection (Galvanic Series):** Ensures that a coated dissimilar metal contact does not produce a dangerous couple.
> - **Selection of Materials for Seawater, Acids, Soils:** All decisions are made by consulting the right series.
> - **Design of Electrochemical Sensors** for corrosion monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section is **exhaustive and complete**. No step is skipped; every algebraic transition and every substitution is shown.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Thermodynamic Equilibrium

Consider a general reversible half-cell reaction written as a reduction:

$$
\text{Oxidant} + ne^{-} \rightleftharpoons \text{Reductant}
$$

At equilibrium, the **chemical potential** of the reactants equals that of the products. The Gibbs free energy change is related to the electrical work done:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln \frac{a_{\text{Reductant}}}{a_{\text{Oxidant}}}
$$

The electrical work done in transferring $n$ moles of electrons across a potential difference $E$ is:

$$
\Delta G = -nFE
$$

(negative sign because the cell does work as electrons flow from anode to cathode through the external circuit).

### 3.1.2 Setting Up the Equations

At standard conditions:

$$
\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ
$$

Therefore:

$$
-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln \frac{a_{\text{Reductant}}}{a_{\text{Oxidant}}}
$$

### 3.1.3 Solving for E

Divide both sides by $-nF$:

$$
E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{a_{\text{Reductant}}}{a_{\text{Oxidant}}}
$$

> **Conversion to base-10 logarithm:**

$$
\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)
$$

So:

$$
E = E^\circ - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log \frac{a_{\text{Reductant}}}{a_{\text{Oxidant}}}
$$

### 3.1.4 Substitution at 25 °C (298.15 K)

$$
\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = \frac{5705.7}{96485} \approx 0.05916\ \text{V}
$$

Hence the **familiar KTU form**:

$$
\boxed{E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log \frac{a_{\text{Reductant}}}{a_{\text{Oxidant}}}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Corrosion Rate Formula (mm/yr)

### 3.2.1 Starting Point — Faraday's First Law

$$
m = \frac{EW \cdot I \cdot t}{F}
$$

### 3.2.2 Expressing Mass in Volume Terms

The metal dissolved has density $\rho$ (g cm⁻³) and equivalent weight $EW$ (g/eq). The volume dissolved is $V = m/\rho$ (cm³).

The corrosion rate in **cm per second** is:

$$
CR = \frac{V}{A \cdot t} = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t} = \frac{EW \cdot I}{\rho \cdot A \cdot F}
$$

Since $I/A = i_{corr}$ (current density, A cm⁻²):

$$
CR = \frac{EW \cdot i_{corr}}{\rho \cdot F} \quad \text{(in cm s}^{-1}\text{)}
$$

### 3.2.3 Converting cm/s to mm/year

- 1 cm = 10 mm
- 1 year = 365.25 × 24 × 3600 = 31,557,600 s

So:

$$
CR\ (\text{mm/yr}) = \frac{EW \cdot i_{corr}}{\rho \cdot F} \times \frac{10\ \text{mm}}{1\ \text{cm}} \times 31{,}557{,}600\ \text{s/yr}
$$

### 3.2.4 Substituting Constants for $i_{corr}$ in µA cm⁻²

Since 1 A = 10⁶ µA:

$$
CR = \frac{EW \cdot i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \times 10^{-6}}{F \cdot \rho} \times 10 \times 31{,}557{,}600
$$

$$
= \frac{315.576 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10}{F \cdot \rho} \times EW \cdot i_{corr}
$$

$$
= \frac{3.15576 \times 10^{-3}}{F \cdot \rho} \times EW \cdot i_{corr}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $10^{5}$ to clean up:

$$
= \frac{0.315576}{96485 \cdot \rho} \times EW \cdot i_{corr}
$$

After rounding:

$$
\boxed{CR\ (\text{mm/yr}) = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** If $i_{corr}$ is given in **A m⁻²**, the constant changes to **0.00327 × 10⁻³** ≈ **3.27 mm·g/(yr·µA·cm·eq)**. Always check the unit of current density given in the problem.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — KTU Style

### Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme Sample):
> A structural steel member ($\rho = 7.86\ \text{g cm}^{-3}$, AW = 55.85, $n = 2$) is found to have a corrosion current density of **$i_{corr} = 20\ \mu\text{A cm}^{-2}$**. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mm/yr** and the **thickness lost in 5 years**.

#### Step 1: Equivalent Weight

$$
EW = \frac{AW}{n} = \frac{55.85}{2} = 27.925\ \text{g/eq}
$$

#### Step 2: Corrosion Rate

$$
CR = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 \times 27.925}{7.86}
$$

$$
CR = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times 558.5}{7.86}
$$

$$
CR = \frac{1.8263}{7.86} = 0.2324\ \text{mm/yr}
$$

#### Step 3: Thickness Lost in 5 Years

$$
\Delta t = 0.2324 \times 5 = 1.162\ \text{mm}
$$

> **Result:** $CR \approx 0.23$ mm/yr, $\Delta t \approx 1.16$ mm in 5 years.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Nernst Equation

### Problem:
> A copper electrode is immersed in a solution of $Cu^{2+}$ ions of concentration $0.01$ M at 25 °C. Given $E^\circ_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.342$ V, calculate the electrode potential.

#### Step 1: Identify $n$ and the Reaction

$$
Cu^{2+} + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons Cu \quad (n = 2)
$$

#### Step 2: Apply the Nernst Equation (Reduction Form)

$$
E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log \frac{[Cu]}{[Cu^{2+}]}
$$

For pure solid copper, activity of $Cu$ = 1:

$$
E = 0.342 - \frac{0.0592}{2} \log \frac{1}{0.01}
$$

#### Step 3: Evaluate the Logarithm

$$
\log \frac{1}{0.01} = \log(100) = 2
$$

#### Step 4: Compute

$$
E = 0.342 - \frac{0.0592}{2} \times 2
$$

$$
E = 0.342 - 0.0592
$$

$$
E = +0.2828\ \text{V vs SHE}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The copper electrode becomes **less noble** as the $Cu^{2+}$ concentration decreases. This is consistent with Le Chatelier's principle — the equilibrium shifts to dissolve more Cu.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Cathode Reaction Selection in Different Media

| Environment | Typical Cathode Reaction | $E^\circ$ (V vs SHE) | Why It Dominates |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strongly acidic, deaerated (HCl, H₂SO₄) | $2H^+ + 2e^- \to H_2$ | 0.000 | High $[H^+]$, no O₂. |
| Strongly acidic, aerated | $O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \to 2H_2O$ | +1.229 | O₂ present + high $[H^+]$. |
| Neutral, aerated (tap water, seawater) | $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \to 4OH^-$ | +0.401 | O₂ present, low $[H^+]$. |
| Neutral, deaerated (boiled water) | $2H_2O + 2e^- \to H_2 + 2OH^-$ | −0.828 | No O₂, low $[H^+]$. |
| Salt water (NaCl) | $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \to 4OH^-$ | +0.401 | O₂ diffusion controlled. |
| Soil (moist, aerated) | $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \to 4OH^-$ | +0.401 | Differential aeration corrosion. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap Question:** *"In which environment is corrosion most severe?"* — The answer is **acidic, aerated, with high chloride**, because *all three* factors push the cathodic reaction to the most positive potential (largest $\Delta E$ for the same anode).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Galvanic Couple (KTU Design Rule)

| Couple | More Active (Anode) | More Noble (Cathode) | Expected Anode Corrosion | Risk Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fe + Cu in seawater | Fe | Cu | Severe | High |
| Fe + Zn in seawater | Zn | Fe | Severe (Zn protects Fe) | Beneficial (cathodic protection) |
| Al + Cu in seawater | Al | Cu | Very severe | **Very High** |
| Al + Zn in seawater | Zn | Al | Moderate | Beneficial (galvanised Al) |
| Stainless Steel + Carbon Steel | Carbon Steel | SS | Moderate (but SS may pit) | Medium |
| Ti + Cu in seawater | Cu* | Ti | Cu may actually corrode in some Ti galvanic cells due to complexing | Special case |
| Pb + Cu in acid | Pb | Cu | Moderate | Medium |

> **KTU Design Takeaway:** Avoid coupling metals separated by **more than ~0.25 V** in the galvanic series in aggressive environments.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10, all diagrams are **Mermaid block diagrams** (functional/architectural), avoiding the pitfalls of physical drawings. Mermaid syntax strictly follows the **Alphanumeric Node Rule** and **No-Markdown-In-Labels** rule.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Electrochemical Corrosion Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ANODE SITE: Metal M] -- OXIDATION M to M n plus plus n e minus --> A
    A -- ELECTRONS FLOW through metal --> C[CATHODE SITE: Inert surface or dissimilar metal]
    C -- REDUCTION of Oxidizer Ox plus n e minus to Red --> C
    A --- P1[Metallic Path: Bulk Metal]
    C --- P1
    A --- P2[Electrolyte Path: Aqueous ions]
    C --- P2
    I[Current Loop: e- through metal, ions through electrolyte] --- A
    I --- C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **anode** (left block) loses mass by oxidation.
- **Electrons** travel through the metal to the cathode (right block).
- **Ions** travel through the electrolyte.
- The cycle is self-sustaining as long as both paths remain intact.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Components Required for Electrochemical Corrosion

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[REQUIREMENTS FOR ELECTROCHEMICAL CORROSION] --> R1[1. Anode with active metal]
    R --> R2[2. Cathode with lower potential]
    R --> R3[3. Continuous electrolyte path]
    R --> R4[4. Continuous metallic path]
    R1 --> O1[Anodic Half Reaction: M gives M to the power n plus plus n e minus]
    R2 --> O2[Cathodic Half Reaction: Oxidizer plus n e minus gives Reductant]
    R3 --> O3[Ion transport: cations move to cathode, anions to anode]
    R4 --> O4[Electron transport: e- flow anode to cathode]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Nernst Equation Decision Flow for KTU Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Given half reaction, T, concentrations] --> Q1{Is T 25 deg C?}
    Q1 -- YES --> N1[Use base 10 form: E equals E zero minus 0.0592 by n times log Q]
    Q1 -- NO --> N2[Use natural log form: E equals E zero minus RT by nF times ln Q]
    N1 --> R1[Compute Q from given activities]
    N2 --> R2[Compute Q from given activities]
    R1 --> R3[Substitute and solve for E]
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> E[Final answer in V vs SHE]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Cathodic Reaction Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Environment] --> B{pH of solution?}
    B -- Acidic pH below 7 --> C{Oxygen present?}
    B -- Neutral or Alkaline pH greater than 7 --> D{Oxygen present?}
    C -- YES --> E1[O2 plus 4H plus plus 4e minus gives 2H2O E zero equals 1.229 V]
    C -- NO --> E2[2H plus plus 2e minus gives H2 E zero equals 0.000 V]
    D -- YES --> E3[O2 plus 2H2O plus 4e minus gives 4OH minus E zero equals 0.401 V]
    D -- NO --> E4[2H2O plus 2e minus gives H2 plus 2OH minus E zero equals minus 0.828 V]
```

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Corrosion Rate Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Given: I corr or i corr, metal identity, density] --> Q[Compute Equivalent Weight EW equals AW by n]
    Q --> R[Apply Faraday: m equals EW times I times t by F]
    R --> S[Convert m to volume: V equals m by rho]
    S --> T[Convert V to thickness: CR equals V by A by t]
    T --> U[Convert units: cm per s to mm per year using constant 3.27 times 10 power minus 3]
    U --> V[Final Corrosion Rate in mm per year]
```

---

## 4.6 Block Diagram — EMF Series vs. Galvanic Series Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EMF[EMF SERIES] --> E1[Basis: Thermodynamic Standard Reduction Potentials]
    EMF --> E2[Scope: Pure elements in 1 M at 25 deg C]
    EMF --> E3[Use: Predict thermodynamic possibility and calculate Nernst potentials]
    GS[GALVANIC SERIES] --> G1[Basis: Empirical corrosion behaviour of commercial alloys]
    GS --> G2[Scope: Real environment, e.g., flowing seawater]
    GS --> G3[Use: Predict actual galvanic corrosion ranking for design]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
