# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> All questions are aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme** pattern, **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (RBT)** levels, and the **Course Outcomes (CO1)** of PEPOT523.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
> **"Define electrochemical corrosion. List the four essential components required for electrochemical corrosion to occur."** **[3 Marks, RBT: Remember/Understand, CO1]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Electrochemical corrosion** is the deterioration of a metal by electrochemical reactions at the metal–electrolyte interface, in which metal oxidation at anodic sites is balanced by reduction of some species at cathodic sites, with electrons flowing through the metal and ions through the electrolyte.

The **four essential components** are:

1. **Anode** — site of metal dissolution (oxidation).
2. **Cathode** — site of reduction (e.g., $H^+$ or $O_2$ reduction).
3. **Electrolyte** — ion-conducting medium.
4. **Metallic path** — internal electron-conducting connection between anode and cathode.

> **Mark Split (as per KTU pattern):** Definition 1.5 marks, List 1.5 marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
> **"Differentiate between the EMF series and the Galvanic series."** **[3 Marks, RBT: Understand, CO1]**

**Model Answer:**

| EMF Series | Galvanic Series |
|---|---|
| Based on standard reduction potentials of pure elements. | Based on observed corrosion potentials of commercial alloys. |
| Theoretical / thermodynamic. | Empirical / practical. |
| Valid only for standard state (1 M, 25 °C, 1 atm). | Valid for a specific real environment (e.g., seawater). |
| Lists individual elements. | Lists commercial alloys and groups them. |
| Used to predict the *possibility* of corrosion. | Used to predict the *direction and severity* of galvanic corrosion. |

> **Mark Split:** Any 3 valid differences × 1 mark each = 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE PROVIDED:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

> **"a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the mechanism of electrochemical corrosion of iron in a neutral aerated aqueous environment. **Write all relevant half-cell reactions and identify the anode, cathode, electrolyte, and metallic path. [7 Marks, RBT: Apply, CO1]**
>
> **"b)** A galvanic couple is formed between **zinc** and **copper** immersed in a solution containing $Zn^{2+}$ (1 M) and $Cu^{2+}$ (0.01 M) at 25 °C. Given $E^\circ_{Zn^{2+}/Zn} = -0.763$ V and $E^\circ_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.342$ V, calculate the **cell EMF**. Comment on the spontaneity. **[7 Marks, RBT: Apply/Analyse, CO1]**"

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Mechanism of Corrosion of Iron in Neutral Aerated Water:**

**Setup description (3 marks):**

- Iron surface in contact with neutral aerated water (containing dissolved $O_2$).
- Heterogeneities on the Fe surface (inclusions, grain boundaries) form microscopic anodes and cathodes.
- Water with dissolved ions acts as the electrolyte.

**Anode Reaction (Oxidation of Iron):**
$$
Fe \longrightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^{-}
$$

**Cathode Reaction (Oxygen Reduction in Neutral Solution):**
$$
O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4OH^{-}
$$

**Net Reaction (combining 2 anodes + 1 cathode for electron balance):**
$$
2Fe + O_2 + 2H_2O \longrightarrow 2Fe^{2+} + 4OH^{-}
$$

**Identification of components (2 marks):**
- Anode: Fe surface (sites of metal loss).
- Cathode: Adjacent sites on Fe (or impurity sites) where $O_2$ is reduced.
- Electrolyte: Aerated water (with dissolved $O_2$ and ions).
- Metallic path: Iron body itself (electrons travel through it).
- **Further reaction:** $Fe^{2+} + 2OH^- \to Fe(OH)_2$ (greenish); later oxidised to $Fe(OH)_3$ (rust-brown).

**Sketch (2 marks):** Block diagram with anode site, cathode site, electron flow through metal, ion flow through water, plus labelled reactions. (See Section 4.1 for equivalent diagram.)

---

**Part (b) — Cell EMF Calculation:**

**Step 1: Identify Anode and Cathode.**
- Zn has the more negative $E^\circ$ → it will oxidise → **Anode**.
- Cu has the more positive $E^\circ$ → it will be reduced → **Cathode**.

**Step 2: Calculate Actual Cathode Potential Using Nernst Equation.**
For the cathode ($Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \to Cu$, $n = 2$):

$$
E_{Cu} = E^\circ_{Cu} - \frac{0.0592}{2} \log \frac{[Cu]}{[Cu^{2+}]}
$$

Since $[Cu] = 1$ (pure solid) and $[Cu^{2+}] = 0.01$ M:

$$
E_{Cu} = +0.342 - \frac{0.0592}{2} \log \frac{1}{0.01}
$$

$$
E_{Cu} = 0.342 - 0.0296 \times 2 = 0.342 - 0.0592 = +0.2828\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 3: Anode Potential (1 M $Zn^{2+}$, so $E_{Zn} = E^\circ_{Zn}$).**

$$
E_{Zn} = -0.763\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 4: Cell EMF.**

$$
E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode} = 0.2828 - (-0.763) = +1.0458\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 5: Spontaneity Comment.**

Since $E_{cell} = +1.046\ \text{V} > 0$, the cell reaction is **spontaneous** as written (Zn dissolves, Cu deposits). $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell} < 0$ confirms spontaneity.

> **Numerical Subtotal (7 marks):** Identification of electrodes (1), Nernst for Cu (2), Anode value (1), Cell EMF (2), Spontaneity comment (1).

---

### Question B (14 Marks, ALTERNATIVE) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

> **"a)** State and explain **Faraday's laws of electrolysis**. Derive the expression for the **corrosion rate in mm/year** in terms of corrosion current density. **[7 Marks, RBT: Understand/Apply, CO1]**
>
> **"b)** The corrosion current density for a copper specimen (AW = 63.55, $n = 2$, $\rho = 8.96\ \text{g cm}^{-3}$) immersed in an aerated acid solution is measured by Tafel extrapolation as **$i_{corr} = 5\ \mu\text{A cm}^{-2}$**. Calculate the **corrosion rate in mm/yr** and the **mass loss per day per square metre** of exposed surface. **[7 Marks, RBT: Apply, CO1]**"

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Faraday's Laws and Derivation:**

**Faraday's First Law (2 marks):** The mass of a substance liberated or dissolved at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed:

$$
m \propto Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \frac{EW \cdot Q}{F} = \frac{EW \cdot I \cdot t}{F}
$$

**Faraday's Second Law (1 mark):** When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their equivalent weights.

**Derivation of Corrosion Rate (4 marks):**
1. Start with $m = (EW \cdot I \cdot t)/F$.
2. Volume dissolved $V = m/\rho$.
3. Thickness loss per unit area $L = V/A = (EW \cdot I \cdot t)/(\rho \cdot A \cdot F)$.
4. Corrosion rate $CR = L/t = (EW \cdot I)/(\rho \cdot A \cdot F) = (EW \cdot i_{corr})/(\rho \cdot F)$.
5. Convert cm/s to mm/yr: multiply by $10 \times 31.5576 \times 10^6$, divide by $F = 96485$.
6. Final: $CR\ (\text{mm/yr}) = (3.27 \times 10^{-3} \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW)/\rho$.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Step 1: Equivalent Weight.**
$$
EW = \frac{63.55}{2} = 31.775\ \text{g/eq}
$$

**Step 2: Corrosion Rate.**
$$
CR = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 31.775}{8.96}
$$

$$
CR = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times 158.875}{8.96}
$$

$$
CR = \frac{0.5195}{8.96} = 0.0580\ \text{mm/yr}
$$

**Step 3: Mass Loss per Day per m².**

Surface area $A = 1\ \text{m}^2 = 10^4\ \text{cm}^2$.
Corrosion current $I = i_{corr} \times A = 5\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2 \times 10^4\ \text{cm}^2 = 5 \times 10^4\ \mu\text{A} = 5 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{A}$.

Time $t = 1\ \text{day} = 86400\ \text{s}$.

Charge $Q = I \cdot t = 0.05 \times 86400 = 4320\ \text{C}$.

Mass loss:
$$
m = \frac{EW \cdot Q}{F} = \frac{31.775 \times 4320}{96485}
$$

$$
m = \frac{137268}{96485} = 1.423\ \text{g/day per m}^2
$$

> **Final Answers:** $CR = 0.058$ mm/yr; Mass loss = **1.42 g/day·m²**.

> **Mark Split (7 marks):** EW (1), CR formula (1.5), CR numerical (1.5), Q (1), m (1.5), unit comment (0.5).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Electrochemical Aspects Questions:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Mix-Up in Nernst Equation** — Writing the log term with the **oxidized form in the denominator** (correct) but forgetting to invert when the reaction is given in the oxidation direction. *Penalty: 1 mark.*
> 2. **Reference Electrode Confusion** — Reporting a potential as "vs SHE" when the question specified "vs SCE", or vice versa. *Penalty: 1–2 marks.*
> 3. **Cathode/Anode Identification Error** — In a galvanic couple, students frequently *swap* the anode and cathode. Always: **Anode is the more active (more negative $E^\circ$) member.**
> 4. **EMF Series vs. Galvanic Series** — Using the EMF series to predict the behaviour of commercial alloys in real environments. *Penalty: full mark loss on that sub-part.*
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency in Corrosion Rate** — Mixing µA cm⁻² with ρ in kg m⁻³. Convert all to **cgs** before applying the 3.27 × 10⁻³ constant, OR use SI-consistent constants. *Penalty: 1 mark.*
> 6. **Forgetting to Square the Number of Electrons** — In Faraday's second law, $EW = AW/n$ where $n$ is the **number of electrons transferred per atom**, not the valency in the crystal structure. *Penalty: 1 mark.*
> 7. **Oxygen vs. Hydrogen Cathode in Neutral Solutions** — Defaulting to $2H^+ + 2e^- \to H_2$ in neutral water. The correct dominant reaction is **$O_2$ reduction** in aerated conditions. *Penalty: 1–2 marks.*
> 8. **Spontaneity Argument** — Writing $E_{cell} > 0$ but failing to convert to $\Delta G < 0$ explicitly. *Penalty: 0.5 mark.*
> 9. **Block Diagram Sketches** — Drawing the diagram but failing to **label the half-cell reactions** at the anode and cathode. The examiner expects *chemical equations inside or adjacent to the blocks*. *Penalty: 1 mark.*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **For last-minute KTU revision — read this section twice.**

- [x] **Electrochemical corrosion** is the *simultaneous* occurrence of anodic oxidation and cathodic reduction on a metal surface, accompanied by electron flow through the metal and ion flow through the electrolyte.
- [x] The **four components** of an electrochemical cell are: **Anode, Cathode, Electrolyte, Metallic path**.
- [x] **Anode** = site of *metal loss* (oxidation); **Cathode** = site of *reduction* (often $O_2$ or $H^+$). The cathode is *not* consumed.
- [x] The **four common cathode reactions** are $O_2/H^+$ (acid), $O_2/H_2O$ (neutral), $2H^+/H_2$ (acid deaerated), and metal-ion reduction.
- [x] The **Nernst Equation (base 10, 25 °C)** is: $E = E^\circ - (0.0592/n) \log([\text{Red}]/[\text{Ox}])$.
- [x] The **Nernst Equation (general)** is: $E = E^\circ - (RT/nF) \ln([\text{Red}]/[\text{Ox}])$.
- [x] **Cell EMF** = $E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ (both in same reference scale).
- [x] **Spontaneity:** $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$. If $E_{cell} > 0$, corrosion proceeds spontaneously.
- [x] **Faraday's First Law:** $m = (EW \cdot I \cdot t)/F$. **Second Law:** $m \propto EW$.
- [x] **Equivalent Weight** $EW = AW/n$ (where $n$ = electrons transferred per atom).
- [x] **Corrosion rate formula** (metric): $CR\ (\text{mm/yr}) = (3.27 \times 10^{-3} \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW)/\rho$.
- [x] **EMF Series** = thermodynamic, pure elements, standard state. **Galvanic Series** = empirical, real alloys, real environment.
- [x] **Reference electrode conversions:** $E_{SHE} = E_{SCE} + 0.244 = E_{Ag/AgCl} + 0.222 = E_{CSE} - 0.318$ (for sat. KCl).
- [x] The **most severe corrosion** occurs in **acidic, aerated, high-chloride** environments due to the highest cathodic potential.
- [x] **Differential aeration corrosion** (e.g., under a water drop) is a classic KTU example of a localised galvanic cell on a *single* metal.
- [x] **Cathodic protection** uses the electrochemical series logic: couple a more active metal (Zn, Mg, Al) to protect Fe.
- [x] Always write **anode first (oxidation)**, **cathode second (reduction)** in net-cell equations.
- [x] **Tafel slopes, polarisation resistance, and Evans diagrams** belong to *kinetics* (Module 2) — but the *thermodynamic* starting point is the EMF/Galvanic Series, which is Module 1 territory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Environmental Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Environmental Effects in Corrosion Engineering** refers to the systematic study of how ambient physical, chemical, and biological parameters of the surrounding medium (atmosphere, soil, water, or process environment) alter the rate, mechanism, and morphology of metallic degradation. The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes these effects under **Module 1** to establish the foundational understanding that corrosion is rarely an intrinsic material property—it is a *coupled response* between the material and its thermodynamic/kinetic environment.

The key environmental variables include:
- **Moisture / Relative Humidity (RH)** — Critical threshold typically at **60% RH**.
- **Temperature** — Governs Arrhenius-type rate acceleration.
- **Atmospheric Pollutants** — SO₂, NOₓ, Cl⁻, H₂S, CO₂, NH₃.
- **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** — Cathodic depolarizer in neutral/aerobic corrosion.
- **pH of Electrolyte** — Determines Pourbaix domain of stability.
- **Biological Activity** — Microbial colonization leading to MIC.
- **Soil Resistivity** — Macro-cell corrosion in buried structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Environmental effects are NOT just "weathering." They are quantified electrochemical drivers that shift the corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$), exchange current density ($i_0$), and limiting diffusion current ($i_L$) — all of which are **examinable concepts** under Course Outcome **CO1**: *"Understand the fundamental mechanisms and environmental factors influencing corrosion."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a metal surface as a **"patient"** lying in a hospital bed. The **metal** itself is the patient's body (genetic predisposition to disease). The **environment** (humidity, temperature, pollutants) is the **hospital room's atmosphere** — a room filled with germs (Cl⁻, SO₂) accelerates sickness; a clean, dry, cool room keeps the patient stable. Corrosion engineers are the **"doctors"** who diagnose the room (environment) and prescribe protective coatings, inhibitors, or cathodic protection. 

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** "Metal doesn't corrode *because* it is weak — it corrodes because its **surroundings feed electrons away from it** at a faster rate than it can replenish them."

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (KTU Must-Know)
- **Corrosion Rate (CR)** — typically expressed in **mm/year (mmpy)** or **mils per year (mpy)**.
- **Atmospheric Corrosivity Categories** — defined by ISO 9223: **C1 (Very Low)** to **CX (Extreme)**.
- **Critical Humidity** — minimum RH (~60%) at which a continuous electrolyte film forms.
- **Tafel Slope** — β_a, β_c, expressed in **V/decade** (typically 0.06 to 0.12 V).
- **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** — Reference at **E° = 0.000 V** at **25°C (298.15 K)**.
- **Faraday's Constant** — **F = 96,485 C/mol** ≈ **96,500 C/mol**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major Environmental Parameters — Structured Logic

#### A. Effect of Moisture and Relative Humidity
1. **Why it matters:** All aqueous corrosion requires an electrolyte. A dry atmosphere (< 30% RH) cannot sustain ionic conduction.
2. **How it acts:** Above the **critical humidity (~60% RH)**, a thin adsorbed water film (≈ 1–10 molecular layers thick) condenses on the metal, dissolving pollutants (SO₂, Cl⁻) and forming a conductive electrolyte.
3. **Threshold Behavior:** Corrosion rate vs. RH shows a **non-linear "knee"** — negligible below 60% RH, exponentially increasing above 70% RH.
4. **Time of Wetness (TOW):** Per **ISO 9223**, TOW is the number of hours per year when RH > 80% AND T > 0°C. It is a primary climatic input for corrosion prediction.

#### B. Effect of Temperature
1. **Arrhenius Relationship:** $k = A \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ — every 10°C rise roughly **doubles** the corrosion rate in aqueous systems (Rule of Thumb).
2. **Paradox in Atmospheric Corrosion:** Increased temperature can *decrease* atmospheric corrosion because it **evaporates the electrolyte film**, lowering TOW. This is why tropical coastal regions (high RH, moderate T) are more corrosive than hot dry deserts.

#### C. Effect of Dissolved Oxygen (DO)
1. DO is the **primary cathodic reactant** in neutral/alkaline media: $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$.
2. Corrosion rate is **diffusion-controlled** — limited by oxygen transport across the boundary layer.
3. **Stagnant water → lower DO** (top of tank); **flowing water → higher DO** but also removes protective scales.

#### D. Effect of Atmospheric Pollutants
1. **SO₂ (Sulfur Dioxide):** The most studied pollutant. Adsorbs on metal, oxidizes to H₂SO₄ via catalytic action of metal oxides (especially Cu, Fe). Mechanism: $\text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_3 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$.
2. **Cl⁻ (Chlorides):** Marine aerosol, de-icing salts. Aggressively breaks down passive films (e.g., on stainless steel — **pitting corrosion**). Radius of Cl⁻ is small (0.181 nm) → penetrates oxide defects.
3. **NOₓ:** Forms HNO₃ in moisture; similar to SO₂ but weaker.
4. **H₂S:** Sour service environments (oil & gas); causes **hydrogen blistering, sulfide stress cracking (SSC)**.

#### E. Effect of pH
1. **Acidic (pH < 4):** Hydrogen evolution dominates; rate rises sharply as H⁺ concentration increases.
2. **Neutral (pH 4–9):** Oxygen reduction dominates; rate roughly **independent of pH**.
3. **Alkaline (pH > 9):** Passivation of Fe, Zn, Al — corrosion rate drops as protective hydroxide film forms.

#### F. Effect of Biological Activity (MIC)
1. **Sulfate-Reducing Bacteria (SRB)** in anaerobic soils/water: $ \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 8\text{H}^+ + 8e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{S} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} $.
2. Produces localized aggressive anodes under tubercles/deposits.

#### G. Effect of Soil (Buried Corrosion)
1. Soil acts as a **heterogeneous electrolyte** with varying resistivity.
2. **Differential aeration cells** form where a pipeline crosses from wet clay (low O₂) to dry sand (high O₂).
3. **Soil resistivity (Ω·cm)** is the most important single parameter: < 1000 Ω·cm → highly corrosive; > 10,000 Ω·cm → mildly corrosive.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G = -nFE$ | Spontaneity criterion | J/mol |
| 2 | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | Nernst Equation | V (vs SHE) |
| 3 | $k = A \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius Rate Law | varies; $E_a$ in J/mol |
| 4 | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | Stern-Geary Equation | A/cm² |
| 5 | $R_p = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\Delta i}$ | Polarization Resistance | Ω·cm² |
| 6 | $CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \times i_{corr} \times EW}{\rho}$ | Corrosion Rate from $i_{corr}$ | mm/year |
| 7 | $EW = \dfrac{\text{Atomic Weight}}{n}$ | Equivalent Weight | g/eq |
| 8 | $i_L = \dfrac{n F D C}{\delta}$ | Limiting Current (diffusion) | A/cm² |
| 9 | $R_a = R_1 (1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ | Atmospheric corrosion (linear, ISO 9223) | µm/year |
| 10 | $V_{corr} = \dfrac{M \cdot i_{corr}}{n F \rho}$ | Volume loss rate | cm³/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Notation:** In the table above, $\Delta G$ is Gibbs free energy change, $n$ is electron count per reaction, $E^\circ$ is standard potential, $Q$ is the reaction quotient, $D$ is diffusivity, $C$ is bulk concentration, $\delta$ is the Nernst diffusion layer thickness, and $\alpha$ is the ISO 9223 environmental factor. **Do not confuse** $R_p$ (polarization resistance) with $R$ (gas constant) in exam scripts.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Infrastructure Design:** Selection of steel reinforcement in coastal bridges (Cl⁻ exposure classification per IS 456 / ACI 318).
- **Oil & Gas Industry:** Defining "sour service" (H₂S partial pressure > 0.0003 MPa) per NACE MR0175.
- **Atmospheric Corrosion Mapping:** ISO 9223 used globally for zinc, copper, steel coating selection (e.g., galvanizing thickness).
- **Cathodic Protection Design:** Soil resistivity surveys dictate anode spacing in buried pipelines.
- **Failure Analysis:** Estimating remaining life of heritage structures (e.g., corroding iron beams in Victorian buildings) by correlating TOW and SO₂ deposition.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Stern-Geary Equation (Linear Polarization)

The Stern-Geary equation is the **most frequently tested derivation** in KTU exams for any corrosion module. It links the measurable polarization resistance $R_p$ to the corrosion current density $i_{corr}$.

**Starting Point:** Near the corrosion potential $E_{corr}$, the Butler-Volmer equation can be linearized because the overpotential $\eta = E - E_{corr}$ is small ($|\eta| < 10$ mV).

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= i_{corr}\left[\exp\left(\dfrac{\eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\left(-\dfrac{\eta}{\beta_c}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply Taylor Series Expansion** for small $\eta$ (since $e^x \approx 1 + x$ for small x):

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &\approx i_{corr}\left[\left(1 + \dfrac{\eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \left(1 - \dfrac{\eta}{\beta_c}\right)\right] \\
i &\approx i_{corr}\left[\dfrac{\eta}{\beta_a} + \dfrac{\eta}{\beta_c}\right] \\
i &\approx i_{corr} \cdot \eta \left[\dfrac{1}{\beta_a} + \dfrac{1}{\beta_c}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for Polarization Resistance.** By definition, $R_p = \dfrac{d\eta}{di}\Big|_{\eta \to 0}$. From the equation above:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{di}{d\eta} &= i_{corr}\left[\dfrac{1}{\beta_a} + \dfrac{1}{\beta_c}\right] \\
R_p &= \dfrac{1}{i_{corr}\left[\dfrac{1}{\beta_a} + \dfrac{1}{\beta_c}\right]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine the fractions** (note: $\beta$ values are in Volts; convert to log form using the 2.303 factor from $\ln 10$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_p &= \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot i_{corr} (\beta_a + \beta_c)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Form (Stern-Geary Equation):**

$$
\boxed{i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \, R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}}
$$

This is the **working equation** examiners expect you to write and use. A typical problem gives you $R_p$ and the Tafel slopes to compute $i_{corr}$, then converts $i_{corr}$ to corrosion rate.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Corrosion Rate from $i_{corr}$ (Faraday's Law)

We use Faraday's law of electrolysis: **Mass lost** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$.

**Step 1 — Express mass loss per unit area per unit time** as the linear corrosion rate $CR$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR \text{ (cm/s)} &= \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t} = \dfrac{I \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A} = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $i_{corr} = I/A$ and $\rho$ is the metal density.

**Step 2 — Introduce Equivalent Weight** $EW = M/n$ to simplify:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR \text{ (cm/s)} &= \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot EW}{F \cdot \rho}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mm/year** (engineering units used by KTU board):
- 1 cm/s = $10 \times 86400 \times 365$ mm/year $= 3.1536 \times 10^8$ mm/year.
- Multiply by $10$ (cm to mm) and include Faraday's constant.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR \text{ (mm/year)} &= \dfrac{i_{corr} \left(\dfrac{A}{cm^2}\right) \times EW \left(\dfrac{g}{eq}\right) \times 3.1536 \times 10^8 \text{ mm/yr per cm/s}}{96{,}500 \text{ C/eq} \times \rho \left(\dfrac{g}{cm^3}\right)} \times \text{unit conversions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Practical Constant.** When $i_{corr}$ is in **µA/cm²** and $\rho$ in g/cm³:

$$
\boxed{CR = 0.00327 \times \dfrac{i_{corr} \times EW}{\rho} \quad \text{(mm/year)}}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU typical):**
Given: Iron in aerated neutral water, $i_{corr} = 10\,\mu A/cm^2$. Find CR.
- $M_{Fe} = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho_{Fe} = 7.87$ g/cm³.
- $EW = 55.85 / 2 = 27.925$ g/eq.
- $CR = 0.00327 \times \dfrac{10 \times 27.925}{7.87}$.
- $CR = 0.00327 \times \dfrac{279.25}{7.87}$.
- $CR = 0.00327 \times 35.482$.
- $\boxed{CR = 0.116 \text{ mm/year} \approx 0.12 \text{ mmpy}}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Limiting Current for Oxygen Diffusion (Cathode Control)

For a planar metal surface in aerated water, oxygen reduction is the cathodic reaction. The rate is limited by how fast O₂ can diffuse across the boundary layer (Nernst diffusion layer of thickness $\delta$).

**Step 1 — Fick's First Law at the interface:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_{O_2} = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx} \approx -D \dfrac{(C_b - C_s)}{\delta} = D \dfrac{C_b}{\delta}
\end{aligned}
$$

(assuming $C_s \to 0$ at the surface — fully depleted).

**Step 2 — Convert flux to current density** using Faraday's law (each O₂ consumes $n = 4$ electrons):

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_L = n F J_{O_2} = \dfrac{n F D C_b}{\delta}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{i_L = \dfrac{n F D C_b}{\delta}}
$$

**Step 3 — Insight:** Since $i_{corr} \leq i_L$, environments with **stagnant water (large $\delta$)** have lower corrosion, while **moderately agitated water (small $\delta$)** increases corrosion — but excessive agitation may strip protective films, leading to impingement/erosion-corrosion.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of pH on Iron (KTU Table Favourite)

| pH Range | Dominant Cathodic Reaction | Corrosion Behaviour | Fe Species Formed |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 – 4 | $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$ | Active; rate ∝ [H⁺] | $Fe^{2+}$ (soluble) |
| 4 – 9 | $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$ | Oxygen-controlled; rate ≈ const | $Fe(OH)_2$ (porous) |
| 9 – 12 | $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$ | Decreasing; passivation onset | $Fe_2O_3 \cdot H_2O$ |
| 12 – 14 | $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$ | Passive; stable film | $Fe_2O_3$ / $Fe_3O_4$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Atmospheric Corrosion Mechanism (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Atmosphere Exposure] --> B[Moisture Adsorption Film Formation]
    B --> C{RH above 60 percent threshold}
    C -- No --> D[Negligible Corrosion Dry Regime]
    C -- Yes --> E[Thin Electrolyte Film Forms]
    E --> F[Pollutant Dissolution SO2 Cl- NOx]
    F --> G[Anodic Sites M to Mn+ ne-]
    F --> H[Cathodic Sites O2 Reduction]
    G --> I[Metal Ion Diffusion]
    H --> J[OH- Production]
    I --> K[Corrosion Product Rust Patina]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Product Layer May be Protective or Porous]
    L --> M{Porous Layer}
    M -- Yes --> N[Continued Active Corrosion]
    M -- No --> O[Reduced Corrosion Passivation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Begin at node A (atmospheric exposure), traverse down through the moisture adsorption step, branch at the critical RH check, then split into parallel anodic/cathodic electrochemical branches that finally converge at the corrosion product formation. The final decision node (M) determines whether the product layer is **protective** (e.g., Al₂O₃ on aluminum, patina on copper) or **non-protective** (e.g., rust on carbon steel), dramatically altering the long-term corrosion rate.

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Differential Aeration Cell in Soil (Buried Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pipeline Section in Clay Soil Low O2] -->|Electrons Flow| B[Anode Corrodes Aggressively]
    B --> C[Metal Dissolution Fe to Fe2+]
    D[Pipeline Section in Sandy Soil High O2] -->|Electron Sink| E[Cathode Protected]
    E --> F[Oxygen Reduction to OH-]
    B -.->|Ionic Path Through Soil Electrolyte| E
    C --> G[Pit Formation at Anodic Zone]
    F --> H[Alkaline Zone at Cathodic Zone]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Connection:** This is precisely why **cast iron water mains** buried beneath roads (compacted clay, low O₂) connected to **septic fields** (aerated soil) fail prematurely at the transition zones. Engineers counter this by applying **cathodic protection** with sacrificial anodes (Zn or Mg) or impressed current systems.

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Effect of Environmental Parameters on Corrosion Rate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Corrosion System] --> T1[Temperature]
    Start --> T2[Relative Humidity]
    Start --> T3[Dissolved Oxygen]
    Start --> T4[pH of Electrolyte]
    Start --> T5[Pollutant Concentration]
    Start --> T6[Flow Velocity]
    Start --> T7[Biological Activity]
    T1 --> R1[Arrhenius Acceleration generally increases]
    T2 --> R2[Knee Behaviour above 60 percent RH]
    T3 --> R3[Diffusion Limited Cathode Control]
    T4 --> R4[Pourbaix Domain Shift]
    T5 --> R5[Localized Attack Pitting Crevice]
    T6 --> R6[Erosion Corrosion Threshold]
    T7 --> R7[MIC Localized Anodes]
    R1 --> Final[Net Corrosion Rate mm per year]
    R2 --> Final
    R3 --> Final
    R4 --> Final
    R5 --> Final
    R6 --> Final
    R7 --> Final
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*Define "Time of Wetness" (TOW) as per ISO 9223. Why is it a critical parameter in atmospheric corrosion prediction?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Time of Wetness (TOW) is defined as the **number of hours per year** during which the **relative humidity exceeds 80% AND the temperature is above 0°C**. It is a critical parameter in ISO 9223 because it directly quantifies the duration during which an electrolyte film can exist on a metal surface, enabling ionic conduction and electrochemical corrosion. TOW is one of the **three primary climatic inputs** (along with SO₂ deposition and Cl⁻ deposition) used to classify atmospheric corrosivity into categories **C1 to CX**. Regions with high TOW (e.g., coastal Kerala with TOW > 3000 hr/year) are predicted to exhibit much higher corrosion rates of carbon steel and zinc coatings than arid regions, even if pollutant levels are identical.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
*Why does the corrosion rate of iron in aerated water show a plateau between pH 4 and pH 9?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Between pH 4 and pH 9, the dominant cathodic reaction in aerated water shifts from hydrogen evolution ($2H^+ + 2e^- \to H_2$) to **oxygen reduction** ($O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \to 4OH^-$). In this pH range, the cathodic rate is controlled by the **diffusion-limited supply of dissolved oxygen** to the metal surface, not by the concentration of H⁺ ions. Since DO concentration in equilibrium with air is nearly constant (≈ 8 ppm at 25°C), the cathodic current density $i_L$ — and consequently $i_{corr}$ — remains approximately **constant and pH-independent**. Only at pH < 4 (acidic) does H⁺ reduction contribute, raising the rate, and at pH > 9 does passivation begin, lowering the rate.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **DO NOT** skip the unit conversions in corrosion rate problems. Marks are specifically reserved for showing the numerical setup.
> - **DO NOT** confuse $\beta_a$ (anodic Tafel slope) with $\beta_c$ (cathodic Tafel slope) — examiners often use different values to test attention.
> - **DO NOT** omit the reference electrode when reporting potentials. Always state "V vs. SHE" or "V vs. SCE".
> - **DO NOT** use the term "galvanic corrosion" when describing differential aeration — they are different mechanisms.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**A(a).** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the mechanism of atmospheric corrosion of iron, highlighting the role of **relative humidity**, **SO₂ pollutant**, and the **anode/cathode reactions**. Comment on the protective nature of rust. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Atmospheric Corrosion of Iron — Mechanism (7 marks):**

Atmospheric corrosion is an electrochemical process occurring within a **thin adsorbed moisture film** on the metal surface. The key requirements are:
1. An electrolyte film (formed above **~60% RH**).
2. An oxidizing agent (typically **dissolved O₂** from air).
3. An ionic path for current flow.

**Step 1 — Adsorption of Moisture:** When RH exceeds 60%, a continuous, microscopically thin water film (≈ 1–10 molecular layers, ~10–100 nm thick) forms on the iron surface via physical adsorption and capillary condensation in micro-crevices. Below this critical humidity, ionic conduction is impossible and corrosion is negligible.

**Step 2 — Anode Reaction (Localized):** At micro-defects, the iron dissolves:
$$\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^- \quad (E^\circ = -0.44\,V \text{ vs SHE})$$

**Step 3 — Cathode Reaction (Distributed):** On adjacent areas covered by the electrolyte:
$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^- \quad (E^\circ = +0.40\,V \text{ vs SHE})$$

**Step 4 — Role of SO₂:** Sulfur dioxide is the most studied atmospheric pollutant. It accelerates corrosion by:
- Adsorbing on the rust surface, which acts as a **catalyst** (Fe³⁺ ions facilitate SO₂ oxidation).
- Oxidizing to sulfuric acid: $\text{SO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_3$; then $\text{SO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$.
- The acid lowers surface pH (< 4), dramatically accelerating Fe²⁺ release.
- Each 1 mg/m³ increase in SO₂ can increase the corrosion rate of steel by 2–4×.

**Step 5 — Rust Formation and Its Nature:** The intermediate products are:
$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_2$$
$$4\text{Fe(OH)}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4\text{Fe(OH)}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O} \text{ (rust)}$$

**Protective Nature of Rust:** Unlike Al₂O₃ (on aluminum) or patina (on copper, CuCO₃·Cu(OH)₂), **rust is non-protective** for iron because:
- It is **porous and flaky**, allowing continued O₂ and moisture penetration.
- It is **non-adherent** — easily spalls off.
- It contains cracks that create differential aeration cells, leading to pitting.

**Conclusion:** Atmospheric corrosion of iron is autocatalytic — once initiated, the porous rust layer actually **accelerates** further corrosion by trapping moisture and pollutants. This is why exposed carbon steel structures (bridges, ships, railway tracks) require protective coatings.

---

**A(b).** A carbon steel component ($\rho = 7.87$ g/cm³, $M = 55.85$ g/mol) is exposed to seawater. A linear polarization test yields $R_p = 4500\,\Omega\cdot cm^2$ with Tafel slopes $\beta_a = 0.090$ V/dec and $\beta_c = 0.110$ V/dec. Calculate the **corrosion current density** $i_{corr}$ and the **corrosion rate in mm/year**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Apply Stern-Geary Equation:**
$$
i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \, R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}
$$

Substituting values (note: $\beta$ values in volts):
$$
i_{corr} = \dfrac{(0.090)(0.110)}{2.303 \times 4500 \times (0.090 + 0.110)}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerator:**
$$
0.090 \times 0.110 = 0.0099 \text{ V}^2/\text{dec}
$$

**Step 3 — Denominator:**
$$
2.303 \times 4500 \times 0.200 = 2.303 \times 900 = 2072.7 \text{ V}\cdot\Omega\cdot cm^2
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $i_{corr}$:**
$$
i_{corr} = \dfrac{0.0099}{2072.7} = 4.776 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A/cm}^2 = 4.78\,\mu A/cm^2
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Equivalent Weight:**
For Fe, $n = 2$:
$$
EW = \dfrac{55.85}{2} = 27.925 \text{ g/eq}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Corrosion Rate Formula (with $i_{corr}$ in µA/cm²):**
$$
CR = 0.00327 \times \dfrac{i_{corr} \times EW}{\rho}
$$
$$
CR = 0.00327 \times \dfrac{4.776 \times 27.925}{7.87}
$$
$$
CR = 0.00327 \times \dfrac{133.36}{7.87} = 0.00327 \times 16.945
$$
$$
\boxed{CR = 0.0554 \text{ mm/year} \approx 0.055 \text{ mmpy}}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark worth within 7):** This CR corresponds to **moderate corrosivity** (per NACE classifications, CR between 0.025 and 0.12 mmpy is "moderate"). For seawater exposure, the result suggests reasonable corrosion resistance, likely due to protective calcareous deposit formation (cathodic scaling) on the steel surface.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]*

**B(a).** Explain **differential aeration corrosion** with a suitable diagram. Discuss the role of **soil resistivity** and **differential aeration** in the corrosion of buried pipelines. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Differential Aeration Corrosion (4 marks):**
Differential aeration corrosion is a form of localized corrosion that occurs when the **same metal surface is exposed to zones of different oxygen concentration**, separated by a continuous electrolyte.

- The **region with lower O₂** (e.g., under a deposit, in a crevice, or in wet clay) becomes the **anode** and corrodes.
- The **region with higher O₂** (e.g., open surface, dry sand) becomes the **cathode** and is protected.
- The potential difference is typically 0.1 – 0.3 V.

**Mechanism (using oxygen concentration cell theory):**

At the **anode (low O₂)**: $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$

At the **cathode (high O₂)**: $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$

Ionic current flows through the electrolyte from anode to cathode; electronic current flows through the metal from anode to cathode.

**Role of Soil Resistivity (3 marks):**
Soil acts as the electrolyte in buried corrosion. Its resistivity ($\rho_{soil}$ in $\Omega\cdot cm$) depends on moisture content, salt content, and grain size. Per NACE/ISO standards:

| Soil Resistivity ($\Omega\cdot cm$) | Corrosivity |
|---|---|
| < 1,000 | Severe |
| 1,000 – 5,000 | Moderate to Severe |
| 5,000 – 10,000 | Mild |
| > 10,000 | Negligible |

**Application to Buried Pipelines (3 marks within 7):**
- A long-distance pipeline crosses soils of varying resistivity (clay vs. sand, water-logged vs. dry).
- **Macro-cell corrosion** develops: the anodic zone is the **poorly aerated, low-resistivity soil section**; the cathodic zone is the **well-aerated, high-resistivity section**.
- Differential aeration becomes critical at **girth welds, supports, and under rocks/debris** where oxygen is excluded.
- **Counter-measures:**
  1. **Protective coatings** (3LPE, FBE) to isolate the metal from the electrolyte.
  2. **Cathodic protection** (sacrificial Zn/Mg anodes or impressed current) to make the entire pipeline cathodic.
  3. **Backfilling with uniform, low-resistivity material** in the trench.
  4. **Periodic Coupon Testing** (per NACE SP0775) to monitor CR in the field.

---

**B(b).** A magnesium alloy component ($\rho = 1.74$ g/cm³, $M = 24.31$ g/mol, $n = 2$) is exposed to an industrial atmosphere. The corrosion current density measured by Tafel extrapolation is $i_{corr} = 5.2\,\mu A/cm^2$. Calculate:
(i) The equivalent weight.  
(ii) The corrosion rate in **mm/year**.  
(iii) The **mass loss per m² per day**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Equivalent Weight (1 mark):**
$$
EW = \dfrac{M}{n} = \dfrac{24.31}{2} = 12.155 \text{ g/eq}
$$

**(ii) Corrosion Rate in mm/year (3 marks):**
$$
CR = 0.00327 \times \dfrac{i_{corr} \times EW}{\rho}
$$
$$
CR = 0.00327 \times \dfrac{5.2 \times 12.155}{1.74}
$$
$$
CR = 0.00327 \times \dfrac{63.206}{1.74} = 0.00327 \times 36.325
$$
$$
\boxed{CR = 0.1188 \text{ mm/year} \approx 0.119 \text{ mmpy}}
$$

**(iii) Mass Loss per m² per day (3 marks):**

From Faraday's law, mass loss per unit area per unit time:
$$
m' = \dfrac{i_{corr} \times EW}{F}
$$

For 1 day = 86400 s, and 1 m² = 10,000 cm², so $i_{corr}$ in A/m² = $i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \times 10^{-6} \times 10{,}000 = i_{corr} \times 10^{-2}$ A/m².

$$
i_{corr} \text{ in A/m}^2 = 5.2 \times 10^{-2} = 0.052 \text{ A/m}^2
$$
$$
m' = \dfrac{0.052 \times 12.155}{96{,}500} \text{ kg/(m}^2\cdot s\text{)} = 6.55 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/(m}^2\cdot s\text{)}
$$

Per day:
$$
m' = 6.55 \times 10^{-6} \times 86400 = 0.566 \text{ kg/m}^2/\text{day}
$$

Or in grams: $\boxed{m' \approx 566 \text{ g/m}^2/\text{day}}$.

**Verification (using $CR$):**
$CR = 0.119$ mm/yr = $0.119 \times 10^{-3}$ m/yr = $1.19 \times 10^{-4}$ m/yr.
Volume loss per m² per year = $1.19 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/yr.
Mass loss per m² per year = $\rho \times V = 1740 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 1.19 \times 10^{-4} = 0.207$ kg/m²/yr.
Per day: $0.207 / 365 = 0.000567$ kg/m²/day = **567 g/m²/day** ✓ (matches).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Environmental Effects in Corrosion**

- ☐ **Critical Humidity Threshold** = **60% RH** (below this, atmospheric corrosion is negligible).
- ☐ **TOW** = hours/year with RH > 80% AND T > 0°C (per ISO 9223).
- ☐ **Stern-Geary Equation** is the **#1 derivation** in KTU Module 1 — memorize $i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$.
- ☐ **Corrosion Rate Constant** $0.00327$ is valid only when $i_{corr}$ is in **µA/cm²**, $\rho$ in **g/cm³**, giving $CR$ in **mm/year**.
- ☐ **Arrhenius Rule of Thumb:** ~2× rate increase per 10°C rise (in aqueous systems).
- ☐ **pH 4–9 plateau** for iron: rate is **pH-independent** because O₂ reduction is the rate-limiting cathodic reaction.
- ☐ **SO₂** is the most aggressive industrial atmospheric pollutant; catalyzes its own oxidation on rust.
- ☐ **Cl⁻** causes **pitting** of stainless steel by penetrating passive Cr₂O₃ film.
- ☐ **Soil corrosivity** is governed primarily by **resistivity**; < 1000 Ω·cm = severe.
- ☐ **Differential aeration** = anode forms in **low O₂** region, cathode in **high O₂** region.
- ☐ **MIC** by **SRB** produces H₂S → pitting, blistering, SSC in buried/submerged structures.
- ☐ Always report potentials as **V vs. SHE** (or specify the reference electrode).
- ☐ **Units consistency check:** $\beta$ in V (not mV), $R_p$ in $\Omega\cdot cm^2$, $i_{corr}$ in A/cm² (or µA/cm²).
- ☐ **Pourbaix Diagram** for Fe-H₂O system shows **passive zone** above pH ~9.5 (for low potentials) and above pH ~12.5 (for higher potentials).
- ☐ Remember the **three climatic inputs** of ISO 9223: **TOW**, **SO₂ deposition** (mg/m²·day), **Cl⁻ deposition** (mg/m²·day).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition — Metallurgical and Other Aspects of Corrosion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metallurgical aspects of corrosion** refer to the intrinsic properties of the metal/alloy itself — its composition, microstructure, grain size, phase distribution, residual stress, heat treatment history, and surface condition — that govern its susceptibility or resistance to corrosive attack. **Other aspects** encompass the *external* non-chemical environmental variables (mechanical stress, fluid velocity, temperature, biological activity, and radiation) that influence corrosion kinetics independently of the bulk electrolyte composition.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, these factors are categorized under **Module 1** because they form the foundational *intrinsic* and *environmental* variables that every corrosion engineer must quantify **before** selecting materials, coatings, or cathodic protection schemes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Skin & Lifestyle" Model

Imagine a metal as a **person's skin and health combined**:

- **Composition (Alloying)** → Like skin type. Some people burn (corrode) easily under the sun (environment); others tan (form protective oxide). Adding **chromium** to iron is like giving the metal "SPF 50 sunscreen" — it forms a stable Cr₂O₃ layer.
- **Grain Size & Microstructure** → Like the weave of fabric. Tightly woven (fine-grained) fabric resists tears better than loosely woven cloth. Similarly, fine-grained metals generally offer more grain boundary area, but specific phases matter.
- **Cold Work & Residual Stress** → Like a stressed, tight muscle. Stressed regions crack under load (stress corrosion cracking).
- **Heat Treatment** → Like physiotherapy — it reorganizes the internal structure, relieving stress or hardening it.
- **Velocity of Fluid** → Like wind erosion on a cliff face. Stagnant water is gentle; rushing water blasts the protective film away.
- **Biological Activity** → Like termites eating wood silently — microbes can destroy metals underground (MICC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** A corrosion engineer **cannot** control the environment easily, but **can** control metallurgical factors during material selection. Hence, this topic is rated *high-yield* for Part A (3-mark) questions.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used Throughout

- **Corrosion Rate (CR)** in **mm/year (mmpy)** or **mils per year (mpy)**.
- **Grain size number (G)** as per ASTM E112.
- **Hardness** in **HV (Vickers)** or **HRC (Rockwell C)**.
- **Stress (σ)** in **MPa** or **N/mm²**.
- **Velocity (v)** in **m/s** or **ft/s**.
- **Temperature (T)** in **°C** or **K**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Factors Affecting Corrosion

Corrosion-influencing factors are grouped into **three families**:

### Family A — Metallurgical (Intrinsic) Factors

1. **Composition of metal/alloy** — purity, alloying additions.
2. **Microstructure** — grain size, phase distribution, inclusions.
3. **Cold working / Mechanical working** — introduces dislocations and residual stress.
4. **Heat treatment** — annealing, quenching, tempering, precipitation hardening.
5. **Welding** — creates HAZ (Heat Affected Zone) with metallurgical heterogeneity.
6. **Surface condition** — roughness, polishing, pre-existing films.

### Family B — Environmental (Other) Factors

1. **Temperature** — accelerates electrochemical kinetics (Arrhenius).
2. **Velocity of flowing media** — affects mass transport, erosion-corrosion.
3. **Stress** — Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC), Corrosion Fatigue (CF).
4. **Biological organisms** — Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC).
5. **Radiation** — affects passivity in nuclear environments.
6. **Solar radiation / UV** — degrades polymeric coatings.

### Family C — Design Factors (sometimes clubbed with "Other")

1. **Geometry** — crevices, dead zones, contact with dissimilar metals.
2. **Galvanic coupling** — different metals in electrical contact.

## 2.2 Detailed Mechanism Analysis — The "Why" Behind Each Factor

### 2.2.1 Effect of Composition
- **Pure metals** are usually more corrosion-resistant than impure metals because impurities act as local cathodes, creating micro-galvanic cells.
- **Alloying additions** can:
  - Form protective oxide films (**Cr → Cr₂O₃**, **Al → Al₂O₃**, **Si → SiO₂**).
  - Shift the alloy into the **passive region** of the Pourbaix diagram.
  - Example: Adding **>10.5% Cr** to Fe produces **stainless steel** (Type 430 is ferritic, 304 is austenitic with Ni).

### 2.2.2 Effect of Grain Size
- **Fine-grained metals** have **larger total grain boundary area**.
- Grain boundaries are regions of higher energy → act as **anodic sites** in many environments → preferential intergranular corrosion.
- However, fine grains also mean **more uniform distribution** of any precipitates, reducing localized attack in some alloys.
- **Empirical relationship (Petch-like for corrosion):**
  $$ CR \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{d}} $$
  where $d$ is the average grain diameter. (Smaller $d$ → higher CR in many cases.)

### 2.2.3 Effect of Cold Work
- Cold work increases **dislocation density** and **residual internal stress**.
- Dislocations raise the internal energy → metal becomes **thermodynamically more reactive** → more anodic.
- Cold-worked regions act as **anodes** relative to annealed regions → **galvanic micro-cell formation**.
- Also promotes **SCC** and **hydrogen embrittlement** susceptibility.

### 2.2.4 Effect of Heat Treatment
- **Quenching** → supersaturated solid solution → higher internal energy → more corrosion.
- **Annealing** → relieves stress, allows equilibrium phases → reduces uniform corrosion.
- **Sensitization** (in austenitic SS, 500–800 °C): **Cr-depleted zones** form at grain boundaries due to **Cr₂₃C₆ precipitation** → **intergranular corrosion (IGC)**. The classic case is **Type 304 SS** after welding.

### 2.2.5 Effect of Stress (Mechanical)
- **Static tensile stress + corrosive environment** → **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)**.
- **Cyclic stress + corrosive environment** → **Corrosion Fatigue (CF)**.
- Threshold stress: **K_ISCC** (Stress Corrosion Cracking threshold stress intensity factor).
- Classic examples: Caustic embrittlement of boiler steel, season cracking of brass.

### 2.2.6 Effect of Velocity
- **Low velocity** → diffusion-controlled corrosion, often uniform.
- **High velocity** → disrupts protective film → **erosion-corrosion** (e.g., impingement attack in condenser tubes).
- **Critical velocity** exists above which protective films cannot re-form.
- Beyond a certain velocity: **cavitation corrosion** (vapor bubble collapse damages metal).

### 2.2.7 Effect of Temperature
- Temperature increases both **anodic dissolution rate** and **oxygen diffusion rate**.
- Arrhenius-type behavior:
  $$ \ln(CR) = \ln(A) - \frac{E_a}{RT} $$
  where $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = absolute temperature (K).
- Rule of thumb: corrosion rate **doubles** for every **10 °C** rise in many neutral aqueous systems.

### 2.2.8 Effect of Biological Factors
- **Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC)** — bacteria produce localized cells.
- **Sulfate-Reducing Bacteria (SRB)** in anaerobic soils → produce H₂S → attack Fe and low-alloy steels.
- **Iron-oxidizing bacteria** → tubercles in water pipelines.
- **Algae/fungi** → produce organic acids under deposits.

## 2.3 Passivity — The Most Exam-Critical Sub-Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Passivity** is the *loss of chemical reactivity* shown by certain metals (Fe, Cr, Ni, Ti, Al, stainless steels) under conditions where the bulk thermodynamics predicts active dissolution. The metal forms an **invisible, thin (1–10 nm) oxide film** that dramatically lowers the corrosion current.

### 2.3.1 Anodic Behavior of a Passivating Metal
A passivating metal shows a characteristic **anodic polarization curve** with four regions:

| Region | Name | Behavior | Current |
|--------|------|----------|---------|
| **A → B** | Active | Metal dissolves actively | Rises |
| **B → C** | Active-Passive Transition | Passive film nucleates | Falls sharply |
| **C → D** | Passive | Film stable, low current | Nearly constant ($i_{passive}$) |
| **D → E** | Transpassive | Film breaks down (e.g., Cr⁶⁺ formation) | Rises again |

Critical parameters:
- **Primary Passivation Potential (E_pp)** at point B
- **Critical (Passive) Current Density ($i_{crit}$)** at point B
- **Passive Current Density ($i_{pass}$)** along CD
- **Flade Potential (E_F)** — the potential below which passive film dissolves

### 2.3.2 Theories of Passivation

#### Theory 1 — Oxide Film Theory (Evans, Langmuir)
- A **continuous, adherent, thin oxide layer** physically separates metal from environment.
- Growth of film follows **inverse logarithmic** or **logarithmic** law (Cabrera-Mott model for thin films).
- **High-field ion conduction** mechanism for film growth:
  $$ i = A \exp\left(\frac{\alpha \cdot E_{field}}{kT}\right) $$
  where $E_{field}$ is the electric field across the film (≈ 10⁶ V/cm), $\alpha$ = half-jump distance.

#### Theory 2 — Adsorption Theory (Hedges, Uhlig)
- Passivation arises from **chemisorbed oxygen** (monolayer) on the metal surface, not a thick oxide.
- Oxygen atoms share electrons with metal → surface electronic structure modified → blocks anodic dissolution.
- Explains passivity of metals where bulk oxide is non-existent or non-protective.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU exam:** Modern view considers **both** mechanisms operative — chemisorbed monolayer initiates passivity, and oxide film thickens on top of it.

### 2.3.3 Passivators (Inhibitors that Promote Passivity)
- Oxidizing anions: **CrO₄²⁻, MoO₄²⁻, WO₄²⁻, NO₂⁻, NO₃⁻** for Fe.
- These shift the corrosion potential into the passive region.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $CR = \frac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | Corrosion rate from Tafel | mmpy |
| 2 | $i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | Stern-Geary (linear polarization) | A/cm² |
| 3 | $EW = \frac{W}{n}$ (W = atomic weight, n = electrons) | Equivalent weight | g/eq |
| 4 | $\ln(CR) = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{RT}$ | Arrhenius (temperature effect) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $v_{crit} = f(\rho, \mu, d)$ | Critical velocity for erosion | m/s |
| 6 | $i = A \exp(\alpha E_{field}/kT)$ | High-field ion conduction | A/cm² |
| 7 | $\sigma_{th} < K_{ISCC}$ | SCC threshold condition | MPa·√m |
| 8 | $\Delta E_{couple} = E_c - E_a$ | Galvanic driving force | V |
| 9 | $E_F = E^0 - 0.059 \cdot pH$ (for some oxides) | Flade potential vs pH | V |
| 10 | $CR \propto 1/\sqrt{d}$ | Grain size influence | mmpy |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Corrosion Rate from Tafel Slopes

**Given:** Cathodic Tafel slope $\beta_c$, anodic Tafel slope $\beta_a$ (in V/decade), and linear polarization resistance $R_p$ ($\Omega \cdot cm^2$).

**Find:** Corrosion current density $i_{corr}$.

**Step 1: Butler-Volmer Equation (Activation-Controlled Corrosion)**

For a corroding metal, the net current is the sum of anodic and cathodic partial currents. Near $E_{corr}$:

$$ i = i_{corr} \left[ \exp\left(\frac{2.303(E - E_{corr})}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\left(\frac{-2.303(E - E_{corr})}{\beta_c}\right) \right] $$

This is the **Butler-Volmer** expression.

**Step 2: Linearization for Small Polarization ($\vert \eta \vert < 10$ mV)**

For small overpotential $\eta = E - E_{corr}$, expand exponentials using Taylor series: $e^x \approx 1 + x$.

$$ i \approx i_{corr} \left[ 1 + \frac{2.303\eta}{\beta_a} - 1 + \frac{2.303\eta}{\beta_c} \right] $$

$$ i \approx i_{corr} \cdot 2.303 \cdot \eta \left[ \frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c} \right] $$

**Step 3: Define Polarization Resistance**

$$ R_p = \frac{\eta}{i} = \frac{1}{i_{corr} \cdot 2.303 \left( \frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c} \right)} $$

**Step 4: Solve for $i_{corr}$ (Stern-Geary Equation)**

$$ i_{corr} = \frac{1}{2.303 \cdot R_p \left( \frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c} \right)} $$

$$ i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot R_p \cdot (\beta_a + \beta_c)} $$

**Step 5: Convert to Corrosion Rate (mmpy)**

$$ CR \text{ (mmpy)} = \frac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho} $$

where $i_{corr}$ is in $\mu A/cm^2$, $EW$ in g/eq, $\rho$ in g/cm³.

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **0.00327** combines Faraday's constant (96485 C/mol), unit conversions, and the 10⁻⁶ factor from $\mu A$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Temperature Effect

**Problem:** A carbon steel pipe shows CR = 0.5 mmpy at 30 °C. Using $E_a = 40$ kJ/mol, estimate CR at 60 °C.

**Step 1:** Convert temperatures to Kelvin.
$$ T_1 = 30 + 273 = 303 \text{ K}, \quad T_2 = 60 + 273 = 333 \text{ K} $$

**Step 2:** Apply Arrhenius ratio.
$$ \ln\left(\frac{CR_2}{CR_1}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) $$

$$ \ln\left(\frac{CR_2}{0.5}\right) = -\frac{40000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{333} - \frac{1}{303}\right) $$

$$ \ln\left(\frac{CR_2}{0.5}\right) = -4811.6 \times (0.003003 - 0.003300) $$

$$ \ln\left(\frac{CR_2}{0.5}\right) = -4811.6 \times (-0.000297) = 1.429 $$

$$ \frac{CR_2}{0.5} = e^{1.429} = 4.175 $$

**Step 3:** Solve for $CR_2$.
$$ CR_2 = 0.5 \times 4.175 = 2.09 \text{ mmpy} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A 30 °C temperature rise **quadruples** the corrosion rate — close to the empirical "doubles per 10 °C" rule-of-thumb.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Each Factor Modifies Corrosion

| Factor | Direction of Effect | Dominant Mechanism | Counter-Measure |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| ↑ Alloy purity (Fe) | ↓ CR | Fewer micro-cathodes | Use high-purity metals |
| ↑ Cr content (>10.5%) | ↓↓ CR (onset of passivity) | Cr₂O₃ film | Stainless steel selection |
| ↑ Cold work | ↑ CR | Dislocations, residual stress | Stress-relief anneal |
| ↑ Quench severity | ↑ CR | Supersaturated, strained | Tempering |
| ↑ Sensitization (500–800 °C) | ↑↑ IGC | Cr-depleted zones | Low-C / stabilized grades (321, 347) |
| ↑ Temperature | ↑ CR (Arrhenius) | Faster kinetics, ↑ DO | Cooling, inhibitors |
| ↑ Velocity (beyond critical) | ↑↑ Erosion-CR | Film removal | Lower velocity, harder alloys |
| ↑ Tensile stress | Enables SCC | Film rupture + anodic dissolution | Anneal, shot-peening |
| ↑ Microbial activity | Localized pitting | Cathodic depolarization (H₂) | Biocides, cleaning |
| ↑ Grain boundary area | ↑ IGC susceptibility | Anodic GB attack | Coarse-grain processing |
| ↑ Surface roughness | ↑ CR | Traps moisture, deposits | Polishing, pickling |
