# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define passivity. Explain why pure iron corrodes actively in dilute H₂SO₄, while chromium does not under the same conditions.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Passivity** is the phenomenon by which a metal or alloy exhibits *unusually low corrosion rates* in an environment where, from thermodynamic data alone, significant active dissolution would be expected. It arises from the formation of a **thin, adherent, and protective oxide film** (1–10 nm thick) on the metal surface that effectively isolates the metal from the electrolyte.

Pure iron lacks any element that can form a stable, self-healing passive film in dilute H₂SO₄. Iron's oxide (FeO/Fe₂O₃) is porous and non-protective, and the bulk thermodynamics favours Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ ($E^0$ = –0.44 V). Hence iron dissolves actively.

Chromium, however, has a very high affinity for oxygen. Even at low applied potentials it forms a thin, electronically insulating Cr₂O₃ layer that is **self-healing** (if damaged, it re-forms instantly in oxidizing conditions). This film shifts the effective anodic reaction to within the passive region of the Pourbaix diagram, reducing the corrosion current to a negligible $i_{passive}$ value (typically 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁸ A/cm²).

This is the metallurgical basis for **stainless steel** — adding ≥10.5% Cr to iron confers passivity.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between erosion-corrosion and cavitation corrosion.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Erosion-Corrosion | Cavitation Corrosion |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Cause | Mechanical abrasion by suspended particles or high-velocity fluid | Collapse of vapor bubbles in low-pressure regions of a flowing liquid |
| Mechanism | Film is mechanically scraped away; fresh metal exposed repeatedly | Implosive bubble generates shock waves (up to 1000 MPa) that disrupt film and deform surface |
| Appearance | Grooves, scallops, horseshoe-shaped pits aligned with flow | Pits with sharp, jagged edges, often clustered near pump impellers orifices |
| Counter-measure | Reduce velocity, use harder alloys, add filters | Improve pump design to maintain pressure above vapor pressure, use tougher alloys |

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the metallurgical factors that affect the corrosion behavior of metals and alloys. Discuss the role of grain size and cold work in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the anodic polarization behavior of a passivating metal such as iron in sulfuric acid. Define the key parameters: primary passivation potential, critical current density, passive current density, and Flade potential.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**1. Composition / Alloying:**
- Impure metals suffer accelerated corrosion because impurity atoms act as local cathodes, establishing micro-galvanic cells on the surface. For example, impure zinc corrodes faster in HCl than electrolytic-grade zinc.
- Alloying can promote protective film formation: Cr → Cr₂O₃, Al → Al₂O₃, Si → SiO₂.
- Adding >10.5% Cr to Fe creates stainless steel; adding 6% Al to Fe creates heat-resistant steel.

**2. Microstructure / Grain Size:**
- A polycrystalline metal contains grains separated by grain boundaries (GBs). GBs are regions of higher energy and impurity segregation.
- GBs typically act as **anodes** in many environments (especially in Fe and austenitic SS).
- The relationship: $CR \propto 1/\sqrt{d}$ — finer grain → more GB area → higher CR.
- However, in some alloys, fine grains **distribute precipitates more uniformly** and reduce localized attack.

**3. Cold Work:**
- Plastic deformation (rolling, drawing, forging) increases dislocation density by factors of 10³ to 10⁶.
- Higher dislocation density → higher stored internal energy → metal becomes more anodic.
- Cold-worked zones become anodes relative to annealed zones — **strain galvanic cells** form.
- Cold work also reduces ductility and elevates SCC susceptibility.

**4. Heat Treatment:**
- Quenching → supersaturated solid solution → high internal energy → more corrosion.
- Annealing → equilibrium phases, stress relief → less corrosion.
- **Sensitization** (specific to austenitic SS 304 heated at 500–800 °C): Cr combines with C to form Cr₂₃C₆ at grain boundaries, leaving a **Cr-depleted zone** that preferentially attacks in oxidizing acids (HNO₃, FeCl₃). Solution: use **low-carbon grade 304L**, or stabilized grades **321 (Ti)** and **347 (Nb)**.

**5. Surface Condition:**
- Rough surfaces trap moisture, salts, and bacteria → differential aeration cells.
- Polished surfaces give uniform attack; rough surfaces give pitting.

#### Model Solution for (b)

Refer to **Section 2.3.1** and **Diagram in Section 4.2**.

Sketch description (in lieu of figure):

```
   Current density (log i)
        |
   i_crit|\
        | \   <-- Active region (A-B)
        |  \
   i_pass|----__________  <-- Passive region (C-D)
        |   \          
        |    \  <-- Transpassive (D-E)
   i    |     \________
        |             \____
        |________________________ Potential (E)
        E_corr  E_pp    E_F   E_transpassive
```

- **Primary passivation potential (E_pp):** the potential at point B where the active-to-passive transition begins.
- **Critical current density (i_crit):** the maximum current density reached just before the metal passivates (at point B).
- **Passive current density (i_pass):** the low, nearly constant current density in the passive range (C–D).
- **Flade potential (E_F):** the potential at which the passive film starts to break down and the metal re-enters the active state (lower edge of passive region).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **Flade potential** with the **transpassive potential**. Remember: Flade is the *lower* edge of the passive region (re-passivation), while transpassive is the *upper* edge (film dissolves). Mislabeling costs 2 marks instantly.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various non-chemical environmental factors ("other aspects") influencing corrosion. How does velocity of the medium affect corrosion behavior? Explain erosion-corrosion and cavitation corrosion with examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC)? Explain the role of sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB) in the corrosion of underground pipelines. List four methods to control MIC.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Non-Chemical Environmental Factors:**

1. **Temperature:** As temperature increases, both the rate of anodic dissolution and the rate of cathodic reactant diffusion (e.g., O₂) increase. Arrhenius equation applies: $CR \propto \exp(-E_a/RT)$. For most neutral aqueous systems, CR doubles per 10 °C rise.

2. **Velocity of the medium:**
   - **Low velocity** → diffusion-controlled; protective film (if any) remains intact.
   - **Moderate velocity** → mass-transfer enhanced; CR increases but is uniform.
   - **High velocity** → film is mechanically removed; CR increases sharply (erosion-corrosion).
   - **Very high velocity / pressure fluctuations** → cavitation.
   - **Critical velocity** $v_{crit}$ depends on fluid density $\rho$, viscosity $\mu$, pipe diameter $d$, and film tenacity.

3. **Mechanical Stress (covered in Section 2.2.5):** SCC, Corrosion Fatigue, Hydrogen Embrittlement.

4. **Biological Activity:** Microbial films on metal surfaces create differential aeration and acid-producing colonies.

5. **Radiation:** In nuclear reactors, gamma radiation can depassivate stainless steel by radiolysis of water → formation of H₂O₂, O₂, radicals.

**Erosion-Corrosion:**
- **Definition:** Accelerated corrosion caused by the relative motion between a corrosive fluid and a metal surface, where the mechanical wear continually removes the protective film.
- **Mechanism:** Film removal → fresh metal exposed → film re-forms → film removed again → accelerated loss.
- **Examples:**
  - Sand-laden seawater in copper alloy condenser tubes.
  - Impingement attack at the inlet of heat exchanger tubes.
  - Slurry pipelines carrying ore particles.
- **Appearance:** Horseshoe-shaped or gull-wing scallops pointing in the flow direction.

**Cavitation Corrosion:**
- **Definition:** Damage caused by the formation and violent collapse of vapor bubbles in a liquid near a metal surface.
- **Mechanism:** Local pressure drops below vapor pressure → bubble forms → bubble moves to high-pressure region → collapses → shock wave (peak pressure ~ 1000 MPa) hits surface → film destroyed + plastic deformation of metal.
- **Examples:**
  - Pump impellers, ship propellers, valve orifices, diesel engine cylinder liners.
- **Appearance:** Pits with sharp edges, often in a honeycomb pattern.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC):**

MIC is corrosion that is initiated, accelerated, or influenced by the presence and metabolic activity of microorganisms (bacteria, algae, fungi) on a metal surface. It accounts for an estimated **20–30% of all corrosion-related pipeline failures** worldwide.

**Mechanism of SRB-Induced Corrosion:**

Sulfate-Reduccing Bacteria (e.g., *Desulfovibrio desulfuricans*) are **anaerobic** bacteria that thrive in oxygen-depleted environments such as waterlogged soils, buried pipelines, and stagnant water in tanks.

**Cathodic Depolarization Theory (Kühr):**

1. At the metal surface, the natural corrosion reaction in anaerobic soil is:
$$ \text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^- \quad \text{(anodic)} $$
$$ 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2 \quad \text{(cathodic, normally sluggish)} $$

2. The hydrogen film on the metal surface *polarizes* the cathode and normally stops further reaction.

3. SRB **consume this cathodic hydrogen** using the enzyme hydrogenase:
$$ \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 4\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{S}^{2-} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(bacterial)} $$

4. By removing H₂, SRB *depolarize* the cathode, allowing the corrosion reaction to continue.

5. The sulfide produced reacts with Fe²⁺ to form iron sulfide (FeS) deposits and also forms H₂S gas that attacks the metal.

**Net result:** Pitting corrosion, often in the form of deep, narrow pits under tubercles or deposits. Products include **FeS (black), Fe(OH)₂, FeCO₃**.

**Methods to Control MIC (any four required):**

1. **Biocide dosing** — injection of chlorine, ozone, glutaraldehyde, or tetrakis-hydroxymethyl phosphonium sulfate (THPS) into the pipeline.
2. **Mechanical cleaning** — periodic pigging of pipelines to remove biofilms and deposits.
3. **Cathodic protection** — coupling to Mg or Zn anodes to shift potential to < –0.85 V (Cu/CuSO₄).
4. **Protective coatings** — fusion-bonded epoxy (FBE) on the external surface; internal linings for tanks.
5. **pH control** — maintaining pH > 9 inhibits most bacterial growth.
6. **Material selection** — using corrosion-resistant alloys (e.g., Cu-Ni, stainless steel) in critical service.
7. **Elimination of stagnant zones** in design (avoid dead legs in piping).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is to attribute MIC to **direct attack** of the metal by bacteria. This is wrong — bacteria do *not* "eat" the metal. They *depolarize* the cathode or produce aggressive metabolites (H₂S, organic acids) that *indirectly* cause corrosion. Mis-stating this mechanism can cost up to 3 marks.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Passivity** = thin oxide film (Cr₂O₃, Al₂O₃) → low $i_{pass}$ → high corrosion resistance.
- **Anodic polarization curve of passivating metal** has four regions: Active → Active-Passive Transition → Passive → Transpassive.
- **Critical parameters:** E_pp, i_crit, i_pass, Flade Potential E_F.
- **Theories of passivity:** Oxide Film Theory (Evans-Langmuir) and Adsorption Theory (Hedges-Uhlig). Modern view: both operate.
- **Grain size:** finer grain → more GB area → higher IGC susceptibility ($CR \propto 1/\sqrt{d}$).
- **Cold work** ↑ dislocation density → ↑ stored energy → ↑ CR → ↑ SCC risk.
- **Sensitization** of Type 304 SS in 500–800 °C range → Cr-depleted zones → IGC. Cure: 304L, 321 (Ti), 347 (Nb).
- **Temperature** effect follows Arrhenius law: $\ln CR = \ln A - E_a/RT$. Rule of thumb: doubles per 10 °C.
- **Velocity** effects: low → uniform; moderate → mass-transfer controlled; high → erosion-corrosion; very high → cavitation.
- **Stress** + **specific environment** + **susceptible alloy** = SCC. Crack below yield strength.
- **MIC** is caused by **cathodic depolarization** by SRB (anaerobic) and tubercles by iron-oxidizing bacteria. Control: biocides, cleaning, coatings, CP, design.
- **EMF Series** is thermodynamic (predicts tendency); **Galvanic Series** is empirical (predicts actual behavior in seawater) — *Galvanic Series is what engineers use in practice*.
- **Composition** rule: pure metals corrode slower than impure metals; alloying can either help (Cr, Al) or harm (S, P in Ni alloys).
- **Heat treatment** summary: quench = high energy = more corrosion; anneal = relaxed = less corrosion; temper = balance of strength and CR.
- **High-field ion conduction** (Cabrera-Mott) explains oxide film growth: $i = A \exp(\alpha E_{field}/kT)$ with $E_{field} \approx 10^6$ V/cm.
- **Stern-Geary equation** for $i_{corr}$: $i_{corr} = \beta_a \beta_c / [2.303 R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)]$.
- **Corrosion rate formula:** $CR \text{ (mmpy)} = 0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW / \rho$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crevice Corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Crevice Corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crevice Corrosion** is a highly localized form of **galvanic attack** that occurs within narrow gaps, recesses, or shielded areas on a metal surface where a stagnant electrolyte is trapped and the access of the bulk environment is severely restricted. It is a form of **differential aeration cell corrosion** where the area inside the crevice (anode) suffers accelerated metal dissolution, while the area outside the crevice (cathode) remains passive or is only mildly attacked.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT523) syllabus, crevice corrosion is classified under **localized (or non-uniform) corrosion** mechanisms, falling under the broader domain of **wet corrosion phenomena**. The attack is driven by the formation of an **occluded corrosion cell** — a small, trapped volume of electrolyte whose chemical composition diverges sharply from the bulk solution over time.

### Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Crevice** | A narrow gap (typically 0.025 mm to 0.1 mm wide) between two surfaces, one often metallic and the other either metal or non-metal. |
| **Occluded Cell** | The trapped, stagnant electrolyte volume inside the crevice whose chemistry evolves independently. |
| **Differential Aeration Cell (DAC)** | An electrochemical cell where two regions of the same metal are exposed to different oxygen concentrations. |
| **Passive Alloy** | A metal (e.g., stainless steel, Al, Ti) that forms a thin, protective oxide film; crevice corrosion usually breaks this film locally. |
| **Critical Crevice Temperature (CCT)** | The lowest temperature at which crevice corrosion can initiate under a standardized test (per ASTM G48). |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stagnant Puddle" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine two flat metal plates bolted tightly together, immersed in seawater. Outside the plates, seawater flows freely and is full of dissolved oxygen. In the tiny gap between the plates, the water is trapped — like a **stagnant puddle in a closed room**. Over time:
> 1. The oxygen trapped inside the puddle gets **consumed** by the metal (it is "breathed in" during corrosion), but no fresh oxygen can enter.
> 2. The metal outside, exposed to fresh, oxygen-rich seawater, becomes the **preferential site for oxygen reduction** (cathode).
> 3. The metal inside the oxygen-starved crevice is forced to **dissolve aggressively** (becomes the anode) to balance the current.
> 4. As metal dissolves, positive metal ions (M^n+) accumulate inside the crevice. To maintain charge neutrality, **aggressive anions like Cl⁻ migrate in from the bulk**, and the local pH **drops sharply** (acidifies). This acidic, chloride-rich brine is highly aggressive, accelerating the attack further — a self-feeding loop.

This is exactly what happens in real crevice corrosion — **a tiny geometry creates a chemistry disaster**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *crevice corrosion* with *pitting corrosion*. While related, the **driving force** is different: pitting is initiated by a **localized passive film breakdown** (often due to Cl⁻ attack), whereas crevice corrosion is driven by **oxygen depletion (differential aeration) inside a confined geometry**. Pitting can occur on a perfectly open surface; crevice corrosion *requires* a geometry.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following **bold** values are essential for KTU board numerical problems:

- **Faraday's Constant: F = 96,485 C/mol ≈ 96,500 C/mol**
- **Width of a typical crevice (w): 0.025 mm to 0.1 mm** (below this, capillary forces and IR drop suppress attack; above this, it behaves like open surface).
- **CCT of 304 SS in 6% FeCl₃ ≈ 22 °C** (a common KTU benchmark).
- **Reference dissolution current density for passive metals: i_passive ≈ 10⁻⁶ A/cm²**.
- **Diffusion coefficient of O₂ in water at 25 °C: D_O₂ ≈ 2.1 × 10⁻⁵ cm²/s**.
- **Density of iron (Fe): ρ = 7.87 g/cm³**.

---

## 1.4 Why Crevice Corrosion Matters in Engineering

- **Failure of aircraft riveted lap joints** in marine atmosphere.
- **Tube-sheet joints in heat exchangers** (cooling water side).
- **Flange gaskets** in chemical process piping.
- **Implant corrosion** in biomedical devices (e.g., modular hip stems).
- **Subsea bolted assemblies** where the geometry is unavoidable.

---

## 1.5 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping

| KTU CO Code | Course Outcome | Crevice Corrosion Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **CO2** | Explain mechanisms of various forms of localized corrosion | Crevice corrosion is a key example of geometry-driven localized attack. |
| **CO3** | Apply electrochemical principles to predict corrosion behaviour | Differential aeration + IR drop mechanism. |
| **CO5** | Recommend mitigation strategies for industrial corrosion problems | Design of crevice-free joints, use of more resistant alloys. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Stages of Crevice Corrosion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Examiners *love* asking students to "explain the mechanism of crevice corrosion in stages." Memorize the three-stage model below.

### Stage I — Initiation (Time: 0 → t₁)

1. The crevice is initially filled with bulk electrolyte having uniform O₂ concentration.
2. The metal both inside and outside is in the **passive state** (for passive alloys like SS) or in **active dissolution** (for non-passive alloys like carbon steel).
3. O₂ is consumed uniformly by the cathodic reaction:
   
   $$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$$

4. Because diffusion into the crevice is restricted, the **O₂ concentration inside the crevice drops to zero** within seconds to minutes. The bulk solution outside remains oxygenated.

### Stage II — Propagation (Time: t₁ → t₂)

1. With O₂ depleted inside, the **oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) shifts entirely to the bulk/external surface**, making it the dominant **cathode**.
2. The metal inside the crevice, unable to sustain ORR, is forced to **oxidize (anodically dissolve)**:

   $$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$$

3. Electrons flow through the metal from the anode (inside) to the cathode (outside), completing the cell.
4. As M^n+ accumulates, **Cl⁻ migrates in** to balance the positive charge → forms metal chlorides (MCl_n).
5. Metal chlorides hydrolyze water:

   $$MCl_n + nH_2O \rightarrow M(OH)_n + nH^+ + nCl^-$$

6. The **H⁺ produced lowers the pH** inside the crevice to as low as **1–2** (highly aggressive).
7. This acidic, chloride-rich, oxygen-starved environment **destabilizes the passive film**, accelerating anodic dissolution.
8. **IR drop** across the resistive crevice solution progressively suppresses the anodic reaction further inside, but enhances it at the mouth — geometry-dependent.

### Stage III — Death / Repassivation (Time: t > t₂)

1. Eventually, the aggressive local chemistry stabilizes or the crevice geometry changes.
2. The metal may **repassivate** if the local conditions allow (e.g., dilution of the aggressive ion).
3. In real engineering, the crevice often continues to grow until perforation, or until the geometry opens up due to metal loss.

---

## 2.2 The Differential Aeration Cell (DAC) Theory — The Heart of Crevice Corrosion

The classical explanation, championed by **Evans and modified by Pourbaix**, is the **differential aeration cell**:

- **Anode (inside crevice):** Low O₂ → metal dissolves.

  $$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^- \quad E_{anode} = E^0_{M^{n+}/M} + \frac{0.059}{n} \log [M^{n+}]$$

- **Cathode (outside crevice):** High O₂ → ORR occurs.

  $$E_{cathode} = 1.23 - 0.059 \cdot pH + \frac{0.059}{4} \log(P_{O_2})$$

- **Net EMF driving the cell:**

  $$\Delta E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$$

  Since the cathode region has higher pO₂ (and frequently higher pH), and the anode region has lower pO₂ (and lower pH), the potential difference is **positive**, meaning the reaction is spontaneous (ΔG < 0).

---

## 2.3 The Pourbaix IR Drop Mechanism (Advanced KTU Theory)

> [!NOTE]
> **Higher-Order Concept (often asked in 14-mark questions):** In the late 1960s, **Pourbaix** and later **Oldham and Mansfield** proposed that the **dominant factor** in maintaining crevice corrosion is not the chemistry alone, but the **ohmic (IR) potential drop** across the resistive solution in the narrow crevice.

The model states:

1. The anodic current density (i_a) required to sustain a passive metal's dissolution at a distance x inside the crevice is **exponentially small** because of the IR drop:

   $$i_a(x) = i_{a,0} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta \phi(x)}{b_a}\right)$$

   where $\Delta \phi(x)$ is the ohmic potential drop and $b_a$ is the Tafel slope of the anodic reaction.

2. The cathodic current outside the crevice is sustained by ORR at a much higher rate.

3. The current is limited by **ionic conduction through the crevice** and by **mass transport of species** (Cl⁻, H⁺, M^n+).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete, exam-ready** formula set for crevice corrosion and its associated numerical problems.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CR = \frac{K \cdot I \cdot EW}{\rho \cdot A}$ | Corrosion rate from Faraday's law | mm/year |
| 2 | $EW = \frac{M}{n}$ | Equivalent weight of metal | g/eq |
| 3 | $K = 3.27 \times 10^{-3}$ | Faraday constant conversion factor | mm·g/(µA·cm·year) |
| 4 | $i = \frac{I}{A}$ | Current density | A/cm² or µA/cm² |
| 5 | $\Delta E = I \cdot R_{crevice}$ | Ohmic (IR) drop across crevice | V |
| 6 | $R_{crevice} = \frac{\rho_{sol} \cdot L}{A_{cs}}$ | Resistance of crevice solution | Ω |
| 7 | $i_L = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot D \cdot C_{bulk}}{\delta}$ | Limiting current density for diffusion of species | A/cm² |
| 8 | $\delta = \sqrt{\pi \cdot D \cdot t}$ | Diffusion layer thickness (Cottrell-type) | cm |
| 9 | $C(x,t) = C_{bulk} \cdot \text{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$ | Concentration profile in crevice (semi-infinite model) | mol/cm³ |
| 10 | $\Delta E_{cell} = E_{cathode}(O_2-rich) - E_{anode}(O_2-poor)$ | Driving EMF of DAC | V |
| 11 | $pH_{crevice} = pH_0 - \sum (\text{H}^+ \text{ from hydrolysis})$ | Acidification in crevice | — |
| 12 | $E_{pit} \text{ or } E_{crevice} = E^0 + \text{Cl}^- \text{ dep. term}$ | Critical pitting/crevice potential | V vs SCE |
| 13 | $\log(i) = \log(i_0) + \frac{\eta}{b}$ | Tafel equation (anodic or cathodic) | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Notation Rule:** In LaTeX equations, **never write** absolute value as `|x|` inside a markdown table — it breaks the table parser. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead.

---

## 2.5 Critical Factors Affecting Crevice Corrosion

These are **frequently tested as 3-mark Part-A questions** in KTU 2024 ESE:

1. **Crevice Geometry** — narrower → more aggressive; optimum gap 0.025–0.1 mm.
2. **Bulk Chloride Concentration** — higher Cl⁻ → lower pitting/crevice resistance.
3. **Temperature** — CCT (Critical Crevice Temperature) is a key alloy ranking metric.
4. **Alloy Composition** — Mo, N, Cr enhance resistance (PREN = %Cr + 3.3×%Mo + 16×%N).
5. **pH of Bulk Solution** — lower pH dramatically increases attack.
6. **Dissolved Oxygen** — higher O₂ increases cathodic driving force.
7. **Stagnation** — flow reduces the effect; stagnant solutions promote it.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ASTM G48** standard test: 6% FeCl₃, 72 h → measure mass loss to rank alloys by CCT.
- **ASTM G78** standard test: galvanic/crevice corrosion in seawater.
- **Materials selection:** In seawater, **super-austenitic (6% Mo) or super-duplex SS** are chosen for crevice-prone joints.
- **Design mitigation:** butt-welded joints instead of lap joints; continuous welds; use of sealants/gaskets designed for the environment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Numerical problems on crevice corrosion usually combine **Faraday's law** with the **ohmic drop across the crevice**, or the **diffusion-limited current inside the crevice**. The following three derivations are the ones most likely to appear in 14-mark ESE questions.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Mass Loss and Penetration Depth from Faraday's Law

**Problem Setup (typical KTU-style):**
A 304 stainless steel plate (Cr = 18%, Ni = 8%, Fe = balance) is exposed in a crevice geometry to aerated 3% NaCl. The anodic current density inside the crevice is measured as **i_a = 5 µA/cm²**. The area of the crevice mouth is **A = 10 cm²**. Determine the **mass loss in 30 days** and the **penetration depth**.

**Given Data:**

- $i_a = 5 \, \mu A/cm^2 = 5 \times 10^{-6} \, A/cm^2$
- $A = 10 \, cm^2$
- $t = 30 \, days = 30 \times 24 \times 3600 = 2.592 \times 10^6 \, s$
- For Fe (the major dissolving species in 304 SS): $M_{Fe} = 55.85 \, g/mol$, $n = 2$
- $F = 96,485 \, C/mol$
- $\rho_{Fe} = 7.87 \, g/cm^3$

**Step 1: Total current**

$$I = i_a \times A = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 = 5 \times 10^{-5} \, A$$

**Step 2: Total charge passed in 30 days**

$$Q = I \times t = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.592 \times 10^6 = 129.6 \, C$$

**Step 3: Moles of electrons transferred**

$$n_{e^-} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{129.6}{96485} = 1.343 \times 10^{-3} \, mol \, e^-$$

**Step 4: Moles of Fe dissolved (since n = 2 for Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻)**

$$n_{Fe} = \frac{n_{e^-}}{2} = \frac{1.343 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 6.715 \times 10^{-4} \, mol$$

**Step 5: Mass loss of iron**

$$m_{Fe} = n_{Fe} \times M_{Fe} = 6.715 \times 10^{-4} \times 55.85 = 0.03751 \, g = 37.51 \, mg$$

**Step 6: Equivalent weight of Fe**

$$EW = \frac{M}{n} = \frac{55.85}{2} = 27.925 \, g/eq$$

**Step 7: Direct application of Faraday's corrosion rate formula**

$$CR = \frac{K \cdot I \cdot t \cdot EW}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t} = \frac{K \cdot I \cdot EW}{\rho \cdot A}$$

$$CR = \frac{(3.27 \times 10^{-3}) \times (5 \times 10^{-5}) \times 27.925}{7.87 \times 10^{-6} \times 10} = \frac{4.567 \times 10^{-6}}{7.87 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$CR = 0.0580 \, mm/year$$

**Step 8: Penetration depth in 30 days**

$$\text{Depth} = CR \times \frac{t}{365} = 0.0580 \times \frac{30}{365} = 4.77 \times 10^{-3} \, mm = 4.77 \, \mu m$$

**Insight for KTU Students:**
The penetration is tiny for the passive case, but the **localized nature** of crevice corrosion (high i_a concentrated at the mouth) means that a small penetration can grow rapidly into deep tunnels. In *real* crevice attack, the local current density can be 100–1000× higher than the average.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Ohmic (IR) Drop Across the Crevice Solution

**Problem Setup:**
A crevice of length **L = 5 mm**, width **w = 0.05 mm**, and depth **d = 10 mm** is filled with seawater (resistivity ρ_sol = 20 Ω·cm). The current flowing through the crevice solution is **I = 0.1 mA = 10⁻⁴ A**. Calculate the **IR drop** and assess whether the crevice will sustain active dissolution.

**Given Data:**

- $\rho_{sol} = 20 \, \Omega \cdot cm = 0.20 \, \Omega \cdot m$
- $L = 0.5 \, cm$ (depth into crevice)
- $w = 0.005 \, cm$
- $d = 1.0 \, cm$ (length of crevice opening)
- $I = 10^{-4} \, A$

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area of solution column inside crevice**

$$A_{cs} = w \times d = 0.005 \times 1.0 = 5 \times 10^{-3} \, cm^2$$

**Step 2: Resistance of the solution column (acting like a resistor)**

$$R_{crevice} = \frac{\rho_{sol} \cdot L}{A_{cs}} = \frac{20 \times 0.5}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{10}{0.005} = 2000 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3: Ohmic voltage drop along the crevice depth**

$$\Delta E_{IR} = I \times R_{crevice} = 10^{-4} \times 2000 = 0.20 \, V = 200 \, mV$$

**Step 4: Interpretation (KTU favourite interpretation step)**

For most passive alloys, the passive range in a Pourbaix diagram is only **~200 to 400 mV wide**. A 200 mV drop is therefore **catastrophic** — it can easily shift the local electrode potential out of the passive zone and into the active dissolution zone. This is the mathematical basis of the **Pourbaix IR drop criterion** for crevice corrosion.

**Step 5: Critical length where IR drop equals the passive range ΔE_pass**

$$L_{critical} = \frac{\Delta E_{pass} \cdot A_{cs}}{\rho_{sol} \cdot i_a}$$

For ΔE_pass = 0.3 V, i_a = 10⁻⁵ A/cm² (typical passive dissolution):

$$L_{critical} = \frac{0.3 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.5 \, cm$$

**Insight:** Once the crevice is deeper than ~7.5 cm under these conditions, the deepest region is **electrolytically isolated** and cannot sustain corrosion. This explains why **shallow crevices and narrow gaps** are often more dangerous than deep ones.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Diffusion-Limited Oxygen Current Inside the Crevice

**Problem Setup:**
Estimate the **maximum possible cathodic current density** inside a crevice of depth L = 1 cm, given that the bulk O₂ concentration is **C_bulk = 8 mg/L = 8 × 10⁻⁶ g/cm³ = 2.5 × 10⁻⁴ mol/L = 2.5 × 10⁻⁷ mol/cm³**. Use the diffusion coefficient of O₂ in water at 25 °C: **D = 2.1 × 10⁻⁵ cm²/s**. The reaction is **O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻** (n = 4).

**Step 1: Limiting current density (Fick's first law at steady state)**

$$i_L = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot D \cdot C_{bulk}}{L}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$i_L = \frac{4 \times 96485 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{1.0}$$

**Step 3: Compute the numerator**

$$4 \times 96485 = 385940$$

$$385940 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-5} = 8.105$$

$$8.105 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7} = 2.026 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4: Final value**

$$i_L = \frac{2.026 \times 10^{-6}}{1.0} = 2.03 \times 10^{-6} \, A/cm^2 = 2.03 \, \mu A/cm^2$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**

This is the **maximum rate at which O₂ can be reduced inside the crevice** by diffusion alone. It is very small because diffusion in a 1 cm deep liquid column is slow. As a result, **ORR cannot be sustained inside the crevice** → it migrates to the external surface → confirming the **anode-inside, cathode-outside** model.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Crevice vs. Pitting Corrosion

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** This comparison table is a guaranteed **3-mark short answer** for Part-A.

| Property | Crevice Corrosion | Pitting Corrosion |
|---|---|---|
| **Required Geometry** | Yes — narrow gap | No — open surface OK |
| **Initiator** | O₂ depletion inside crevice | Localized passive film breakdown (Cl⁻) |
| **Driving Force** | Differential aeration | Local chemistry / Cl⁻ attack |
| **Anode Location** | Inside the crevice | At the pit bottom |
| **Cathode Location** | Outside the crevice | Around the pit mouth (passive surface) |
| **Morphology** | Wide, shallow, "tongues" of attack | Deep, narrow, often undercut pits |
| **Alloys Most Affected** | All SS, Al alloys, Ti | SS, Al, Ni alloys |
| **Test Standards** | ASTM G48, G78 | ASTM G48, G46 |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical: CCT Determination (Kinetics Insight)

**Problem:**
The Critical Crevice Temperature of alloy A is 25 °C, and of alloy B is 40 °C, in 6% FeCl₃ per ASTM G48. A heat exchanger operates at 30 °C in a chloride environment. Recommend the alloy.

**Solution:**

- At 30 °C, alloy A (CCT = 25 °C) **fails the criterion** (operating T > CCT → crevice attack expected).
- At 30 °C, alloy B (CCT = 40 °C) **passes the criterion** (operating T < CCT).
- **Recommendation: Use Alloy B.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Error:** Students sometimes confuse *CCT* (crevice) with *CPT* (pitting temperature). They are *not the same* — CCT is always **lower** than CPT for the same alloy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Visualization Mandate:** All diagrams below are pure Mermaid (no external image dependencies), adhering to alphanumeric node ID rules and label formatting restrictions.

---

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Three-Stage Crevice Corrosion Mechanism

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[STAGE I: INITIATION] --> A2[O2 in bulk and crevice initially equal]
    A2 --> A3[O2 inside crevice consumed by ORR]
    A3 --> A4[No fresh O2 can diffuse in - crevice is starved]
    A4 --> B1[STAGE II: PROPAGATION]
    B1 --> B2[ORR shifts to external surface - cathode outside]
    B2 --> B3[Metal inside crevice anodically dissolves - Mn+]
    B3 --> B4[Cl- migrates in to balance charge]
    B4 --> B5[Metal chlorides hydrolyze water - H+ produced]
    B5 --> B6[Local pH drops to 1 to 2 - aggressive]
    B6 --> B7[Passive film locally destroyed - attack accelerates]
    B7 --> C1[STAGE III: DEATH OR PERFORATION]
    C1 --> C2[Repassivation OR continued growth to perforation]
    C2 --> C3[Engineering failure]

    style A1 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style B1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style C1 fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#333333
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Differential Aeration Cell Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Anode: Inside Crevice - O2 POOR] -->|Electrons flow through metal| B[Cathode: Outside Crevice - O2 RICH]
    B -->|ORR: O2 plus H2O plus 4e minus gives 4OH minus| B
    A -->|Anodic dissolution: M gives Mn+ plus ne minus| A
    A -->|Mn+ accumulates, Cl- migrates inward| A
    A -->|H+ from hydrolysis drops pH to 1 to 2| A
    A -->|IR drop through narrow gap| A

    style A fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000
    style B fill:#99ccff,stroke:#003366
```

---

```mermaid

    G1[Metal Plate Top] --> G2[Gap width w: 0.025 to 0.1 mm - ACTIVE]
    G2 --> G3[Capillary forces hold electrolyte]
    G3 --> G4[Below 0.025 mm - attack suppressed by IR drop]
    G4 --> G5[Above 0.1 mm - behaves like open surface]
    G5 --> G6[Bulk solution: O2 rich, well mixed]
    G6 --> G7[Crevice solution: O2 poor, acidic, Cl- rich]

    style G7 fill:#ff8080,stroke:#990000
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Active dissolution site at crevice mouth] --> P2[Current flows through resistive crevice solution]
    P2 --> P3[IR drop along the depth]
    P3 --> P4[Deeper regions shift to more negative potential]
    P4 --> P5[If potential exits passive zone - attack sustained]
    P4 --> P6[If potential remains in passive zone - repassivation]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question Bank Format:** Aligned to the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) pattern for a 14-credit lab-cum-theory course like PEPOT523. Two Part-A (3 marks each) and two Part-B choices (14 marks each, with internal choice between A and B).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define crevice corrosion. List any four engineering situations where it commonly occurs. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Crevice corrosion is a localized form of wet corrosion that occurs within narrow gaps, recesses, or shielded regions on a metal surface where a stagnant electrolyte becomes trapped. It is driven by a **differential aeration cell**, in which the metal inside the crevice (oxygen-depleted) acts as the anode and dissolves aggressively, while the external oxygenated surface acts as the cathode. The local chemistry becomes acidic and chloride-rich, leading to accelerated, self-sustaining attack.

**Engineering situations (1/2 mark each, 4 situations):**

1. **Riveted lap joints in aircraft structures** operating in marine/coastal environments.
2. **Tube-to-tube-sheet joints in shell-and-tube heat exchangers** where cooling water stagnates.
3. **Flange gaskets and O-ring grooves** in chemical process piping.
4. **Bolted assemblies and threaded fasteners** submerged in seawater.
5. **Biomedical implants** such as modular hip prostheses where taper junctions trap body fluid.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Critical Pitting Temperature (CPT) and Critical Crevice Temperature (CCT). Why is CCT always lower than CPT for the same alloy? **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | CPT | CCT |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Lowest temperature at which stable pitting initiates on an open surface | Lowest temperature at which stable crevice corrosion initiates inside a shielded geometry |
| **Test Standard** | ASTM G150 (electrochemical) or ASTM G48 | ASTM G48 (mass loss in 6% FeCl₃) |
| **Geometry** | None — open surface | Required — crevice former (e.g., TFE block) |

**Why CCT < CPT (1 mark):**

In a crevice, oxygen is depleted within seconds by ORR, generating a **differential aeration cell** even at temperatures where the open surface remains passive. The aggressive local chemistry (low pH, high Cl⁻) develops automatically, lowering the energy barrier for attack. Therefore, the same alloy will fail inside a crevice at a **lower temperature** than on an open surface.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A: (a)** Explain the mechanism of crevice corrosion in stainless steel with neat sketches. Discuss the role of the differential aeration cell. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 304 SS plate is exposed in a 0.1 mm wide crevice to aerated 3% NaCl solution. The anodic current density inside the crevice is 8 µA/cm² over an area of 5 cm². The resistivity of the crevice solution is 25 Ω·cm. The depth of the crevice is 2 cm. Calculate:
(i) The total mass of Fe dissolved in 60 days.
(ii) The IR drop across the crevice.
(iii) Whether the crevice is "active" given that the passive range of 304 SS is ~300 mV. **(7 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

#### Model Solution A(a): Mechanism (7 marks)

> **Step 1: Introduction (1 mark)**
> Crevice corrosion in stainless steel is a localized attack driven by the **geometry-restricted access of oxygen** to the metal surface within a narrow gap. The attack is sustained by a **differential aeration cell** (DAC) — first described by **U. R. Evans** and later refined by **Pourbaix**.

> **Step 2: Stage I — Initiation (2 marks)**
> Initially, the electrolyte inside the crevice and the bulk outside have identical O₂ concentration. The passive film on the SS is intact everywhere. O₂ inside the crevice is reduced:

> $$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$$

> Because the crevice is narrow and the electrolyte is stagnant, fresh O₂ cannot diffuse in fast enough. Within a short time, the O₂ concentration inside the crevice drops to essentially zero. The bulk solution outside remains oxygenated.

> **Step 3: Stage II — Propagation with DAC (2 marks)**
> With O₂ absent inside, the ORR shifts entirely to the **external (oxygenated) surface**, which becomes the dominant **cathode**:

> $$E_{cathode} = 1.23 - 0.059 \, pH + 0.0148 \, \log(P_{O_2})$$

> The metal inside the crevice is forced to act as the **anode** and dissolve:

> $$Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$$

> The DAC driving EMF is:

> $$\Delta E = E_{cathode}(O_2\text{-rich}) - E_{anode}(O_2\text{-poor}) > 0$$

> As Fe²⁺ accumulates, Cl⁻ migrates inward, and the chloride salt hydrolyzes:

> $$FeCl_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow Fe(OH)_2 \downarrow + 2H^+ + 2Cl^-$$

> This releases HCl, dropping the local pH to **1–2** and reinforcing the attack.

> **Step 4: Stage III — Death / IR Drop stabilization (1 mark)**
> The Pourbaix IR drop mechanism dictates that the deepest crevice regions become cathodically protected by the anodic regions at the mouth, while the resistance of the crevice solution limits the maximum sustainable current. The attack continues until perforation or until the geometry opens.

> **Step 5: Sketch (1 mark)**
> [Student should draw: (i) cross-section of crevice, (ii) labeling anode inside, cathode outside, (iii) Cl⁻ and H⁺ arrows, (iv) pH profile and O₂ profile along the depth.]

---

#### Model Solution A(b): Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $i_a = 8 \, \mu A/cm^2 = 8 \times 10^{-6} \, A/cm^2$
- $A = 5 \, cm^2$
- $t = 60 \, days = 60 \times 86400 = 5.184 \times 10^6 \, s$
- $\rho_{sol} = 25 \, \Omega \cdot cm$
- $L = 2 \, cm$
- $w = 0.01 \, cm$ (0.1 mm = 0.01 cm)
- $M_{Fe} = 55.85 \, g/mol, \quad n = 2, \quad F = 96485 \, C/mol$

**Solution:**

**(i) Mass of Fe dissolved in 60 days (3 marks):**

$$I = i_a \cdot A = 8 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 = 4 \times 10^{-5} \, A$$

$$Q = I \cdot t = 4 \times 10^{-5} \times 5.184 \times 10^6 = 207.36 \, C$$

$$n_{e^-} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{207.36}{96485} = 2.149 \times 10^{-3} \, mol \, e^-$$

$$n_{Fe} = \frac{n_{e^-}}{2} = 1.075 \times 10^{-3} \, mol$$

$$m_{Fe} = n_{Fe} \cdot M_{Fe} = 1.075 \times 10^{-3} \times 55.85 = 0.0600 \, g = 60.0 \, mg$$

**(ii) IR drop across the crevice (2 marks):**

Cross-sectional area of solution column:
$$A_{cs} = w \times 1 \, cm = 0.01 \times 1 = 0.01 \, cm^2$$
(per unit width along the crevice mouth)

Resistance:
$$R_{crevice} = \frac{\rho_{sol} \cdot L}{A_{cs}} = \frac{25 \times 2}{0.01} = 5000 \, \Omega$$

IR drop:
$$\Delta E_{IR} = I \cdot R_{crevice} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \times 5000 = 0.20 \, V = 200 \, mV$$

**(iii) Is the crevice active? (2 marks):**

Passive range of 304 SS in chloride solution ≈ **300 mV**.
Calculated IR drop = **200 mV**.

Since **200 mV < 300 mV**, the IR drop alone is **insufficient** to push the deepest crevice region out of the passive zone. However, when combined with the **acidification** (pH drop to ~1) and **Cl⁻ enrichment**, the **practical passivity is broken** and the crevice is **active**. Engineering verdict: **The crevice WILL sustain active corrosion** under these conditions.

---

### **Question B: (a)** What are the various factors affecting crevice corrosion? Discuss in detail how crevice geometry (width and depth) influences the attack. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the Pourbaix IR drop mechanism of crevice corrosion. Derive the expression for the maximum depth at which a crevice can sustain active corrosion, given a passive range of ΔE_pass and a crevice solution resistivity ρ_sol. (7 marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Alternative Choice]**

#### Model Solution B(a): Factors and Geometry (7 marks)

> **Step 1: Introduction (1 mark)**
> Crevice corrosion is influenced by a complex interplay of **metallurgical, geometric, and environmental** factors. The geometry itself is the **primary** controlling variable, since without a confined electrolyte, none of the other factors can establish the differential aeration cell.

> **Step 2: Crevice Geometry (Width) (2 marks)**
> The crevice width **w** is the single most important geometric variable. There exists an **optimum range** for attack:
> - **w < 0.025 mm:** Capillary forces hold electrolyte tightly, but the **IR drop is enormous** — passive film is protected, attack is suppressed.
> - **w = 0.025 to 0.1 mm:** **Most dangerous range** — electrolyte is held, but IR drop is moderate. Diffusion of O₂ is restricted. Differential aeration cell thrives.
> - **w > 0.1 mm:** Solution convection removes the stagnant chemistry; behaves like an open surface. Crevice attack weakens.

> **Step 3: Crevice Geometry (Depth) (2 marks)**
> The depth **L** affects:
> - **Diffusion path length:** Deeper crevice → longer diffusion path for O₂ in → O₂ is more thoroughly depleted → stronger DAC.
> - **IR drop:** Deeper crevice → larger ohmic drop. Beyond a **critical depth**, the deepest region becomes so resistive that it cannot sustain the anodic current → attack is limited to a shallow band near the mouth.
> - **Mass transport of Cl⁻ and H⁺:** Deeper crevice → slower equilibration → chemistry remains aggressive longer.

> **Step 4: Other Factors (2 marks)**
> 1. **Bulk chloride concentration** — Higher Cl⁻ → lower CCT → attack at lower temperatures.
> 2. **Temperature** — increases diffusion rates and reaction kinetics; quantified via CCT.
> 3. **Alloy composition** — Mo and N improve resistance; quantified via PREN.
> 4. **pH of bulk solution** — Lower pH → more aggressive; acidification inside the crevice amplifies this.
> 5. **Dissolved oxygen** — Higher pO₂ increases cathodic driving force.
> 6. **Stagnation vs. flow** — Stagnant solutions allow chemistry to evolve; flowing solutions dilute the aggressive crevice chemistry.
> 7. **Alloy surface condition** — Rough surfaces trap electrolyte more easily.

---

#### Model Solution B(b): Pourbaix IR Drop Mechanism (7 marks)

> **Step 1: Concept (2 marks)**
> Pourbaix argued that the **decisive** factor in maintaining active crevice corrosion is the **ohmic (IR) potential drop** along the resistive electrolyte column in the crevice. The mechanism is illustrated in the diagram below:

```
[Bulk solution - O2 rich]
         |
         v  <-- Cathode (ORR dominant)
[Metal surface - external] -----------> Mn+ accumulates
         |                              Cl- migrates in
[Crevice mouth - aggressive]            H+ produced, pH ~ 1-2
         |
   IR drop, ΔE_ohm
         |
         v
[Crevice bottom - protected if IR drop > passive range]
```

> **Step 2: Derivation (4 marks)**

> The crevice solution acts as a resistor of cross-section $A_{cs}$ and length $x$ (depth from the mouth).

> Resistance of solution from mouth to depth $x$:

> $$R(x) = \frac{\rho_{sol} \cdot x}{A_{cs}}$$

> Anodic current density at depth $x$:

> $$i_a(x) = i_{a,0} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta \phi(x)}{b_a}\right)$$

> where $b_a$ is the anodic Tafel slope.

> Ohmic drop:

> $$\Delta \phi(x) = I \cdot R(x) = i_a(x) \cdot A_{cs} \cdot \frac{\rho_{sol} \cdot x}{A_{cs}} = i_a(x) \cdot \rho_{sol} \cdot x$$

> **Critical condition:** crevice is active at depth $x$ if and only if the potential drop does **not** exceed the passive range $\Delta E_{pass}$:

> $$\Delta \phi(x) \leq \Delta E_{pass}$$

> $$i_a(x) \cdot \rho_{sol} \cdot x \leq \Delta E_{pass}$$

> **Maximum depth for active corrosion:**

> $$\boxed{x_{max} = \frac{\Delta E_{pass}}{i_a \cdot \rho_{sol}}}$$

> **Step 3: Interpretation (1 mark)**
> Beyond $x_{max}$, the IR drop exceeds the passive range, and the deepest region can no longer sustain anodic dissolution — it becomes **electrolytically protected** by the active mouth. This is why the most severe attack is concentrated at a shallow band at the crevice mouth, and why **moderate-depth** crevices are often more destructive than very deep ones.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing CCT and CPT** — Always declare which temperature you are using and why.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — current density in µA/cm² must be converted to A/cm² before using Faraday's law. Time must be in **seconds**, not days.
> 3. **Neglecting the hydrolysis step** — Many students write MCl_n accumulation but **forget** the hydrolysis that produces H⁺. This is mandatory for full marks on the "pH drops to 1–2" question.
> 4. **Using the wrong "n"** — For iron, n = 2 (Fe²⁺); for Cr, n = 3 (Cr³⁺); for SS as an alloy, an **effective n** must be used or the calculation must specify which element is dissolving.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch** in long answers — a labelled diagram carries **at least 1–2 marks** in every 7-mark sub-question.
> 6. **Mixing up anodic and cathodic zones** — the **inside of the crevice is the anode**, the **outside is the cathode**. Reversing this loses full marks on the DAC question.
> 7. **Not writing the units in the final answer** — KTU examiners **deduct 0.5 marks** if units are missing in numericals.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Pin This Section.**

- [x] **Crevice corrosion** = geometry-driven localized attack inside narrow gaps.
- [x] It is a **differential aeration cell (DAC)** corrosion — anode inside, cathode outside.
- [x] **Three stages:** Initiation (O₂ depletion) → Propagation (Cl⁻ + H⁺ buildup, pH 1–2) → Death or perforation.
- [x] **Critical crevice width:** 0.025–0.1 mm. Below or above this, attack is suppressed.
- [x] **CCT < CPT** for the same alloy (always).
- [x] **Pourbaix IR drop mechanism:** deepest crevice regions become protected by ohmic drop; attack concentrated near the mouth.
- [x] **Driving equations:** Faraday's law for mass loss; Fick's law for diffusion-limited current; Ohm's law for IR drop.
- [x] **Maximum active depth:** $x_{max} = \Delta E_{pass} / (i_a \cdot \rho_{sol})$.
- [x] **Most susceptible alloys:** stainless steels (especially 304, 316), Al alloys, Ti.
- [x] **Most aggressive environments:** chloride-rich, acidic, oxygenated, stagnant.
- [x] **Prevention:** design out crevices, use Mo-bearing SS, use sealants, lower Cl⁻, cathodic protection.
- [x] **Test standards:** ASTM G48 (mass loss), ASTM G78 (galvanic/crevice), ASTM G150 (CPT electrochemical).
- [x] **Key constant to remember:** F = 96,485 C/mol; K = 3.27 × 10⁻³ mm·g/(µA·cm·year).
- [x] **Hydrolysis equation is mandatory** in any "explain the mechanism" answer.
- [x] **Always include a labelled sketch** in 7-mark and 14-mark mechanism questions.

> **End of Module 2 — Crevice Corrosion Notes (PEPOT523 / KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pitting Corrosion** is an extremely localized form of corrosion attack that produces small holes, cavities, or "pits" on a metal surface, while the vast majority of the surrounding surface remains virtually unattacked. It is classified as a **non-uniform (localized) corrosion** phenomenon and is considered one of the most insidious and destructive forms of material degradation because the cumulative metal loss is small while the penetration depth can lead to catastrophic structural failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Pitting is treated under *Localized Forms of Corrosion*. The student must master the mechanistic stages, the electrochemical criterion (pitting potential $E_{pit}$), the role of chloride/bromide/halide ions, and the **electrochemical measurement techniques** like the cyclic potentiodynamic polarization method.

According to the **ASTM G46-21** standard, pitting is characterized by pits whose **diameter is approximately equal to (or greater than) their depth**. If the diameter is much smaller than the depth, the attack is called **crevice corrosion** (a closely related phenomenon).

$$d_{pit} \approx h_{depth} \quad \text{(Pitting)}$$

$$d_{pit} \ll h_{depth} \quad \text{(Crevice Corrosion)}$$

where $d_{pit}$ is the pit mouth diameter and $h_{depth}$ is the penetration depth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome (CO) Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO2** of PEPOT523 — *"Identify and analyze various forms of localized corrosion and their controlling parameters."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaking Bucket" Mental Model

Imagine a galvanized steel water tank placed in a damp coastal basement. From the outside, the tank looks perfectly shiny and new for years. But one day, the basement floods. A few weeks later, you notice a tiny **drip** from the bottom of the tank. On inspection, you find a single pinhole — about **1 mm wide but 4 mm deep** — through which all the water is leaking.

This is exactly how pitting corrosion behaves:

- **The shiny surface = Passivated metal** (e.g., stainless steel, Al, Ti) where a thin protective oxide film has formed.
- **The pinhole = An active pit** where the protective film has been locally broken.
- **The slow, silent leak = The aggressive anodic reaction** (e.g., $Fe \to Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$) sustained at the pit bottom, while the rest of the surface acts as a giant cathode.

You can lose **80% of your wall thickness** through a single pit without any visible warning, leading to perforation of pressure vessels, pipeline leaks, or stress-corrosion cracking initiation. Pitting is therefore a **"silent assassin"** in materials engineering.

---

## 1.3 Pitting as a Special Case of Differential Aeration Cell

Pitting is essentially a **micro-galvanic cell** with an extremely small anode area and a very large cathode area.

$$i_{a} \times A_{a} = i_{c} \times A_{c} \quad \Rightarrow \quad i_{a} = i_{c} \cdot \frac{A_{c}}{A_{a}}$$

Since $A_{c} \gg A_{a}$, the anodic current density $i_{a}$ inside the pit becomes **enormous**, causing rapid penetration even though total weight loss is negligible. This is the fundamental geometric reason pitting is so destructive.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Pitting Analysis

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pitting potential | $E_{pit}$ | mV (vs SCE) | Critical potential above which pits nucleate |
| Protection potential | $E_{prot}$ | mV (vs SCE) | Below which existing pits stop growing |
| Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number | PREN | dimensionless | Alloy ranking parameter |
| Pitting depth | $d$ | µm or mm | Geometric extent of attack |
| Pitting density | $\rho_{pit}$ | pits/m² | Population of pits |
| Pitting factor | $PF$ | dimensionless | Ratio of deepest pit to average penetration |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Pitting Factor (PF)** is a key exam-relevant parameter:
> $$PF = \frac{d_{max}}{d_{avg}}$$
> A value of $PF = 1$ indicates uniform corrosion; $PF \gg 1$ indicates severe localized pitting.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Mechanistic Stages of Pitting

Pitting is **not instantaneous**. It follows a sequence of well-defined electrochemical stages. The KTU examiner often asks students to "describe the mechanism of pitting" — the following five-stage model is the canonical answer.

### Stage 1 — Pit Initiation (Induction Period, $\tau_{i}$)
- A passive film (e.g., $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$ on stainless steel) is locally attacked by aggressive anions — most commonly **chloride (Cl⁻)**, but also **Br⁻, I⁻, SCN⁻**.
- The aggressive anion **adsorbs** on the oxide surface, displacing oxygen from M–O bonds, and **penetrates** the passive film at defect sites (inclusions, grain boundaries, scratches).
- Duration: $\tau_{i}$ can range from **seconds** (high Cl⁻, high E) to **months** (mild conditions). This is the *incubation* stage — no visible pit yet.

$$M + Cl^- \longrightarrow MCl_{ads} + e^-$$

### Stage 2 — Pit Nucleation (Stable Pit Formation)
- The local film breakdown exposes bare metal. The aggressive Cl⁻ creates a localized acidic environment due to metal cation hydrolysis:

$$M^{n+} + H_2O \longrightarrow M(OH)^{(n-1)+} + H^+$$

- The local pH inside the incipient pit can drop to **1 – 3**, far below the bulk solution pH.
- Once $E > E_{pit}$ (or $[\text{Cl}^-]$ exceeds the critical value), a stable pit nucleus forms.

### Stage 3 — Meta-Stable Pit Growth (Sub-Critical Growth)
- The pit grows, but the system continually tries to **repassivate** the bare metal.
- Pit growth is **intermittent** — characterized by current spikes (called "current transients" or "metastable pitting events") seen in potentiostatic experiments.
- The pit may **die** if repassivation wins the kinetic race.

### Stage 4 — Stable Pit Growth (Propagation)
- If the local chemistry (low pH, high Cl⁻, low O₂) becomes aggressive enough, the pit can no longer repassivate.
- An **occluded corrosion cell** is established: the pit interior becomes the anode; the surrounding passive surface becomes the cathode.
- Cathodic reaction on the external surface:
$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \longrightarrow 4OH^-$$
- Anodic reaction inside the pit:
$$M \longrightarrow M^{n+} + n e^-$$
- $\text{M}^{n+}$ cations attract Cl⁻ ions to maintain electroneutrality, and metal chloride salts (e.g., $\text{FeCl}_2$, $\text{CrCl}_3$) form highly soluble, acidic solutions inside the pit.

### Stage 5 — Pit Death (Re-passivation)
- Occurs when applied potential falls **below $E_{prot}$**, or when the local Cl⁻ concentration drops (e.g., due to dilution by bulk solution).
- New pits can also nucleate once conditions allow.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Two-Mark Short-Answer Tip:** If asked "Define pitting potential," answer: *"The pitting potential $E_{pit}$ is the lowest potential at which stable growing pits can nucleate and propagate on a passive metal in a given environment."*

---

## 2.2 The Critical Role of $E_{pit}$ and $E_{prot}$ (Cyclic Polarization)

This is the **single most examined concept** in pitting from Module 2.

When you perform a **cyclic potentiodynamic polarization scan** on a passive metal (e.g., SS 304 in 3.5% NaCl), the polarization curve looks like this:

- The anodic scan shows a low passive current $i_{pass}$ over a wide range.
- At a critical potential, the current rises **sharply** — this is $E_{pit}$.
- When the scan direction is reversed, the current loop closes at a **protection potential** $E_{prot}$ (also called $E_{repassivation}$ or $E_{rp}$).
- The **hysteresis loop** between $E_{pit}$ and $E_{prot}$ represents the energy of new pit nucleation.

The standard ranking is:

$$E_{prot} < E_{pit}$$

And the three regions are:

| Region | Condition | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| $E < E_{prot}$ | Safe — no new pits, old pits die | Immune zone |
| $E_{prot} < E < E_{pit}$ | Old pits grow, no new pits | Propagation zone |
| $E > E_{pit}$ | New pits nucleate AND grow | Active pitting zone |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Design Implication:** A safe operating potential for the metal must be kept **below $E_{prot}$**, not just below $E_{pit}$. Many textbook problems ask this — students often mistakenly choose $E < E_{pit}$, which is only a "pitting-resistant" not "pitting-immune" condition.

---

## 2.3 Critical Pitting Temperature (CPT)

For alloys tested in ferric chloride (ASTM G48), the **Critical Pitting Temperature** is the lowest temperature at which pitting occurs under standard conditions. A related parameter, the **Critical Crevice Temperature (CCT)**, is also used.

> **General rule:** $\text{CCT} < \text{CPT}$ (crevice corrosion usually occurs at a lower temperature than pitting).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PF = \dfrac{d_{max}}{d_{avg}}$ | Pitting Factor — degree of localization |
| 2 | $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$ | Alloy ranking for pitting resistance |
| 3 | $E_{pit}$ = lowest potential for stable pitting | Cyclic polarization parameter |
| 4 | $E_{prot}$ = repassivation potential | Below this, pits die |
| 5 | $\text{CPT}$ (°C) | Critical Pitting Temperature |
| 6 | $i_a \cdot A_a = i_c \cdot A_c$ | Charge balance → high anodic current density |
| 7 | $\Delta E = E_{pit} - E_{prot}$ | Hysteresis loop size — pit nucleation energy |
| 8 | $\log i_L = \log i_0 + \dfrac{\alpha F \eta}{2.303 RT}$ | Butler–Volmer for pit growth kinetics |
| 9 | $v_{pit} = \dfrac{M \cdot i_a}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | Pit penetration rate (m/s) |
| 10 | $\text{Pit depth} = \int_0^t v_{pit} \, dt$ | Time-integrated pit growth |

> [!NOTE]
> In the **PREN** formula above, the **16 × %N** term is more commonly used in **duplex stainless steels**; for standard austenitic SS, the simplified form $\%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo}$ is used. Always state which one you are using in your KTU answer.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Pitting Analysis

- **Materials selection** in chemical, marine, oil & gas industries.
- **Cathodic protection design** — knowing $E_{prot}$ ensures the structure is polarized into the safe region.
- **Failure analysis** — pitting is the most common initiator of **stress-corrosion cracking (SCC)** and **corrosion fatigue**.
- **Quality control** of stainless steel welds, fasteners, and heat-exchanger tubes.
- **Inhibitor selection** — many organic inhibitors shift $E_{pit}$ to more positive (noble) values, raising the safe operating window.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Penetration Rate of a Pit (Faraday's Law Applied Locally)

The local penetration rate of a pit is governed by the **anodic current density** $i_a$ inside the pit. We derive the linear penetration velocity $v$ (m/s) from Faraday's first law.

**Step 1: Faraday's First Law**

The mass $m$ of metal dissolved is proportional to the total charge $Q$ passed:

$$m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

where $M$ is the molar mass (kg/mol), $n$ is the valence number of dissolution, and $F = 96485$ C/mol is Faraday's constant.

**Step 2: Express Charge in Terms of Current Density**

The anodic current $I_a$ acts on the pit internal surface $A_{pit}$:

$$Q = I_a \cdot t = i_a \cdot A_{pit} \cdot t$$

**Step 3: Convert Mass to Volume Using Density**

The dissolved metal occupies a volume $V_{diss} = \dfrac{m}{\rho}$, where $\rho$ is the metal density (kg/m³).

**Step 4: Convert Volume to Linear Depth**

If we approximate the pit as a cylinder of cross-section $A_{pit}$ and depth $h$:

$$V_{diss} = A_{pit} \cdot h$$

**Step 5: Combine All Steps**

$$A_{pit} \cdot h = \frac{i_a \cdot A_{pit} \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 6: Cancel $A_{pit}$ on both sides:**

$$h = \frac{i_a \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 7: Penetration Velocity (constant $i_a$):**

$$\boxed{v_{pit} = \frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{i_a \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}}$$

**Engineering Check (Numerical Example):** For iron in a pit with $i_a = 1 \, \text{A/m}^2$, $M = 55.85 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96485$ C/mol, $\rho = 7874$ kg/m³:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{pit} &= \frac{(1)(55.85 \times 10^{-3})}{(2)(96485)(7874)} \\
&= \frac{0.05585}{1.5194 \times 10^9} \\
&= 3.676 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{m/s} \\
&\approx 1.16 \, \text{mm/year}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **average penetration** — a real pit with $i_a = 100 \, \text{A/m}^2$ would penetrate at **~116 mm/year**, fully justifying why pitting is so dangerous.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pitting Factor from Pit Depth Measurements

Given a metal plate of original thickness $t_0$ and weight $W_0$ after exposure to a corrosive environment:
- Final weight: $W_f$
- Final thickness at the deepest pit: $t_{deep}$
- Average thickness loss: $\Delta t_{avg} = (W_0 - W_f)/(\rho \cdot A)$

**Step 1:** Total metal loss in volume:

$$V_{loss} = \frac{W_0 - W_f}{\rho}$$

**Step 2:** Average penetration depth (uniform spread):

$$d_{avg} = \frac{V_{loss}}{A}$$

**Step 3:** Maximum pit depth (measured microscopically from the original surface):

$$d_{max} = t_0 - t_{deep}$$

**Step 4:** Pitting Factor (definition):

$$PF = \frac{d_{max}}{d_{avg}}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation:

$$\begin{cases}
PF = 1 & \Rightarrow \text{Uniform corrosion} \\
PF > 1 & \Rightarrow \text{Localized corrosion; severity increases with PF} \\
PF \to \infty & \Rightarrow \text{One extreme pit, no general loss} \\
\end{cases}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** If the question gives a pit depth (e.g., 0.5 mm) and a weight loss (e.g., equivalent to 0.05 mm uniform), then $PF = 0.5 / 0.05 = 10$, indicating **severe localized pitting**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Chloride Threshold for Pitting (Linearized Pourbaix-Type Criterion)

A widely cited empirical relationship between chloride concentration $[\text{Cl}^-]$ (ppm) and pitting potential $E_{pit}$ (mV vs SCE) for stainless steel is:

$$E_{pit} = E_{pit}^0 - k \cdot \log[\text{Cl}^-]$$

where $E_{pit}^0$ and $k$ are empirical constants (for 304 SS in NaCl: $E_{pit}^0 \approx +200$ mV, $k \approx 150$ mV/decade).

**Step 1: Set $E_{pit}$ equal to the corrosion potential $E_{corr}$:**

$$E_{corr} = E_{pit}^0 - k \cdot \log[\text{Cl}^-]_{crit}$$

**Step 2: Solve for critical chloride concentration:**

$$\log[\text{Cl}^-]_{crit} = \frac{E_{pit}^0 - E_{corr}}{k}$$

**Step 3: Take the antilog:**

$$[\text{Cl}^-]_{crit} = 10^{(E_{pit}^0 - E_{corr})/k}$$

**Step 4: Example Calculation** for $E_{corr} = -100$ mV, $E_{pit}^0 = +200$ mV, $k = 150$ mV:

$$\begin{aligned}
[\text{Cl}^-]_{crit} &= 10^{(200 - (-100))/150} \\
&= 10^{300/150} \\
&= 10^{2} \\
&= 100 \, \text{ppm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering Implication:** If the actual chloride content of the process stream exceeds 100 ppm, the alloy is at risk of pitting at its free corrosion potential. This is a quick design check used widely in **water-treatment and chemical-process engineering**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Pitting Mechanism — Block Functional Flow

```mermaid

    G -->|No| I[Stable Pit - Acidic Occluded Cell]
    I --> J[Metal Dissolution M to M to the n+]
    J --> K[Cation Hydrolysis Generates H+]
    K --> L[Local pH drops to 1 to 3]
    L --> M[Cl- Migrates In - Maintains Electroneutrality]
    M --> N[Pit Propagates Deep into Metal]
    N --> O[E External Surface Acts as Cathode O2 Reduction]
```

```mermaid
    A --> F[Vertical - Subsurface Growth]
```
