
> [!NOTE]
> **ASTM G46 Standard Categories of Pits:** (1) Narrow, deep; (2) Elliptical; (3) Wide, shallow; (4) Subsurface; (5) Vertical; (6) Undercutting. These classifications are based on **depth/diameter ratios** and are key for failure analysis reports.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style, Module 2]
**"Define pitting corrosion. How is it distinguished from crevice corrosion?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1.5 marks):** Pitting corrosion is a form of extremely localized attack that produces small cavities or holes on a metal surface, while the surrounding area remains largely unaffected. It typically occurs on passive metals like stainless steel, aluminum, and titanium in the presence of aggressive anions such as Cl⁻.
2. **Distinguishing features (1.5 marks):**
   - **Pitting:** Initiates on a freely exposed surface; pit diameter $\approx$ depth.
   - **Crevice corrosion:** Initiates inside crevices, joints, gasket surfaces, or under deposits; pit depth $\gg$ diameter.
   - Mechanistically, pitting arises from localized film breakdown by halide ions, whereas crevice corrosion arises from oxygen-depletion / differential aeration inside a confined geometry.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style, Module 2]
**"What is pitting potential? Why is the protection potential more critical for design than the pitting potential?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Pitting potential (1 mark):** $E_{pit}$ is the lowest electrode potential above which stable pits nucleate and propagate on a passive metal. It is determined from the cyclic potentiodynamic polarization curve as the potential where anodic current rises sharply.
2. **Protection potential $E_{prot}$ (1 mark):** $E_{prot}$ (or $E_{rp}$, repassivation potential) is the potential below which existing pits stop growing and new pits cannot form. It is the lower vertex of the hysteresis loop in cyclic polarization.
3. **Why $E_{prot}$ is more critical (1 mark):** Even if the operating potential lies between $E_{prot}$ and $E_{pit}$, any pre-existing pits will continue to grow. Therefore, the **safe operating potential** for long-term design must be **below $E_{prot}$**, ensuring both pit-death and pit-immunity. Operating only below $E_{pit}$ leaves the structure vulnerable if even one pit has already nucleated.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **mechanism of pitting corrosion** in stainless steel exposed to chloride-rich media. With a neat sketch of the cyclic potentiodynamic polarization curve, define and explain the significance of $E_{pit}$, $E_{prot}$, and the hysteresis loop.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 304 stainless steel plate of original thickness **6.0 mm** is exposed to a 3% NaCl solution for 90 days. After exposure, the plate weighs **498.2 g** (original weight: 502.5 g), and the deepest pit measured microscopically is **1.8 mm**. The plate area is **120 cm²** and the density of SS 304 is **7.93 g/cm³**. Calculate:
   (i) Average corrosion penetration depth.
   (ii) Pitting Factor (PF).
   (iii) Comment on the type of corrosion.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Mechanism of Pitting [7 Marks]**

Pitting corrosion in stainless steel (which is protected by a Cr₂O₃-rich passive film) develops in the following stages:

**Stage 1 — Passive Film Adsorption & Initiation [1 mark]**
- In neutral/alkaline Cl⁻ solution, the passive film on SS is attacked preferentially at defect sites (MnS inclusions, grain boundaries).
- Chloride ions adsorb on the film surface, displacing oxygen from Cr–O bonds and weakening the oxide lattice.

**Stage 2 — Local Film Breakdown & Pit Nucleation [1 mark]**
- Once the local Cl⁻ concentration and potential exceed the threshold, the passive film ruptures, exposing fresh metal.
- Anodic dissolution begins:
$$Fe \to Fe^{2+} + 2e^-; \quad Cr \to Cr^{3+} + 3e^-$$
- Metal cations hydrolyze:
$$Fe^{2+} + 2H_2O \to Fe(OH)_2 + 2H^+$$
- Local pH inside the incipient pit can drop to **1 – 3**, accelerating the attack.

**Stage 3 — Stable Pit Growth (Occluded Cell) [2 marks]**
- The pit interior is the anode; the bulk external surface is the cathode.
- Cathode: $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \to 4OH^-$ (oxygen reduction on the passive surface).
- Anode: $Fe \to Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$ (in the pit).
- Cl⁻ ions migrate into the pit to maintain electroneutrality, forming soluble metal chlorides (e.g., $FeCl_2$).
- The geometric condition $A_{cathode} \gg A_{anode}$ makes the anodic current density extremely high.

**Stage 4 — Repassivation or Propagation [1 mark]**
- If local chemistry dilutes or potential drops below $E_{prot}$, the pit dies.
- Otherwise, the pit propagates, possibly leading to perforation or SCC initiation.

**Polarization Curve Description [2 marks]**

*Sketch Description (since KTU evaluates content):*

```
   log i  |
           |          / (Tafel region above E_pit)
           |         /
           |   -----/---- (Passive region, low i_pass)
           |   |  /
           |   | / <-- E_pit (sharp rise)
           |   |/
           |   |
           |   |\
           |   | \  (Reverse scan, hysteresis loop)
           |   |  \
           |   |   \
           |   |____\___ <-- E_prot
           |________________________________  E (SCE)
              E_corr  E_prot  E_pit
```

- **$E_{pit}$:** The potential where the anodic current rises sharply from the passive plateau. Above $E_{pit}$, new pits form.
- **$E_{prot}$:** The potential on the reverse scan where the current returns to the passive level. Below $E_{prot}$, pits die.
- **Hysteresis loop:** The area enclosed represents the energy barrier for pit nucleation. A large loop indicates high susceptibility to stable pitting.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $t_0 = 6.0$ mm (original thickness)
- $W_0 = 502.5$ g, $W_f = 498.2$ g
- Area $A = 120$ cm²
- Density $\rho = 7.93$ g/cm³
- Exposure time $t = 90$ days
- Deepest pit $d_{max} = 1.8$ mm

**Step 1: Total mass loss**

$$\Delta W = W_0 - W_f = 502.5 - 498.2 = 4.3 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2: Volume of metal lost**

$$V_{loss} = \frac{\Delta W}{\rho} = \frac{4.3}{7.93} = 0.5422 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 3: Average penetration depth (uniform-spread assumption)**

$$d_{avg} = \frac{V_{loss}}{A} = \frac{0.5422}{120} = 4.518 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm} = 0.0452 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Pitting Factor**

$$PF = \frac{d_{max}}{d_{avg}} = \frac{1.8}{0.0452} = 39.82 \approx 40$$

**Step 5: Comment on Type of Corrosion [2 marks]**
- $PF \approx 40 \gg 1$ indicates **severe localized pitting corrosion**.
- For comparison, a value of $PF = 1$ would be uniform corrosion, and $PF > 10$ is typically classified as **"highly localized"** per ASTM G46.
- **Design recommendation:** The operating environment should be checked for chloride concentration; consider upgrading to a higher-PREN alloy (e.g., 316 SS with PREN ~25 vs. 304 SS with PREN ~18).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **factors that influence pitting corrosion** in passive alloys, covering environmental, metallurgical, and electrochemical variables. How is the **Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)** used in alloy selection?

**(b) [7 Marks]** The corrosion engineer of a seawater desalination plant is selecting a material for the brine heater tubes. Seawater contains 19,000 ppm Cl⁻, and the operating temperature is 60 °C. Calculate the **critical Cl⁻ threshold** for pitting of a candidate stainless steel with $E_{pit}^0 = +180$ mV vs SCE, $k = 140$ mV/decade, and a free corrosion potential $E_{corr} = -150$ mV vs SCE. If the actual Cl⁻ in the system is **3.5 wt% (35,000 ppm)**, advise whether the alloy is safe. What practical mitigation strategies would you recommend?

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Factors Influencing Pitting [7 Marks]**

**A. Environmental Factors [2.5 marks]**
1. **Halide ion concentration** — Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻ aggressively break passive films. Higher [Cl⁻] lowers $E_{pit}$.
2. **pH of bulk solution** — Lower pH (more acidic) shifts $E_{pit}$ to more negative (active) values, increasing pitting risk.
3. **Dissolved oxygen** — Supports the cathodic reaction; aerated solutions are more pitting-prone.
4. **Temperature** — Higher T increases ionic conductivity and reaction kinetics. Critical Pitting Temperature (CPT) marks the threshold.
5. **Stagnation vs. flow** — Stagnant solutions allow Cl⁻ buildup in pits; high-velocity flow may strip films and worsen pitting by erosion-corrosion.

**B. Metallurgical Factors [2.5 marks]**
1. **Chromium content** — Increases PREN; Cr forms Cr₂O₃ passive film.
2. **Molybdenum addition** — Even 2–4% Mo greatly enhances Cl⁻ resistance; appears in the PREN formula with multiplier 3.3.
3. **Nitrogen addition** — Stabilizes austenite, forms ammonium ions in pits, raises $E_{pit}$. Appears with multiplier 16 in duplex grades.
4. **Inclusions and second phases** — MnS inclusions in 304 SS are well-known pit initiation sites.
5. **Surface finish** — Polished surfaces resist pitting; rough or cold-worked surfaces are more susceptible.
6. **Cold work** — Introduces strain and dislocation pile-ups; can increase susceptibility.

**C. Electrochemical Factors [1 mark]**
- Higher applied potential → more likely to exceed $E_{pit}$.
- Galvanic coupling with more noble alloys can shift the passive metal to potentials above $E_{pit}$.

**PREN in Alloy Selection [1 mark]**
- Formula: $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$
- **Rule of thumb:**
  - PREN < 18 → unsuitable for marine/seawater (e.g., 304 SS)
  - PREN 18 – 25 → suitable for mild marine service (e.g., 316 SS, PREN ~25)
  - PREN 25 – 40 → seawater and chemical process industry (e.g., 904L, duplex 2205)
  - PREN > 40 → severe service (super-austenitic, e.g., 254 SMO, AL-6XN)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Empirical relationship [1 mark]**
$$E_{pit} = E_{pit}^0 - k \cdot \log[\text{Cl}^-]$$

**Step 2: Set $E_{pit} = E_{corr}$ to find critical chloride [2 marks]**
$$E_{corr} = E_{pit}^0 - k \cdot \log[\text{Cl}^-]_{crit}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $[\text{Cl}^-]_{crit}$ [1 mark]**
$$\log[\text{Cl}^-]_{crit} = \frac{E_{pit}^0 - E_{corr}}{k}$$

**Step 4: Substitute values [1 mark]**
$$\log[\text{Cl}^-]_{crit} = \frac{180 - (-150)}{140} = \frac{330}{140} = 2.357$$

**Step 5: Take antilog [1 mark]**
$$[\text{Cl}^-]_{crit} = 10^{2.357} \approx 228 \, \text{ppm}$$

**Step 6: Compare with actual [1 mark]**
- Critical threshold: **228 ppm Cl⁻**
- Actual: **35,000 ppm Cl⁻**
- Since 35,000 ppm $\gg$ 228 ppm, the alloy is **NOT safe** and will pit under these conditions.

**Recommended Mitigation Strategies (any 2 for full credit):**
- **Upgrade alloy:** Select a higher-PREN grade (e.g., super-austenitic 254 SMO with PREN ~43 or duplex 2205 with PREN ~35).
- **Cathodic protection:** Use sacrificial anodes (zinc or Al) to keep tube potential below $E_{prot}$.
- **Use corrosion inhibitors:** Add nitrite or molybdate-based inhibitors to raise $E_{pit}$.
- **Process control:** Lower Cl⁻ via upstream desalination, deaerate to suppress O₂, or reduce operating temperature below CPT.
- **Coatings:** Apply epoxy or polyurethane coatings on tube surfaces.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $E_{pit}$ and $E_{prot}$** — Many students use $E < E_{pit}$ as the safe criterion, but the *true* safe design region is $E < E_{prot}$. Lose 1 mark if you mix these up.
> 2. **Wrong PREN formula version** — Use $\%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo}$ for standard austenitic SS; add the $+ 16 \times \%\text{N}$ term only for duplex grades or when explicitly stated. Wrong formula → 0.5 mark deducted.
> 3. **Forgetting units in penetration rate derivation** — Always end with units in m/s or mm/year; examiners mark this strictly.
> 4. **Omitting the role of MnS inclusions** — A common reason pits initiate in 304 SS. Mentioning this in the mechanism question shows depth.
> 5. **Calculating pitting factor incorrectly** — It is the ratio of *deepest pit* to *average penetration*, not the ratio to original thickness.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency KTU Module 2 — Pitting Cheat Sheet**

- **Pitting = Localized attack on passive metals** producing small deep holes while the rest of the surface is intact.
- **Most affected alloys:** Stainless steel (304, 316), Al and its alloys, Ti, Ni-Cr-Mo alloys.
- **Most aggressive ion:** Chloride (Cl⁻); also Br⁻, I⁻, SCN⁻.
- **Driving force:** Local film breakdown by halide adsorption at defect sites (MnS inclusions, grain boundaries).
- **Self-sustaining via:** Cation hydrolysis → low pH inside pit → Cl⁻ migration → autocatalytic growth.
- **$E_{pit}$:** Pitting potential — onset of stable pitting in cyclic polarization.
- **$E_{prot}$:** Protection potential — repassivation; true safe boundary.
- **Operating potential must be below $E_{prot}$** for long-term design safety.
- **Hysteresis loop area** $\propto$ pit nucleation energy.
- **Pitting Factor (PF):** $PF = d_{max} / d_{avg}$. Higher PF = more localized.
- **PREN:** $\%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \cdot \%\text{Mo} + 16 \cdot \%\text{N}$. Higher PREN = more resistant.
- **CPT (Critical Pitting Temperature)** — minimum T for pitting under standard test (ASTM G48).
- **Pit geometries (ASTM G46):** Narrow-deep, elliptical, wide-shallow, subsurface, undercut.
- **Mitigation:** Cathodic protection, high-PREN alloys, inhibitor dosing, Cl⁻ control, surface polishing, deaeration.
- **Penetration velocity:** $v_{pit} = i_a M / (n F \rho)$ — derived from Faraday's law.
- **Pitting is the most common initiator** of stress-corrosion cracking and corrosion fatigue.
- **ASTM standards to remember:** G46 (pitting), G48 (CPT), G61 (cyclic polarization for pitting).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intergranular Corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Intergranular Corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Intergranular Corrosion (IGC)** is a *localized* form of electrochemical corrosion in which the attack proceeds preferentially along the **grain boundaries** of a polycrystalline metal or alloy, while the interior of the grains (the grain body) remains practically unattacked. The driving force is a difference in chemical composition, electrochemical potential, or protective film integrity between the thin grain-boundary region and the adjacent grain matrix.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box**
> Intergranular Corrosion is a localized corrosion phenomenon where the corroding medium preferentially attacks the **grain boundaries** of an alloy, leaving the grain interiors relatively intact. The mechanical consequence is a sudden loss of strength and ductility with little or no visible surface indication — making it one of the *most dangerous* forms of corrosion in service.

The standard quantitative metric is the **weight loss (mg/dm²/day)** or the **depth of penetration (µm/month)** measured after exposure per **ASTM A262** (for austenitic stainless steels).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Forms of Corrosion)**
> KTU 2024 PEPOT523 specifically requires students to distinguish IGC from:
> - Pitting corrosion (point attack),
> - Crevice corrosion (geometric attack),
> - Stress-corrosion cracking (mechanical + chemical),
> - Dealloying / selective leaching.
> Always use a comparative table in answers to fetch full marks.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Weak Mortar in a Brick Wall"

Imagine a brick wall where each **brick** is a *grain* and the **thin cement layer between the bricks** is the *grain boundary*.

The bricks themselves are dense, strong, and chemically resistant. The cement (grain boundary) is slightly different — maybe a little weaker, slightly more porous, or chemically distinct. If acidic rain falls on the wall day after day, the **bricks look perfectly fine from outside**, but the cement silently dissolves. One day, when someone leans against the wall, **the whole structure crumbles with no visible warning**.

That silent, invisible structural failure is exactly what Intergranular Corrosion does to metals. A stainless-steel pipe may look shiny on the outside while the grain boundaries inside have been hollowed out completely — a phenomenon engineers call **"weld decay"** in the heat-affected zone (HAZ) of welded austenitic stainless steel.

---

## 1.3 Why IGC is Treated as a "High-Yield" KTU Topic

1. It is **industrially catastrophic** (boiler tube failures, aircraft engine discs, nuclear reactor piping).
2. It is directly linked to **sensitization** — a metallurgical phenomenon you must understand for any metallurgy or materials question paper.
3. The prevention methods (stabilization with Ti/Nb, low-carbon grades, solution annealing) are **routinely asked as 7-mark sub-parts** in KTU ESE papers.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor (for KTU Viva + ESE)**
> Just remember the triad: **SENSITIZATION → Cr-DEPLETED ZONE → IGC**.
> Every IGC answer in stainless steel should logically walk through this triad.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

For the most commonly examined case — **austenitic stainless steel (e.g., AISI 304)** — IGC proceeds through the following **six-stage logic chain**:

| Step | Physical / Chemical Event | Why It Matters |
|:----:|:--------------------------|:---------------|
| **1** | Austenitic SS contains **Cr ≈ 18%** and **C ≈ 0.08%** (max) as primary alloying elements. | Chromium forms the passive Cr₂O₃ film that gives "stainlessness." |
| **2** | The alloy is heated in the **sensitization temperature window: 400 °C – 850 °C** (most critical: 600–700 °C). | This range is encountered during **welding, stress relieving, or slow cooling**. |
| **3** | Carbon (a smaller, faster-diffusing atom) migrates to the grain boundaries and reacts with chromium: **Cr + C → Cr₂₃C₆**. | The chromium carbide precipitates *preferentially at the grain boundaries*. |
| **4** | Each Cr atom tied up in the carbide is **removed from the adjacent solid solution**. | The Cr content in a thin zone (~10–20 nm) along the boundary drops **below the critical 12%** needed to maintain passivity. |
| **5** | The Cr-depleted zone becomes **anodic** with respect to the Cr-rich grain interior. | A **micro-galvanic cell** is established: anodic GB ⟷ cathodic grain body. |
| **6** | In a corrosive medium (e.g., H₂SO₄, HNO₃, NaCl + oxidizer), the anodic grain boundaries **dissolve preferentially**, leaving the grains to fall out. | Mechanical integrity collapses with negligible weight loss. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Chromium Depletion — Quantitative**
> The Cr concentration profile across a grain boundary is a classic diffusion-limited profile. Below the **passivity threshold of ~12 wt% Cr**, the protective oxide film cannot reform — the boundary is electrochemically *active*. This is the heart of the **Streicher / Strauss** theory of IGC in stainless steels.

---

## 2.2 The Three "Classical" Variants of IGC in Stainless Steels

The KTU examiner expects you to differentiate these:

1. **Weld Decay** — Attack in the HAZ a few mm away from the fusion line (typical 304 grade, slow cooled).
2. **Knife-Line Attack (KLA)** — A thin, knife-edge attack *right at the fusion line* of a stabilized grade (e.g., 321 / 347) after *multi-pass welding*.
3. **End-Grain Attack** — Preferential corrosion along elongated grain boundaries in heavily cold-worked components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stabilization Logic (KTU Favourite)**
> Adding strong carbide-formers — **Ti (321) or Nb (347)** — "ties up" the carbon as **TiC** or **NbC** *before* it can react with chromium. This is why **321 and 347 grades are immune to weld decay** in single-pass welding. However, in *multi-pass* welding, TiC/NbC near the fusion line can themselves dissolve and re-precipitate as Cr-depleting carbides → **Knife-Line Attack**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units / Boundary Condition |
|:-:|:---------------------|:--------|:---------------------------|
| 1 | $\mathrm{Cr_{crit}} \ge 12\ \mathrm{wt\%}$ | Minimum Cr required in solid solution to maintain passivity (per **Streicher / ASTM G48** guidance) | wt % |
| 2 | $\Delta C_{Cr}(x,t) = C_0 - C_s\,\mathrm{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{Cr}\,t}}\right)$ | Cr depletion profile (error-function diffusion solution) at depth $x$, time $t$, diffusivity $D_{Cr}$ | $D_{Cr}$: m²/s; $t$: s; $x$: m |
| 3 | $D = D_0\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_d}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius temperature dependence of $D$ (Cr diffusion in austenite: $D_0 \approx 0.08\ \mathrm{cm^2/s}$, $Q_d \approx 264\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}$) | $R = 8.314\ \mathrm{J/mol\cdot K}$ |
| 4 | Sensitization index (per **ASTM A262-E Strauss**) | $I_S = \dfrac{\text{depth of GB attack}}{\text{grain size}}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Maximum safe carbon content (L-grade) | $\mathrm{C} \le 0.03\ \mathrm{wt\%}$ (e.g., **304L, 316L**) | wt % |
| 6 | Stabilizer stoichiometry (Nb rule) | $\mathrm{\%Nb} \ge 10 \times \%C$ | wt % |
| 7 | Stabilizer stoichiometry (Ti rule) | $\mathrm{\%Ti} \ge 5 \times (\%C - 0.02)$ | wt % |
| 8 | Corrosion rate (weight loss) | $\mathrm{CR} = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$ where $K = 8.76\times10^4$ for mm/yr, $T$ = hours, $A$ = cm², $W$ = mg, $D$ = g/cm³ | mm/year |
| 9 | Time-temperature-sensitization (TTS) diagram | Empirical C-curve with **nose at ~675 °C**; sensitized if $(T,t)$ lies inside the curve | °C, s |
| 10 | ASTM A262 practice selection | A=Boiling 65% HNO₃ (Huey); B=Streicher (Fe₂(SO₄)₃–H₂SO₄); E=Strauss (Cu–CuSO₄–16% H₂SO₄) | — |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power generation:** IGC of 304 SS superheater tubes in coal-fired boilers has caused **catastrophic ruptures**; modern plants use **304H, 321H, or 347H** with controlled C and stabilized microstructure.
- **Nuclear industry:** Reactor core components use **low-carbon 304L / 316L** to avoid sensitization during stress-relief at 600 °C.
- **Chemical process industry:** Pickling tanks, H₂SO₄ storage — Strauss-test acceptance is mandatory.
- **Aerospace & defense:** Turbine discs (Inconel 718) suffer a related form called **"serrated grain boundary attack"** under sustained high-T exposure.

> The **economic utility** of understanding IGC is enormous: a single failure in a petrochemical heat exchanger can cost **crores of rupees** in downtime, while prevention by simply specifying a **low-carbon (L) grade** adds only **2–5% to the material cost**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Chromium Depletion Profile at a Grain Boundary

We model the precipitation of Cr₂₃C₆ at the grain boundary as a *diffusion-controlled* depletion problem. This is a classic **moving-boundary diffusion** situation.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

- The alloy is held isothermally at temperature $T$ for time $t$.
- The grain boundary acts as a **perfect sink** for C, instantly precipitating all incoming C as Cr₂₃C₆.
- Diffusion of Cr in austenite is **Fickian** with constant diffusivity $D_{Cr}$.
- Geometry: semi-infinite solid with a planar sink at $x = 0$.

### 3.1.2 Governing Equation (Fick's Second Law)

$$
\dfrac{\partial C_{Cr}}{\partial t} \;=\; D_{Cr}\,\dfrac{\partial^{2} C_{Cr}}{\partial x^{2}}
$$

with initial and boundary conditions:

$$
C_{Cr}(x,\,0) \;=\; C_{0} \quad ; \quad C_{Cr}(0,\,t>0) \;=\; C_{s} \quad ; \quad C_{Cr}(x \to \infty,\,t) \;=\; C_{0}
$$

where $C_{0}$ is the bulk Cr content and $C_{s}$ is the equilibrium solubility of Cr in austenite at $T$.

### 3.1.3 Solution

Using the standard semi-infinite diffusion solution (Boltzmann / error-function method):

$$
C_{Cr}(x,\,t) \;=\; C_{s} \;+\; (C_{0}-C_{s})\,\mathrm{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{Cr}\,t}}\right)
$$

The **depletion depth** $\delta$ (the distance over which Cr falls below the critical $C_{crit} = 12\ \mathrm{wt\%}$) is obtained by setting $C_{Cr}(\delta,t) = C_{crit}$:

$$
\mathrm{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{\delta}{2\sqrt{D_{Cr}\,t}}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{C_{crit}-C_{s}}{C_{0}-C_{s}}
$$

Since the right-hand side is a constant $\eta$ for a given $T$ and bulk chemistry, we obtain:

$$
\delta \;=\; 2\sqrt{D_{Cr}\,t}\;\cdot\;\mathrm{erf}^{-1}(\eta)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key physical insight:** $\delta \propto \sqrt{t}$. The depleted zone widens as the square root of the holding time — this is why **longer sensitization times produce deeper IGC attack**.

### 3.1.4 Temperature Dependence (Arrhenius)

Substituting $D_{Cr}(T) = D_0\exp(-Q_d/RT)$:

$$
\delta(T,t) \;=\; 2\,\sqrt{D_0\,t\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_d}{RT}\right)}\;\cdot\;\mathrm{erf}^{-1}(\eta)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\delta(T,t) \;=\; 2\,\mathrm{erf}^{-1}(\eta)\;\sqrt{D_0\,t}\;\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_d}{2RT}\right)\;}
$$

This equation, together with empirical data for $D_0$ and $Q_d$, is the basis of the **Time–Temperature–Sensitization (TTS) diagram** used in ASTM A262 practice E.

### 3.1.5 Numerical Sanity Check (304 SS, $T=675^\circ \mathrm{C}$, $t=1\ \mathrm{h}$)

$$
T = 675+273 = 948\ \mathrm{K}
$$

$$
D_{Cr} \approx 0.08\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{264000}{8.314 \times 948}\right)\ \mathrm{cm^2/s}
$$

Exponent:

$$
-\dfrac{264000}{8.314 \times 948} \;=\; -\dfrac{264000}{7881.7} \;\approx\; -33.49
$$

$$
D_{Cr} \approx 0.08 \times e^{-33.49} \;\approx\; 0.08 \times 2.92\times 10^{-15} \;\approx\; 2.34\times 10^{-16}\ \mathrm{cm^2/s}
$$

$$
D_{Cr}\,t \;=\; 2.34\times 10^{-16} \times 3600 \;\approx\; 8.42\times 10^{-13}\ \mathrm{cm^2}
$$

$$
\sqrt{D_{Cr}\,t} \;\approx\; 9.18\times 10^{-7}\ \mathrm{cm} \;\approx\; 9.18\ \mathrm{nm}
$$

$$
\delta \;\approx\; 2 \times 0.6 \times 9.18\ \mathrm{nm} \;\approx\; 11\ \mathrm{nm}
$$

This is **in excellent agreement** with experimental TEM measurements of Cr-depleted zones (≈10–20 nm), confirming that the diffusion model captures the essential physics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters in KTU ESE:**
> A 14-mark question on IGC almost always expects one numerical/analytical sub-part. Using the Arrhenius-TTS framework with cited diffusivity values guarantees full marks under the KTU 2024 marking scheme (CO₂ — *Apply corrosion engineering principles to design against specific forms of corrosion*).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — IGC vs. Related Forms of Corrosion

| Parameter | **Intergranular (IGC)** | **Pitting** | **Crevice** | **SCC** | **Dealloying** |
|:----------|:-----------------------|:-----------|:-----------|:-------|:--------------|
| Attack location | Grain boundaries | Random surface points | Gaps / under deposits | Across grains, follows GB or slip lines | One element of an alloy |
| Visibility | **Internal, hard to detect** | Surface pits | Hidden in joints | Surface cracks | Surface colour change |
| Driving force | Cr depletion + galvanic micro-cell | Cl⁻ breakdown of passive film | Differential aeration | Tensile stress + specific environment | Electrochemical selectivity |
| Critical agent | Carbon (sensitization) | Halides (Cl⁻) | Stagnant electrolyte | Cl⁻, OH⁻, H₂S | Acid + noble/active pair |
| Prevention | Low C, Ti/Nb stabilization, solution anneal | Higher Cr/Mo, clean surfaces | Design to avoid gaps, gasket care | Lower residual stress, alternate alloy | Cathodic protection, alloy selection |
| KTU board frequency | **Very High** | High | Medium | High | Low |
| Key ASTM test | A262 / G28 | G48 | G78 | G30 / G36 | G34 (dezincification) |

> [!TIP]
> **Use this matrix directly as a 7-mark comparative answer in Part B.** The KTU examiner awards full marks only when the table is *accompanied by a brief explanatory paragraph (3–4 lines)* justifying each row.
