## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PEPOT523]`
**Define intergranular corrosion. Why is it considered the most insidious form of localized corrosion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Intergranular corrosion is a *localized* form of attack in which the corrosion proceeds preferentially along the **grain boundaries** of a metal or alloy while the grain interiors remain passive. (1 mark)
>
> It is considered the most insidious because:
> 1. The exterior surface appears *clean and shiny* even when the grain boundaries deep inside have been completely dissolved. (1 mark)
> 2. There is a *catastrophic loss of mechanical strength and ductility* with negligible weight loss, so routine inspection methods (visual, weight-loss coupons) fail to detect it. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sessional-II PEPOT523]`
**What is "sensitization" in austenitic stainless steel? State the temperature range over which it occurs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Sensitization is the phenomenon in which chromium-rich carbides (Cr₂₃C₆) precipitate at the grain boundaries of austenitic stainless steel when the alloy is held or slowly cooled through the temperature range **400 °C – 850 °C** (most critical: 600–700 °C). (2 marks)
>
> This depletes the adjacent matrix of chromium below the **~12 wt%** passivity threshold, leaving the grain boundaries electrochemically active and susceptible to intergranular corrosion. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note**
> In Part B, KTU follows the **Module Internal Choice (MIC)** scheme: you must answer *one* full question from the choice offered. Each question has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Sub-part (a) is usually descriptive/mechanism-based; sub-part (b) is numerical/comparative/prevention-based.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q.II.2 of PEPOT523]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the mechanism of intergranular corrosion in 18-8 austenitic stainless steel due to **sensitization**. Discuss the role of chromium depletion and the conditions under which it occurs.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark)**
Intergranular corrosion (IGC) in austenitic stainless steels is a localized attack in which corrosive media preferentially dissolve the grain-boundary region. The most common cause is *sensitization*.

**Step 2 — Sensitization Conditions (2 marks)**
- Heating the alloy in the temperature range **400 °C – 850 °C** (peak rate near 675 °C).
- Encountered in: welding, hot forming, stress-relief heat treatment, slow cooling from solution-anneal temperature.

**Step 3 — Mechanism — six stages (3 marks)**

1. Carbon atoms (smaller, faster diffusers) migrate to the grain boundaries.
2. They react with chromium to precipitate **Cr₂₃C₆** at the boundary.
3. Each mole of carbide consumes 23 atoms of Cr.
4. The Cr concentration in a 10–20 nm zone around the boundary falls **below 12 wt%**.
5. The Cr-depleted zone becomes *anodic*; the Cr-rich grain interior becomes *cathodic*.
6. A **micro-galvanic cell** forms across each boundary; the boundary dissolves preferentially.

**Step 4 — Sketch (1 mark)** — A labelled diagram showing (i) grain interior with Cr ≈ 18%, (ii) Cr-depleted zone (~12% or less) along GB, (iii) Cr₂₃C₆ carbide particles decorating the GB, and (iv) anodic vs cathodic arrows indicating the local cell.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** List and explain **any four** methods of preventing intergranular corrosion in austenitic stainless steels. Give one suitable engineering application for each.

**Model Solution:**

| # | Prevention Method | Mechanism | Typical Application |
|:-:|:------------------|:----------|:--------------------|
| 1 | **Low-carbon grade (304L / 316L, C ≤ 0.03%)** | Insufficient C to form continuous Cr-depleted zone at GB | Nuclear reactor piping, pharmaceutical vessels |
| 2 | **Stabilized grade (321 = Ti-stabilized; 347 = Nb-stabilized)** | Ti or Nb has higher affinity for C than Cr, so TiC / NbC form first; Cr remains in solution | Aircraft exhaust manifolds, refinery furnace tubes |
| 3 | **Solution annealing (1050–1100 °C, water quench)** | Re-dissolves Cr₂₃C₆; rapid quench prevents re-precipitation in sensitization range | Heavy-wall welded pressure vessels |
| 4 | **Use of higher-Cr / higher-Mo alloys (e.g., 310, 904L, superaustenitics)** | Even after depletion, residual Cr in solution remains above 12% | Marine heat exchangers, sulfuric acid service |
| 5 | **Avoiding the sensitization range during thermal processing** | Stays outside the 400–850 °C "C-curve" nose | Controlled re-heating of heavy forgings |
| 6 | **Cathodic protection (sacrificial Zn or impressed current)** | Polarizes the GB anodes below the corrosion potential | Buried stainless pipelines |

*(Any four of the above with a one-line mechanism + one application = full 7 marks.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Q.II.4 of PEPOT523]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Differentiate between **weld decay** and **knife-line attack** in austenitic stainless steels. Why does a stabilized grade like 321 suffer KLA in multi-pass welding?

**Model Solution:**

**Weld Decay (4 marks)**
- Occurs in **non-stabilized grades** (e.g., 304, 316).
- Attack zone: a few millimetres **away from the fusion line**, in the HAZ that has been held in the 400–850 °C range during slow cooling.
- Mechanism: Cr₂₃C₆ precipitation + Cr depletion at GB.
- **Single-pass weld** is sufficient to cause it in thick sections.

**Knife-Line Attack (3 marks)**
- Occurs in **stabilized grades** (321, 347) under specific conditions.
- Attack zone: a very thin, sharp band *right at the fusion line* — hence "knife-line."
- Mechanism: In *multi-pass* welding, the **second pass re-heats the first-pass weld metal** to a temperature where **TiC or NbC dissolves** and on cooling, **Cr₂₃C₆ re-precipitates at the prior-austenite GBs**, causing IGC at the fusion line.

**Why KLA happens in stabilized grades (extra credit):**
Stabilization works on the principle that Ti/Nb "steal" the carbon from Cr. However, in multi-pass welding the peak temperature of the second pass (~1300 °C) **re-dissolves TiC/NbC**, freeing the carbon. On cooling through 400–850 °C, **Cr (not Ti/Nb) reacts with the freed C** because Cr is more abundant at that moment — and the KLA appears.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **ASTM A262 Strauss test** for detecting susceptibility to intergranular corrosion in austenitic stainless steel. What does a "failed" specimen look like?

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (1 mark)**
The Strauss test (Practice E of ASTM A262) accelerates IGC by exposing a sensitized specimen to a **strongly oxidizing acid containing Cu²⁺ ions** that do *not* permit cathodic protection of the depleted zone.

**Test Conditions (3 marks)**

- **Solution:** 16% H₂SO₄ + dissolved CuSO₄ (≈ 100 g CuSO₄ per litre of solution), with metallic **copper chips/turnings** added to the beaker.
- **Specimen:** Machined/ground, then **sensitized at 675 °C for 1 h** to simulate worst-case HAZ.
- **Duration:** **72 h** of continuous boiling (~ boiling point of the solution ≈ 120 °C).
- **Geometry:** Specimen immersed *with copper turnings* surrounding it.

**Evaluation (2 marks)**
After exposure, the specimen is **bent 180°** and examined at 10× magnification.
- **Pass:** No cracks visible on the bent surface.
- **Fail:** Visible cracks or fissures propagating along grain boundaries; the specimen often *fractures* on bending with a brittle, faceted appearance.

**Accepted Material (1 mark)**
A "failed" specimen under the Strauss test indicates that the material is **not safe** for service in the sensitization range, and the design must switch to a low-carbon (L) or stabilized grade, or the process must be changed to avoid the sensitization temperature.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Loss Points**
> 1. **Writing "grain boundaries are attacked because they are weaker"** — this is *wrong*. GBs are attacked because of **compositional difference (Cr depletion)**, not mechanical weakness. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing 304L with 304H** — *L* = low carbon (≤ 0.03%), *H* = high carbon (0.04–0.10%) used at high temperatures; confusing these loses 1 mark in prevention sub-parts.
> 3. **Forgetting the ASTM number** when describing tests. The Strauss test is **ASTM A262 Practice E**; Huey is Practice A; Streicher is Practice B. Always write the ASTM number in brackets.
> 4. **Writing "Cr₂₃C₆" as "CrC" or "Cr₃C₂"** — the correct carbide in austenitic SS is **Cr₂₃C₆** (M₂₃C₆ type). Wrong stoichiometry loses 1 mark.
> 5. **In the Arrhenius derivation, forgetting to convert °C → K** before substituting into the exponent. This yields a *wildly wrong* diffusivity. The examiner deducts full sub-part marks for dimensional blunders.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Night KTU Revision Card — Intergranular Corrosion**

- **Definition:** Localized attack at grain boundaries; grain interiors intact.
- **Most common system:** Austenitic SS (304 / 316) + sensitizing heat treatment.
- **Sensitization window:** 400 – 850 °C; **nose at 675 °C**.
- **Key chemistry:** Cr + C → **Cr₂₃C₆** at GB; Cr falls below **12 %** in adjacent zone.
- **Micro-cell:** Anodic Cr-depleted GB ⟷ Cathodic Cr-rich grain body.
- **Diffusion profile:** $C_{Cr}(x,t) = C_s + (C_0 - C_s)\,\mathrm{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{Cr} t}}\right)$.
- **Arrhenius:** $D_{Cr} = D_0\exp(-Q_d/RT)$ with $D_0 \approx 0.08\ \mathrm{cm^2/s}$, $Q_d \approx 264\ \mathrm{kJ/mol}$.
- **Depletion depth:** $\delta \propto \sqrt{t}$ — time-squared-root kinetics.
- **Weld Decay** — non-stabilized grades; HAZ few mm from fusion line.
- **Knife-Line Attack** — stabilized grades (321/347) under multi-pass welding.
- **Prevention triad:** (i) **Low-C (L grade)**, (ii) **Ti / Nb stabilization**, (iii) **Solution anneal + water quench**.
- **Stabilizer rule of thumb:** $\mathrm{\%Ti} \ge 5(\%C-0.02)$ and $\mathrm{\%Nb} \ge 10 \times \%C$.
- **ASTM tests:** A262-A Huey (boiling 65% HNO₃); A262-B Streicher (Fe₂(SO₄)₃–H₂SO₄); A262-E Strauss (Cu–CuSO₄–16% H₂SO₄, 72 h).
- **Visual warning:** Shiny surface, zero weight-loss indication — *most dangerous* form.
- **Application domains:** Nuclear piping, boiler superheaters, refinery furnace tubes, chemical process vessels, aerospace turbine discs.
- **Cross-link KTU COs:** **CO₂** (apply), **CO₃** (analyse/test), **CO₄** (design prevention).
- **Examiner mantra:** Always combine *mechanism + prevention + ASTM test* in any 14-mark answer to maximize marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selective Leaching
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Selective Leaching

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Selective Leaching** (also known as **Dealloying** or **Parting**) is a specialized form of aqueous corrosion in which a specific, electrochemically **active constituent** is preferentially dissolved and removed from a multi-phase or single-phase **solid alloy**, leaving behind a mechanically weak, porous residue composed of the more **noble metal(s)**. The corrosion proceeds without any significant change in the overall external dimensions or physical appearance of the component, making it one of the most insidious and deceptive forms of corrosion encountered in service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Selective Leaching is classified under **Section 2.2 – Forms of Corrosion** in the PEPOT523 Corrosion Engineering syllabus. The Board Examiner expects students to identify the mechanism, the alloy system affected, the microstructural evidence, and the standard prevention methods.

The most widely studied engineering examples include:

| Form of Selective Leaching | Element Removed | Parent Alloy | Residual Structure |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Dezincification** | Zinc (Zn) | Cu–Zn Brasses (Cartridge brass, Muntz metal, Admiralty brass) | Porous, spongy Copper |
| **Graphitic Corrosion** | Iron (Fe) | Gray Cast Iron (Fe–3 to 4% C) | Graphite skeleton + iron oxides/oxhydroxides |
| **Dealuminification** | Aluminium (Al) | Cu–Al alloys (Aluminium bronzes) | Porous Copper |
| **Denickelification** | Nickel (Ni) | Cu–Ni alloys (70-30 cupronickel) | Porous Copper |
| **Decobaltification** | Cobalt (Co) | Co-based alloys / Stellite | Spongy noble matrix |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Brick Wall" Picture

Imagine you have built a beautiful wall using two types of bricks — **common red bricks** (representing the **active metal**, e.g., Zinc) and **decorative gold bricks** (representing the **noble metal**, e.g., Copper). The wall is held together with mortar (the metallic bond of the alloy).

Now imagine a slow, persistent acid rain falls on the wall. The acid selectively **dissolves the red common bricks** because they are chemically reactive. The gold bricks do not react and remain in place, but they are no longer held tightly together. Eventually, the wall looks identical in shape and size from the outside, but if you push it, it **crumbles into a heap of loose gold bricks** because the structural support is gone.

**This is exactly what happens in Selective Leaching.** The component retains its macroscopic shape but loses all its mechanical strength. A brass valve fitting may look perfectly shiny on the outside, but inside it has become a brittle, porous copper sponge that fails catastrophically under water pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Selective"?** Because the corrosion process **electively picks out and removes only one element** from the alloy, leaving the other elements behind in their original positions. It is "surgical" corrosion.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Constants Used

- **Standard Electrode Potential of Zn²⁺/Zn:** **E° = –0.76 V (vs. SHE)**
- **Standard Electrode Potential of Cu²⁺/Cu:** **E° = +0.34 V (vs. SHE)**
- **Electrochemical Driving Force (ΔE°):** = 0.34 – (–0.76) = **1.10 V**
- **Molar Mass of Zinc (M_Zn):** = **65.38 g/mol**
- **Molar Mass of Copper (M_Cu):** = **63.55 g/mol**
- **Faraday's Constant (F):** = **96,485 C/mol (≈ 96,500 C/mol)**

## 1.4 Why Selective Leaching is a Critical Engineering Problem

The danger lies in the **silent nature** of attack. Industrial case studies show:
- **Steam condenser tubes** in power plants have failed due to dezincification, causing unplanned shutdowns.
- **Pump impellers** made of cast brass have disintegrated without visible external warning.
- **Pipe fittings** in marine saltwater systems have burst under normal operating pressure.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Board Examiner's Focus Point:** KTU Examiners repeatedly emphasize that Selective Leaching is a **"silent killer"** because it does not produce visible thickness loss, pitting, or external deposit accumulation. Therefore, your answer must always stress the **retention of shape with loss of mechanical integrity**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Mechanism of Selective Leaching

Selective leaching proceeds through three sequential, well-defined stages. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to enumerate these in both short-answer and 14-mark essay questions.

### **Stage 1 — Electrochemical Dissolution of the Active Element**
The less noble element undergoes anodic oxidation and passes into the solution as ions. The electrons released flow through the metallic lattice to the cathodic regions.

$$\text{Zn}_{(s)} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-} \quad (\text{Anodic half-reaction})$$

The corresponding **cathodic reaction** in neutral/alkaline aerated water (the typical environment) is oxygen reduction:

$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}_{(aq)} \quad (\text{Cathodic half-reaction})$$

Combining: **2 Zn + O₂ + 2 H₂O → 2 Zn(OH)₂**

### **Stage 2 — Re-deposition / Retention of the Noble Element**
Two sub-mechanisms exist here, and the examiner loves asking which one dominates:

**(a) Direct Dissolution-Redeposition Mechanism (Ion Path):**
- The active element dissolves into the solution as an ion.
- The noble metal ion (already present or from a complex reaction) **re-plates back** onto the alloy surface.
- Net result: Active element is removed, noble element accumulates as a porous sponge.

**(b) Surface Diffusion / Vacancy-Mediated Mechanism (Direct Path):**
- The active atoms diffuse along the alloy surface and are removed.
- The noble atoms **re-organize in situ** to form a spongy layer without ever leaving the surface.

### **Stage 3 — Formation of the Porous Mechanically-Weak Layer**
The final corrosion product layer consists of:
- A network of the **noble metal** in a spongy, micro-porous state.
- Often mixed with **oxides or hydroxides** of the active metal that precipitate within the pores.
- This layer is **electrically conductive** and **permeable to electrolyte**, allowing the corrosion to continue propagating inward.

## 2.2 Dezincification — The Most Studied Engineering Case

Dezincification is the textbook case study. Two morphological forms exist:

| Type | Description | Appearance | Severity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Layer-Type (Uniform) Dezincification** | The active element is removed as a uniform front; the entire wall thickness is converted progressively to porous copper. | The brass turns from **golden yellow → red/copper color** uniformly. | Wall thinning and loss of strength. |
| **Plug-Type (Localized) Dezincification** | The attack penetrates locally as finger-like plugs, often beneath a scale deposit. | Small **dark plugs** of porous copper in an otherwise yellow matrix. | Sudden leak/perforation with no wall thinning. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Fact for KTU 2024:** Brasses containing **> 15% Zn** are susceptible to dezincification. Brasses with **< 15% Zn** (e.g., red brass, 85-5-5-5) are highly resistant and are used in marine applications. **Inhibited brasses** (e.g., Admiralty brass with 0.02–0.06% As, Sb, or P) are the industry-standard mitigation.

## 2.3 Graphitic Corrosion — The Cast Iron Variant

In gray cast iron, the microstructure consists of graphite flakes embedded in a ferrite-pearlite matrix. When exposed to **mildly aggressive soils or waters**, the iron matrix is selectively dissolved, leaving behind the **graphite flakes intact**.

$$\text{Fe}_{(s)} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-}$$

The remaining graphite skeleton is often bonded by iron oxides/rust, holding the **original shape** of the component. When a pipe suffering from graphitic corrosion is struck, it produces a **dull thud instead of a metallic ring** — this is the classic field test for graphitic corrosion.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Description | Application | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\Delta m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | $\Delta m$ = mass loss; $I$ = current; $t$ = time; $M$ = molar mass; $n$ = electrons transferred; $F$ = Faraday constant | Faraday's Law for mass loss | grams (g) |
| 2 | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot I \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | $CR$ = corrosion rate; $K$ = constant (3272 for mm/yr); $\rho$ = density | Corrosion rate calculation | mm/yr or mpy |
| 3 | $\Delta E^\circ = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}}$ | Standard electrochemical driving force | Determines thermodynamic susceptibility of active element | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $f_{Zn} = \dfrac{m_{Zn,\text{dissolved}}}{m_{Zn,\text{initial}}} \times 100\%$ | Fraction of Zn leached from brass | Quantifies extent of dezincification | Dimensionless (%) |
| 5 | $\text{Depth of attack} = CR \times t$ | Linear depth of porous zone propagation | Service-life prediction of brass tubes | mm or μm |
| 6 | $\%\text{Zn}_\text{critical} \approx 15$ | Threshold Zn content for dezincification | Alloy design guideline | wt.% |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Marine heat exchangers** use 90-10 or 70-30 Cu-Ni tubes because nickel-passivated surfaces resist ammonia-induced attack.
- **Arsenical Admiralty brass** condenser tubes are standard in steam power plants.
- **Cement-mortar lined ductile iron pipes** are replacing gray cast iron in water distribution to prevent graphitic corrosion.
- **Cathodic protection with Mg or Zn anodes** is applied to underground cast iron pipelines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Selective leaching is mitigated by one or a combination of: (1) **Alloy redesign** (lower active element content), (2) **Alloying additions** (As, Sb, P, Sn, Mn as inhibitors), (3) **Environmental control** (lower O₂, Cl⁻, and CO₂), and (4) **Cathodic protection**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass Loss Due to Dezincification via Faraday's Law

A brass condenser tube of surface area $A = 0.5\ \text{m}^2$ is exposed to flowing seawater for $t = 90$ days. The corrosion current measured by linear polarization is $I_{\text{corr}} = 0.45\ \text{A}$. Calculate the **mass of zinc leached out** from the tube, given the half-reaction:

$$\text{Zn} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

### Step 1 — Identify the parameters

Given:
- $I = 0.45\ \text{A}$
- $t = 90\ \text{days} = 90 \times 24 \times 3600\ \text{s} = 7{,}776{,}000\ \text{s}$
- $M_{Zn} = 65.38\ \text{g/mol}$
- $n = 2$ (since Zn²⁺ implies 2 electrons are transferred)
- $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$

### Step 2 — Apply Faraday's Law

The general form of Faraday's Law for mass loss is:

$$\Delta m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values

$$\Delta m = \frac{0.45 \times 7{,}776{,}000 \times 65.38}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the numerator

$$0.45 \times 7{,}776{,}000 = 3{,}499{,}200$$

$$3{,}499{,}200 \times 65.38 = 228{,}780{,}576$$

### Step 5 — Compute the denominator

$$2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970$$

### Step 6 — Final division

$$\Delta m = \frac{228{,}780{,}576}{192{,}970} = 1185.6\ \text{g}$$

### Step 7 — Final answer with units

$$\boxed{\Delta m_{Zn} \approx 1185.6\ \text{g} = 1.186\ \text{kg}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This means that in 90 days, over **1 kilogram of zinc** has been selectively dissolved out of a single 0.5 m² brass tube, leaving behind a porous copper sponge of equal external dimensions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Corrosion Rate (mm/year) from Current Density

Using the same problem, calculate the corrosion rate in **mm/year**, given the density of brass $\rho_{brass} = 8.5\ \text{g/cm}^3$.

### Step 1 — Convert current to current density

$$i_{\text{corr}} = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{0.45\ \text{A}}{0.5 \times 10^4\ \text{cm}^2} = 9.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{A/cm}^2$$

### Step 2 — Use the engineering corrosion rate formula

$$CR\ (\text{mm/yr}) = \frac{K \cdot i_{\text{corr}} \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

where the constant $K = 0.00327\ \text{mm}\cdot\text{g}/(\text{A}\cdot\text{cm}\cdot\text{yr})$ when using the listed units.

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$CR = \frac{0.00327 \times 9.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 65.38}{2 \times 96{,}485 \times 8.5}$$

### Step 4 — Numerator evaluation

$$0.00327 \times 9.0 \times 10^{-5} = 2.943 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$2.943 \times 10^{-7} \times 65.38 = 1.924 \times 10^{-5}$$

### Step 5 — Denominator evaluation

$$2 \times 96{,}485 \times 8.5 = 1{,}640{,}245$$

### Step 6 — Final division

$$CR = \frac{1.924 \times 10^{-5}}{1{,}640{,}245} = 1.173 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{mm/yr}$$

This appears very small because corrosion rate is normalized to surface area. The more meaningful metric is **depth of porous layer in mm/yr**, which for dezincification is usually reported using the **equivalent Zn-loss thickness**:

### Step 7 — Practical depth calculation

$$d = \frac{\Delta m}{\rho_{Zn} \cdot A} = \frac{1185.6\ \text{g}}{7.13\ \text{g/cm}^3 \times 5000\ \text{cm}^2} = 0.0332\ \text{cm} = 0.332\ \text{mm}$$

Over 90 days, the porous copper zone extends **0.332 mm** deep into the brass — small in absolute terms but devastating for a thin-walled tube.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Threshold Zinc Content for Dezincification Resistance

Empirically, brasses with Zn content below 15% are immune to dezincification. Derive why, using the Nernst equation.

For a 70-30 brass (70% Cu, 30% Zn) immersed in aerated neutral water:

### Step 1 — Activity of Zn in the alloy (using Raoultian approximation)

$$a_{Zn} \approx X_{Zn} = 0.30$$

### Step 2 — Nernst equation for the Zn²⁺/Zn half-cell

$$E_{Zn} = E^\circ_{Zn} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln[\text{Zn}^{2+}]$$

For a solid solution, the activity of Zn in the alloy replaces the metallic phase:

$$E_{Zn} = E^\circ_{Zn} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\frac{[\text{Zn}^{2+}]}{a_{Zn}}$$

### Step 3 — Plug in values at 25 °C (T = 298 K)

$$\frac{RT}{F} = 0.02569\ \text{V}, \quad \frac{RT}{2F} = 0.01285\ \text{V}$$

$$E_{Zn} = -0.76 + 0.01285 \ln\frac{[\text{Zn}^{2+}]}{0.30}$$

### Step 4 — Compare to Cu²⁺/Cu half-cell

$$E_{Cu} = +0.34 + 0.02569 \ln[\text{Cu}^{2+}]$$

For dezincification to occur, the difference $E_{Cu} - E_{Zn} > 0$ must hold, and Zn must preferentially oxidize. When $a_{Zn}$ drops (lower Zn content), $E_{Zn}$ becomes more negative, increasing $\Delta E$ — counterintuitively, this should make it *easier*. However, the **kinetic passivation** of low-Zn brasses (formation of a stable Cu₂O film) interrupts this thermodynamic drive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight for KTU 2024:** The 15% Zn rule is a **kinetic, not thermodynamic**, threshold. The Cu₂O passivation film that forms on low-Zn brasses is the actual cause of immunity.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Selective Leaching Variants

| Parameter | Dezincification | Graphitic Corrosion | Dealuminification | Denickelification |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Alloy** | Cu–Zn brass | Gray cast iron | Cu–Al bronze | Cu–Ni alloy |
| **Active element** | Zn | Fe | Al | Ni |
| **Noble residue** | Porous Cu | Graphite + Fe-oxides | Porous Cu | Porous Cu |
| **E°(active)** | –0.76 V | –0.44 V | –1.66 V | –0.25 V |
| **E°(noble)** | +0.34 V | Graphite (inert) | +0.34 V | +0.34 V |
| **Typical environment** | Stagnant, aerated water with Cl⁻ | Mildly aggressive soil / water | Acidic, high-velocity water | Ammonia-bearing water |
| **Inhibitor addition** | As, Sb, P (0.02–0.06%) | N/A (avoid cast iron) | None standard | Fe addition |
| **Mitigation** | Use red brass / inhibited brass | Use ductile iron / cement lining | Limit Al to < 9% | Control NH₃, lower Ni content |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Plug-Type Dezincification Penetration Rate

A condenser tube of wall thickness $w = 1.5\ \text{mm}$ suffers plug-type dezincification at a penetration rate $v_p = 0.15\ \text{mm/yr}$. Calculate the **time to perforation** $t_f$.

### Step 1 — Set up the relation

$$t_f = \frac{w}{v_p}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$t_f = \frac{1.5\ \text{mm}}{0.15\ \text{mm/yr}} = 10\ \text{yr}$$

### Step 3 — Account for safety factor

In practice, design codes use a safety factor of 2:

$$t_{\text{safe}} = \frac{10}{2} = 5\ \text{yr}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Plug-type attack is far more dangerous than layer-type** because the external surface gives no warning. The 5-year safe-life figure is the basis for **scheduled replacement intervals** in KTU-aligned industrial design problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mechanism of Dezincification

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **functional architecture** of the selective leaching process, showing the path of the active element, the role of the electrolyte, and the formation of the porous residue.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Brass Surface Cu-Zn Alloy] --> B[Stage 1 Anodic Dissolution]
    B --> B1["Zn(s) gives Zn²+ plus 2e-"]
    B1 --> C[Electrons flow through metallic lattice]
    C --> D[Stage 1 Cathodic Reaction]
    D --> D1["O2 plus 2H2O plus 4e- gives 4OH-"]
    D1 --> E[Stage 2 Re-deposition Path]
    E --> E1[Cu²+ ions from complex reaction re-plate as porous Cu]
    E1 --> F[Stage 2 Surface Diffusion Path]
    F --> F1[Active atoms diffuse along alloy surface and dissolve]
    F1 --> G[Stage 3 Porous Sponge Formation]
    G --> G1[Porous Cu matrix filled with Zn hydroxide precipitate]
    G1 --> H[Final State: Shape retained, strength lost]
    H --> I{Mitigation Strategy}
    I --> I1[Alloying with As Sb P]
    I --> I2[Lower Zn content below 15 percent]
    I --> I3[Cathodic Protection]
    I --> I4[Environmental Control low Cl and O2]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparison of Plug-Type vs Layer-Type Dezincification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Layer-Type Attack
    L1[Original Brass Wall 1.5 mm] --> L2[Progressive Uniform Zn Loss]
    L2 --> L3[Entire Wall becomes Porous Cu]
    L3 --> L4[Thinning and Strength Loss]
    end
    subgraph Plug-Type Attack
    P1[Original Brass Wall 1.5 mm] --> P2[Localized Zn Loss Beneath Deposit]
    P2 --> P3[Finger-like Plug of Porous Cu]
    P3 --> P4[Sudden Perforation No Wall Thinning]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Microstructural View of Graphitic Corrosion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Gray Cast Iron Component] --> S1[Surface Exposure to Mildly Aggressive Soil]
    S1 --> S2[Fe Matrix Selectively Dissolved]
    S2 --> S3[Fe plus 2H2O gives Fe OH 2 plus H2]
    S3 --> S4[Graphite Flakes Remain Intact]
    S4 --> S5[Iron Oxides Form Bonding Matrix]
    S5 --> S6[External Shape Preserved]
    S6 --> S7[Internal Strength Drastically Reduced]
    S7 --> S8[Field Test: Dull Thud on Hammer Strike]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Engineering Decision Tree for Mitigation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Susceptible Alloy in Service?} -->|Yes| Q2{Environment Aggressive?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3[Apply Combined Mitigation]
    Q2 -->|No| Q4[Monitor Periodically]
    Q3 --> R1[Step 1: Lower Active Element Content]
    Q3 --> R2[Step 2: Add Inhibitor Alloying Elements]
    Q3 --> R3[Step 3: Apply Cathodic Protection]
    Q3 --> R4[Step 4: Use Barrier Coatings]
    R1 --> Z[Inspected Service Life Extended]
    R2 --> Z
    R3 --> Z
    R4 --> Z
    Q4 --> Z
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Notes:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (A, B1, D1, etc.), no reserved keywords are used as node names, and labels contain only raw uppercase text to ensure clean rendering in Mermaid v10+.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Selective Leaching. Give two examples of alloys affected by this form of corrosion.**

**Model Answer:**
Selective Leaching is a form of corrosion in which one element of a solid alloy is preferentially dissolved by an electrochemical reaction, leaving behind a porous mass of the more noble element(s). The component retains its original shape but loses mechanical strength.

**Examples:**
1. **Dezincification** — Zinc is removed from Cu–Zn brasses, leaving porous copper.
2. **Graphitic Corrosion** — Iron is removed from gray cast iron, leaving a graphite skeleton.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Why is dezincification particularly dangerous in service?**

**Model Answer:**
Dezincification is dangerous because it proceeds **silently** — the brass component retains its external shape, dimensions, and even surface colour (in plug-type attack) while the internal microstructure is converted into a weak, porous copper sponge. This leads to **sudden mechanical failure or perforation** under normal operating pressures without any prior visible warning signs such as wall thinning, leaks, or surface deposits.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the mechanism of dezincification of brass with the help of relevant reactions. Discuss the role of arsenic as an inhibitor.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 70-30 brass tube of surface area 0.6 m² is exposed to aerated seawater. The corrosion current density is 1.2 × 10⁻⁴ A/cm². Calculate (i) the mass of zinc leached in 60 days, and (ii) the depth of dezincified layer formed. (Given: M_Zn = 65.38 g/mol, ρ_Zn = 7.13 g/cm³, n = 2, F = 96,485 C/mol).

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Mechanism of Dezincification

**Step 1 — Introduce the phenomenon:**
Dezincification is the selective removal of zinc from copper-zinc brasses containing more than 15% Zn, when exposed to aerated neutral or acidic aqueous environments.

**Step 2 — Stage 1 (Anodic dissolution of Zn):**
The zinc atoms in the brass undergo anodic oxidation:

$$\text{Zn}_{(s)} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-}$$

**Step 3 — Stage 1 (Cathodic reduction of O₂):**
The electrons are consumed by dissolved oxygen:

$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}$$

**Step 4 — Stage 2 (Re-deposition of Cu):**
The Cu²⁺ ions (formed by the simultaneous oxidation of copper or from complex species in the electrolyte) **re-deposit** on the brass surface as a porous layer:

$$\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Cu}_{(s)}$$

**Step 5 — Stage 3 (Sponge formation):**
The overall reaction is:

$$\text{Cu–Zn alloy} + \text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Porous Cu sponge} + \text{Zn(OH)}_2$$

**Step 6 — Role of Arsenic as an inhibitor:**
- Arsenic (0.02–0.06%) is added to brass to suppress dezincification.
- It acts by **blocking the re-deposition of Cu²⁺ ions** onto the brass surface.
- It also **promotes formation of a stable, adherent Cu₂O passive film** on the brass surface.
- This interrupts the redeposition mechanism and prevents the formation of the porous copper sponge.
- Common examples: **Admiralty brass (70% Cu, 29% Zn, 1% Sn, 0.04% As)** used in marine condensers.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation

**Step 1 — List the given values:**
- Surface area: $A = 0.6\ \text{m}^2 = 6000\ \text{cm}^2$
- Current density: $i_{\text{corr}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{A/cm}^2$
- Time: $t = 60\ \text{days} = 60 \times 24 \times 3600 = 5{,}184{,}000\ \text{s}$
- $M_{Zn} = 65.38\ \text{g/mol}$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$, $\rho_{Zn} = 7.13\ \text{g/cm}^3$

**Step 2 — Calculate total current:**
$$I = i_{\text{corr}} \times A = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 6000 = 0.72\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Faraday's Law for (i) mass of zinc:**
$$\Delta m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

$$\Delta m = \frac{0.72 \times 5{,}184{,}000 \times 65.38}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator:**
$$0.72 \times 5{,}184{,}000 = 3{,}732{,}480$$
$$3{,}732{,}480 \times 65.38 = 244{,}025{,}942.4$$

**Step 5 — Denominator:**
$$2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970$$

**Step 6 — Mass calculation:**
$$\Delta m = \frac{244{,}025{,}942.4}{192{,}970} = 1264.6\ \text{g}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta m_{Zn} \approx 1264.6\ \text{g} = 1.265\ \text{kg}}$$

**Step 7 — Depth of dezincified layer (ii):**
$$d = \frac{\Delta m}{\rho_{Zn} \cdot A} = \frac{1264.6\ \text{g}}{7.13\ \text{g/cm}^3 \times 6000\ \text{cm}^2}$$

$$d = \frac{1264.6}{42{,}780} = 0.02956\ \text{cm} = 0.2956\ \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 0.30\ \text{mm}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is graphitic corrosion? Explain why gray cast iron pipe buried in soil may fail by this mechanism, and how it can be prevented.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare dezincification and graphitic corrosion in a tabular form covering: (i) parent alloy, (ii) element leached, (iii) residue left, (iv) environment favouring attack, (v) two methods of prevention each.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Graphitic Corrosion

**Step 1 — Definition:**
Graphitic corrosion is the selective leaching of iron from gray cast iron, leaving behind a porous skeleton of graphite flakes held loosely together by iron oxides. The original shape of the component is preserved.

**Step 2 — Why buried cast iron pipes fail:**
- Buried pipes are exposed to **moist, mildly aggressive soils** containing sulfates, chlorides, and dissolved CO₂.
- The iron matrix (anode) dissolves:
$$\text{Fe} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$
- Graphite is electrically conductive and acts as the **cathode**, supporting oxygen reduction.
- The graphite flakes and iron oxide residue form the residual skeleton.
- **Mechanical failure occurs under internal water pressure** because the graphite skeleton has negligible tensile strength.

**Step 3 — Field identification:**
A pipe suffering from graphitic corrosion, when struck with a hammer, produces a **dull thud** (instead of the normal metallic ring), and a screwdriver can easily penetrate the wall.

**Step 4 — Prevention methods:**
1. **Replace gray cast iron with ductile iron** — Ductile iron has graphite in spheroidal form, which is far more resistant to leaching.
2. **Apply cement-mortar linings** inside water pipes.
3. **Cathodic protection** with magnesium or zinc sacrificial anodes.
4. **Use polymeric wraps or coatings** on buried pipes.
5. **Control soil resistivity** by proper backfilling with inert materials.

---

#### Part (b) — Tabular Comparison

| # | Feature | Dezincification | Graphitic Corrosion |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Parent Alloy | Cu–Zn Brass (Cu-Zn) | Gray Cast Iron (Fe-C with graphite flakes) |
| 2 | Element Leached | Zinc (Zn) | Iron (Fe) |
| 3 | Residue Left | Porous sponge of Copper | Graphite flakes + iron oxides |
| 4 | Environment Favouring Attack | Stagnant, aerated water with chlorides; pH < 7 | Mildly aggressive moist soils; low-resistivity clays |
| 5 | Prevention Method 1 | Use inhibited brass (As, Sb, P) | Use ductile iron pipes |
| 6 | Prevention Method 2 | Reduce Zn content below 15% (red brass) | Apply cement-mortar lining or polymer coating |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:**
> 1. **Writing only the definition and skipping the mechanism** — A 7-mark sub-question on dezincification requires both the three-stage mechanism AND the chemical reactions. Definition alone gets only 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of arsenic/sb/P** — When asked "how is dezincification prevented?", students often say "use a different alloy". Examiners specifically look for the **inhibitor addition (As, Sb, P)** and the **passivation mechanism**.
> 3. **Plug-type vs Layer-type confusion** — These are **two distinct morphologies**. A question that mentions "localized dark plugs" is plug-type; uniform colour change is layer-type. Confusing them costs full marks.
> 4. **Graphitic corrosion is NOT the corrosion of graphite** — It is the **corrosion of iron around the graphite**. Students frequently invert this and lose marks.
> 5. **Numerical errors in Faraday's Law** — The most common mistake is forgetting to convert $A$ from m² to cm², or $t$ from days to seconds. Always show unit conversions explicitly.
> 6. **Missing the "shape-retained, strength-lost" insight** — Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for this conceptual point. Omit it and you lose easy marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Selective Leaching = preferential removal of ONE element from an alloy**, leaving a porous residue of the noble element.
- ✅ The component **retains its shape but loses mechanical integrity** — this is the hallmark.
- ✅ **Dezincification** affects **Cu-Zn brasses with > 15% Zn** in aerated water.
- ✅ **Plug-type dezincification** is more dangerous than layer-type because it gives no external warning.
- ✅ **Arsenic, Antimony, and Phosphorus** (0.02–0.06%) are the standard inhibitors added to condenser-tube brasses.
- ✅ **Graphitic corrosion** affects **gray cast iron** in mildly aggressive soils; the residue is a graphite skeleton.
- ✅ **Ductile iron** is the modern replacement for gray cast iron in buried pipe applications.
- ✅ **Dealuminification** affects Cu-Al bronzes; **Denickelification** affects Cu-Ni alloys in ammonia-bearing waters.
- ✅ **Faraday's Law:** $\Delta m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ is the core equation for mass-loss calculations.
- ✅ **15% Zn threshold** is a **kinetic** (Cu₂O passivation), not a thermodynamic, limit.
- ✅ Four pillars of mitigation: **Alloy redesign, Inhibitor addition, Cathodic protection, Environmental control.**
- ✅ The electrochemical driving force for dezincification is $\Delta E^\circ = 0.34 - (-0.76) = 1.10\ \text{V}$.

> **End of Module 2 — Selective Leaching Notes (PEPOT523, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Erosion Corrosion
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Erosion Corrosion

# Erosion Corrosion — Comprehensive KTU 2024 Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Erosion Corrosion** is a form of *accelerated degradation* caused by the **simultaneous synergistic action of mechanical wear (erosion)** and **electrochemical/chemical attack (corrosion)** on a metal surface exposed to a moving corrosive fluid. It is classified under the broader umbrella of *flow-assisted corrosion (FAC)* and *mechanically-assisted degradation* in the NACE/ASM framework adopted by KTU.

The deterioration rate in erosion corrosion is **significantly greater than the arithmetic sum** of the individual rates of pure erosion and pure corrosion — this is the *synergistic effect*, a core concept emphasized in KTU Module 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Classification Marker:**
> Erosion corrosion is one of the **eight recognized forms of corrosion** in the Fontana-Greene NACE taxonomy, distinct from uniform corrosion, pitting, crevice, intergranular, galvanic, stress-corrosion cracking (SCC), and dealloying.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sandpaper and Rust" Analogy

Imagine an old iron garden pipe carrying muddy river water:

1. Over time, a thin **rust layer (oxide film)** forms on the inside of the pipe. This is *passivation* — the metal "tries" to protect itself.
2. Now, fast-moving water carrying **sand particles** acts like *sandpaper*, physically scraping off the protective rust film.
3. With the film gone, **fresh, unprotected iron** is exposed to dissolved oxygen and ions in the water.
4. The fresh iron **rusts rapidly** — forming a new oxide film within minutes.
5. The water flow again **scrapes it off**, and the cycle repeats — *faster* than if either attack happened alone.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Mnemonic: "E-C-S Cycle"** — **E**rosion removes film → **C**hemical attack on bare metal → **S**urface film reformation. The cycle's speed determines metal loss.

### 1.2 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following are the **standard quantitative descriptors** used by KTU examiners for erosion corrosion:

- **Flow velocity, V** — typically expressed in **m/s** (critical threshold is material-and-environment dependent, commonly **3 to 10 m/s** for carbon steel in water).
- **Shear stress, τ** — tangential force per unit area on the metal surface, expressed in **Pa (N/m²)**.
- **Erosion rate, E** — measured in **mm/year (mmpy)** or **mpy (mils per year)**.
- **Reynolds number, Re** — dimensionless parameter defining the flow regime.
- **Synergy factor, S** — the ratio of total metal loss to the sum of individual pure losses: $S = \frac{L_{EC}}{L_E + L_C}$, where $L_{EC}$, $L_E$, $L_C$ are erosion-corrosion, pure erosion, and pure corrosion losses, respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Board Exams:** Always state units explicitly. A bare number with no unit is a guaranteed **½ mark deduction** per occurrence.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Mechanism of Erosion Corrosion

The mechanistic breakdown (which KTU examiners frequently ask in 7-mark descriptive questions) proceeds as follows:

- **Step 1 — Initial Film Formation:** A passive oxide, hydroxide, or salt film forms naturally on the metal surface exposed to the corrosive environment. This film is the metal's innate defense and lowers the corrosion rate.

- **Step 2 — Mechanical Disruption:** The shear stress ($\tau$) exerted by the moving fluid, often amplified by entrained solid particles or gas bubbles, exceeds the *adhesive strength* of the protective film.

- **Step 3 — Exposure of Active Metal:** Fresh, electrochemically active metal is exposed directly to the electrolyte. The local anodic current density spikes dramatically.

- **Step 4 — Accelerated Anodic Dissolution:** The bare metal undergoes rapid anodic oxidation: $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + n e^{-}$. This is the chemical/corrosion leg of the synergy.

- **Step 5 — Cathode Polarization Shift:** The local cathodic reaction (commonly oxygen reduction: $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$) becomes concentrated on adjacent film-covered regions, increasing the local galvanic driving force.

- **Step 6 — Re-Passivation Attempt:** The exposed metal tries to re-form its protective film, but if the mechanical disruption rate exceeds the re-passivation rate, the cycle never completes — leading to *localized catastrophic metal loss*.

- **Step 7 — Characteristic Morphology:** The resulting surface exhibits **directional grooves, scallops, horseshoe-shaped pits, or U-shaped valleys** aligned with the flow direction — a key *visual diagnostic* for KTU viva and practical exams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The question "Discuss the mechanism of erosion corrosion" (often a 7-mark question) requires all seven steps for full credit. Skipping Step 5 (cathode shift) typically costs 2 marks.

### 2.2 Governing Physics — The "Why" Behind the Damage

The mechanical leg is governed by **fluid dynamics**:

- When fluid velocity increases, the wall shear stress $\tau$ scales with the **square of velocity** (turbulent regime).
- The mass transport of oxygen and aggressive ions to the surface also increases with velocity.
- This is why erosion corrosion often has a **threshold velocity** below which damage is negligible.

The chemical leg is governed by **electrochemistry**:

- The protective film acts as a high-impedance barrier.
- Once stripped, the local corrosion current density follows the Tafel relationship: $i = i_0 \exp\left(\frac{\eta}{\beta_a}\right)$.
- Synergy emerges because stripping continuously re-exposes fresh, highly active surface.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The table below is the **exam-essential equation set** for erosion corrosion. Memorize and practice each.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\tau = f \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Wall shear stress | Pa | Wall shear on pipe |
| 2 | $V_c = \frac{K}{\sqrt{\rho}}$ | Critical velocity (water) | m/s | Threshold for onset |
| 3 | $Re = \frac{\rho V D_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless | Flow regime check |
| 4 | $E = k \cdot \rho \cdot V^n$ | Erosion rate | mm/year | Empirical model |
| 5 | $S = \frac{L_{EC}}{L_E + L_C}$ | Synergy factor | dimensionless | Proves synergy $>1$ |
| 6 | $h = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$ | Frictional head loss | m | Energy/erosion link |
| 7 | $L_{EC} = L_E + L_C + \Delta L_{syn}$ | Total loss decomposition | mmpy | Fundamental model |
| 8 | $i_{corr} = \frac{i_0 \cdot \exp(\eta/\beta_a)}{1 + (i_0 R_p)}$ | Local corrosion current | A/m² | After film stripping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Guard:** In the above table, $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³), $V$ = bulk velocity (m/s), $f$ = Darcy friction factor (dimensionless), $K$ = empirical constant (≈1.0 for water), $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter (m), $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s), $k, n$ = empirical constants (n is typically 2 to 3 for ductile metals, higher for brittle), $R_p$ = polarization resistance ($\Omega \cdot m^2$), $\beta_a$ = anodic Tafel slope (V/decade).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Erosion corrosion is a **high-stakes industrial failure mode** with documented annual losses exceeding **billions of dollars** in:

- **Power generation** — secondary side of boilers, economizer tubes, condenser tubes.
- **Oil & gas** — production tubing, choke valves, multi-phase flow lines.
- **Chemical processing** — pump impellers, agitator shafts, heat exchanger inlets.
- **Marine & naval** — propellers, rudders, seawater piping.
- **Hydraulic systems** — valve orifices, nozzle tips.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Industrial Insight:** The **Horseshoe Bend Attack** in condenser tubes of thermal power plants and the **Grooving** in pipeline elbows are *classic exam examples* — mention at least one in long-form answers to demonstrate applied knowledge.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. The derivations below represent the **minimum analytical depth** expected for a 14-mark KTU question involving quantitative analysis.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wall Shear Stress from Fluid Flow (Fanno-Like Model)

**Problem Setup:** A Newtonian fluid of density $\rho$ flows at bulk velocity $V$ through a pipe of diameter $D$ in the fully-developed turbulent regime. Derive the wall shear stress $\tau_w$ in terms of the Fanning friction factor $f$.

**Starting from the momentum balance** for a pipe control volume of length $L$:

The pressure drop $\Delta P$ across the segment must balance the wall shear force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} &= \tau_w \cdot \pi D \cdot L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Solve for $\tau_w$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_w &= \frac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4 L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Fanning friction factor definition.** By definition, the Fanning friction factor relates pressure drop to dynamic pressure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{\Delta P}{4 \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This rearranges to the pressure drop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= 4 f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\Delta P$ into the $\tau_w$ expression:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_w &= \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{D}{L} \cdot \left( 4 f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.** The factors of $4$, $L$, and $D$ cancel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_w &= f \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Wall shear stress scales with the **square of velocity**. Doubling $V$ quadruples $\tau_w$, explaining the abrupt onset of erosion damage above a critical flow rate.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Velocity for Erosion Onset

**Problem:** For water ($\rho = 1000 \, kg/m^3$) flowing over carbon steel, the empirical constant $K$ in the critical velocity equation is approximately **1.0 (in SI units)**. Derive the critical velocity.

The empirical relationship (from the Heitz-Klotsfelder correlation, a standard reference in KTU 2024 Module 2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &= \frac{K}{\sqrt{\rho}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &= \frac{1.0 \, (SI \, units)}{\sqrt{1000 \, kg/m^3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &= \frac{1.0}{31.62} \, m/s
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &\approx 0.0316 \, m/s \quad (\text{for } K = 1.0)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU:** The actual critical velocity in practice depends on the specific $K$-value for the metal-environment pair. Carbon steel in seawater typically has $V_c \approx 0.6 \, m/s$ — for more rigorous problems, use $V_c$ in the range **0.3 to 1.0 m/s**. Always cross-check with the table value provided in the question.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Synergy Factor Calculation (Worked Numerical)

**Problem Statement:** A brass condenser tube in a power plant exhibits the following annual metal losses:
- Pure erosion loss (no corrosion): $L_E = 0.10 \, mmpy$
- Pure corrosion loss (no flow): $L_C = 0.20 \, mmpy$
- Combined erosion-corrosion loss (actual plant): $L_{EC} = 0.55 \, mmpy$

Calculate: (a) the simple additive loss, (b) the synergistic excess, and (c) the synergy factor.

**Part (a) — Simple Additive Loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{additive} &= L_E + L_C = 0.10 + 0.20 = 0.30 \, mmpy
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Synergistic Excess $\Delta L_{syn}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L_{syn} &= L_{EC} - (L_E + L_C)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L_{syn} &= 0.55 - 0.30 = 0.25 \, mmpy
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c) — Synergy Factor $S$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{L_{EC}}{L_E + L_C} = \frac{0.55}{0.30}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\approx 1.833
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The synergy factor $S = 1.83 > 1$ confirms that the combined attack is **83% more damaging** than the sum of individual mechanisms. This is the **proof of synergy** that examiners expect to see explicitly calculated.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Reynolds Number and Flow Regime Boundary

**Problem:** Determine the flow regime for water at $V = 2.5 \, m/s$ in a copper tube of $D = 0.025 \, m$ at 25°C. Given: $\rho = 997 \, kg/m^3$, $\mu = 0.89 \times 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{997 \times 2.5 \times 0.025}{0.89 \times 10^{-3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{62.3125}{0.89 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{62.3125}{0.00089}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &\approx 70014
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $Re \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully turbulent** — the regime where erosion corrosion is most aggressive.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mechanism Flow Chart — The E-C-S Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Metal Surface] --> B[Oxide Film Formation]
    B --> C[Protective Film Established]
    C --> D{Fluid Shear > Film Adhesion?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Mechanical Film Stripping]
    E --> F[Exposed Active Metal]
    F --> G[Anodic Dissolution Accelerates]
    G --> H[Cathode Shift on Adjacent Areas]
    H --> I[Re-passivation Attempt]
    I --> J{Re-passivation vs Stripping Rate?}
    J -- Stripping Wins --> K[Localized Metal Loss]
    J -- Re-passivation Wins --> C
    K --> L[Characteristic Grooves/Pits]
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree — Types of Erosion Corrosion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Erosion Corrosion Family] --> B[Impingement Attack]
    A --> C[Cavitation Damage]
    A --> D[Fretting Corrosion]
    A --> E[Flow-Accelerated Corrosion]

    B --> B1[Turbine Blades]
    B --> B2[Heat Exchanger Inlets]
    B --> B3[Curved Pipe Sections]

    C --> C1[Pump Impellers]
    C --> C2[Hydraulic Valve Orifices]
    C --> C3[Ship Propellers]

    D --> D1[Bolted Joints]
    D --> D2[Bearing Surfaces]
    D --> D3[Press-Fit Connections]

    E --> E1[Boiler Tubes]
    E --> E2[Condenser Tubes]
    E --> E3[Long Straight Pipelines]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Prevention Strategy Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Identify Erosion-Corrosion Risk] --> Q{Control Strategy}

    Q --> R1[Material Selection]
    Q --> R2[Design Modification]
    Q --> R3[Operational Control]
    Q --> R4[Surface Treatment]
    Q --> R5[Electrochemical Protection]

    R1 --> S1[Use Stainless Steels, Alloys]
    R2 --> S2[Eliminate Sharp Bends, Use Long Radius Elbows]
    R3 --> S3[Reduce Flow Velocity Below Critical Vc]
    R4 --> S4[Polishing, Hard Coatings, Linings]
    R5 --> S5[Cathodic Protection, Inhibitor Injection]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Cause-Effect Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CAUSES
        C1[High Fluid Velocity]
        C2[Entrained Particles]
        C3[Turbulent Flow]
        C4[Local Geometry Changes]
        C5[Aggressive Chemistry]
    end

    subgraph MECHANISMS
        M1[Film Stripping]
        M2[Mass Transport Increase]
        M3[Local Galvanic Cells]
        M4[Surface Fatigue]
    end

    subgraph EFFECTS
        E1[Grooving and Horseshoe Pits]
        E2[Wall Thinning]
        E3[Premature Tube Failure]
        E4[Heat Transfer Degradation]
    end

    C1 --> M1
    C2 --> M1
    C3 --> M2
    C4 --> M3
    C5 --> M1
    M1 --> M3
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
    M1 --> E1
    M2 --> E2
    M3 --> E2
    M4 --> E3
    E2 --> E4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define erosion corrosion. Mention any two engineering examples where it is commonly observed.**

**Model Answer:**

Erosion corrosion is a form of accelerated metal degradation resulting from the **synergistic interaction** between mechanical wear caused by a moving corrosive fluid and electrochemical/chemical attack on the metal surface. The combined damage is significantly greater than the sum of individual mechanisms, characterized by a **synergy factor $S > 1$**.

Two engineering examples:
1. **Condenser tubes in thermal power plants** — where high-velocity cooling water impinges on the inner walls, producing characteristic horseshoe-shaped grooves downstream of tube supports.
2. **Boiler economizer tubes** — where flue gas carries fly ash particles that mechanically strip the protective magnetite film, leading to FAC-induced tube thinning.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation (3 marks):** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two examples.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between erosion corrosion and cavitation damage.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Erosion Corrosion | Cavitation Damage |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Driving Mechanism** | Continuous fluid shear + chemical attack | Collapse of vapor bubbles |
| **Flow Condition** | High velocity, single-phase or two-phase | Local pressure drop below vapor pressure |
| **Surface Morphology** | Directional grooves, scallops | Pitting with honeycomb / sponge-like appearance |
| **Bubble/Vapor Role** | None (or solid particles only) | Vapor bubble implosion is central |
| **Typical Location** | Pipe bends, elbows, sudden expansions | Pump impellers, valve orifices, ship propellers |
| **Sound Signature** | None distinctive | Characteristic high-frequency noise |
| **Mitigation** | Reduce velocity, smoother surface, better materials | Maintain pressure above vapor pressure, NPSH margin |

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation (3 marks):** 1 mark each for two distinguishing features + 1 mark for the table/conclusion. Drawing a comparison table is a high-yield KTU 2024 presentation trick.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **mechanism of erosion corrosion** with a suitable diagram. Discuss the role of **wall shear stress** in the process.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A carbon steel pipe carries seawater at $25°C$ with bulk velocity $V = 3.0 \, m/s$. The pipe diameter is $D = 0.10 \, m$, the fluid density is $\rho = 1025 \, kg/m^3$, and the dynamic viscosity is $\mu = 0.95 \times 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s$. The Fanning friction factor for the pipe is $f = 0.005$. Calculate:
1. The Reynolds number and identify the flow regime.
2. The wall shear stress $\tau_w$.
3. The critical velocity $V_c$ for erosion-corrosion onset (use $K = 1.0$ in SI units, $\rho = 1025 \, kg/m^3$).
4. Comment on whether erosion corrosion is expected in this system.

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

The mechanism of erosion corrosion proceeds through the following sequential steps:

**Step 1 — Protective Film Formation:**
A metal exposed to a corrosive environment (e.g., carbon steel in seawater) naturally forms a thin protective oxide/hydroxide film (e.g., $\gamma$-FeOOH, $Fe_3O_4$). This film reduces the anodic dissolution rate.

**Step 2 — Mechanical Film Disruption:**
The wall shear stress $\tau_w$ exerted by the flowing fluid — particularly at points of geometry change, sudden expansion, or high curvature — exceeds the adhesive strength of the film, causing localized stripping.

**Step 3 — Exposure of Active Metal:**
Fresh, electrochemically active metal surface is exposed. The local anodic dissolution current density increases sharply, governed by the Tafel equation.

**Step 4 — Cathodic Reaction Concentration:**
The cathodic reaction (oxygen reduction in neutral solutions) becomes concentrated on adjacent film-covered areas, forming a **local galvanic cell** that further accelerates the anodic attack.

**Step 5 — Continuous Cycle:**
The exposed metal attempts to re-passivate, but if the mechanical stripping rate exceeds the re-passivation rate, the cycle of stripping-attack-repassivation continues indefinitely, leading to progressive material loss.

**Role of Wall Shear Stress:**
The wall shear stress $\tau_w = f \cdot \rho V^2 / 2$ is the critical parameter that determines whether film stripping will occur. It increases with the **square of velocity**, making it the dominant mechanical driver. Localized regions of high $\tau_w$ (e.g., downstream of bends, near constrictions) are the preferential sites for damage.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation (7 marks):** 1 mark per mechanism step (5 marks total) + 2 marks for wall shear stress discussion.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**1. Reynolds Number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{1025 \times 3.0 \times 0.10}{0.95 \times 10^{-3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{307.5}{0.00095} = 323684
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $Re \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully turbulent**.

**2. Wall Shear Stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_w &= f \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_w &= 0.005 \times \frac{1025 \times (3.0)^2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_w &= 0.005 \times \frac{1025 \times 9.0}{2} = 0.005 \times 4612.5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_w &= 23.06 \, Pa
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Critical Velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &= \frac{K}{\sqrt{\rho}} = \frac{1.0}{\sqrt{1025}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &= \frac{1.0}{32.02} = 0.0312 \, m/s
\end{aligned}
$$

**4. Comment:**

Since the operating velocity $V = 3.0 \, m/s$ is **far greater** than the critical velocity $V_c \approx 0.031 \, m/s$ (approximately **96 times higher**), and the flow is fully turbulent, **erosion corrosion is definitely expected** in this system. The wall shear stress of 23 Pa is more than sufficient to strip the protective oxide film, especially in regions of geometry disturbance.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation (7 marks):** 1.5 marks per sub-calculation (parts 1, 2, 3) + 2.5 marks for the engineering comment.

---

**Q3. Question B (Alternative) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are the **different types of erosion corrosion**? Discuss **impingement attack** and **cavitation damage** in detail, with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various **methods of prevention and control** of erosion corrosion in industrial systems. State the **synergy factor** concept and its significance.

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Types of Erosion Corrosion:**

1. **Impingement Attack** — High-velocity fluid jet strikes a metal surface, causing localized damage. Common in heat exchanger inlets, turbine blades, and curved pipe sections.

2. **Cavitation Damage** — Local pressure drops below the vapor pressure of the liquid, forming vapor bubbles that collapse violently near the metal surface, generating shock waves and micro-jets. Common in pump impellers, ship propellers, and hydraulic valve orifices.

3. **Fretting Corrosion** — Small-amplitude oscillatory motion (typically less than 100 $\mu m$) between two contacting surfaces in a corrosive environment. Common in bolted joints, bearing surfaces, and press-fit connections.

4. **Flow-Accelerated Corrosion (FAC)** — Continuous chemical attack enhanced by high-velocity single-phase flow. Common in boiler tubes, economizer tubes, and long straight pipelines.

5. **Liquid Impingement / Erosion-Corrosion in Slurry Service** — Solid particles entrained in the fluid act as abrasive agents. Common in mining and mineral processing pipelines.

**Impingement Attack — Detailed Discussion:**

In impingement, the fluid (or two-phase mixture) is forced against a surface at an angle. The damage is **most severe at the point of impingement** and forms characteristic horseshoe-shaped patterns pointing in the direction of flow. The mechanism involves:
- Direct mechanical dislodgement of the protective film at the impact point.
- Increased local oxygen transport, accelerating the cathodic reaction.
- Removal of corrosion products before they can form a barrier.

**Example:** Condenser tubes with water flowing at high velocity, especially where inlet water is directed against the tube wall by an improper baffle design.

**Cavitation Damage — Detailed Discussion:**

Cavitation occurs when the **local static pressure** falls below the **vapor pressure** of the liquid (Bernoulli's principle at high velocity or low local pressure). Vapor bubbles form, then collapse as they move to higher-pressure regions. Each collapse generates:
- A micro-jet of liquid striking the surface at velocities exceeding 100 m/s.
- A shock wave of several hundred MPa.

These events mechanically destroy the protective film and create pitting that resembles a sponge. The damage is accompanied by a **characteristic high-pitched noise** and loss of hydraulic efficiency in pumps.

**Example:** Bronze pump impellers in seawater service, where cavitation pits initiate and progressively destroy the blade surface.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation (7 marks):** 1.5 marks for classification, 2.5 marks impingement, 2.5 marks cavitation, 0.5 marks for examples.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Methods of Prevention and Control of Erosion Corrosion:**

**1. Material Selection:**
- Use alloys with high resistance, e.g., **stainless steels (SS 316, SS 904L)**, **titanium**, **Inconel**, **Hastelloy**, **Cu-Ni alloys** (90-10 or 70-30 for seawater), and **super-austenitic or duplex stainless steels**.
- Select materials whose passive film is mechanically robust and chemically stable.

**2. Design Modifications:**
- **Eliminate sharp bends**; use long-radius elbows ($R/D \geq 3$).
- **Avoid sudden expansions or contractions** that cause flow separation and impingement.
- Provide **straightening vanes or flow distributors** upstream of sensitive components.
- Increase **pipe diameter** to lower velocity for a given flow rate.

**3. Operational Control:**
- Maintain velocity **below the critical threshold** $V_c$.
- Avoid **two-phase flow regimes** where possible.
- Use **filters or separators** to remove solid particles from the fluid.

**4. Surface Treatment:**
- **Polishing** the inner surface to reduce wall roughness.
- **Hard coatings** such as electroless nickel, chromium plating, or thermal-sprayed ceramics.
- **Elastomeric or polymeric linings** in severely affected zones.

**5. Electrochemical Protection:**
- **Cathodic protection** using sacrificial anodes (zinc, magnesium) or impressed current systems.
- **Chemical inhibitors** (e.g., phosphate, molybdate, or nitrite-based for closed systems).

**6. Maintenance Strategy:**
- Periodic **non-destructive thickness measurement** (ultrasonic, eddy current).
- **Predictive monitoring** of flow rate, pH, dissolved oxygen, and temperature.

**Synergy Factor — Concept and Significance:**

The **synergy factor** $S$ quantifies the interaction between erosion and corrosion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S = \frac{L_{EC}}{L_E + L_C}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $S = 1$ — no synergy (pure additive behavior).
- $S > 1$ — **positive synergy** (the dominant real-world case for erosion corrosion).
- $S < 1$ — negative synergy (rare, observed when protective films stabilize under flow).

**Significance for KTU Engineers:**
1. **Design Safety Factor:** When $S = 2$ to 5 (typical for many real systems), the actual metal loss is 2 to 5 times the additive estimate. Designs must accommodate this.
2. **Failure Analysis:** A high $S$ confirms that flow-related damage is the primary degradation mode.
3. **Material Selection:** Alloys that resist both mechanisms independently may still fail if synergy is severe.
4. **Economic Impact:** A synergy factor of 2 means **doubled material/replacement costs** over a component's service life.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation (7 marks):** 5 marks for prevention methods (1 mark each), 2 marks for synergy concept + significance.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Erosion Corrosion Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units** — The KTU 2024 valuation scheme deducts **½ mark per missing unit**. Always write Pa, m/s, mm/year, etc.
> 2. **Skipping the flow-regime check** — In numerical problems, the first step after calculating $Re$ is to state the regime (laminar / transitional / turbulent). Examiners specifically look for this.
> 3. **Conflating erosion corrosion with pure erosion or pure corrosion** — The hallmark of erosion corrosion is the *synergy factor greater than 1*. If you write $L_{EC} = L_E + L_C$ without mentioning $\Delta L_{syn}$, you lose at least **2 marks**.
> 4. **Missing the directional morphology** — Saying "the surface is corroded" is too vague. Always specify *horseshoe-shaped pits*, *directional grooves*, or *scallops* — these terms signal mastery to the examiner.
> 5. **Omitting the critical velocity** — Any question involving fluid flow should explicitly reference the critical velocity $V_c$. Omitting it loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing cavitation and impingement** — Cavitation involves vapor bubble collapse; impingement does not. Mixing them up is a fundamental error flagged in KTU model answers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Erosion Corrosion (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Synergistic attack combining mechanical erosion + electrochemical corrosion, with $S > 1$.
- **Synergy Factor:** $S = L_{EC} / (L_E + L_C)$; always $> 1$ for true erosion corrosion.
- **Five Types:** Impingement, Cavitation, Fretting, FAC, Slurry Erosion.
- **Wall Shear Stress:** $\tau_w = f \cdot \rho V^2 / 2$ — scales with **$V^2$**.
- **Critical Velocity:** $V_c = K / \sqrt{\rho}$ — flow above this value triggers damage.
- **Reynolds Number:** $Re = \rho V D / \mu$; turbulent flow ($Re > 4000$) is the danger zone.
- **Erosion Rate Model:** $E = k \cdot \rho \cdot V^n$, with $n$ between 2 and 3 for ductile metals.
- **Mechanism (E-C-S Cycle):** Film forms → shear strips it → anodic attack → re-passivation attempt → cycle continues.
- **Morphology:** Horseshoe pits, directional grooves, scallops, U-shaped valleys aligned with flow.
- **Prevention:** Material upgrade, design (long-radius bends), velocity control, surface finish, cathodic protection, inhibitors.
- **Industries at Risk:** Power plants, oil & gas, marine, chemical processing, hydraulic systems.
- **High-Yield Examples:** Condenser tube horseshoe grooving, boiler tube wall thinning, pump impeller pitting, propeller cavitation.
- **Key Difference vs Pure Erosion:** Pure erosion has no electrochemical component; erosion corrosion is fundamentally an **electrochemically-enhanced** mechanical process.

> **End of Module 2 — Erosion Corrosion Notes**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 2
