# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEPOT523]**

> **Q:** Differentiate between non-electrochemical and electrochemical methods of corrosion monitoring. Give **two examples** of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Non-Electrochemical Methods | Electrochemical Methods |
|--------|----------------------------|-------------------------|
| **Driving Force** | No external electrical signal | Uses external polarization |
| **Quantity Measured** | Mass loss, thickness, or ion concentration | Current or potential |
| **Time Resolution** | Hours to days (slow) | Minutes to seconds (fast) |
| **In-situ Capability** | Limited | Yes (real-time) |
| **Examples** | (1) Weight loss coupon test, (2) Ultrasonic thickness measurement | (1) Tafel polarization, (2) Linear polarization resistance (LPR) |
| **Equation Used** | $CR = \dfrac{8.76 \times 10^4 \times W}{D \times A \times t}$ | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 R_p(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ |

**Examples of Non-Electrochemical Methods:**
1. **Weight Loss (Coupon) Test** — Metal coupons are exposed to the environment, removed periodically, cleaned, and weighed. Corrosion rate is calculated from mass loss.
2. **Hydrogen Permeation Test** — Measures atomic hydrogen flux through a metal membrane.

**Examples of Electrochemical Methods:**
1. **Potentiodynamic Polarization** — Sweeps potential and records current; Tafel slopes are obtained.
2. **Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)** — Applies a small AC perturbation and measures impedance.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEPOT523]**

> **Q:** Define a **potentiostat**. State the function of the three electrodes used in a potentiostat-based corrosion experiment.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A potentiostat is an electronic feedback-controlled instrument that maintains the potential of a working electrode (WE) at a constant, operator-defined value with respect to a reference electrode (RE), by automatically adjusting the current flowing between the working electrode and a counter electrode (CE) through the electrolyte.

**Functions of the Three Electrodes:**

1. **Working Electrode (WE):** The metal sample whose corrosion behaviour is being studied. Current flows through this electrode.
2. **Reference Electrode (RE):** Provides a stable, known, fixed potential against which the WE potential is measured. Examples: Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE, $E = +0.244$ V vs SHE), Ag/AgCl, SHE. **No current** is allowed to flow through this electrode.
3. **Counter (Auxiliary) Electrode (CE):** Completes the electrical circuit by carrying the current. Made of inert material (Pt, graphite, stainless steel) to avoid contamination.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEPOT523 Module 3]**

> **Q (a)** With a neat **block diagram**, explain the working of a **potentiostat**. Describe the role of the **negative feedback loop** in maintaining a constant potential. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** Derive the **Butler–Volmer equation** starting from the anodic and cathodic current expressions. Show how the **Stern–Geary equation** is obtained in the small-overpotential limit. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (Already Shown in Section 4.1, refer to Mermaid diagram):**

1. **Differential Amplifier (1 Mark):** Compares the set-point potential $E_{set}$ with the measured potential $E_{we} - E_{re}$ across the WE and RE. The amplifier has a very high input impedance ($10^{12}\ \Omega$ or more) so that negligible current flows through the reference electrode, preserving its stable potential.

2. **Error Computation (1 Mark):** The output of the differential amplifier is an error voltage:
   $\Delta E = E_{set} - (E_{we} - E_{re})$
   If $E_{we}$ deviates from $E_{set}$, a non-zero $\Delta E$ is produced.

3. **Power Amplifier (1 Mark):** Amplifies the error signal and drives current through the counter electrode. This is the "muscle" of the potentiostat.

4. **Current and Voltage Measurement (1 Mark):** A current follower (with feedback resistor) measures the current through WE–CE. A high-impedance voltmeter measures $E_{we} - E_{re}$.

5. **Negative Feedback Loop (2 Marks):**
   - If the corrosion tendency tries to **lower** $E_{we}$, the differential amplifier senses a positive error and the power amplifier pushes more **cathodic current** through CE, raising $E_{we}$ back to $E_{set}$.
   - If $E_{we}$ drifts **higher**, current is reduced or reversed.
   - The feedback is **negative** because any deviation produces a corrective action in the opposite direction, ensuring stability and accuracy within microvolts.

6. **Data Acquisition and Plotting (1 Mark):** The computer interface plots $E$ vs $i$ (or $\log i$), enabling Tafel, LPR, and CV analyses.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(Full derivation already provided in Section 3.1 — summarize the key steps):**

1. **Anodic current expression:** $i_a = i_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right)$ **(1 Mark)**
2. **Cathodic current expression:** $i_c = i_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right)$ **(1 Mark)**
3. **Net current by KCL:** $i_{net} = i_a - i_c$ **(1 Mark)**
4. **Butler–Volmer equation stated:** $i_{net} = i_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$ **(1 Mark)**
5. **Small-$\eta$ linearization using Taylor series** to obtain $i_{net} = \dfrac{i_0 F \eta}{RT}$ **(1 Mark)**
6. **Polarization resistance:** $R_p = \dfrac{RT}{F i_0}$ **(1 Mark)**
7. **Extension to Stern–Geary with both branches:** $i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ **(1 Mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEPOT523 Module 3]**

> **Q (a)** Explain the **Tafel extrapolation method** for determining the corrosion current density $i_{corr}$. Sketch a typical **E vs log i** plot and label the anodic, cathodic, and linear Tafel regions. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** A polarization experiment on mild steel in 0.5 M H$_2$SO$_4$ gives Tafel slopes $\beta_a = 0.12$ V/decade and $\beta_c = 0.15$ V/decade. The polarization resistance measured by LPR is $R_p = 250\ \Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$. Calculate the **corrosion current density** and **corrosion rate in mm/yr** (atomic mass of Fe = 55.85 g/mol, density = 7.87 g/cm³, $n = 2$). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

1. **Principle (1 Mark):** Tafel extrapolation uses the Butler–Volmer equation in the **high-overpotential** region where one of the exponential terms dominates and a straight-line (semi-log) behaviour emerges.
2. **Procedure (2 Marks):**
   - Stabilize the WE at OCP for ~1 hour to reach $E_{corr}$.
   - Sweep potential from $E_{corr} - 250$ mV to $E_{corr} + 250$ mV at a constant scan rate (typically 0.5 mV/s or 1 mV/s).
   - Record current $i$ as a function of $E$.
3. **Plot Description (2 Marks):**
   - Y-axis: $\log_{10} \vert i \vert$ (A/cm²)
   - X-axis: $E$ (V vs RE)
   - The plot shows a curved region near $E_{corr}$ and **two linear branches** (anodic with positive slope, cathodic with negative slope) at higher $|\eta|$.
4. **Extrapolation (1 Mark):** Extend the linear anodic and cathodic branches back to $E = E_{corr}$. Their intersection gives $\log_{10}(i_{corr})$.
5. **Information Obtained (1 Mark):** Corrosion current density $i_{corr}$, Tafel slopes $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$, and an insight into the corrosion mechanism.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $\beta_a = 0.12$ V/decade
- $\beta_c = 0.15$ V/decade
- $R_p = 250\ \Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$
- $M_{Fe} = 55.85$ g/mol
- $\rho_{Fe} = 7.87$ g/cm³
- $n = 2$

**Step 1: Compute the Stern–Geary constant $B$ (2 Marks):**

$$ B = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{0.12 \times 0.15}{2.303 \times (0.12 + 0.15)} $$

$$ B = \frac{0.018}{2.303 \times 0.27} = \frac{0.018}{0.62181} = 0.02895\ \text{V} \approx 28.95\ \text{mV} $$

**Step 2: Compute $i_{corr}$ (2 Marks):**

$$ i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{0.02895\ \text{V}}{250\ \Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2} $$

$$ i_{corr} = 1.158 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{A/cm}^2 = 1.158 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{A/cm}^2 $$

**Step 3: Convert to Corrosion Rate in mm/yr (3 Marks):**

The corrosion rate formula derived from Faraday's law:

$$ CR\ (\text{mm/yr}) = \frac{0.00327 \times i_{corr} \times M}{n \times \rho} $$

where the constant 0.00327 accounts for unit conversion (A/cm² → mm/yr).

$$ CR = \frac{0.00327 \times (1.158 \times 10^{-4}) \times 55.85}{2 \times 7.87} $$

$$ CR = \frac{0.00327 \times 1.158 \times 10^{-4} \times 55.85}{15.74} $$

$$ CR = \frac{2.114 \times 10^{-5}}{15.74} $$

$$ CR \approx 1.343 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{mm/yr} \approx 0.00134\ \text{mm/yr} $$

**Final Answer:** $i_{corr} = 1.158 \times 10^{-4}$ A/cm² and $CR \approx 1.34 \times 10^{-3}$ mm/yr.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Forgetting the **2.303** factor in the Stern–Geary denominator (loss of 2 marks).
> - Mixing up **A/cm²** with **A/m²** (1 A/m² = 10⁻⁴ A/cm²) — a unit conversion error worth 1 mark.
> - Using $i_{corr} = B/R_p$ with $B$ in **Volts** (correct) but $R_p$ in **ohms** (correct, since V/Ω = A). If you express $R_p$ in $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$, the result is in A/cm².
> - Skipping the 0.00327 constant justification in the mm/yr formula (loss of 1 mark).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PEPOT523 — Module 3**

- **Two classes of methods:** Non-electrochemical (weight loss, thickness) and Electrochemical (Tafel, LPR, EIS). Potentiostats enable the latter.
- **Potentiostat definition:** An electronic instrument using **negative feedback** to hold the WE potential constant vs RE, with current flowing through CE.
- **Three electrodes:** WE (the sample), RE (stable potential, SCE / Ag-AgCl / SHE), CE (inert, Pt / graphite).
- **No current flows through RE** — its high-impedance measurement preserves its reference potential.
- **Butler–Volmer equation** is the master kinetic equation; it reduces to Tafel at high $|\eta|$ and to the linear form at low $|\eta|$.
- **Tafel slopes** at 298 K with $\alpha = 0.5$ yield ~120 mV/decade (anodic) and ~120 mV/decade (cathodic) — verify with the formula $\beta = 2.303RT/(\alpha F)$.
- **Stern–Geary equation** $i_{corr} = B/R_p$ links polarization resistance to corrosion current; $B$ is typically 13 to 52 mV.
- **Tafel extrapolation method** gives the most accurate $i_{corr}$, $\beta_a$, and $\beta_c$ from a single potentiodynamic sweep of $\pm 250$ mV around $E_{corr}$.
- **LPR method** uses a small perturbation ($\pm 10$ mV) and is suitable for online, real-time corrosion rate monitoring.
- **Key constants:** $F = 96,485$ C/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), and $2.303RT/F = 0.05916$ V at 298 K.
- **Faraday's Law** converts $i_{corr}$ to mass loss, then to corrosion rate in mm/yr or mpy.
- **Examiner tip:** Always state the **units** explicitly in numerical answers and show the **substitution step** clearly when applying Stern–Geary and Faraday's Law.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tafel Extrapolation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tafel Extrapolation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Tafel Extrapolation** is an electrochemical experimental technique used to determine the **corrosion current density ($i_{corr}$)** and the **corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$)** of a corroding metal–electrolyte system by extrapolating the linear (Tafel) regions of the anodic and cathodic branches of a semi-logarithmic polarization curve (E vs. $\log i$) back to their point of intersection, which corresponds to the mixed corrosion potential.

It is rooted in the **Tafel equation**, first proposed by Julius Tafel (1905), which describes the relationship between the **overpotential ($\eta$)** and the **current density ($i$)** in an electrochemical reaction far from equilibrium:

$$\eta = a \pm b \log(i)$$

where:
- $\eta = E - E_{eq}$ = overpotential (V)
- $a$ = Tafel intercept constant (V)
- $b$ = **Tafel slope** (V/decade)
- $i$ = current density (A/cm² or A/m²)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Tafel Extrapolation is a fundamental tool in **polarization methods** of corrosion rate measurement. Students are expected to derive the Tafel equation, sketch the Tafel plot, and demonstrate the extrapolation method to evaluate $i_{corr}$, $\beta_a$, and $\beta_c$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to measure the **flow rate of a hidden leak** in a pipe buried behind a thick wall. You cannot see the leak directly, but you can hear two distinct hissing sounds — one from the water rushing **out** (anodic dissolution) and one from the air rushing **in** (cathodic reduction). If you measure the loudness (current) at various distances from the wall (overpotential), you will find that at very large distances, the loudness **increases linearly with the logarithm** of the distance — a predictable, "tidy" linear relationship.

Now, **extrapolate** both hiss-curves backwards toward the wall. The point where they would have intersected tells you the exact leak rate ($i_{corr}$) and the position of the leak ($E_{corr}$). You never had to break the wall open.

> **In electrochemistry**, the "wall" is the corroding metal surface, the "hiss" is the current density, and the "logarithmic tidiness" at high overpotentials is the **Tafel behavior**. The extrapolation gives the true corrosion rate without needing weight-loss measurements that take days or weeks.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Value (Mild Steel in Acid) |
|----------|--------|---------------|-----------------------------------|
| Anodic Tafel slope | $\beta_a$ | V/decade | **0.06 – 0.12 V/dec** |
| Cathodic Tafel slope | $\beta_c$ | V/decade | **0.10 – 0.20 V/dec** |
| Corrosion current density | $i_{corr}$ | $\mu A/cm^2$ | **10 – 1000 $\mu A/cm^2$** |
| Corrosion potential | $E_{corr}$ | V (vs. SCE) | **–0.4 to –0.7 V** |
| Exchange current density | $i_0$ | A/cm² | $10^{-12}$ – $10^{-3}$ A/cm² |

> [!NOTE]
> The Tafel slope ($b$) is **NOT** the natural logarithm; it is the slope in the semi-log plot of E vs. $\log_{10} i$. Sometimes it is also called the **"$\beta$" slope** in corrosion literature. Always clarify the base (decade = base 10).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundation: The Butler–Volmer Equation

The starting point of the Tafel analysis is the **Butler–Volmer equation**, which describes the net current density of a single electrochemical reaction under activation control:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left( \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT} \right) - \exp\left( -\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT} \right) \right]$$

where:
- $i_0$ = **exchange current density** (A/cm²)
- $\alpha_a, \alpha_c$ = **anodic and cathodic transfer coefficients** (dimensionless, sum ≈ 1)
- $F$ = **Faraday's constant = 96,485 C/mol**
- $R$ = **Universal gas constant = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $\eta$ = overpotential (V)
- $\alpha_a n$ and $\alpha_c n$ are often lumped together as $n$ in simplified forms

---

## 2.2 Reduction to the Tafel Equation (High Overpotential Approximation)

The Butler–Volmer equation contains **two** exponential terms — one anodic (positive) and one cathodic (negative). At **large overpotentials** $\left( \vert \eta \vert \gg \frac{RT}{F} \right)$:

- For **large anodic** $\eta$ ($\eta \gg 0$): the anodic exponential dominates, the cathodic exponential becomes negligible.
- For **large cathodic** $\eta$ ($\eta \ll 0$): the cathodic exponential dominates, the anodic term vanishes.

This **decoupling** yields the linear (in log scale) Tafel behavior.

---

## 2.3 Derivation Logic — Step by Step

### Anodic Branch (Large Positive $\eta$)

$$i_a \approx i_0 \exp\left( \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT} \right)$$

Taking the base-10 logarithm:

$$\log(i_a) = \log(i_0) + \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{2.303 \, RT}$$

Solving for $\eta$:

$$\eta_a = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F} \log(i_a) - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F} \log(i_0)$$

Defining the **anodic Tafel slope** $\beta_a$:

$$\beta_a = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F} \quad \text{(V/decade)}$$

And the intercept:

$$a_a = -\beta_a \log(i_0)$$

We obtain the **anodic Tafel equation**:

$$\eta_a = a_a + \beta_a \log(i_a) \quad \text{(Anodic branch)}$$

### Cathodic Branch (Large Negative $\eta$)

Following identical algebra:

$$\beta_c = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_c F} \quad \text{(V/decade)}$$

$$\eta_c = a_c - \beta_c \log(i_c) \quad \text{(Cathodic branch)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention (KTU convention):** In most corrosion textbooks, $\eta$ is defined as $E_{applied} - E_{corr}$, and the cathodic branch uses a **minus sign** before $\beta_c \log i$ because the slope of E vs. $\log i$ is **negative** for cathodic reactions on a standard semi-log plot.

---

## 2.4 The Polarization Resistance Relationship (Stern–Geary)

When $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$ are known, the **Stern–Geary equation** allows calculation of $i_{corr}$ from the **linear polarization resistance** $R_p$ (slope of E vs. i near $E_{corr}$):

$$i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \, \beta_c}{2.303 \, R_p \, (\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{B}{R_p}$$

where:

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \, \beta_c}{2.303 \, (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

$B$ is called the **Stern–Geary constant**, typically **0.020 – 0.060 V**.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Use / Meaning** |
|-------------|----------------|-------------------|
| Tafel equation (general) | $\eta = a \pm b \log(i)$ | Defines linear semi-log E–i relationship |
| Anodic Tafel slope | $\beta_a = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F}$ | Slope of anodic linear region (V/dec) |
| Cathodic Tafel slope | $\beta_c = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_c F}$ | Slope of cathodic linear region (V/dec) |
| Anodic overpotential | $\eta_a = a_a + \beta_a \log(i_a)$ | Used for anodic extrapolation |
| Cathodic overpotential | $\eta_c = a_c - \beta_c \log(i_c)$ | Used for cathodic extrapolation |
| Exchange current density | $i_0 = i_{corr} \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{F(E_{corr} - E_{eq})}{RT}\right)$ | Equilibrium kinetics reference |
| Stern–Geary equation | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | Linear polarization method |
| Corrosion rate (from $i_{corr}$) | $CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ (mm/yr) | Faraday's law conversion |
| Equivalent weight | $EW = \dfrac{M}{n}$ (M = atomic wt, n = electron transfer) | For Faraday conversion |
| B-value | $B = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | Stern–Geary constant (~0.026 V) |
| Activation energy (Tafel) | $\log i_0 = \text{const} - \dfrac{E_a}{2.303 RT}$ | Temperature effect on $i_0$ |

> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When you use absolute values in the formula sheet, always write $\vert \eta \vert$ as `\vert \eta \vert` in LaTeX — never use a single pipe `|`, as it breaks markdown tables.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Pipeline integrity management:** Tafel extrapolation of polarization data from coupon tests gives rapid $i_{corr}$ estimates for oil & gas pipelines in $\mathrm{H_2S}$/$\mathrm{CO_2}$ service.
2. **Coating evaluation:** Painted/coated samples are scratched, and the exposed metal's Tafel parameters are compared to bare metal to rank inhibitor performance.
3. **Alloy selection:** Automotive and marine industries use Tafel analysis to compare corrosion resistance of candidate alloys in chloride environments.
4. **Pharmaceutical & biomedical implants:** Ti and Co–Cr alloy corrosion in simulated body fluids is quantified by Tafel extrapolation.
5. **Failure analysis:** Post-failure forensic studies often use Tafel slopes and $i_{corr}$ to deduce whether failure was due to uniform corrosion, pitting, or sensitization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Butler–Volmer to Tafel

### Given
A single electrochemical reaction under **activation control** with Butler–Volmer kinetics:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left( \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT} \right) - \exp\left( -\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT} \right) \right]$$

### Goal
Derive the **anodic Tafel equation** and the **anodic Tafel slope** rigorously.

---

### **STEP 1 — Apply the High-Overpotential Condition**

For the **anodic branch**, take $\eta \gg 0$ and large. The anodic exponential grows much larger than the cathodic exponential, which decays to near zero:

$$\exp\left( \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT} \right) \gg \exp\left( -\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT} \right)$$

Therefore the net current is approximated as:

$$i \approx i_0 \exp\left( \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT} \right)$$

> **Conversion logic:** At high anodic overpotentials, the **reduction** (cathodic) reaction is suppressed, leaving only **oxidation** (anodic) current flowing outward.

---

### **STEP 2 — Isolate the Overpotential**

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln(i) = \ln(i_0) + \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}$$

Solve for $\eta$:

$$\eta = \frac{RT}{\alpha_a F} \left[ \ln(i) - \ln(i_0) \right] = \frac{RT}{\alpha_a F} \ln\left( \frac{i}{i_0} \right)$$

> **Conversion logic:** We have inverted the exponential to a logarithm — a standard algebraic step.

---

### **STEP 3 — Convert to Base-10 Logarithm (Engineering Convention)**

Use the identity $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$\eta = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F} \log\left( \frac{i}{i_0} \right)$$

Expand the log of a ratio:

$$\eta = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F} \left[ \log(i) - \log(i_0) \right]$$

---

### **STEP 4 — Identify the Tafel Slope and Intercept**

Define:

$$\beta_a = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F} \quad \text{(V/decade)}$$

$$a_a = -\beta_a \log(i_0) \quad \text{(V)}$$

Then:

$$\eta_a = a_a + \beta_a \log(i)$$

This is the **anodic Tafel equation**. $\blacksquare$

---

### **STEP 5 — Cathodic Branch (Analogous Derivation)**

For $\eta \ll 0$ and large in magnitude, the anodic exponential vanishes:

$$i \approx -i_0 \exp\left( -\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT} \right)$$

Let $i_c = -i$ (cathodic current is negative in sign convention):

$$i_c = i_0 \exp\left( -\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT} \right)$$

Take $\log_{10}$:

$$\log(i_c) = \log(i_0) - \frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{2.303 \, RT}$$

Solve:

$$\eta = -\frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_c F} \log\left( \frac{i_c}{i_0} \right)$$

Rearrange to obtain:

$$\eta_c = a_c - \beta_c \log(i_c)$$

with $\beta_c = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_c F}$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Finding $i_{corr}$ and $E_{corr}$

### Problem Statement
A polarization experiment on mild steel in 1 M $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$ at 25 °C yields the following anodic and cathodic Tafel lines:

- **Anodic line:** $E \, (\text{V vs. SCE}) = -0.520 + 0.090 \log(i)$  
  (where $i$ is in $\mu A/cm^2$)
- **Cathodic line:** $E \, (\text{V vs. SCE}) = -0.640 - 0.120 \log(i)$

Determine:
1. The corrosion potential $E_{corr}$
2. The corrosion current density $i_{corr}$
3. The Tafel slopes $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$
4. The polarization resistance $R_p$ (using Stern–Geary)

---

### **SOLUTION — Part (a): $E_{corr}$ and $i_{corr}$ by Extrapolation**

At the corrosion point, both lines intersect, so $E_{anodic} = E_{cathodic} = E_{corr}$:

$$-0.520 + 0.090 \log(i_{corr}) = -0.640 - 0.120 \log(i_{corr})$$

Group the log terms:

$$0.090 \log(i_{corr}) + 0.120 \log(i_{corr}) = -0.640 + 0.520$$

$$0.210 \log(i_{corr}) = -0.120$$

$$\log(i_{corr}) = \frac{-0.120}{0.210} = -0.5714$$

$$i_{corr} = 10^{-0.5714} = 0.2685 \, \mu A/cm^2$$

**Compute $E_{corr}$** by substituting back into the anodic line:

$$E_{corr} = -0.520 + 0.090 \times (-0.5714) = -0.520 - 0.0514 = -0.5714 \, \text{V (vs. SCE)}$$

**Verification** using the cathodic line:

$$E_{corr} = -0.640 - 0.120 \times (-0.5714) = -0.640 + 0.0686 = -0.5714 \, \text{V (vs. SCE)} \;\checkmark$$

> **Final result:** $i_{corr} \approx 0.27 \, \mu A/cm^2$, $E_{corr} \approx -0.571 \, V$ (vs. SCE).

---

### **SOLUTION — Part (b): Tafel Slopes and Polarization Resistance**

From the line equations:
- $\beta_a = +0.090$ V/dec (anodic slope)
- $\beta_c = -0.120$ V/dec (cathodic slope, magnitude 0.120 V/dec)

**Stern–Geary constant** $B$:

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \vert \beta_c \vert}{2.303 (\beta_a + \vert \beta_c \vert)} = \frac{0.090 \times 0.120}{2.303 \times (0.090 + 0.120)}$$

$$B = \frac{0.01080}{2.303 \times 0.210} = \frac{0.01080}{0.4836} = 0.02233 \, \text{V}$$

**Polarization resistance** $R_p$:

$$R_p = \frac{B}{i_{corr}} = \frac{0.02233 \, V}{0.2685 \times 10^{-6} \, A/cm^2} = 83,168 \, \Omega \cdot cm^2 \approx 83.2 \, k\Omega \cdot cm^2$$

**Corrosion rate in mm/yr** (for Fe, $EW = 27.92$, $\rho = 7.87 \, g/cm^3$):

$$CR = \frac{0.00327 \times i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \times EW}{\rho}$$

$$CR = \frac{0.00327 \times 0.2685 \times 27.92}{7.87} = \frac{0.02453}{7.87} = 3.12 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{mm/yr}$$

$$CR \approx 3.12 \, \mu m/yr \quad \text{(very low — consistent with a well-passivated system)}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Tafel Extrapolation Procedure

| **Step** | **Action** | **Expected Output** |
|----------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Mount sample as working electrode in 3-electrode cell | Reference (SCE), counter (Pt), working (test metal) |
| 2 | Immerse in electrolyte; wait for stable $E_{corr}$ (OCP) | Stable potential within ±2 mV for 10 min |
| 3 | Apply potentiostatic scan from $E_{corr} - 250$ mV to $E_{corr} + 250$ mV | E vs. i curve |
| 4 | Convert current to $\log \vert i \vert$ and re-plot E vs. $\log i$ | Semi-log Tafel plot |
| 5 | Identify linear regions (typically $\vert \eta \vert \ge 50$ mV) | Two straight-line segments |
| 6 | Fit straight lines to anodic and cathodic regions | Get $\beta_a, \beta_c$, intercepts $a_a, a_c$ |
| 7 | Extrapolate both lines back to their intersection | $E_{corr}$ and $i_{corr}$ |
| 8 | Apply Stern–Geary to compute $R_p$ if low-overpotential data available | Polarization resistance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Tafel Extrapolation Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Prepare 3-Electrode Cell] --> B0[Immerse Working Electrode in Electrolyte]
    B0 --> C0[Wait for Stable OCP / Ecorr]
    C0 --> D0[Apply Potentiodynamic Scan]
    D0 --> E0[Record E vs i Data]
    E0 --> F0[Convert to E vs log i Plot]
    F0 --> G0{Identify Linear Tafel Regions}
    G0 -->|Anodic Slope| H0[Fit Anodic Line: Ea = aa + ba log i]
    G0 -->|Cathodic Slope| I0[Fit Cathodic Line: Ec = ac - bc log i]
    H0 --> J0[Extrapolate Anodic Line Backward]
    I0 --> K0[Extrapolate Cathodic Line Backward]
    J0 --> L0[Intersection Point]
    K0 --> L0
    L0 --> M0[Read Ecorr from Y-axis]
    L0 --> N0[Read icorr from X-axis]
    N0 --> O0[Compute ba and bc]
    O0 --> P0[Apply Stern-Geary: icorr = B divided by Rp]
    P0 --> Q0[Convert icorr to Corrosion Rate via Faraday Law]
    Q0 --> R0[Report Final Result]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: E vs. log i Tafel Plot (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[E axis: Potential V vs SCE] --> B1[Cathodic Branch]
    A1 --> C1[Anodic Branch]
    B1 --> D1[Linear Region with slope minus bc]
    C1 --> E1[Linear Region with slope plus ba]
    D1 --> F1[Extrapolation Lines Crossing at Point P]
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Point P gives Ecorr and icorr]
    G1 --> H1[Below eta = 50 mV: Non-linear Activation Region]
    H1 --> I1[Above eta = 120 mV: Valid Tafel Region]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Mapping of Corrosion Rate Determination Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Measured Tafel Data] --> B2[Determine ba from Anodic Slope]
    A2 --> C2[Determine bc from Cathodic Slope]
    B2 --> D2[Compute Stern-Geary Constant B]
    C2 --> D2
    D2 --> E2[Measure Rp from Linear Polarization Near Ecorr]
    E2 --> F2[icorr equals B divided by Rp]
    F2 --> G2[Apply Faraday Law]
    G2 --> H2[Equivalent Weight equals Atomic Weight divided by n]
    H2 --> I2[CR equals 0.00327 times icorr times EW divided by density]
    I2 --> J2[Final Corrosion Rate in mm per year]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Sources of Error in Tafel Extrapolation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A3[Error Sources in Tafel Method] --> B3[Concentration Polarization]
    A3 --> C3[IR Drop in Solution]
    A3 --> D3[Passivation or Pitting]
    A3 --> E3[Non-Uniform Current Distribution]
    A3 --> F3[Scan Rate Too High]
    B3 --> G3[Inaccurate Linear Regions]
    C3 --> G3
    D3 --> G3
    E3 --> G3
    F3 --> G3
    G3 --> H3[Erroneous icorr and Ecorr]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** Define the Tafel equation. What do the constants $a$ and $b$ represent?

**Model Answer:**

The Tafel equation expresses the linear (semi-logarithmic) relationship between the overpotential ($\eta$) and the current density ($i$) for an electrochemical reaction at high overpotentials:

$$\eta = a \pm b \log(i)$$

- $a$ = **Tafel intercept** (V) — depends on the **exchange current density** $i_0$; mathematically $a = -b \log(i_0)$.
- $b$ = **Tafel slope** (V/decade) — depends on temperature and the **charge transfer coefficient** $\alpha$; for the anodic branch, $b_a = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F}$.

> The "+" sign is used for the anodic branch (oxidation), and the "−" sign is used for the cathodic branch (reduction).

> **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

---

### **Q2.** Sketch a typical Tafel plot (E vs. $\log i$) for a corroding metal and label the key regions.

**Model Answer:**

A Tafel plot has the following features (sketch and labels):

- **Y-axis:** Potential $E$ (V vs. reference electrode).
- **X-axis:** $\log \vert i \vert$ (current density, log scale).
- **Cathodic branch** (left): nearly linear with **negative slope** of magnitude $\beta_c$, located below $E_{corr}$.
- **Anodic branch** (right): nearly linear with **positive slope** $\beta_a$, located above $E_{corr}$.
- **Curved region near $E_{corr}$** ($\vert \eta \vert < 50$ mV): non-linear, used for **polarization resistance** $R_p$ measurement.
- **Linear Tafel regions** ($\vert \eta \vert \ge 50$–$100$ mV): used for extrapolation.
- **Intersection point** of the two extrapolated lines gives $(E_{corr}, \, i_{corr})$.

> **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Modified for PEPOT523]`

**(a)** Starting from the **Butler–Volmer equation**, derive the **anodic and cathodic Tafel equations**. Clearly state the assumptions and define all symbols. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The Tafel parameters for mild steel in de-aerated 1 M HCl at 25 °C are:
- Anodic line: $E \, (\text{V vs. SCE}) = -0.480 + 0.075 \log(i)$  
- Cathodic line: $E \, (\text{V vs. SCE}) = -0.620 - 0.110 \log(i)$  
  where $i$ is in $\mu A/cm^2$.

Determine:
1. Corrosion potential $E_{corr}$ (V vs. SCE)  
2. Corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ ($\mu A/cm^2$)  
3. Polarization resistance $R_p$ ($\Omega \cdot cm^2$) using Stern–Geary  
4. Corrosion rate in mm/yr (take $EW = 27.92$, $\rho = 7.87 \, g/cm^3$) **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)**

**Step 1 — State the Butler–Volmer equation:**

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left( \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT} \right) - \exp\left( -\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT} \right) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the high-overpotential assumption:**

For anodic $\eta \gg 0$: the cathodic exponential is negligible, so

$$i \approx i_0 \exp\left( \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT} \right)$$

For cathodic $\eta \ll 0$: the anodic exponential is negligible, so

$$i \approx -i_0 \exp\left( -\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Take $\log_{10}$ of both sides (anodic):**

$$\log(i) = \log(i_0) + \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{2.303 \, RT}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\eta$:**

$$\eta = -\frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F} \log(i_0) + \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F} \log(i)$$

**Step 5 — Define Tafel constants:**

$$\beta_a = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F}, \quad a_a = -\beta_a \log(i_0)$$

**Final anodic Tafel equation:**

$$\eta_a = a_a + \beta_a \log(i) \quad \text{(Anodic branch)}$$

**Cathodic branch (analogous derivation):**

$$\beta_c = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_c F}, \quad a_c = -\beta_c \log(i_0)$$

$$\eta_c = a_c - \beta_c \log(i) \quad \text{(Cathodic branch)}$$

> **[7 Marks]** — Assumptions worth **2 marks**, derivation **3 marks**, final equations **2 marks**.

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)**

**Step 1 — Set $E_{anodic} = E_{cathodic}$ at the intersection:**

$$-0.480 + 0.075 \log(i_{corr}) = -0.620 - 0.110 \log(i_{corr})$$

**Step 2 — Combine log terms:**

$$0.075 \log(i_{corr}) + 0.110 \log(i_{corr}) = -0.620 + 0.480$$

$$0.185 \log(i_{corr}) = -0.140$$

$$\log(i_{corr}) = \frac{-0.140}{0.185} = -0.7568$$

$$i_{corr} = 10^{-0.7568} = 0.1751 \, \mu A/cm^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute $E_{corr}$ using the anodic line:**

$$E_{corr} = -0.480 + 0.075 \times (-0.7568) = -0.480 - 0.0568 = -0.5368 \, V \text{ (vs. SCE)}$$

$$\boxed{E_{corr} \approx -0.537 \, V \text{ vs. SCE}, \quad i_{corr} \approx 0.175 \, \mu A/cm^2}$$

**Step 4 — Stern–Geary constant $B$:**

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \vert \beta_c \vert}{2.303(\beta_a + \vert \beta_c \vert)} = \frac{0.075 \times 0.110}{2.303 \times (0.075 + 0.110)}$$

$$B = \frac{0.00825}{2.303 \times 0.185} = \frac{0.00825}{0.4260} = 0.01937 \, V$$

**Step 5 — Polarization resistance:**

$$R_p = \frac{B}{i_{corr}} = \frac{0.01937 \, V}{0.1751 \times 10^{-6} \, A/cm^2} = 1.106 \times 10^{5} \, \Omega \cdot cm^2 \approx 110.6 \, k\Omega \cdot cm^2$$

**Step 6 — Corrosion rate via Faraday's law:**

$$CR = \frac{0.00327 \times i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \times EW}{\rho} = \frac{0.00327 \times 0.1751 \times 27.92}{7.87}$$

$$CR = \frac{0.01600}{7.87} = 2.034 \times 10^{-3} \, mm/yr \approx 2.03 \, \mu m/yr$$

$$\boxed{R_p \approx 110.6 \, k\Omega \cdot cm^2, \quad CR \approx 2.03 \, \mu m/yr}$$

> **[7 Marks]** — Each sub-part is approximately 1.75 marks. Show every algebraic step to avoid mark deductions.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the **Wagner–Traud mixed potential theory** and explain how it forms the theoretical basis of Tafel extrapolation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A polarization experiment on aluminium in 0.5 M NaCl at 30 °C gives:
- Anodic slope $\beta_a = 0.080$ V/dec, intercept $a_a = -0.450$ V  
- Cathodic slope magnitude $\vert \beta_c \vert = 0.150$ V/dec, intercept $a_c = -0.700$ V  
  All potentials are vs. SCE.

Determine:
1. The values of $\log(i_{corr})$ and $E_{corr}$  
2. The exchange current densities $i_0$ for the anodic and cathodic reactions  
3. The percentage change in $i_{corr}$ if $\beta_a$ were to increase to 0.110 V/dec (keeping $\beta_c$ unchanged and $a_a, a_c$ unchanged) **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)**

**Wagner–Traud Mixed Potential Theory (1938):**

The Wagner–Traud hypothesis states that:

1. **Any electrochemical corrosion process can be decomposed into two or more independent partial reactions** — at least one anodic (metal dissolution) and one cathodic (e.g., $\mathrm{H^+}$ reduction, $\mathrm{O_2}$ reduction).
2. **There is no accumulation of electrical charge** in the system at the corrosion potential; the **net sum of all partial anodic currents equals the net sum of all partial cathodic currents** at $E_{corr}$.
3. The total polarization curve is the **algebraic sum** of the individual partial anodic and cathodic polarization curves.
4. Each partial reaction obeys its **own** Butler–Volmer / Tafel kinetics, independent of the others.

**Connection to Tafel Extrapolation:**

- Tafel extrapolation assumes the Wagner–Traud additivity of partial currents.
- At high overpotentials, each partial reaction exhibits Tafel behavior.
- The **linear Tafel regions** of the **partial anodic** and **partial cathodic** curves can be extrapolated back.
- Their intersection is **NOT** at $E_{corr} = 0$ (as in a single equilibrium reaction), but at the **mixed corrosion potential** $E_{corr}$, where $i_{a,partial} = i_{c,partial} = i_{corr}$.
- The Tafel method thus yields $i_{corr}$ — the **rate of the metal dissolution reaction** at the corrosion potential — even though neither the anodic nor the cathodic reaction is at equilibrium.

> **[7 Marks]** — Theory 4 marks, connection 3 marks.

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)**

**Step 1 — Express both lines in $E$ vs. $\log i$ form:**

$$E = -0.450 + 0.080 \log(i) \quad \text{(anodic)}$$

$$E = -0.700 - 0.150 \log(i) \quad \text{(cathodic)}$$

**Step 2 — Equate to find $\log(i_{corr})$:**

$$-0.450 + 0.080 \log(i) = -0.700 - 0.150 \log(i)$$

$$0.230 \log(i) = -0.250$$

$$\log(i_{corr}) = \frac{-0.250}{0.230} = -1.0870$$

$$i_{corr} = 10^{-1.0870} = 0.0817 \, \mu A/cm^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute $E_{corr}$:**

$$E_{corr} = -0.450 + 0.080 \times (-1.0870) = -0.450 - 0.0869 = -0.5369 \, V \text{ (vs. SCE)}$$

$$\boxed{\log(i_{corr}) = -1.087, \quad E_{corr} \approx -0.537 \, V \text{ vs. SCE}}$$

**Step 4 — Exchange current density $i_0$ for the anodic reaction:**

The anodic Tafel line gives, at $i = i_0$ (i.e., at $\eta = 0$ relative to the partial reaction's equilibrium):

$$0 = a_a + \beta_a \log(i_{0,a}) \Rightarrow \log(i_{0,a}) = -\frac{a_a}{\beta_a} = -\frac{-0.450}{0.080} = 5.625$$

$$i_{0,a} = 10^{5.625} = 4.22 \times 10^{5} \, \mu A/cm^2 = 0.422 \, A/cm^2$$

**Step 5 — $i_0$ for the cathodic reaction:**

$$\log(i_{0,c}) = -\frac{a_c}{\vert \beta_c \vert} = -\frac{-0.700}{0.150} = 4.667$$

$$i_{0,c} = 10^{4.667} = 4.64 \times 10^{4} \, \mu A/cm^2 = 0.0464 \, A/cm^2$$

**Step 6 — New $i_{corr}$ with $\beta_a = 0.110$ V/dec:**

$$-0.450 + 0.110 \log(i) = -0.700 - 0.150 \log(i)$$

$$0.260 \log(i) = -0.250$$

$$\log(i_{corr,new}) = -0.9615 \Rightarrow i_{corr,new} = 10^{-0.9615} = 0.1094 \, \mu A/cm^2$$

**Step 7 — Percentage change:**

$$\Delta i_{corr}\% = \frac{0.1094 - 0.0817}{0.0817} \times 100\% = \frac{0.0277}{0.0817} \times 100\% \approx 33.9\%$$

$$\boxed{i_{corr} \text{ increased by } \approx 33.9\% \text{ when } \beta_a \text{ rose from 0.080 to 0.110 V/dec}}$$

> **[7 Marks]** — Sub-part (1) 2 marks, sub-part (2) 2 marks, sub-part (3) 3 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Tafel Extrapolation Problems:**
> 1. **Sign error in cathodic Tafel equation:** Students often write $\eta_c = a_c + \beta_c \log(i_c)$, missing the **minus sign**. The cathodic slope on a Tafel plot is **negative** (E decreases as i increases).
> 2. **Confusing $\log$ with $\ln$:** The Tafel slope in engineering convention is in $\log_{10}$. Using $\ln$ yields a slope that is **2.303 times smaller** — a factor-of-2.3 error in $i_{corr}$!
> 3. **Forgetting units of $i_{corr}$:** $i_{corr}$ must be in **A/cm²** (or $\mu A/cm^2$) for Stern–Geary and Faraday's law. Mixing units (e.g., A/m² with EW in grams) leads to a **factor of 10⁴ error**.
> 4. **Linear region identification:** Students often force-fit the entire curve, including the curved $\pm 50$ mV region near $E_{corr}$. Valid Tafel data is **only** for $\vert \eta \vert \ge 50$–$100$ mV.
> 5. **In R$_{p}$ calculation:** Using $\beta_a + \beta_c$ **without absolute values** when one is negative leads to a smaller $B$ and an under-prediction of $i_{corr}$.
> 6. **Faraday's law constant:** $CR = 0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW / \rho$ is valid **only** when $i_{corr}$ is in $\mu A/cm^2$, $\rho$ in $g/cm^3$, and $CR$ is in mm/yr. State units explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Tafel Extrapolation**

- [x] **Tafel equation:** $\eta = a \pm b \log(i)$ — valid only at **high** overpotentials ($\vert \eta \vert \ge 50$–$100$ mV).
- [x] **Anodic Tafel slope:** $\beta_a = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F}$ (V/decade)
- [x] **Cathodic Tafel slope:** $\beta_c = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_c F}$ (V/decade, magnitude)
- [x] **Stern–Geary equation:** $i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \, R_p \, (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$
- [x] **Extrapolation method** yields both $E_{corr}$ (Y-axis) and $i_{corr}$ (X-axis) at the intersection of anodic and cathodic Tafel lines.
- [x] **Wagner–Traud hypothesis** is the **theoretical foundation** — total current is the sum of partial anodic and cathodic currents.
- [x] **At $E_{corr}$:** $\sum i_a = \sum i_c = i_{corr}$ (charge neutrality).
- [x] **Faraday's law conversion:** $CR \, (\text{mm/yr}) = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \cdot EW}{\rho \, (g/cm^3)}$
- [x] **Equivalent weight:** $EW = \dfrac{\text{Atomic weight}}{n}$, where $n$ = electrons transferred per metal atom.
- [x] **Limitations of Tafel method:**
  - Requires activation-controlled kinetics (no concentration polarization).
  - Linear regions must span at least **one decade** of current.
  - Sensitive to **IR drop** in poorly conductive electrolytes.
  - Not valid for systems exhibiting **passivation** or **pitting** (non-linear Tafel).
  - **Scan rate** must be slow enough for steady state.
- [x] **At 25 °C**, the thermal voltage $\dfrac{RT}{F} = 0.02569$ V; hence $2.303 \dfrac{RT}{F} = 0.05916$ V. If $\alpha = 0.5$, then $\beta \approx 0.118$ V/dec.
- [x] **Units consistency:** $\beta$ in V/dec, $i$ in A/cm² or $\mu A/cm^2$, $E$ in V vs. reference.
- [x] **High overpotential** means one exponential term in Butler–Volmer dominates — this is the **mathematical justification** for the Tafel equation.
- [x] **Exchange current density** $i_0$ is the rate of an electrochemical reaction at equilibrium ($\eta = 0$). It appears in the Tafel intercept.
- [x] **KTU examiner prefers:** labeled sketches, step-by-step algebra, and explicit unit mention. Skip the sketch, lose 1 mark. Skip units, lose 0.5 mark.
- [x] **Tafel slopes are diagnostic:** $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$ reveal the **mechanism** of the rate-determining step (1-electron, 2-electron, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Polarization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Linear Polarization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
**Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR)** is a non-destructive electrochemical technique used to instantaneously measure the instantaneous corrosion rate of a metal in an electrolyte. The technique, developed by Stern and Geary (1957), involves polarizing a working electrode by a small amount (typically **±10 mV to ±20 mV**) around its open circuit / corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$) and measuring the resulting direct current. The slope of the resulting linear potential-versus-current density plot near $E_{corr}$ is defined as the **Polarization Resistance ($R_p$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):** Linear polarization is the experimental foundation for polarization resistance measurements and forms the theoretical basis for commercial corrosion rate monitoring probes used in oil & gas, marine, and chemical plant industries.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a metal sample immersed in seawater slowly rusting away. How fast is it rusting? Traditionally, you would have to wait weeks, weigh the metal, and then calculate a mass loss rate — far too slow for a working engineer.

**Linear Polarization** acts like a "stethoscope" for corrosion. The technique applies a very small "nudge" of electrical potential (a tiny push) to the metal and listens to how much current flows back. The easier it is for current to flow (i.e., the lower the resistance), the faster the metal is corroding. It is analogous to checking the flow rate of a leaky pipe by applying a small pressure and measuring the water that escapes — the more water that escapes for a given pressure, the bigger the leak.

### Key Metrics & Constants
- **Stern-Geary Constant ($B$):** Typically falls in the range **0.026 V to 0.052 V** depending on the Tafel slopes.
- **Standard Tafel Slope assumption:** $\beta_a = \beta_c = 0.120$ V/decade (for active metals), giving $B \approx 0.026$ V.
- **Polarization range:** **±10 mV to ±20 mV** around $E_{corr}$ ensures the response remains linear (ohmic).
- **Reference electrodes commonly used:** Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE), Ag/AgCl, or Cu/CuSO$_4$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** Within ±20 mV of $E_{corr}$, the Butler-Volmer equation can be linearized, and the net current becomes directly proportional to the overpotential. This linearity is what gives the technique its name — *Linear* Polarization.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Theoretical Background — The Butler-Volmer Foundation
The current density at a polarized electrode is governed by the **Butler-Volmer equation**:

$$i = i_{corr} \left[ \exp\left(\frac{2.303(\eta)}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{2.303(\eta)}{\beta_c}\right) \right]$$

Where:
- $i$ = Net current density (A/cm²)
- $i_{corr}$ = Corrosion current density (A/cm²)
- $\eta$ = Overpotential = $E - E_{corr}$ (V)
- $\beta_a$ = Anodic Tafel slope (V/decade)
- $\beta_c$ = Cathodic Tafel slope (V/decade)

### Linearization Step
When $|\eta|$ is very small (≤ 20 mV), the exponential terms can be expanded using the Maclaurin series. Keeping only the first-order linear term:

$$i = i_{corr} \cdot 2.303 \left( \frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c} \right) \cdot \eta$$

Rearranging this into an Ohm's Law-like form ($\eta = i \cdot R_p$):

$$\eta = \frac{\eta}{i} = \frac{1}{2.303 \cdot i_{corr}\left(\frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c}\right)} = R_p$$

The **Polarization Resistance** is therefore defined as:

$$R_p = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta i} \bigg|_{E = E_{corr}}$$

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $R_p = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\Delta i}$ | Polarization resistance definition | $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$ |
| 2 | $R_p = \dfrac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | Stern-Geary relation | $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$ |
| 3 | $B = \dfrac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | Stern-Geary constant | V |
| 4 | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{B}{R_p}$ | Corrosion current density from LPR | A/cm² |
| 5 | $B = \dfrac{0.026}{\text{(active metals)}}$; $B = \dfrac{0.052}{\text{(passive systems)}}$ | Typical B-values | V |
| 6 | $\text{CR (mpy)} = 0.129 \cdot \dfrac{B}{R_p} \cdot \dfrac{EW}{\rho}$ | Corrosion rate in mpy | mpy |
| 7 | $\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \dfrac{3270 \cdot B}{R_p \cdot \rho} \cdot EW$ | Corrosion rate in mm/year | mm/yr |
| 8 | $EW = \dfrac{\text{Atomic Weight}}{\text{No. of electrons transferred}}$ | Equivalent weight | g/eq |

Where:
- $EW$ = Equivalent weight of the corroding metal
- $\rho$ = Density of the metal (g/cm³)
- mpy = mils per year (1 mil = 0.001 inch)

### Engineering Utility
- **Real-time corrosion monitoring** in refineries, pipelines, cooling water systems, and offshore platforms.
- Enables **continuous, non-destructive** monitoring without dismantling equipment.
- Provides instant feedback for **inhibitor dosing optimization** in chemical plants.
- Used in **laboratory ranking tests** to evaluate inhibitor efficiency quickly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Stern-Geary Equation

**Starting Point:** The Butler-Volmer equation for a corroding electrode under activation control.

$$i_a = i_{corr} \exp\left(\frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_a}\right) \quad \text{(anodic branch)}$$

$$i_c = -i_{corr} \exp\left(-\frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_c}\right) \quad \text{(cathodic branch)}$$

**Step 1:** Net current density is the sum of anodic and cathodic contributions.

$$i_{net} = i_{corr} \left[ \exp\left(\frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_c}\right) \right]$$

**Step 2:** For small values of $|\eta| \leq 20$ mV, apply the Taylor series expansion $e^x \approx 1 + x$ (valid for $|x| \ll 1$).

$$\exp\left(\frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_a}\right) \approx 1 + \frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_a}$$

$$\exp\left(-\frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_c}\right) \approx 1 - \frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_c}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute these approximations back into the net current expression.

$$i_{net} = i_{corr} \left[ \left(1 + \frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \left(1 - \frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_c}\right) \right]$$

**Step 4:** Simplify by cancelling the "1" terms.

$$i_{net} = i_{corr} \left[ \frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_a} + \frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_c} \right]$$

**Step 5:** Factor out $2.303 \cdot \eta$.

$$i_{net} = 2.303 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot \eta \left[ \frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c} \right]$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to express $\eta$ in terms of $i_{net}$ (Ohm's Law form).

$$\eta = \frac{i_{net}}{2.303 \cdot i_{corr} \left(\frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c}\right)} = i_{net} \cdot R_p$$

**Step 7:** Identify the denominator as the **Polarization Resistance $R_p$**.

$$R_p = \frac{1}{2.303 \cdot i_{corr} \left(\frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c}\right)}$$

**Step 8:** Take the common denominator inside the bracket.

$$\frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c} = \frac{\beta_a + \beta_c}{\beta_a \beta_c}$$

**Step 9:** Substitute back into $R_p$.

$$R_p = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot i_{corr} (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

**Step 10:** Define the **Stern-Geary constant** $B$.

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

**Final Form (Stern-Geary Equation):**

$$\boxed{i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p}}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem:** A steel specimen ($\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³, atomic weight = 55.85, valency = 2) is subjected to a linear polarization test in seawater. A potential perturbation of **±10 mV** around $E_{corr}$ produced a current density of **±9.62 μA/cm²**. Assuming the metal is actively corroding, calculate:
> (a) The polarization resistance $R_p$
> (b) The corrosion current density $i_{corr}$
> (c) The corrosion rate in **mm/year**

**Given Data:**
- $\Delta E = 10 \text{ mV} = 0.010 \text{ V}$
- $\Delta i = 9.62 \text{ μA/cm}^2 = 9.62 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A/cm}^2$
- $\rho = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- $EW = 55.85 / 2 = 27.925 \text{ g/eq}$
- $B = 0.026 \text{ V}$ (active corrosion assumption)

**Part (a) — Polarization Resistance:**

$$R_p = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta i} = \frac{0.010 \text{ V}}{9.62 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A/cm}^2}$$

$$R_p = 1039.5 \text{ }\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$$

**Part (b) — Corrosion Current Density:**

$$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{0.026 \text{ V}}{1039.5 \text{ }\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2}$$

$$i_{corr} = 2.501 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A/cm}^2 = 25.01 \text{ μA/cm}^2$$

**Part (c) — Corrosion Rate in mm/yr:**

Using the standard Faraday-based conversion:

$$\text{CR} = \frac{3270 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$$

$$\text{CR} = \frac{3270 \times 2.501 \times 10^{-5} \times 27.925}{7.85}$$

$$\text{CR} = \frac{2.2848}{7.85}$$

$$\boxed{\text{CR} = 0.291 \text{ mm/year}}$$

**Interpretation:** A corrosion rate of 0.291 mm/yr is classified as **"Moderate"** per NACE standards (0.025–0.1 mm/yr is low, 0.1–0.5 mm/yr is moderate, >0.5 mm/yr is high).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Three-Electrode Linear Polarization Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PSU[Potentiostat\nDC Power Source] -- applies +/-10 mV | WE | WE
    PSU -- measures current | WE-CE | CE
    V[Voltmeter\nHigh Impedance] -- reads E_WE vs RE | RE
    RE[Reference Electrode\nSCE or Ag/AgCl]
    WE[Working Electrode\nMetal Sample Under Test]
    CE[Counter Electrode\nPt or Graphite]
    SOL[Corrosive Electrolyte\nSeawater / Acid]
    RE --- SOL
    WE --- SOL
    CE --- SOL
    classDef instr fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef elec fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef sol fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    class PSU,V instr
    class RE,WE,CE elec
    class SOL sol
```

**Working Principle of the Setup:**
1. The **Potentiostat** applies a small potential sweep (±10 to ±20 mV) between the Working Electrode and Reference Electrode.
2. The **Reference Electrode** maintains a stable, known potential for accurate voltage measurement.
3. The **Counter Electrode** completes the circuit, carrying the polarizing current.
4. The **Voltmeter** measures potential with high input impedance so that negligible current flows through the reference (preventing its polarization).
5. The **Working Electrode** is the actual metal sample whose corrosion rate is being determined.

---

### 4.2 Typical Linear Polarization Curve (ΔE vs Δi)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Apply small overpotential\neta = +/- 10 mV around Ecorr] --> B[Measure resulting current density i_net]
    B --> C[Plot eta versus i_net]
    C --> D{Slope near Ecorr}
    D -->|Linear region| E[Slope = Polarization Resistance Rp]
    D -->|Non-linear at high eta| F[Region of Tafel behavior\nNOT used for LPR]
    E --> G[Apply Stern-Geary equation\nicorr = B / Rp]
    G --> H[Convert icorr to corrosion rate\nmm/yr or mpy]
    classDef proc fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2,color:#000
    classDef res fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E,G,H proc
    class F res
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LPR Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_BLOCK
        I1[Signal Generator\nSweep +/- 10 mV]
        I2[Current to Voltage\nConverter]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL_BLOCK
        C1[Potentiostat\nFeedback Loop]
        C2[Reference Electrode\nPotential Sensor]
    end
    subgraph PROCESSING_BLOCK
        P1[Analog to Digital\nConverter ADC]
        P2[Microcontroller\nRp and icorr computation]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT_BLOCK
        O1[Digital Display\nCorrosion Rate]
        O2[Data Logger\nTrend Analysis]
        O3[Alarm System\nThreshold Alert]
    end
    I1 --> C1
    C2 --> C1
    C1 --> I2
    I2 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> O1
    P2 --> O2
    P2 --> O3
    classDef input fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c63a4,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    class I1,I2 input
    class C1,C2 control
    class P1,P2 process
    class O1,O2,O3 output
```

**Key Functional Stages Explained:**
- **Input Stage:** Generates the low-amplitude triangular or step waveform.
- **Control Stage:** The potentiostat enforces the set potential between WE and RE, while sourcing current through the CE.
- **Processing Stage:** ADC digitizes both $\eta$ and $i$ signals; the microcontroller applies the Stern-Geary equation in real time.
- **Output Stage:** Displays the corrosion rate numerically, logs historical data for trend analysis, and triggers alarms when the rate exceeds preset limits.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
Define **Polarization Resistance ($R_p$)** and explain its physical significance in corrosion rate measurement.

**Model Answer:**
Polarization resistance ($R_p$) is defined as the slope of the potential-versus-current density plot at the corrosion potential, mathematically expressed as:

$$R_p = \left( \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta i} \right)_{E = E_{corr}}$$

It has units of $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$ and represents the kinetic resistance of an electrode to small perturbations from its corrosion potential. Physically, a **low $R_p$** indicates an actively corroding surface (high $i_{corr}$), while a **high $R_p$** suggests a passive or well-protected surface. It forms the experimental basis of the LPR technique developed by Stern and Geary.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
What is the **Stern-Geary constant ($B$)**? Write the equation and state typical values for active and passive systems.

**Model Answer:**
The Stern-Geary constant ($B$) is a proportionality factor that relates polarization resistance to corrosion current density:

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

Typical values are:
- **Active corrosion (e.g., steel in HCl):** $B \approx 0.026$ V
- **Passive systems (e.g., stainless steel):** $B \approx 0.052$ V

It is typically assumed when individual Tafel slopes are not independently measured.

---

### Part B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the **Stern-Geary equation** starting from the Butler-Volmer relationship, clearly stating the linearization assumption. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Start with the Butler-Volmer equation:**

$$i = i_{corr} \left[ \exp\left(\frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_c}\right) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the small overpotential assumption** ($|\eta| \leq 20$ mV, so the exponentials can be linearized using $e^x \approx 1 + x$):

$$\exp\left(\frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_a}\right) \approx 1 + \frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_a}, \quad \exp\left(-\frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_c}\right) \approx 1 - \frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_c}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify** (the constant "1" terms cancel):

$$i = i_{corr} \cdot 2.303 \cdot \eta \left( \frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into Ohm's Law form** ($\eta = i \cdot R_p$):

$$R_p = \frac{1}{2.303 \cdot i_{corr} \left( \frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c} \right)} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot i_{corr} (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

**Step 5 — Define the Stern-Geary constant $B$:**

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p}}$$

**Linearization Assumption:** $|\eta| \ll \frac{\beta}{2.303}$ (typically $|\eta| \leq 20$ mV), ensuring that higher-order terms in the Taylor expansion are negligible.

---

**(b)** In a linear polarization experiment on **mild steel** in 0.5 M H$_2$SO$_4$, the working electrode was polarized to **±15 mV** from $E_{corr}$, producing a current density of **±18.5 μA/cm²**. Calculate: **(i)** $R_p$, **(ii)** $i_{corr}$, and **(iii)** corrosion rate in **mm/yr**. Given: $\rho = 7.86$ g/cm³, atomic weight of Fe = 55.85, valency = 2, $B = 0.026$ V. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\Delta E = 15$ mV $= 0.015$ V; $\Delta i = 18.5$ μA/cm² $= 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ A/cm²; $B = 0.026$ V; $EW = 55.85/2 = 27.925$ g/eq; $\rho = 7.86$ g/cm³.

**(i) Polarization Resistance:**

$$R_p = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta i} = \frac{0.015}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}} = 810.81 \text{ }\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$$

**(ii) Corrosion Current Density:**

$$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{0.026}{810.81} = 3.207 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A/cm}^2 = 32.07 \text{ μA/cm}^2$$

**(iii) Corrosion Rate in mm/yr:**

$$\text{CR} = \frac{3270 \times i_{corr} \times EW}{\rho} = \frac{3270 \times 3.207 \times 10^{-5} \times 27.925}{7.86}$$

$$\text{CR} = \frac{2.9297}{7.86} = 0.3727 \text{ mm/yr}$$

$$\boxed{\text{CR} \approx 0.373 \text{ mm/year}}$$

**Classification:** Moderate-to-high corrosion rate (NACE standard).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **three-electrode linear polarization setup**. Explain the role of each electrode. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (text representation):**

$$\text{Potentiostat} \longrightarrow \text{WE (-)}\;\;\big|\;\;\text{CE (+) (current path)}$$
$$\text{Potentiostat} \longrightarrow \text{RE (high-Z voltmeter)} \;\;\big|\;\;\text{WE (potential sense)}$$

**Role of Each Electrode:**

1. **Working Electrode (WE):** This is the metal sample under investigation. It is the electrode whose corrosion behaviour is being measured. The surface area, surface finish, and orientation must be controlled for reproducible results.

2. **Reference Electrode (RE):** Provides a stable, fixed potential against which the WE potential is measured and controlled. Common choices include Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE, +0.244 V vs SHE) and Ag/AgCl. It carries **negligible current** so as not to be polarized itself.

3. **Counter Electrode (CE):** Also called the auxiliary electrode, it completes the electrical circuit by carrying the polarizing current. Made of inert materials (Pt, graphite, stainless steel), its surface area is usually larger than the WE to avoid limiting the current.

4. **Potentiostat:** The electronic instrument that controls the potential between WE and RE, while measuring the current flowing between WE and CE. It uses a feedback loop to maintain the set potential accurately.

---

**(b)** A corrosion engineer performs a linear polarization test on an **aluminium alloy** ($\rho = 2.70$ g/cm³, atomic weight = 26.98, valency = 3) in a 3% NaCl solution. The data obtained is: $R_p = 4500 \text{ }\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$, $\beta_a = 0.090$ V/dec, $\beta_c = 0.110$ V/dec. Calculate: **(i)** the Stern-Geary constant $B$, **(ii)** the corrosion current density $i_{corr}$, and **(iii)** the corrosion rate in **mpy (mils per year)**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $R_p = 4500 \text{ }\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$; $\beta_a = 0.090$ V; $\beta_c = 0.110$ V; $EW = 26.98/3 = 8.993$ g/eq; $\rho = 2.70$ g/cm³.

**(i) Stern-Geary Constant:**

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{0.090 \times 0.110}{2.303 \times (0.090 + 0.110)}$$

$$B = \frac{0.0099}{2.303 \times 0.200} = \frac{0.0099}{0.4606} = 0.0215 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Corrosion Current Density:**

$$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{0.0215}{4500} = 4.778 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A/cm}^2 = 4.778 \text{ μA/cm}^2$$

**(iii) Corrosion Rate in mpy:**

Using the standard conversion:

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{0.129 \times i_{corr} \times EW}{\rho}$$

$$\text{CR} = \frac{0.129 \times 4.778 \times 10^{-6} \times 8.993}{2.70}$$

$$\text{CR} = \frac{5.544 \times 10^{-6}}{2.70} = 2.053 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mpy}$$

$$\boxed{\text{CR} \approx 2.05 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mpy}}$$

**Interpretation:** This is an extremely low corrosion rate, indicating excellent corrosion resistance of the aluminium alloy in 3% NaCl — consistent with the protective nature of the passive Al$_2$O$_3$ film.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch is the #1 mark loser.** Always convert mV to V and μA/cm² to A/cm² before plugging into formulas.
> 2. **Confusing $R_p$ with solution resistance ($R_s$).** $R_p$ is the kinetic charge-transfer resistance at the interface, not the bulk electrolyte resistance. Modern instruments compensate for $R_s$ using EIS or current interruption.
> 3. **Wrong $B$-value assumption.** Using $B = 0.026$ for a passive system (e.g., stainless steel) gives erroneous low corrosion rates. Always check whether the system is active or passive.
> 4. **Forgetting EW = Atomic Weight / n.** Students often use atomic weight directly, leading to a 2× or 3× error in the final corrosion rate.
> 5. **Polarization range too large.** If you polarize more than ±30 mV, the linear assumption breaks down, and the Stern-Geary equation no longer holds.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR)** is a rapid, non-destructive electrochemical technique for instantaneous corrosion rate measurement.
- ✅ The technique works only within **±10 to ±20 mV** of $E_{corr}$, where the Butler-Volmer equation can be linearized.
- ✅ The core relationship is the **Stern-Geary equation**: $i_{corr} = \dfrac{B}{R_p}$, where $B \approx 0.026$ V (active) or $0.052$ V (passive).
- ✅ $R_p$ is the **slope of the $\eta$ vs $i$ plot at $E_{corr}$**, with units of $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$.
- ✅ A standard **3-electrode setup** (WE, RE, CE) is used with a **potentiostat** to apply and measure signals.
- ✅ LPR is widely used for **real-time corrosion monitoring** in pipelines, oil & gas, marine structures, and cooling systems.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Not suitable when $i_{corr}$ is extremely low (< 0.1 μA/cm²), when solution resistance is significant, or when the system exhibits strong concentration polarization.
- ✅ **Equivalent Weight (EW)** is always calculated as $EW = \dfrac{\text{Atomic Weight}}{\text{Valency (n)}}$.
- ✅ Always remember to convert units: **mV → V**, **μA → A**, and use the correct final CR formula depending on whether **mm/yr** or **mpy** is asked.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Galvanostat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Galvanostat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

A **Galvanostat** is a precision electronic instrument used in electrochemistry and corrosion science that maintains a **constant direct current (DC)** flowing between the working electrode (WE) and the counter electrode (CE) of an electrochemical cell, irrespective of variations in the cell impedance, electrolyte resistance, or the electrochemical reactions occurring at the electrode-electrolyte interface. The device automatically adjusts the cell voltage across the working-counter electrode pair to keep the measured current equal to a pre-set reference value, while simultaneously monitoring the potential of the working electrode against a reference electrode (RE).

In the context of **KTU Corrosion Engineering (PEPOT523)**, the galvanostat is the cornerstone instrument for conducting **galvanostatic polarization experiments**, **cathodic protection current calibration**, and **chronoamperometric studies** for determining corrosion rates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Decoded**
> - **Galvano** = Relating to current (named after Luigi Galvani).
> - **Stat** = Stable / constant (suffix denoting a regulating device).
> - **Galvanostat = Current-stabilizing device.** It is the current-domain counterpart of the **Potentiostat** (potential-stabilizing device).

## Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine you are filling a complex network of water pipes (the electrochemical cell) where the pipe diameter keeps changing unpredictably because of valves opening and closing (chemical reactions altering the cell resistance). If you want a **fixed flow rate of water (constant current)** to come out of the tap, you cannot just set the tap once — you must constantly read the flow meter and adjust the water pressure (voltage) to compensate for the resistance changes.

A **galvanostat** acts exactly like an "automatic pressure controller" in this analogy:
- You **set the desired flow rate (current)** on the dial.
- A **flow sensor (current-measuring shunt resistor)** continuously monitors the actual flow.
- A **feedback controller (op-amp + transistor bank)** instantly raises or lowers the pressure (voltage) across the pipes (cell) to maintain the set flow rate.
- A separate **pressure gauge (high-impedance voltmeter)** reads the back-pressure developed (working electrode potential vs. reference electrode), which gives you the corrosion/polarization information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Electrode Mandate:** A galvanostat is **only useful** with a three-electrode cell configuration: **Working Electrode (WE)**, **Counter Electrode (CE)**, and **Reference Electrode (RE)**. The current flows between WE ↔ CE, but the potential is *sensed* (no current flows) between WE and RE through a high-impedance probe. This is the same cell topology used in potentiostats.

## Standard Reference Metrics

- **Current Range:** Typically **1 nA to 10 A** in modern laboratory galvanostats.
- **Compliance Voltage:** The maximum voltage the instrument can apply across the cell, usually **±10 V, ±20 V, or ±100 V** depending on model.
- **Current Accuracy:** **±0.1 % to ±0.01 %** of full scale.
- **Bandwidth:** DC to **1 MHz** for fast transient response.
- **Faraday's Constant (F):** **96485 C/mol** — the fundamental conversion constant linking charge to moles of electrons in corrosion calculations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Principle — The Negative Feedback Loop

A galvanostat operates on the principle of **closed-loop negative feedback**, identical in mathematical structure to a control system. The logic chain is as follows:

1. **Reference Setting:** The user programs a set-point current $I_{set}$ using a precision digital-to-analog converter (DAC) or a front-panel dial.
2. **Current Sensing:** The actual current $I_{cell}$ flowing through the cell is measured by sensing the voltage drop across a precision **shunt resistor** $R_{shunt}$ (typically **0.1 Ω to 10 Ω**, with low temperature coefficient).
3. **Error Generation:** The instrument computes an error signal:
   
   $$V_{error} = V_{reference} - V_{sense}$$
   
   where $V_{sense} = I_{cell} \cdot R_{shunt}$.
4. **Amplification & Control:** A high-gain operational amplifier (gain typically **>10⁵**) amplifies this error and drives a power stage (a push-pull transistor bank or a bipolar power supply).
5. **Compliance Adjustment:** The power stage applies a voltage $V_{cell}$ across the WE–CE terminals. The polarity and magnitude of $V_{cell}$ are continuously adjusted so that $I_{cell}$ converges toward $I_{set}$.
6. **Potential Read-out:** A separate, **high-input-impedance electrometer (input impedance > 10¹² Ω)** buffers the potential between WE and RE, ensuring negligible current draw (< 1 pA) so as not to disturb the reference electrode.
7. **Convergence:** Because the loop gain is extremely high, in steady state $V_{error} \to 0$, which forces $I_{cell} \to I_{set}$ to within the instrument's accuracy.

> [!TIP]
> **Why negative feedback?** Without it, the slightest change in cell resistance (e.g., due to passivation, gas evolution, or film growth) would cause the current to drift. Negative feedback forces the system to *self-correct* thousands of times per second, achieving stability orders of magnitude better than an open-loop constant-voltage source could.

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Expression | SI Units | Typical Magnitude in Corrosion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $I$ | Current | $I = \frac{dQ}{dt}$ | A (Ampere) | $\mu$A to A |
| $i$ | Current Density | $i = \frac{I}{A_{WE}}$ | A/m² or $\mu$A/cm² | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{3}$ A/m² |
| $Q$ | Charge | $Q = I \cdot t$ | C (Coulomb) | Depends on experiment |
| $V_{cell}$ | Cell Voltage (WE–CE) | $V_{cell} = I \cdot R_{cell}$ | V (Volt) | 0.1 to 20 V |
| $E_{cell}$ | Cell Potential (WE–RE) | Measured directly by high-Z voltmeter | V | -2.0 to +2.0 V vs RE |
| $\eta$ | Overpotential | $\eta = E - E_{eq}$ | V | mV to V |
| $\eta_{a}$ | Anodic Overpotential (Tafel) | $\eta_{a} = \beta_{a} \log_{10}\left(\frac{i}{i_{0}}\right)$ | V | 0.05 to 0.5 V |
| $\eta_{c}$ | Cathodic Overpotential (Tafel) | $\eta_{c} = \beta_{c} \log_{10}\left(\frac{i}{i_{0}}\right)$ | V | 0.05 to 0.5 V |
| $i_{corr}$ | Corrosion Current Density | $i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_{a}\beta_{c}}{2.303 R_{p}(\beta_{a}+\beta_{c})}$ (Stern-Geary) | A/m² | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-3}$ A/m² |
| $CR$ | Corrosion Rate | $CR = \frac{K \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | mm/yr or mpy | 0.01 to 10 mm/yr |
| $m$ | Mass Dissolved | $m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ (Faraday's Law) | kg | — |
| $R_{p}$ | Polarization Resistance | $R_{p} = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta I}\bigg\vert_{E=E_{corr}}$ | $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^{2}$ | $10^{2}$ to $10^{6}$ $\Omega\cdot$cm² |
| $EW$ | Equivalent Weight | $EW = \frac{M}{n}$ | g/eq | 7.87 (Fe) to 108 (Ag) |
| $F$ | Faraday's Constant | $96485$ | C/mol | — |
| $K$ | Corrosion Rate Constant | $K = \frac{M}{nF\rho} \cdot \text{time factor}$ | — | $3272$ for mm/yr |

> [!CAUTION]
> **LaTeX Pipe-Symbol Rule:** When transcribing the Stern-Geary equation in your KTU answer scripts, **never** write the denominator as $\beta_a \vert \beta_c$. Always use the product form $(\beta_{a} \cdot \beta_{c})$ or a fraction. The pipe `$\vert$` symbol is **not** a recognized LaTeX math operator and will cost you marks.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Specific Use of Galvanostat |
|---|---|
| **Cathodic Protection (CP)** | Impressing a constant DC current onto pipelines, ship hulls, and offshore platforms to shift the metal potential into the immune region. |
| **Coating Evaluation** | Running anodic constant-current tests to determine coating breakdown voltage and service life. |
| **Corrosion Rate Measurement** | Combining with linear polarization resistance (LPR) probes to compute $i_{corr}$ via the Stern-Geary equation. |
| **Electroplating & Electrowinning** | Depositing uniform metal layers at fixed current density, ensuring reproducible thickness. |
| **Battery & Fuel-Cell Testing** | Performing charge-discharge cycles at constant current (CC mode) for capacity and cycle-life testing. |
| **Chlor-Alkali & Electrolysis** | Industrial chlor-alkali cells are essentially massive galvanostatic systems. |
| **Biomedical Implants** | Evaluating corrosion of Ti-alloy orthopedic implants in simulated body fluid (SBF). |
