# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Shunt Resistor Must Be Small

A galvanostat must impose a constant current *without* disturbing the cell. Let us derive the maximum allowable shunt resistance.

**Given:**
- The shunt resistor $R_{shunt}$ is in series with the cell.
- Total voltage $V_{compliance}$ available from the internal power supply.
- Cell resistance (which we cannot control): $R_{cell}$.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the loop:**

$$V_{compliance} = I_{cell} \cdot (R_{cell} + R_{shunt})$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the current:**

$$I_{cell} = \frac{V_{compliance}}{R_{cell} + R_{shunt}}$$

**Step 3 — Define the "current error" if the shunt were too large:**

If $R_{shunt}$ is not negligible compared to $R_{cell}$, then the effective compliance voltage delivered to the cell is reduced. The fractional loss in voltage available to drive the electrochemical reaction is:

$$\text{Voltage Loss Fraction} = \frac{R_{shunt}}{R_{cell} + R_{shunt}}$$

**Step 4 — Design requirement:**

To keep this loss below **0.1 %**, we require:

$$\frac{R_{shunt}}{R_{cell} + R_{shunt}} \le 0.001$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the upper bound on $R_{shunt}$:**

Assume $R_{cell} \gg R_{shunt}$ (typical for corrosion cells, $R_{cell}$ ranges from **10 Ω to 10 kΩ**), so $R_{cell} + R_{shunt} \approx R_{cell}$:

$$R_{shunt} \le 0.001 \cdot R_{cell}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example (typical KTU problem):**

If $R_{cell} = 1000\ \Omega$, then:

$$R_{shunt} \le 1\ \Omega$$

This is why commercial galvanostats use shunt resistors in the **0.1 Ω to 10 Ω** range — small enough to be negligible in the cell loop, but large enough to develop a measurable voltage (typically **10 mV to 1 V**) for the feedback amplifier.

## 3.2 Derivation: From Galvanostatic Data to Corrosion Rate

This is the **most frequently asked KTU long-answer derivation** for Module 3. The chain is: **Galvanostat data → Tafel slope → Stern-Geary → Corrosion rate.**

**Step 1 — Tafel Equation (anodic branch):**

For an anodic-controlled corrosion reaction (e.g., $\text{Fe} \to \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$), the Butler-Volmer equation reduces at high overpotentials to:

$$\eta_{a} = E - E_{corr} = \beta_{a} \log_{10}\left(\frac{i_{a}}{i_{0}}\right)$$

where $\beta_{a}$ is the anodic Tafel slope (V/decade) and $i_{0}$ is the exchange current density.

**Step 2 — Linearize around $E_{corr}$ (small $\eta$):**

For small perturbations, take the derivative of the Butler-Volmer equation at $E = E_{corr}$:

$$\left.\frac{d\eta}{di}\right\vert_{E=E_{corr}} = R_{p} = \frac{\beta_{a}\beta_{c}}{2.303\,i_{corr}(\beta_{a}+\beta_{c})}$$

This is the **Stern-Geary equation** (1957), the foundation of modern electrochemical corrosion monitoring.

**Step 3 — Solve for $i_{corr}$:**

Rearrange the Stern-Geary equation to isolate the corrosion current density:

$$i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_{a}\beta_{c}}{2.303\,R_{p}(\beta_{a}+\beta_{c})}$$

**Step 4 — Faraday's Law to convert $i_{corr}$ to mass loss rate:**

The mass of metal dissolved per unit time per unit area is:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot F} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^{2}\cdot\text{s}}\right]$$

where $M$ is the molar mass (kg/mol) and $n$ is the number of electrons transferred.

**Step 5 — Convert mass loss to linear corrosion rate (mm/yr):**

The linear penetration rate is:

$$CR = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \quad \left[\frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Insert time-conversion factors (s → yr) and length-conversion (m → mm):**

$$CR \left[\frac{\text{mm}}{\text{yr}}\right] = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M \cdot 3.156 \times 10^{7}}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot 10^{3}}$$

Combining all numerical constants:

$$CR = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{3} \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$$

where $i_{corr}$ is in **A/m²**, $M$ in **g/mol**, and $\rho$ in **g/cm³**, yielding $CR$ in **mm/yr**.

**Step 7 — Worked example (KTU-style numerical):**

**Given:** Mild steel in aerated 3 % NaCl solution. Galvanostatic LPR measurement gives $R_{p} = 1500\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}^{2}$. Tafel slopes: $\beta_{a} = 0.090$ V/dec, $\beta_{c} = 0.120$ V/dec. For Fe: $M = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho = 7.87$ g/cm³.

**Compute $i_{corr}$:**

$$i_{corr} = \frac{(0.090)(0.120)}{2.303 \times 1500 \times (0.090+0.120)}$$

$$i_{corr} = \frac{0.0108}{725.45} = 1.489 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{A/cm}^{2} = 14.89\ \mu\text{A/cm}^{2}$$

**Compute $CR$:**

$$CR = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{3} \times (1.489 \times 10^{-5}) \times 55.85}{2 \times 7.87}$$

$$CR = \frac{2.722}{15.74} = 0.173\ \text{mm/yr}$$

**Conclusion:** This corresponds to a corrosion rate of approximately **0.173 mm/year**, which is a *moderate* corrosion regime — consistent with mild steel in saline environments.

## 3.3 Derivation: Charge-Passed → Mass-Loss via Faraday's Law

When a galvanostat is used in **chronoamperometry** (constant $I$ for time $t$):

**Step 1 — Total charge passed:**

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Moles of electrons:**

$$n_{e^-} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{96485}$$

**Step 3 — Moles of metal dissolved (assuming $n$ electrons per atom):**

$$n_{metal} = \frac{n_{e^-}}{n} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 4 — Mass dissolved:**

$$m = n_{metal} \cdot M = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

This is the direct **Faraday's Law expression for mass loss under galvanostatic control**.

**Sanity check:** For $I = 1$ mA, $t = 1$ hr, $M = 55.85$ g/mol (Fe), $n = 2$:

$$m = \frac{(10^{-3})(3600)(55.85)}{2 \times 96485} = 1.04 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{g} = 1.04\ \text{mg}$$

This is a useful, experimentally verifiable quantity for KTU lab viva questions.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a galvanostat. Mention its compliance voltage and typical current ranges.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **galvanostat** is an electronic instrument that maintains a **constant direct current** between the working and counter electrodes of an electrochemical cell, regardless of changes in cell impedance.

- **Compliance voltage:** The maximum voltage the galvanostat can apply across the cell to maintain the set current. Typical values are **±10 V to ±100 V**.
- **Current range:** Modern laboratory galvanostats cover **1 nA to 10 A** with an accuracy of **±0.1 % of full scale**.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for compliance voltage, 1 mark for current range.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between a galvanostat and a potentiostat (any three points).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Galvanostat | Potentiostat |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Controls **current** to a constant value | Controls **potential** to a constant value |
| 2 | Measures **potential (E vs. t)** as output | Measures **current (I vs. t or I vs. E)** as output |
| 3 | Feedback variable is the **cell current** sensed via shunt resistor | Feedback variable is the **WE–RE potential** sensed via electrometer |
| 4 | Used in cathodic protection, chronoamperometry | Used in potentiodynamic polarization, cyclic voltammetry |

*(1 mark per correct distinction; any 3 acceptable.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a galvanostat. Discuss the role of the shunt resistor and the high-impedance electrometer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mild steel specimen is subjected to a galvanostatic linear polarization test. The Tafel slopes are $\beta_{a} = 0.090$ V/dec and $\beta_{c} = 0.120$ V/dec. The polarization resistance is $R_{p} = 1200\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}^{2}$. Calculate the corrosion current density and the corrosion rate in mm/yr. Given: $M_{Fe} = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho_{Fe} = 7.87$ g/cm³, $F = 96485$ C/mol. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Working Principle (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition and purpose (2 marks):**
A galvanostat is a closed-loop electronic controller that maintains a constant DC current through an electrochemical cell. The block diagram comprises:
1. **Set-point reference** (DAC) — user-programmed target current.
2. **Current sensor** — precision shunt resistor $R_{shunt}$ in series with the cell.
3. **Error amplifier** — high-gain op-amp summing junction.
4. **Power stage** — push-pull transistor bank that supplies compliance voltage.
5. **Three-electrode cell** — WE, CE, RE immersed in electrolyte.
6. **Electrometer** — high-input-impedance ($>10^{12}\ \Omega$) voltmeter to read $E_{WE}$ vs. RE.

**Step 2 — Working sequence (3 marks):**
1. The reference DAC outputs $V_{ref}$ proportional to the desired current $I_{set}$.
2. The shunt resistor develops $V_{sense} = I_{cell} \cdot R_{shunt}$.
3. The error amplifier computes $\varepsilon = V_{ref} - V_{sense}$ and amplifies it by gain $A$.
4. The power stage applies a voltage $V_{cell} = A\varepsilon$ across the WE–CE terminals.
5. Negative feedback drives $\varepsilon \to 0$, forcing $I_{cell} \to I_{set}$.

**Step 3 — Role of the shunt resistor (1 mark):**
The shunt resistor must be **small (0.1 to 10 Ω)** to minimize voltage loss in the cell loop, yet **large enough** to produce a measurable voltage (10 mV to 1 V) for the error amplifier. It is the **current-sensing element** in the feedback loop.

**Step 4 — Role of the high-impedance electrometer (1 mark):**
The electrometer measures $E_{WE}$ vs. $E_{RE}$ without drawing current from the reference electrode. Its input impedance ($>10^{12}\ \Omega$) ensures the reference electrode potential remains **unpolarized and stable**, which is essential for accurate potential measurement.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Apply the Stern-Geary equation (3 marks):**

$$i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_{a}\beta_{c}}{2.303\,R_{p}(\beta_{a}+\beta_{c})}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$i_{corr} = \frac{(0.090)(0.120)}{2.303 \times 1200 \times (0.090 + 0.120)}$$

$$i_{corr} = \frac{0.0108}{2.303 \times 1200 \times 0.210}$$

$$i_{corr} = \frac{0.0108}{580.36} = 1.861 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{A/cm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to mm/yr using the corrosion rate constant (3 marks):**

$$CR = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{3} \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$$

$$CR = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{3} \times (1.861 \times 10^{-5}) \times 55.85}{2 \times 7.87}$$

$$CR = \frac{3.4016}{15.74} = 0.216\ \text{mm/yr}$$

**Step 3 — Final result (1 mark):**
$$\boxed{i_{corr} = 1.861 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{A/cm}^{2} = 18.61\ \mu\text{A/cm}^{2}}$$
$$\boxed{CR = 0.216\ \text{mm/yr}}$$

This corresponds to **moderate-to-high corrosion**, typical of mild steel in aggressive environments without protective coatings.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of negative feedback in a galvanostat. Why is it necessary, and what would happen if the loop were opened? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Stern-Geary equation starting from the Butler-Volmer equation. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Negative Feedback (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):**
Negative feedback is a control mechanism in which a fraction of the output signal is **subtracted from the input reference** to produce an *error signal* that drives the actuator. In a galvanostat, the output is the cell current $I_{cell}$, the input is the set-point $I_{set}$, and the error $\varepsilon = I_{set} - I_{cell}$ controls the cell voltage $V_{cell}$.

**Step 2 — Why it is necessary (3 marks):**
1. The cell impedance $R_{cell}$ is **not constant** — it changes as corrosion proceeds, as passive films form or break down, and as the electrolyte composition evolves.
2. Without feedback, the current would drift with these changes, making controlled experiments impossible.
3. Negative feedback **automatically adjusts $V_{cell}$** to compensate, achieving current stability typically within **±0.01 %** of the set-point.
4. It also **linearizes** the system response and **extends the bandwidth** of accurate control.

**Step 3 — What happens if the loop is opened (2 marks):**
1. The galvanostat becomes a simple **constant-voltage source** with $V_{cell}$ fixed at whatever the DAC was set to.
2. The current becomes $I = V_{cell}/R_{cell}$, which drifts unpredictably as $R_{cell}$ varies.
3. Controlled experiments (e.g., Tafel slope determination) become impossible because the independent variable is no longer controlled.
4. The system is **susceptible to oscillations and runaway** if the constant voltage drives the cell into a region of negative differential resistance (e.g., passivity breakdown).

#### Part (b) — Stern-Geary Derivation (7 marks)

**Starting point — Butler-Volmer equation (1 mark):**

$$i = i_{0}\left[\exp\left(\frac{(1-\alpha)nF\eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(\frac{-\alpha nF\eta}{RT}\right)\right]$$

where $i$ is the net current density, $i_{0}$ is the exchange current density, $\eta = E - E_{eq}$ is the overpotential, and $\alpha$ is the charge-transfer coefficient (typically **0.5**).

**Assumption 1 — Symmetric charge transfer (1 mark):**
For corroding systems, set $\alpha = 0.5$ so that $(1-\alpha) = \alpha = 0.5$.

**Assumption 2 — Small overpotential (1 mark):**
When $|\eta| \ll RT/F \approx 25.7$ mV at 25 °C, the exponentials can be linearized using $e^{x} \approx 1 + x$:

$$i \approx i_{0}\left[\left(1 + \frac{0.5 nF\eta}{RT}\right) - \left(1 - \frac{0.5 nF\eta}{RT}\right)\right]$$

**Simplify (1 mark):**

$$i \approx i_{0} \cdot \frac{nF\eta}{RT}$$

**Definition of polarization resistance (1 mark):**
By definition, $R_{p} = (d\eta/di)\vert_{E=E_{corr}}$. Differentiating:

$$R_{p} = \frac{RT}{nFi_{0}}$$

**Generalize to mixed anodic-cathodic control (1 mark):**
For a corroding system with two distinct Tafel slopes $\beta_{a}$ and $\beta_{c}$ (asymmetric kinetics), the linearized expression becomes:

$$R_{p} = \frac{\beta_{a}\beta_{c}}{2.303\,i_{corr}(\beta_{a}+\beta_{c})}$$

where $\beta = \frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha nF}$ is the Tafel slope in V/decade.

**Final Stern-Geary equation (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_{a}\beta_{c}}{2.303\,R_{p}(\beta_{a}+\beta_{c})}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Galvanostat Problems**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion:** $i_{corr}$ from $R_{p}$ is in **A/cm²**, but the corrosion rate constant $K$ expects **A/m²** for some formulations. Mixing units is the **#1 mark-loser**. Always write the units explicitly at every step.
> 2. **Sign convention for anodic vs. cathodic Tafel slopes:** Both $\beta_{a}$ and $\beta_{c}$ are **positive numbers** in the Stern-Geary equation. Do **not** insert negative signs based on the sign of $\eta$.
> 3. **Confusing galvanostat with ammeter:** A galvanostat *imposes* current; an ammeter only *measures* it. Examiners will deduct marks if you describe a galvanostat as a "current measuring device."
> 4. **Forgetting the role of the Luggin capillary:** In the three-electrode cell, the reference electrode is placed via a **Luggin capillary** to minimize the IR drop between RE and WE. Mentioning this in diagrams earns **bonus marks**.
> 5. **Open-loop vs. closed-loop:** Always emphasize that a galvanostat is a **closed-loop** device. Simply writing "it supplies constant current" without mentioning **negative feedback** is treated as an incomplete answer.
> 6. **Faraday's Law constant:** The value is **96485 C/mol** (sometimes rounded to **96500**). Use the exact value unless the problem states otherwise.
> 7. **Plot axes on Tafel plots:** The x-axis is **log(i)**, not **i**. Many students lose marks by labeling the axis incorrectly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Galvanostat = constant-current source** for electrochemical cells; the *current-domain* counterpart of the potentiostat.
- [x] Operates on **closed-loop negative feedback** using a precision **shunt resistor** as the current sensor.
- [x] The **three-electrode cell** (WE, CE, RE) is mandatory; current flows WE ↔ CE, potential sensed WE vs. RE.
- [x] The **high-impedance electrometer** ($Z_{in} > 10^{12}\ \Omega$) prevents polarization of the reference electrode.
- [x] **Compliance voltage** is the maximum voltage the instrument can apply (typically ±10 V to ±100 V) — important for high-resistance cells.
- [x] **Current range** spans **nA to A** with accuracy of **±0.1 %** or better.
- [x] **Tafel equation:** $\eta = \beta \log(i/i_{0})$ — gives Tafel slopes in V/decade.
- [x] **Stern-Geary equation:** $i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_{a}\beta_{c}}{2.303\,R_{p}(\beta_{a}+\beta_{c})}$ — the workhorse of corrosion rate determination.
- [x] **Corrosion rate formula:** $CR = \dfrac{3.27 \times 10^{3} \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$ in mm/yr.
- [x] **Faraday's Law:** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ — for chronoamperometric mass-loss calculations.
- [x] **Applications:** Cathodic protection, LPR probes, electroplating, battery testing, coating evaluation.
- [x] **KTU pitfall:** Always state **assumptions** (e.g., small $\eta$, $\alpha = 0.5$, steady state) when deriving Stern-Geary.
- [x] **Key constant:** $F = 96485$ C/mol.
- [x] **Distinction from potentiostat:** Galvanostat controls I, measures E; Potentiostat controls E, measures I.
- [x] **Without feedback** (open loop), the device becomes a constant-voltage source and cannot maintain stable current.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impedance Spectroscopy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Impedance Spectroscopy # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)** is a non-destructive, steady-state AC electrochemical technique in which a small-amplitude sinusoidal perturbation (typically **5 mV to 20 mV**) in potential is applied to an electrochemical system over a wide range of frequencies (typically **10⁵ Hz to 10⁻³ Hz** or 1 mHz), and the resulting current response is measured. The frequency-dependent impedance **Z(ω)** is then computed as the ratio of the voltage phasor to the current phasor, providing detailed information about the kinetics of charge transfer, mass transport, and interfacial phenomena occurring at the metal/electrolyte interface. The technique is governed by the linear systems theory assumption: the perturbation signal must be small enough that the system's response remains pseudo-linear, allowing the use of **Ohm's Law in its complex form**. The fundamental governing equation is: $$Z(\omega) = \frac{\tilde{V}(\omega)}{\tilde{I}(\omega)} = \frac{V_m \sin(\omega t)}{I_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)}$$ where $\phi$ is the phase shift between voltage and current, and $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency in **rad·s⁻¹**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spring-Dashpot-Mechanical System" Picture > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:** Imagine pushing a child on a swing. If you push exactly **in sync** with the swing's natural rhythm (low frequency, in phase), the swing responds strongly. If you push in a **chaotic, out-of-sync** manner (high frequency), the swing barely moves. EIS does the same thing electrically — it "pushes" the corroding metal with AC signals of varying frequency and "listens" to how the corrosion interface responds. The **resistor** is like a stiff, instantly-responding object; the **capacitor** is like a spring that resists sudden change; the **Warburg element** is like a sponge that soaks up ions slowly. By recording the response across all frequencies, we can identify each component hidden inside the corrosion cell. ## 1.3 Why EIS Matters in Corrosion Engineering EIS is the **gold-standard non-destructive technique** for: - Determining **instantaneous corrosion rates** without removing the corrosion product layer. - Evaluating **organic coatings, paints, and inhibitors** quantitatively. - Studying **localized corrosion** (pitting, crevice) initiation mechanisms. - Probing **double-layer capacitance** ($C_{dl}$) and **charge transfer resistance** ($R_{ct}$). - Distinguishing between **faradaic** and **non-faradaic** processes occurring simultaneously. The three standard graphical representations are: | Plot Type | X-axis | Y-axis | Primary Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Nyquist Plot** | $Z'$ (Real Impedance) | $-Z''$ (Imaginary Impedance) | Identifying RC loops, diffusion tails | | **Bode Magnitude Plot** | $\log(f)$ | $\log \vert Z \vert$ | Reading low-frequency impedance | | **Bode Phase Plot** | $\log(f)$ | $\phi$ (Phase angle) | Detecting time constants | > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Stern–Geary equation combined with $R_{ct}$ from EIS gives the corrosion current density directly: $i_{corr} = B/R_{ct}$, where $B \approx 25$–**52 mV/decade** for active-passive metals. Always mention this in numerical problems. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The AC Circuit Theory Behind Impedance In a DC circuit, only resistance $R$ opposes current flow. In an AC circuit, **three fundamental elements** oppose current, each with a characteristic frequency dependence: ### 2.1.1 Pure Resistor The voltage and current are **always in phase** ($\phi = 0$). $$Z_R = R \quad \text{(no frequency dependence)}$$ ### 2.1.2 Pure Capacitor A capacitor stores charge; the current **leads** the voltage by exactly $90°$. $$Z_C = \frac{1}{j\omega C} = -\frac{j}{\omega C}$$ The impedance **decreases with increasing frequency** — at high frequency, AC passes "through" the capacitor easily. ### 2.1.3 Pure Inductor An inductor stores energy in a magnetic field; the current **lags** the voltage by $90°$. $$Z_L = j\omega L$$ The impedance **increases with increasing frequency** (rare in corrosion systems but appears in some battery corrosion studies). ## 2.2 The Warburg Impedance — Diffusion in Corrosion When mass transport (diffusion of reactive species to/from the metal surface) controls the corrosion rate, a **Warburg impedance** ($Z_W$) appears. It has equal real and imaginary components of magnitude proportional to $\omega^{-1/2}$. $$Z_W = \sigma (1 - j)\omega^{-1/2} = \sigma \omega^{-0.5}(1 - j)$$ where $\sigma$ is the **Warburg coefficient** in $\Omega \cdot s^{-1/2}$, related to the diffusion coefficient $D$ of the electroactive species by: $$\sigma = \frac{RT}{n^2 F^2 A \sqrt{2}} \left( \frac{1}{D_O^{1/2} C_O^*} + \frac{1}{D_R^{1/2} C_R^*} \right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** A Warburg element of finite length (with a diffusion layer thickness $\delta$) appears as a tilted line of slope ~1 in the Nyquist plot. A Warburg of infinite length (semi-infinite diffusion) appears as a $45°$ line at low frequencies. In real coated systems, the $45°$ line usually transitions into a capacitive loop at very low $f$, indicating a **finite diffusion length**. ## 2.3 Constant Phase Element (CPE) — The Real-World Imperfection Ideal capacitors yield perfect semicircles in Nyquist plots; real corroding systems produce **depressed semicircles** due to surface roughness, inhomogeneous coatings, distributed reactivity, and 2D/3D current distributions. This is modelled by a **CPE**: $$Z_{CPE} = \frac{1}{Y_0 (j\omega)^n}$$ where $Y_0$ has units of $\text{S} \cdot \text{s}^n$ and $n$ is a dimensionless exponent: | $n$ value | Physical Meaning | |---|---| | $n = 1$ | Pure capacitor (ideal) | | $n = 0$ | Pure resistor | | $n = 0.5$ | Warburg-like diffusion behaviour | | $0.8 \leq n \leq 0.9$ | Typical for rough, coated, or porous metal surfaces | ## 2.4 The Randles Equivalent Circuit — The Heart of Corrosion EIS The **Randles circuit** is the foundational model for an activated, charge-transfer-controlled corrosion process: ``` Rs Rct ──/\/\/\──┬──/\/\/\──┬── │ │ Cdl Zw (optional) │ │ ───────────┴──────────┴── ``` - $R_s$ → **Solution resistance** (electrolyte between Luggin capillary and working electrode). - $C_{dl}$ → **Double-layer capacitance** at the metal/solution interface. - $R_{ct}$ → **Charge-transfer resistance** (the most important parameter for corrosion rate). - $Z_W$ → **Warburg impedance** (added when diffusion is significant). The total impedance is: $$Z(\omega) = R_s + \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2} - j\frac{\omega R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}}{1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2}$$ ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Z = V/I$ | Ohm's law in complex form | $\Omega$ | | 2 | $Z^* = Z' - jZ''$ | Complex conjugate of impedance | $\Omega$ | | 3 | $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{Z'^2 + Z''^2}$ | Modulus of impedance | $\Omega$ | | 4 | $\phi = \tan^{-1}(Z''/Z')$ | Phase angle | degrees or rad | | 5 | $Z_C = 1/(j\omega C)$ | Capacitor impedance | $\Omega$ | | 6 | $Z_W = \sigma \omega^{-0.5}(1-j)$ | Warburg impedance | $\Omega$ | | 7 | $Z_{CPE} = 1/[Y_0(j\omega)^n]$ | CPE impedance | $\Omega$ | | 8 | $i_{corr} = B/R_{ct}$ | Stern–Geary equation | A·cm⁻² | | 9 | $B = \beta_a \beta_c / [2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)]$ | Stern–Geary constant | V/decade | | 10 | $\tau = R_{ct} C_{dl}$ | Time constant of charge transfer | s | | 11 | $C_{dl} = (\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A)/d$ | Double-layer capacitance | F | | 12 | $f_{max} = 1/(2\pi R_{ct} C_{dl})$ | Frequency at semicircle apex | Hz | | 13 | $\sigma_W = RT/(n^2 F^2 A \sqrt{2 D})$ | Warburg coefficient | $\Omega \cdot s^{-1/2}$ | | 14 | $CR = K \cdot i_{corr} / (\rho \cdot n)$ | Corrosion rate from $i_{corr}$ | mm/yr | | 15 | $\eta = E - E_{corr}$ | Overpotential | V | > [!WARNING] > **Units Pitfall:** In KTU exams, students frequently write $B$ in V without converting $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$ from mV/decade. Always convert Tafel slopes to **V/decade** before substituting into the Stern–Geary equation. ## 2.6 Why This Matters in Industry - **Offshore oil platforms** use EIS to monitor **cathodic protection efficiency** without dismantling the structure. - **Automotive paint manufacturers** use EIS to grade **coating quality** in accelerated salt-spray tests. - **Pharmaceutical pipelines** (316L SS) use EIS to qualify **CIP (clean-in-place) corrosion** after acid washes. - **Battery R&D** uses EIS to track **SEI layer growth** on anodes — closely linked to corrosion science. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Impedance of a Parallel RC Combination The parallel combination models the **double-layer capacitance in parallel with the charge-transfer resistance** at the corroding interface. **Step 1 — Start with admittance (sum of parallel admittances is simpler):** $$Y_{total} = Y_R + Y_C = \frac{1}{R_{ct}} + j\omega C_{dl}$$ **Step 2 — Take the reciprocal to obtain impedance:** $$Z_{total} = \frac{1}{Y_{total}} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{R_{ct}} + j\omega C_{dl}}$$ **Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator:** $$Z_{total} = \frac{\frac{1}{R_{ct}} - j\omega C_{dl}}{\left(\frac{1}{R_{ct}}\right)^2 + (\omega C_{dl})^2}$$ **Step 4 — Rationalize the expression by separating real and imaginary parts:** $$Z_{total} = \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2} - j\frac{\omega R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}}{1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2}$$ **Step 5 — Identify the components:** $$Z'(\omega) = \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2} \quad \text{(Real part)}$$ $$Z''(\omega) = \frac{\omega R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}}{1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2} \quad \text{(Imaginary part)}$$ **Step 6 — Verify limiting behaviour:** - At $\omega \to 0$: $Z' \to R_{ct}$ and $Z'' \to 0$ → point on the real axis at $R_{ct}$. - At $\omega \to \infty$: $Z' \to 0$ and $Z'' \to 0$ → origin. - At $\omega = 1/(R_{ct} C_{dl})$: peak of the imaginary part (apex of the semicircle). ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nyquist Equation of a Randles Cell Adding the series solution resistance $R_s$ shifts the entire semicircle to the right by $R_s$ along the real axis. **Step 1 — Write the full Randles impedance (without Warburg):** $$Z_{Randles}(\omega) = R_s + \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2} - j\frac{\omega R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}}{1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2}$$ **Step 2 — Show that $(Z' - R_s - R_{ct}/2)^2 + (Z'')^2 = (R_{ct}/2)^2$:** Compute the real deviation from the centre: $$Z' - R_s - \frac{R_{ct}}{2} = \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2} - \frac{R_{ct}}{2} = \frac{R_{ct}[2 - 1 - (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2]}{2[1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2]}$$ $$= \frac{R_{ct}[1 - (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2]}{2[1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2]}$$ Square it: $$\left(Z' - R_s - \frac{R_{ct}}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{R_{ct}^2 [1 - (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2]^2}{4[1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2]^2}$$ Square the imaginary part: $$(Z'')^2 = \frac{(\omega R_{ct}^2 C_{dl})^2}{[1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2]^2} = \frac{R_{ct}^2 (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2}{[1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2]^2}$$ **Step 3 — Add the two squared terms:** $$\left(Z' - R_s - \frac{R_{ct}}{2}\right)^2 + (Z'')^2 = \frac{R_{ct}^2}{4[1+(\omega R_{ct}C_{dl})^2]^2}\left[ [1-(\omega R_{ct}C_{dl})^2]^2 + 4(\omega R_{ct}C_{dl})^2 \right]$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the bracket using $(a-b)^2 + 4ab = (a+b)^2$ with $a=1$ and $b=(\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2$:** $$= \frac{R_{ct}^2}{4[1+(\omega R_{ct}C_{dl})^2]^2}\left[ 1 + (\omega R_{ct}C_{dl})^2 \right]^2 = \frac{R_{ct}^2}{4}$$ **Step 5 — Conclude:** $$\boxed{\left(Z' - R_s - \frac{R_{ct}}{2}\right)^2 + (Z'')^2 = \left(\frac{R_{ct}}{2}\right)^2}$$ This is the **equation of a circle** in the $Z'–Z''$ plane with: - **Centre** at $\left(R_s + \frac{R_{ct}}{2}, \, 0\right)$ - **Radius** equal to $\frac{R_{ct}}{2}$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Interpretation Tip:** The diameter of the Nyquist semicircle (from $R_s$ to $R_s + R_{ct}$ on the real axis) gives **$R_{ct}$ directly**, and the **frequency at the apex** gives the **time constant** $R_{ct} C_{dl}$. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stern–Geary Equation (from Butler–Volmer) **Step 1 — The Butler–Volmer equation near the corrosion potential ($E \approx E_{corr}$, $\eta \approx 0$):** $$i = i_{corr}\left[\exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right)\right]$$ **Step 2 — Linearize the exponentials using the Taylor series $\exp(x) \approx 1 + x$ for small $x$:** $$i \approx i_{corr}\left[1 + \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT} - 1 + \frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right] = i_{corr} \cdot \frac{(\alpha_a + \alpha_c) F \eta}{RT}$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange into the linearized form $\eta = (RT/i_{corr}(\alpha_a + \alpha_c)F) \cdot i$:** The slope $d\eta/di$ at $E_{corr}$ is the **polarization resistance $R_p$**: $$R_p = \left.\frac{d\eta}{di}\right|_{E=E_{corr}} = \frac{RT}{i_{corr}(\alpha_a + \alpha_c)F}$$ **Step 4 — Use the Tafel approximation at large overpotentials to obtain the Stern–Geary constant:** For large $\eta$, $\alpha F \eta / RT \gg 1$ and the cathodic term becomes negligible. The Tafel relations are: $$\eta_a = \beta_a \log\left(\frac{i_a}{i_{corr}}\right), \quad \eta_c = -\beta_c \log\left(\frac{|i_c|}{i_{corr}}\right)$$ where $\beta_a = 2.303 RT / \alpha_a F$ and $\beta_c = 2.303 RT / \alpha_c F$. **Step 5 — Substituting $\alpha_a = 2.303 RT/(\beta_a F)$ and similarly for $\alpha_c$ into the $R_p$ equation:** $$R_p = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 (\beta_a + \beta_c) i_{corr}} = \frac{B}{i_{corr}}$$ **Step 6 — Invert to obtain the Stern–Geary equation:** $$\boxed{i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c) R_p}}$$ For a corroding system (no significant diffusion control), $R_p \approx R_{ct}$, so EIS directly gives the corrosion rate. > [!TIP] > **Numerical shortcut for KTU:** For a corroding system where both Tafel slopes are ~0.12 V/decade, $B \approx 26$ mV. For systems where one slope is ~0.06 V/decade and the other ~0.12 V/decade, $B \approx 52$ mV — the famous "B = 26 or 52 mV" rule of thumb. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Diagnostic Feature Recognition | Feature in Nyquist Plot | Equivalent Circuit | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | Single semicircle, centre on real axis | $R_s(R_{ct}C_{dl})$ | Single charge-transfer controlled process (clean metal) | | Depressed semicircle | $R_s(R_{ct}\,CPE)$ | Surface roughness, inhomogeneous coating, distributed $R_{ct}$ | | Semicircle + $45°$ line at low $f$ | $R_s(R_{ct}C_{dl})Z_W$ | Mixed kinetic + diffusion control | | Two semicircles | $R_s(C_{coat}R_{coat})(C_{dl}R_{ct})$ | Coated metal — coating layer + metal interface | | Vertical line at low $f$ | $R_sC$ | Blocking / ideally polarizable electrode | | Inductive loop at low $f$ | $R_s(R_{ct}C_{dl})(LR_{ads})$ | Adsorbed intermediate, surface relaxation | | Small loop at high $f$ | $R_s(R_{film}C_{film})(R_{ct}C_{dl})$ | Oxide film on passive metal | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 EIS Experimental Setup — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Potentiostat Sig[Signal Generator
Sine Wave 5mV] --> Sum[Summer] Sum --> Amp[Current Amplifier] Amp --> WE end subgraph Cell[Electrochemical Cell] WE[Working Electrode
Corroding Metal] CE[Counter Electrode
Pt Mesh] RE[Reference Electrode
SCE or AgAgCl] WE --- El[Electrolyte] CE --- El RE --- El end Amp --> Cell Cell --> Rec[Phase Sensitive
Detector Lock in Amp] Rec --> DAQ[Data Acquisition
10 mHz to 100 kHz] DAQ --> Fit[Equivalent Circuit
Fitting ZView or NOVA] Fit --> Out[Nyquist and Bode
Plots Rs Rct Cdl] Rec --> Rec ``` ## 4.2 Frequency Sweep Strategy in EIS ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start at High f
100 kHz]) --> M1[Measure Z at 100 kHz
Rs dominates] M1 --> Sweep[Sweep Logarithmically
Downward] Sweep --> M2[Mid f Range
10 Hz to 1 kHz
Charge Transfer Loop] M2 --> M3[Low f Range
10 mHz to 1 Hz
Diffusion or Coating] M3 --> Stop([End of Sweep
Data Ready for Fitting]) Sweep -->|5 to 10 points per decade| Sweep ``` ## 4.3 Randles Equivalent Circuit — Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR In([Input AC Signal]) --> Rs[R_s
Solution Resistance] Rs --> N1((Node 1)) N1 --> Rct[R_ct
Charge Transfer R] Rct --> N2((Node 2)) N1 --> Cdl[C_dl
Double Layer Capacitor] Cdl --> N2 N2 --> Zw[Z_W
Warburg Impedance] Zw --> Out([To Ground]) Rct -.-> N2 ``` ## 4.4 Decision Tree for Identifying Corrosion Mechanism from Nyquist Plot ```mermaid flowchart TD Plot[Nyquist Plot Recorded] --> Check{High freq intercept Z real axis} Check -->|Rs present| HasRs[Solution Resistance Identified] Check -->|Origin| NoRs[Two Electrode Setup or Rs Negligible] HasRs --> Loop1{One or Two Loops} Loop1 -->|One| Single[Single Time Constant
Bare Metal
R_s R_ct C_dl] Loop1 -->|Two| Double[Two Time Constants
Coated or Filmed Metal
R_s R_coat C_coat R_ct C_dl] Single --> Tail{Low freq tail 45 deg?} Tail -->|Yes| Diff[Add Warburg Z_W
Diffusion Controlled] Tail -->|No| Kinetic[Pure Charge Transfer Control] Double --> Coating[Coating Degradation Studied
R_coat indicates barrier property] Coating --> CoatingQuality{C_dl vs initial} CoatingQuality -->|Increasing| Pore[Pore Formation in Coating] CoatingQuality -->|Stable| Good[Coating Intact] ``` ## 4.5 Bode Plot Annotation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BodeMod[Log Z vs Log f] H[High f
Rs plateau
Phase near 0] --- M[Mid f
Slope 1
Phase Peak] M --- L[Low f
Rct Rs plateau
Phase near 0] end subgraph BodePhase[Phase vs Log f] H2[High f
Phase 0] --- M2[Mid f
Phase peak near 90 deg] M2 --- L2[Low f
Phase 0 again] end BodeMod --> BodePhase ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visualization Note:** In an ideal RC system, the Bode magnitude plot shows a slope of **−1 (in log–log scale)** in the mid-frequency region, and the phase plot peaks at exactly **−90°** at the characteristic frequency $f_c = 1/(2\pi R_{ct}C_{dl})$. Real systems show shallower slopes and phase peaks between **−60° and −80°** due to CPE behaviour. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]** ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **Define Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy. What are the advantages of using a small amplitude AC perturbation (5–20 mV) in EIS measurements?** **Model Answer:** EIS is an AC electrochemical technique in which a small-amplitude sinusoidal voltage is applied to an electrochemical system and the resulting current response is measured over a range of frequencies. The complex impedance $Z = V/I$ is computed as a function of frequency. **Advantages of small amplitude:** - The system remains in the **linear regime** (linearity assumption holds), allowing the use of Ohm's law in complex form. - The **steady-state** of the corrosion process is not disturbed — non-destructive technique. - The signal-to-noise ratio is improved by **lock-in amplification**. - The Kramers–Kronig transform validity is maintained, enabling consistency checks. > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **What is a Constant Phase Element (CPE)? Under what circumstances is it used instead of an ideal capacitor in corrosion studies?** **Model Answer:** A

topics.title: Thermogravimetric Technique
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermogravimetric Technique

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA)** is a branch of thermal analysis in which the **mass of a specimen** is measured as a function of **temperature** or **time** while the specimen is subjected to a **controlled temperature programme** (heating, cooling, or isothermal hold) under a defined atmosphere (inert, oxidising, or reducing).

According to the **International Confederation for Thermal Analysis and Calorimetry (ICTAC)** nomenclature, the recorded output is the **thermogravimetric (TG) curve**, plotting **mass (m) versus temperature (T)** or **mass versus time (t)**. The first derivative, **dm/dT** or **dm/dt**, yields the **Derivative Thermogravimetric (DTG) curve**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Corrosion Engineering, TGA is classified as a **non-electrochemical technique** used specifically to study **high-temperature oxidation, sulphidation, and hot-corrosion kinetics** of metals and alloys — falling under Module 3: "Corrosion Measurement and Monitoring Techniques."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The parameter directly obtained from TGA is the **mass change (Δm)**, which in corrosion studies is conventionally reported as **mass gain per unit area (mg cm⁻²)** or in **g m⁻² h⁻¹** for rate calculations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Continuous Weighing Scale Under Fire"

Imagine placing a piece of rusted iron on a digital weighing scale and then slowly heating it in a furnace. The weighing scale is connected to a computer that records the weight **continuously** every second while the temperature is steadily rising.

- If the iron **gains mass** (oxidation, scale formation), the curve goes **up**.
- If the iron **loses mass** (oxide scale spallation, volatile corrosion products, dehydration), the curve goes **down**.
- If nothing happens at a given temperature, the curve is **flat (plateau)**.

That continuous record is exactly what a thermogravimetric analyser produces. The TGA instrument is essentially an **ultra-precise balance (sensitivity ≈ 0.1 µg)** sitting inside a programmable furnace.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Universal gas constant | R | **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** |
| Sensitivity of TGA balance | — | **0.1 µg to 1 µg** |
| Heating rate (β) | β | **1 to 20 °C min⁻¹** (typical), up to **100 °C min⁻¹** |
| Sample mass | m₀ | **5 to 20 mg** (standard) |
| Atmosphere | — | **Air, O₂, N₂, Ar, H₂** (purge gas flow 30–100 mL min⁻¹) |
| Temperature range | T | **Ambient to 1500 °C** (standard), up to **2400 °C** (specialised) |

> [!TIP]
> For **KTU board exams**, always mention the **heating rate β (in °C min⁻¹ or K min⁻¹)** along with the **purge gas** whenever describing a TGA experiment — these are the two most-asked descriptive parameters.

## 1.4 Position of TGA in the Corrosion Engineer's Toolkit

TGA complements but does **not replace** electrochemical techniques:

| Technique | What it measures | Best for |
|---|---|---|
| Potentiodynamic Polarisation | Current density (i) | Aqueous corrosion rate, Tafel slopes |
| EIS | Impedance (Z) | Coating evaluation, mechanism study |
| **TGA (this topic)** | **Mass change (Δm)** | **High-temperature gas-phase corrosion, oxide growth kinetics** |
| Salt-spray test | Visual rating | Comparative ranking of coatings |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The TGA experiment follows a **six-stage logical workflow**:

1. **Sample Preparation:** A small, representative mass (typically 5–20 mg) of the metal, alloy, or pre-oxidised coupon is placed in a **crucible** (platinum, alumina, or quartz), which is suspended from one arm of a **microbalance**.

2. **Atmosphere Control:** A continuous flow of **purge gas** (oxidising, inert, or reducing) is maintained at a controlled rate to either simulate service conditions or to prevent unwanted side reactions.

3. **Temperature Programme:** The furnace is programmed to one of three modes:
   - **Isothermal mode:** Temperature held constant (T = constant); mass recorded vs. time.
   - **Linear non-isothermal mode:** Temperature ramped at a fixed rate β = dT/dt (constant); mass recorded vs. T.
   - **Modulated or step-mode:** Combines isothermal holds with linear ramps.

4. **Continuous Mass Sensing:** The balance generates an analogue/digital signal proportional to the instantaneous mass. Modern instruments use **null-type or top-loading electronic microbalances** with electromagnetic force compensation.

5. **Data Acquisition:** The signal is logged as a function of T (or t) at sampling rates of typically 1–10 Hz, producing the **TG curve**.

6. **Interpretation:** Features of the TG curve (slopes, plateaus, inflections) are correlated with physical/chemical events: **oxidation, reduction, decomposition, phase transformation, volatilisation, scale spallation**.

## 2.2 The Core Physics — Why Mass Changes During High-Temperature Corrosion

When a metal **M** is exposed to a reactive gas (e.g., O₂, S₂, Cl₂) at elevated temperature, the surface forms a solid corrosion product (scale):

$$M_{(s)} + \tfrac{1}{2}O_{2(g)} \rightarrow MO_{(s)}$$

- If **MO is solid and adherent**, mass **increases** with time → the TG curve rises.
- If the scale **decomposes** or **volatilises** (e.g., MoO₃ sublimes above 795 °C, CrO₃ above 200 °C), mass **decreases**.
- If the scale **spalls off** the crucible (poor adhesion), an abrupt **mass drop** is seen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **rate of mass change (dm/dt)** is the **direct experimental proxy for the instantaneous corrosion rate** in high-temperature gas-phase systems, replacing the electrochemical current density used in aqueous corrosion.

## 2.3 Kinetic Rate Laws from TGA — The Heart of the Topic

The most commonly encountered **kinetic rate laws** for high-temperature oxidation (derived from TGA data) are summarised below.

### 2.3.1 Linear Rate Law

$$(\Delta m/A) = k_{l}\,t$$

- Observed when the **scale is porous or non-protective** (e.g., alkali metals, some transition metals above their breakdown temperature).
- The **rate constant k_l** has units of **g cm⁻² s⁻¹** (or mg cm⁻² h⁻¹).

### 2.3.2 Parabolic Rate Law (Most Common for Protective Scales)

$$(\Delta m/A)^{2} = k_{p}\,t$$

- Applies when **diffusion of reactants through a growing compact scale** is rate-limiting (Wagner's theory of oxidation).
- This is the **default answer** in KTU exams for Fe, Ni, Co, Cr alloys forming Cr₂O₃, Al₂O₃, SiO₂ scales.
- Units of k_p: **g² cm⁻⁴ s⁻¹**.

### 2.3.3 Logarithmic and Inverse-Logarithmic Laws

For **thin films at low temperatures** (initial oxide formation < 100 nm):

$$\Delta m/A = k_{\log}\,\ln(t + c) \quad \text{or} \quad A/( \Delta m ) = B - k_{i}\,\ln(t)$$

### 2.3.4 Cubic Rate Law

$$(\Delta m/A)^{3} = k_{c}\,t$$

- Sometimes observed in **intermediate temperature ranges** for NiO and Fe₃O₄ growth.

### 2.3.5 Arrhenius Temperature Dependence

Every rate constant k obeys:

$$k = A\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{a}}{RT}\right)$$

- **A** = pre-exponential factor
- **E_a** = activation energy (J mol⁻¹ or kJ mol⁻¹)
- **R** = **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**
- **T** = absolute temperature (K)

A plot of **ln k vs. 1/T** (Arrhenius plot) is a straight line of slope **−E_a/R**, from which **E_a** is calculated.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Equation | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear rate | $\Delta m/A = k_{l}\,t$ | g cm⁻² s⁻¹ | Non-protective, porous scales |
| Parabolic rate | $(\Delta m/A)^{2} = k_{p}\,t$ | g² cm⁻⁴ s⁻¹ | Diffusion-controlled, protective scales (most common) |
| Cubic rate | $(\Delta m/A)^{3} = k_{c}\,t$ | g³ cm⁻⁶ s⁻¹ | Intermediate-temperature NiO, Fe₃O₄ |
| Logarithmic | $\Delta m/A = k_{\log}\ln(t+c)$ | g cm⁻² | Thin initial films, low T |
| Arrhenius | $k = A\exp(-E_{a}/RT)$ | varies | Temperature dependence of rate |
| Slope for E_a | $\text{slope} = -E_{a}/R$ | K | From ln k vs. 1/T plot |
| Heating rate | $\beta = dT/dt$ | K min⁻¹ | Non-isothermal TGA |
| Total mass change | $\Delta m = m_{\text{final}} - m_{\text{initial}}$ | mg or µg | Read directly from TG curve |
| DTG peak temperature | $T_{p}$ (°C) | — | Most rapid mass-loss point |
| Fractional conversion | $\alpha = (m_{0}-m)/(m_{0}-m_{\infty})$ | dimensionless | Kinetic modelling |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Mark-Loss Pitfall:** Students frequently write $(\Delta m)^{2} = k_{p}\,t$ **without normalising by surface area A**. The parabolic law is **strictly** a statement about *mass gain per unit area*. Always include $(\Delta m/A)^{2}$ in your final expression.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Power-plant boiler tube alloys:** TGA simulates steam-oxidation and fireside corrosion to rank candidate alloys (e.g., TP347H, Super304H).
2. **Gas-turbine superalloys:** TGA determines the **activation energy** of aluminide and MCrAlY-coating oxidation to predict service life at 900–1050 °C.
3. **Nuclear cladding materials:** TGA measures Zircaloy steam-oxidation kinetics under LOCA conditions.
4. **Refinery and petrochemical:** Sulphidation rates of Fe-base alloys in H₂/H₂S atmospheres at 300–600 °C.
5. **Coating selection:** Comparing mass gains of aluminised, chromised, and siliconised coatings in molten-salt environments.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Parabolic Rate Constant from TGA Data

**Statement:** For a metal M oxidising parabolically in a thermogravimetric analyser, show that the slope of a plot of $(\Delta m/A)^{2}$ versus time $t$ equals the parabolic rate constant $k_p$.

**Starting point (definition of the parabolic law):**

$$(\Delta m/A)^{2} = k_{p}\,t$$

where $\Delta m = m(t) - m_0$ and $A$ is the geometric surface area of the coupon in cm².

**Step 1:** Rearrange into the linear (straight-line) form suitable for plotting.

$$y = k_{p}\,x$$

**Step 2:** Identify the dependent and independent variables for graphical work.

$$y = (\Delta m/A)^{2} \quad \text{(y-axis, in g² cm⁻⁴)}$$
$$x = t \quad \text{(x-axis, in seconds)}$$

**Step 3:** Reading the slope of the straight line passing through the origin.

$$\text{slope} = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{(\Delta m/A)^{2}}{t} = k_{p}$$

**Step 4:** The intercept is theoretically zero (parabolic law implies zero mass change at zero time), but in practice a small offset $c$ is sometimes added to account for **initial transient oxidation**.

$$(\Delta m/A)^{2} = k_{p}\,t + c$$

**Step 5:** Report the final answer in standard KTU-exam form.

$$\boxed{\,k_{p} = \text{slope of } (\Delta m/A)^{2} \text{ vs. } t\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU boards award **full marks** only when the student explicitly states the **units of k_p (g² cm⁻⁴ s⁻¹)** and notes that the **intercept should be zero** for an ideal diffusion-controlled process.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Activation Energy from Multiple Isothermal TGA Runs

**Statement:** A corrosion engineer performs isothermal TGA oxidation experiments on a Ni-base superalloy at four temperatures. Derive the procedure to obtain the **activation energy E_a** from the resulting parabolic rate constants.

**Step 1 — At each temperature T_i, compute the parabolic rate constant k_{p,i}** using Derivation 1.

$$k_{p,i} = \text{slope}_i \quad (i = 1, 2, 3, 4)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Arrhenius equation.**

$$k_{p,i} = A\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{a}}{R\,T_i}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$\ln k_{p,i} = \ln A - \dfrac{E_{a}}{R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T_i}$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the linear form.**

$$\underbrace{\ln k_{p,i}}_{y} = \underbrace{\ln A}_{\text{intercept}} \;+\; \underbrace{\left(-\dfrac{E_{a}}{R}\right)}_{\text{slope}}\;\cdot\;\underbrace{\dfrac{1}{T_i}}_{x}$$

**Step 5 — Plot $\ln k_{p,i}$ on the y-axis vs. $1/T_i$ on the x-axis (in K⁻¹).** The data should fall on a straight line.

**Step 6 — Determine the slope m of the best-fit line (using least-squares if numerical).**

$$m = -\dfrac{E_{a}}{R}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for E_a.**

$$\boxed{\,E_{a} = -m\,R\,}$$

where $m$ is the slope (in K) and $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹.

**Step 8 — Example numerical illustration:** Suppose the slope $m = -18{,}500$ K. Then:

$$E_{a} = -(-18{,}500)(8.314) = 1.538\times 10^{5}\ \text{J mol}^{-1} \approx 153.8\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A value of **E_a ≈ 150–250 kJ mol⁻¹** is typical for **Cr₂O₃- and Al₂O₃-scale growth** on Ni-base superalloys. KTU examiners expect students to **comment on the physical meaning** of the calculated value.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Linear Heating Rate (Non-Isothermal) Kinetic Analysis

**Statement:** In a non-isothermal TGA run with constant heating rate $\beta = dT/dt$, derive the relationship between the instantaneous rate of mass change and the temperature at which a particular event occurs.

**Step 1 — Define the heating rate.**

$$\beta = \dfrac{dT}{dt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad dt = \dfrac{dT}{\beta}$$

**Step 2 — Express the rate of mass change with respect to temperature (the DTG signal).**

$$\dfrac{dm}{dT} = \dfrac{dm/dt}{dT/dt} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\beta}$$

**Step 3 — The instantaneous corrosion rate (dm/dt) is the product of the DTG signal and the heating rate.**

$$\dot{m} = \beta \cdot \left(\dfrac{dm}{dT}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Substituting into the Arrhenius expression for the rate:**

$$\beta\cdot\dfrac{dm}{dT} = A\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{a}}{R\,T}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Rearranging into an integrable form for kinetic analysis (Kissinger-style):**

$$\dfrac{dm}{m_{0}-m} = \dfrac{A}{\beta}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{a}}{R\,T}\right) dT$$

**Step 6 — Integrating from the onset of the event to the peak temperature T_p:**

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{T_{p}^{2}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A\,R}{E_{a}}\right) - \dfrac{E_{a}}{R\,T_{p}}$$

**Step 7 — A plot of $\ln(\beta/T_{p}^{2})$ vs. $1/T_p$** at different heating rates gives a straight line of slope $-E_a/R$.

$$\boxed{\,E_{a} = -R \cdot \text{slope of } \ln(\beta/T_{p}^{2})\ \text{vs.}\ 1/T_{p}\,}$$

This is the **Kissinger method**, frequently used in corrosion-product decomposition studies.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)

**Problem:** A steel coupon of area 10 cm² is oxidised isothermally in air at 700 °C in a TGA. The mass gains recorded are:

| Time (h) | Mass gain Δm (mg) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 1.20 |
| 4 | 1.70 |
| 6 | 2.08 |
| 8 | 2.40 |

**Verify the parabolic law and calculate k_p.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Normalise by area: $A = 10$ cm², so $\Delta m/A$ is in mg cm⁻².

| t (h) | Δm/A (mg cm⁻²) | (Δm/A)² (mg² cm⁻⁴) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.000 | 0.0000 |
| 2 | 0.120 | 0.0144 |
| 4 | 0.170 | 0.0289 |
| 6 | 0.208 | 0.0433 |
| 8 | 0.240 | 0.0576 |

**Step 2:** Plot $(\Delta m/A)^{2}$ vs. $t$. Using a linear regression, the slope is approximately:

$$k_{p} = \dfrac{0.0576 - 0}{8 - 0} = 7.20\times 10^{-3}\ \text{mg}^{2}\,\text{cm}^{-4}\,\text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to SI-equivalent units if required:

$$k_{p} = 7.20\times 10^{-3}\ \text{mg}^{2}\,\text{cm}^{-4}\,\text{h}^{-1} \times \left(\dfrac{1\ \text{g}}{1000\ \text{mg}}\right)^{2} \times \left(\dfrac{1\ \text{h}}{3600\ \text{s}}\right)$$

$$k_{p} = 2.00\times 10^{-12}\ \text{g}^{2}\,\text{cm}^{-4}\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4:** Since $(\Delta m/A)^{2}$ is linearly proportional to $t$, **the parabolic law is confirmed**. The protective scale (likely Fe₂O₃/Fe₃O₄) obeys diffusion-controlled growth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** When asked to "verify" a rate law, you must show (a) the **rearranged linearised form** of the equation, (b) the **tabulated data**, and (c) a **clear statement** that the linear relationship is observed (with a numerical value of the slope or rate constant).

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Instrumental Architecture of a Modern TGA

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["SAMPLE COUPLE"] --> nodeB["MICRO BALANCE"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["ELECTROMAGNETIC COMPENSATION UNIT"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["DATA ACQUISITION COMPUTER"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["TG CURVE OUTPUT"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["DTG CURVE OUTPUT"]
    nodeH["PURGE GAS CYLINDER"] --> nodeI["GAS FLOW CONTROLLER"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["FURNACE CHAMBER"]
    nodeJ --> nodeB
    nodeK["TEMPERATURE PROGRAMMER"] --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeL["THERMOCOUPLE NEAR SAMPLE"]
    nodeL --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeE
    nodeM["EXHAUST VENT"] --> nodeJ
    style nodeA fill:#FFE4B5
    style nodeF fill:#E0FFE0
    style nodeG fill:#E0FFE0
    style nodeJ fill:#FFD0D0
```

**Description of Flow:**

1. **Sample Coupons (nodeA):** Metal/alloy specimen placed in a crucible suspended from the microbalance arm.
2. **Microbalance (nodeB):** Continuous, high-precision mass sensing (resolution ≈ 0.1 µg).
3. **Electromagnetic Compensation (nodeC):** Maintains the balance at null position; the compensation current is proportional to the mass.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (nodeD):** Converts the analogue balance signal into a digital stream.
5. **Data Acquisition Computer (nodeE):** Plots **TG curve (nodeF)** and computes derivative for **DTG curve (nodeG)**.
6. **Purge Gas System (nodeH → nodeI):** Controls the reactive or inert atmosphere entering the **Furnace Chamber (nodeJ)**.
7. **Temperature Programmer (nodeK) + Thermocouple (nodeL):** Closed-loop control of the sample temperature; signal also fed to the computer for plotting mass vs. T.
8. **Exhaust Vent (nodeM):** Removes reaction gases safely.

## 4.2 Functional Flow — TGA Experiment for a Corrosion Study

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["PREPARE COUPON POLISH WEIGH MEASURE AREA"] --> stepB["LOAD COUPON INTO CRUCIBLE"]
    stepB --> stepC["SET TEMPERATURE PROGRAMME ISOTHERMAL OR RAMP"]
    stepC --> stepD["FLOW PURGE GAS AIR O2 N2 H2S MIXTURE"]
    stepD --> stepE["RECORD MASS VERSUS TIME TG CURVE"]
    stepE --> stepF["PLOT DELTA M BY A VERSUS TIME"]
    stepF --> stepG["LINEARISE TO IDENTIFY RATE LAW"]
    stepG --> stepH["CALCULATE RATE CONSTANT KP OR KL"]
    stepH --> stepI["REPEAT AT MULTIPLE TEMPERATURES"]
    stepI --> stepJ["ARRHENIUS PLOT LNK VERSUS 1 BY T"]
    stepJ --> stepK["DETERMINE ACTIVATION ENERGY EA"]
    style stepA fill:#FFEFD5
    style stepK fill:#90EE90
```

## 4.3 Typical TGA Curve Schematic (Mermaid Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pA["ORIGIN MASS M0 AT T0"] -->|"MASS GAIN OXIDATION"| pB["MASS INCREASES SLOWLY PARABOLIC REGION"]
    pB -->|"MASS PLATEAU PROTECTIVE SCALE"| pC["NEARLY HORIZONTAL SEGMENT"]
    pC -->|"ABRUPT DROP SCALE SPALLATION OR VOLATILISATION"| pD["MASS DECREASE EVENT"]
    pD --> pE["FINAL MASS MF"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> A typical TGA curve for a **parabolic oxidation** shows a **smoothly rising curve** whose slope **decreases with time** (concave-down profile). A **spallation event** appears as a **sudden vertical drop**, and a **protective plateau** appears as a **near-horizontal section**.

## 4.4 Schematic of Data Interpretation Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    qA["RAW TG CURVE MASS VS T"] --> qB["READ INITIAL MASS M0 AND FINAL MASS MF"]
    qB --> qC["COMPUTE DELTA M PER UNIT AREA"]
    qC --> qD{"PLOT DELTA M BY A VERSUS T"}
    qD -->|"STRAIGHT LINE"| qE["LINEAR LAW KL EQUALS SLOPE"]
    qD -->|"PARABOLA CONCAVE DOWN"| qF["PARABOLIC LAW KP EQUALS SLOPE OF DELTA M BY A SQUARED VS T"]
    qD -->|"OTHER CURVATURE"| qG["LOGARITHMIC OR CUBIC FIT"]
    qF --> qH["ARRHENIUS PLOT FOR EA"]
    qE --> qH
    style qA fill:#E6E6FA
    style qH fill:#98FB98
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA). What information does a TG curve provide in a high-temperature corrosion study?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) is a thermal analysis technique in which the **mass of a specimen is continuously measured** as a function of **temperature or time** while the specimen is subjected to a controlled temperature programme under a defined atmosphere (2 marks).

In a high-temperature corrosion study, the TG curve provides:
- The **mass gain per unit area** $\Delta m/A$ (a direct measure of oxidation/sulphidation extent) (0.5 mark).
- The **rate of mass change** $dm/dt$ (related to the instantaneous corrosion rate). (0.5 mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse TGA with **DTA (Differential Thermal Analysis)**. DTA measures *temperature difference*; TGA measures *mass*. Examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this mix-up.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between isothermal and non-isothermal TGA techniques. State one application of each in corrosion studies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Isothermal TGA | Non-isothermal TGA |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature programme | Constant T; mass vs. time recorded | Linear ramp (β = dT/dt constant); mass vs. T recorded |
| Output | Rate constants k_l, k_p at one T | Activation energy E_a from multiple heating rates |
| Application (corrosion) | Long-term oxidation kinetics of alloys at service T (e.g., 700 °C) | Kissinger-style analysis of oxide-scale decomposition temperature (1.5 marks) |

**(1.5 marks for the table, 1.5 marks for the applications.)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **instrumentation of a thermogravimetric analyser**. List the **essential components** and explain the role of the **purge-gas system**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **parabolic rate law** $(\Delta m/A)^{2} = k_p\,t$ for high-temperature oxidation. Show how the **parabolic rate constant k_p** is determined graphically from TGA data. A steel coupon of area 5 cm² is oxidised isothermally at 800 °C. The mass gains at different times are: 0.80 mg (1 h), 1.15 mg (2 h), 1.40 mg (3 h), 1.60 mg (4 h). **Verify the parabolic law and calculate k_p in mg² cm⁻⁴ h⁻¹.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block diagram** (3 marks):

```
Purge Gas → Flow Controller → Furnace
                                   ↓
   Sample in Crucible ← Microbalance ← Thermocouple
            ↓                ↓              ↓
        Mass Signal     Compensation    Temperature
            └───────► Data Acquisition Computer ◄────┘
                              ↓
                     TG and DTG curves
```

**Component-wise description (3 marks):**

1. **Microbalance (Electromagnetic type):** Resolution ≈ 0.1 µg; continuously senses the sample mass; uses null-balance principle with electromagnetic force compensation.
2. **Furnace:** Programmable resistance furnace (RT to 1500 °C); uniform hot zone ±1 °C; sample suspended in the hot zone on a thin wire/rod.
3. **Temperature Programmer + Thermocouple:** Closed-loop controller; type-S or type-K thermocouple positioned near (not touching) the sample.
4. **Purge Gas System:** Cylinder, pressure regulator, mass-flow controller (30–100 mL min⁻¹), inlet tubing; gases can be air, O₂, N₂, Ar, or corrosive mixtures like H₂/H₂S or SO₂.
5. **Data Acquisition System:** Computer with A/D converter; plots **TG curve (m vs. T)** and computes **DTG curve (dm/dT or dm/dt)**.

**Role of purge-gas system (1 mark):**
- Establishes a **defined atmosphere** (oxidising, inert, reducing) to simulate service conditions.
- Prevents **back-diffusion** of ambient air that would contaminate the system.
- Carries away **volatile corrosion products** (e.g., MoO₃, CrO₃) safely to the exhaust.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation (3 marks):**

For a metal M forming a **compact, adherent oxide scale** MO, the oxidation rate is governed by **solid-state diffusion** of reactant ions (Mⁿ⁺ or O²⁻) through the growing scale.

Let $x$ be the scale thickness at time $t$. By Fick's first law, the flux $J$ is inversely proportional to $x$:

$$J = -D\,\dfrac{dC}{dx} \propto \dfrac{1}{x}$$

The growth rate of the scale is therefore:

$$\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{k'}{x}$$

Integrating from $t=0$ (where $x=0$):

$$x^{2} = 2k'\,t \quad \text{or} \quad x^{2} = k''\,t$$

Since the mass gain per unit area is proportional to the scale thickness ($\Delta m/A = \rho\,x\,M_{O}/M_{MO}$, where $\rho$ is the scale density):

$$(\Delta m/A)^{2} = k_{p}\,t$$

**Graphical determination (1 mark):**
- Plot $(\Delta m/A)^{2}$ on y-axis vs. $t$ on x-axis.
- The slope of the best-fit straight line = $k_p$.

**Numerical verification (3 marks):**

| $t$ (h) | $\Delta m$ (mg) | $\Delta m/A$ (mg cm⁻²) | $(\Delta m/A)^{2}$ (mg² cm⁻⁴) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 0.80 | 0.160 | 0.0256 |
| 2 | 1.15 | 0.230 | 0.0529 |
| 3 | 1.40 | 0.280 | 0.0784 |
| 4 | 1.60 | 0.320 | 0.1024 |

Plotting $(\Delta m/A)^{2}$ vs. $t$ gives a **straight line** passing through the origin (within experimental error). **Parabolic law verified.**

Slope $k_p$ (using endpoints 0 and 4 h):

$$k_{p} = \dfrac{0.1024 - 0}{4 - 0} = 0.0256\ \text{mg}^{2}\,\text{cm}^{-4}\,\text{h}^{-1}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** Many students plot $\Delta m$ vs. $\sqrt{t}$ instead of $(\Delta m/A)^2$ vs. $t$. Both should give a straight line, but **only the $(\Delta m/A)^2$ vs. $t$ plot yields $k_p$ as the slope**. Mark deduction: 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** What is the **Arrhenius-type temperature dependence** of the parabolic rate constant? Derive the expression used to obtain the **activation energy E_a** of the oxidation process from isothermal TGA experiments conducted at multiple temperatures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The parabolic rate constants for the oxidation of a Ni-base superalloy are determined as:

| Temperature (°C) | k_p (g² cm⁻⁴ s⁻¹) |
|---|---|
| 700 | $1.20 \times 10^{-12}$ |
| 800 | $5.80 \times 10^{-12}$ |
| 900 | $2.10 \times 10^{-11}$ |
| 1000 | $6.40 \times 10^{-11}$ |

**Calculate the activation energy E_a in kJ mol⁻¹.** Comment on the **type of oxide scale** likely formed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Arrhenius statement (2 marks):**

The parabolic rate constant $k_p$ varies with absolute temperature $T$ according to the Arrhenius equation:

$$k_{p} = A\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{a}}{R\,T}\right)$$

where $A$ is the pre-exponential factor, $E_a$ is the activation energy, $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹, and $T$ is the absolute temperature.

**Derivation of graphical procedure (3 marks):**

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln k_{p} = \ln A - \dfrac{E_{a}}{R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T}$$

Rearranged into the straight-line form $y = mx + c$:

$$\underbrace{\ln k_{p}}_{y} \;=\; \underbrace{\ln A}_{\text{intercept}}\;+\;\underbrace{\left(-\dfrac{E_{a}}{R}\right)}_{\text{slope}}\cdot\underbrace{\dfrac{1}{T}}_{x}$$

**Procedure:**
1. Perform isothermal TGA at several temperatures (e.g., 600, 700, 800, 900 °C).
2. Determine $k_p$ at each $T$ from the $(\Delta m/A)^2$ vs. $t$ plot.
3. Convert each $T$ to Kelvin: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273$.
4. Plot $\ln k_p$ on the y-axis vs. $1/T$ (in K⁻¹) on the x-axis.
5. Determine the slope $m$ of the best-fit straight line.
6. Calculate $E_a$:

$$\boxed{\,E_{a} = -m \cdot R\,}$$

(2 marks for the complete procedure.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Tabulate data and compute $\ln k_p$ and $1/T$ (2 marks):**

| T (°C) | T (K) | $1/T$ (K⁻¹) | $k_p$ (g² cm⁻⁴ s⁻¹) | $\ln k_p$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 700 | 973 | $1.028 \times 10^{-3}$ | $1.20 \times 10^{-12}$ | $-27.446$ |
| 800 | 1073 | $9.320 \times 10^{-4}$ | $5.80 \times 10^{-12}$ | $-25.867$ |
| 900 | 1173 | $8.525 \times 10^{-4}$ | $2.10 \times 10^{-11}$ | $-24.588$ |
| 1000 | 1273 | $7.856 \times 10^{-4}$ | $6.40 \times 10^{-11}$ | $-23.473$ |

**Determine slope of $\ln k_p$ vs. $1/T$ (3 marks):**

Using the endpoints (700 °C and 1000 °C):

$$m = \dfrac{\ln k_{p}(1000\,°C) - \ln k_{p}(700\,°C)}{1/T_{1000} - 1/T_{700}}$$

$$m = \dfrac{-23.473 - (-27.446)}{7.856\times 10^{-4} - 1.028\times 10^{-3}}$$

$$m = \dfrac{3.973}{-2.424\times 10^{-4}} = -1.639\times 10^{4}\ \text{K}$$

**Calculate E_a (1 mark):**

$$E_{a} = -m \cdot R = -(-1.639\times 10^{4})(8.314) = 1.363\times 10^{5}\ \text{J mol}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{a} \approx 136.3\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}\,}$$

**Comment on the oxide scale (1 mark):**

An activation energy of **~136 kJ mol⁻¹** is characteristic of **diffusion-controlled growth of a chromia (Cr₂O₃) or alumina (Al₂O₃) protective scale**, typical of Ni-base superalloys containing significant Cr and Al. The value is consistent with **outward diffusion of Cr³⁺ (or Al³⁺) cations** through the compact scale being the rate-limiting step.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** A common mistake is forgetting to convert °C → K before computing $1/T$. Always write $T(K) = T(°C) + 273$. Deduct **1 mark** for unit error in $1/T$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TGA = mass vs. temperature/time under controlled atmosphere.** It is the **primary non-electrochemical tool** for high-temperature corrosion kinetics.
- The **TG curve** is the raw output; the **DTG curve** is its first derivative (rate).
- The **most common rate law** in high-temperature oxidation is the **parabolic law** $(\Delta m/A)^2 = k_p t$, valid for **diffusion-controlled, protective-scale growth** (Wagner mechanism).
- Other rate laws — **linear, cubic, logarithmic** — apply to porous, intermediate-temperature, or thin-film regimes respectively.
- The **rate constant** follows an **Arrhenius temperature dependence**: $k = A\exp(-E_a/RT)$.
- **Activation energy $E_a$** is obtained from the **slope of $\ln k$ vs. $1/T$**: $E_a = -(\text{slope}) \times R$.
- A typical $E_a$ of **150–250 kJ mol⁻¹** indicates **Cr₂O₃/Al₂O₃ scale growth**; **50–100 kJ mol⁻¹** suggests **Fe-oxide (wüstite/magnetite) scale**.
- **Always normalise** mass change by **surface area**: report $\Delta m/A$ in **mg cm⁻²** (or g m⁻²).
- **Purge gas** must be specified (air, O₂, N₂, Ar, H₂S, SO₂) — it defines the corrosion environment.
- **Heating rate $\beta$** must be quoted in **°C min⁻¹ or K min⁻¹** for any non-isothermal TGA.
- The **Kissinger equation** $\ln(\beta/T_p^2) = \text{const} - E_a/(R T_p)$ is the **standard non-isothermal method** for finding $E_a$ from peak temperatures.
- **TGA complements** (not replaces) electrochemical techniques such as **polarisation and EIS** — they address **different corrosion regimes** (high-T gaseous vs. ambient aqueous).
- **Common KTU exam traps:** mixing TGA with DTA; omitting the $/A$ (area normalisation); using $°C$ instead of $K$ in $1/T$; mis-stating the units of $k_p$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Salt Spray Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Salt Spray Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Salt Spray Test (SST)**, also referred to as the **Salt Fog Test**, is a standardized accelerated corrosion testing method employed to evaluate the relative corrosion resistance of metallic materials, protective coatings, surface treatments, and finished products when exposed to a controlled saline environment. The test is conducted inside a sealed enclosed chamber where a **5 wt% NaCl solution** is atomized into a fine mist and maintained at a constant elevated temperature (typically **35 °C ± 2 °C** for neutral salt spray).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Governing Standards:**
> - **ASTM B117** – Standard Practice for Operating Salt Spray (Fog) Apparatus
> - **ISO 9227** – Corrosion tests in artificial atmospheres – Salt spray tests
> - **JIS Z 2371** – Japanese Industrial Standard for Salt Spray Testing
> - **ASTM G85** – Modified Salt Spray Practices (for acidic variants)

The test fundamentally does **not** represent real-world service conditions. Instead, it provides a **controlled, reproducible, and accelerated** means to compare the anti-corrosive performance of similar materials or coating systems under identical aggressive chloride-ion exposure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Accelerated Beach Experiment"**

Imagine placing a freshly painted iron gate at a beach. Over months, the salty sea breeze causes it to rust rapidly. A salt spray test essentially **simulates years of beachside exposure in just a few hundred hours** inside a small laboratory chamber.

Think of the salt spray chamber as a **"weather simulation machine"**:
- The **salt mist** represents ocean aerosols and chloride deposition.
- The **heated chamber** acts as a tropical, humid climate accelerator.
- The **specimens** are the "guinea pigs" being subjected to this artificial but uniform hostile environment.

Just as a doctor runs a stress test on a heart to detect weaknesses, the salt spray test is a **"corrosion stress test"** — designed to expose weak points, coating defects, and metallurgical vulnerabilities quickly.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Test Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| NaCl Concentration | **5 ± 1 wt%** in deionized water |
| pH of Collected Solution | **6.5 – 7.2** (neutral salt spray) |
| Chamber Temperature | **35 °C ± 2 °C** |
| Solution Temperature | **33 °C – 35 °C** |
| Air Pressure (atomization) | **0.7 – 1.4 kg/cm²** |
| Fog Collection Rate | **1.0 – 2.0 mL/hr per 80 cm²** |
| Test Duration | **24, 48, 96, 240, 480, 1000, 2000 hrs** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principles (Step-by-Step Logic)

The salt spray test functions on the principle of **accelerated electrochemical corrosion under controlled chloride-rich atmospheric conditions**. The operational logic is as follows:

- **Step 1 — Solution Preparation:** A 5 wt% sodium chloride (NaCl) solution is prepared using reagent-grade NaCl dissolved in distilled or deionized water. The pH is adjusted to **6.5 – 7.2** using dilute NaOH or HCl.

- **Step 2 — Atomization:** Compressed clean air is passed through a humidifier and then through a spray nozzle, which atomizes the salt solution into a fine, dense fog of microscopic droplets (typically **1 – 5 μm diameter**).

- **Step 3 — Chamber Environment Maintenance:** The chamber temperature is held at **35 °C ± 2 °C**. The fog is dispersed uniformly so that it settles on the specimens via gravity. A salt solution reservoir maintains continuous atomization.

- **Step 4 — Specimen Exposure:** Test panels are placed at a **15° to 30° angle** from the vertical (typically **20°**) to prevent direct drip runoff while ensuring even fog deposition. Specimens are exposed for predetermined durations.

- **Step 5 — Periodic Evaluation:** At defined intervals, specimens are removed and evaluated for:
  - **Visual inspection** (blistering, rusting, coating loss)
  - **Adhesion testing** (tape test, cross-hatch)
  - **Creep from scribe** (if scribe mark is present)
  - **Mass loss measurement**

- **Step 6 — Rating and Documentation:** Results are rated using standardized scales such as **ASTM D610** (rusting), **ASTM D714** (blistering), and **ASTM D1654** (scribe creep).

### 2.2 Why Salt Spray Works — The Electrochemistry Behind It

The accelerated corrosion occurs via:
$$\text{NaCl (aq)} \rightarrow \text{Na}^+ + \text{Cl}^-$$

The chloride ion (Cl⁻) is a **highly aggressive anion** that:
1. **Penetrates passive films** (e.g., breaks down Cr₂O₃ on stainless steel).
2. **Disrupts protective coatings** through osmotic blistering.
3. **Increases electrolyte conductivity**, accelerating galvanic micro-cells on the surface.
4. **Forms soluble metal-chloride complexes** (e.g., FeCl₂, FeCl₃), preventing reformation of protective oxide layers.

### 2.3 Types of Salt Spray Tests (KTU High-Yield)

| Test Type | Solution Composition | pH | Temperature | Standard | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **NSS** (Neutral Salt Spray) | 5% NaCl | 6.5 – 7.2 | 35 °C | ASTM B117 | General coatings, Zn, Al |
| **AASS** (Acetic Acid Salt Spray) | 5% NaCl + Glacial Acetic Acid | 3.1 – 3.3 | 35 °C | ISO 9227 | Decorative Cr/Ni coatings |
| **CASS** (Copper-Accelerated Acetic Acid Salt Spray) | 5% NaCl + CuCl₂ + Acetic Acid | 3.1 – 3.3 | 50 °C | ASTM B368 | Cu, Brass, decorative plating |
| **SWAAT** (Sea Water Acidified Accelerated Test) | Synthetic seawater + acetic acid | 2.8 – 3.0 | 49 °C | ASTM G85 | Aerospace aluminum alloys |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to Remember:** "**N**ormal, **A**cidic, **C**atalyzed, **S**ynthetic" — **NACS** — describes the increasing aggressiveness in order.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas critical for KTU 2024 numerical problems on corrosion rate and test evaluations are tabulated below.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$ | Corrosion rate (mass loss method) | mpy or mm/yr |
| 2 | $K = 3.45 \times 10^6$ | Constant for mpy (with W in g, A in cm², T in hr, D in g/cm³) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $K = 87.6$ | Constant for mm/yr (W in mg, A in cm², T in hr, D in g/cm³) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $K = 534$ | Constant for mm/yr (W in mg, A in in², T in hr, D in g/cm³) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_{mass} = \dfrac{m_{salt}}{m_{salt} + m_{water}} \times 100$ | Mass concentration of salt solution | wt% |
| 6 | $\rho_{solution} = \rho_{water} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{C_{mass}}{100}\right)$ (approx.) | Approximate density of dilute NaCl solution | g/cm³ |
| 7 | $\text{Collection rate} = \dfrac{V_{collected}}{A_{funnel} \times t}$ | Fog collection rate verification | mL/hr·cm² |
| 8 | $\text{Creep} = \dfrac{W_{max} - W_{initial}}{2}$ | Average scribe creep rating | mm |
| 9 | $\text{Protection efficiency (\%)} = \dfrac{CR_{uncoated} - CR_{coated}}{CR_{uncoated}} \times 100$ | Coating efficiency | % |
| 10 | $\text{Efficiency (\%)} = \left(1 - \dfrac{T_{coat}}{T_{bare}}\right) \times 100$ | Time-based coating protection | % |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Validates body panels, fasteners, brake components, and underbody coatings.
- **Marine engineering:** Screens hull paints, sacrificial anodes, and offshore structural coatings.
- **Aerospace:** Evaluates aluminum alloy claddings and surface treatments (chromate conversion coatings).
- **Electronics:** Tests connector platings, PCB surface finishes (ENIG, HASL, tin).
- **Construction:** Verifies galvanized steel, powder-coated aluminum extrusions, and architectural fasteners.
- **Defense:** NATO and MIL-STD specifications mandate salt spray compliance for equipment destined for maritime/tropical deployment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Corrosion Rate from Salt Spray Mass Loss Data

**Problem Context:** A galvanized steel specimen of density **$\rho = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$**, exposed area **$A = 50 \text{ cm}^2$**, undergoes a mass loss of **$\Delta W = 0.245 \text{ g}$** after **$T = 240$ hours** of NSS exposure. Determine the corrosion rate in mpy (mils per year) and mm/yr.

**Given:**
- $W = 0.245 \text{ g}$
- $A = 50 \text{ cm}^2$
- $T = 240 \text{ hr}$
- $D = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$

**Step 1 — Identify the correct formula for mpy:**

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$$

where $K = 3.45 \times 10^6$ for mpy when the units specified are used.

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{(3.45 \times 10^6) \times (0.245)}{(50) \times (240) \times (7.85)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 3.45 \times 10^6 \times 0.245 = 845{,}250$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$\text{Denominator} = 50 \times 240 \times 7.85 = 94{,}200$$

**Step 5 — Divide to obtain CR in mpy:**

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{845{,}250}{94{,}200} = 8.97 \text{ mpy}$$

**Step 6 — Convert mpy to mm/yr using the conversion factor $1 \text{ mpy} = 0.0254 \text{ mm/yr}$:**

$$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = 8.97 \times 0.0254 = 0.228 \text{ mm/yr}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\text{CR} = 8.97 \text{ mpy} \approx 0.228 \text{ mm/yr}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Preparation of 5 wt% NaCl Solution

**Problem Context:** A laboratory technician needs to prepare **10 liters** of 5 wt% NaCl solution for the salt spray reservoir. Calculate the required mass of NaCl and water. Assume solution density $\rho_{sol} \approx 1.03 \text{ g/cm}^3$ (typical for 5% NaCl).

**Step 1 — Total mass of 10 L solution:**

$$m_{solution} = V \times \rho_{sol} = 10{,}000 \text{ cm}^3 \times 1.03 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 10{,}300 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of NaCl required (5 wt%):**

$$m_{NaCl} = 0.05 \times 10{,}300 \text{ g} = 515 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of water required:**

$$m_{water} = m_{solution} - m_{NaCl} = 10{,}300 - 515 = 9{,}785 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4 — Verify pH adjustment:**
The pH of fresh 5% NaCl in DI water typically lies between **6.0 – 7.0**. Add small drops of 0.1 N NaOH or HCl to bring within **6.5 – 7.2**.

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{m_{NaCl} = 515 \text{ g}, \quad m_{water} = 9{,}785 \text{ g of distilled water}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Coating Protection Efficiency from Salt Spray Data

**Problem Context:** An uncoated mild steel specimen exhibits corrosion rate **$\text{CR}_{uncoated} = 25.4 \text{ mpy}$** under 480 hours of NSS. A coated version (same base metal, same dimensions) shows **$\text{CR}_{coated} = 1.8 \text{ mpy}$** under identical conditions. Calculate protection efficiency.

**Step 1 — Recall the protection efficiency formula:**

$$\eta (\%) = \dfrac{CR_{uncoated} - CR_{coated}}{CR_{uncoated}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\eta (\%) = \dfrac{25.4 - 1.8}{25.4} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$25.4 - 1.8 = 23.6$$

**Step 4 — Compute ratio:**

$$\dfrac{23.6}{25.4} = 0.9291$$

**Step 5 — Convert to percentage:**

$$\eta = 0.9291 \times 100 = 92.91\%$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\eta = 92.91\% \approx 93\%}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Fog Collection Rate Verification (Chamber Calibration)
