**Problem Context:** A salt spray chamber is calibrated using a **100 cm² horizontal funnel** placed inside. Over **16 hours** of operation, **24 mL** of solution is collected. Is the chamber within ASTM B117 specification (1.0 – 2.0 mL/hr per 80 cm²)?

**Step 1 — Calculate collection rate per cm² per hour:**

$$R = \dfrac{24 \text{ mL}}{100 \text{ cm}^2 \times 16 \text{ hr}} = 0.015 \text{ mL/hr·cm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Normalize to 80 cm² standard:**

$$R_{80} = 0.015 \times 80 = 1.2 \text{ mL/hr per 80 cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compare with ASTM B117 range:**

Since $1.0 \leq 1.2 \leq 2.0$, the chamber **passes calibration**.

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{R = 1.2 \text{ mL/hr per 80 cm}^2 \;\; (\text{Chamber is in compliance})}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Salt Spray Chamber Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compressed Air Supply] --> B[Air Filter and Pressure Regulator]
    B --> C[Humidifier Tower]
    C --> D[Salt Solution Reservoir]
    D --> E[Atomizing Spray Nozzle]
    E --> F[Sealed Test Chamber]
    F --> G[Heated Test Specimens]
    F --> H[Fog Distribution Baffle]
    F --> I[Salt Fog Mist Environment]
    I --> J[Specimen Rack at 20 deg Angle]
    J --> K[Test Panels]
    F --> L[Collection Funnels]
    L --> M[Graduated Cylinders for Calibration]
    F --> N[Temperature Controller 35C]
    F --> O[Exhaust and Drain System]
    P[Operator Console and Timer] --> F
```

### 4.2 Process Flowchart: Salt Spray Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Prepare 5 wt percent NaCl Solution] --> S2[Step 2: Adjust pH to 6.5 to 7.2]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Clean and Mask Test Specimens]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Weigh Initial Mass and Record]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Mount Specimens on Rack at 15 to 30 deg Angle]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Seal Chamber and Set Temperature to 35C]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Start Compressed Air and Atomizer]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Continuous Exposure for Planned Duration]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Periodic Withdrawal for Visual Inspection]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Final Cleaning Drying and Mass Loss Measurement]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Rate Corrosion Using ASTM and ISO Standards]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12: Document Results and Compare with Reference Panels]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Salt Spray Test Standard Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Root: Salt Spray Family] --> N[Neutral Salt Spray NSS]
    R --> AC[Acetic Acid Salt Spray AASS]
    R --> CP[Copper Accelerated CASS]
    R --> SW[Synthetic Sea Water SWAAT]
    N --> A1[ASTM B117]
    N --> I1[ISO 9227 NSS]
    AC --> I2[ISO 9227 AASS]
    CP --> A2[ASTM B368]
    CP --> I3[ISO 9227 CASS]
    SW --> A3[ASTM G85 Annex A3]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** The chamber's core functional loop is **air → humidify → atomize salt → expose specimens → drain/vent**. Any failure in this loop compromises the validity of the test. Always verify atomizer nozzle cleanliness, chamber temperature uniformity, and pH stability before declaring a test valid.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the salt spray test. Mention the standard test conditions of NSS as per ASTM B117.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Salt Spray Test (SST)** is a standardized accelerated corrosion test used to evaluate the corrosion resistance of metals, alloys, and protective coatings by exposing them to a controlled saline fog environment inside a closed chamber.

**Standard NSS Conditions (ASTM B117):**
- **NaCl concentration:** 5 ± 1 wt% in deionized water
- **pH:** 6.5 – 7.2
- **Chamber temperature:** 35 °C ± 2 °C
- **Fog collection rate:** 1.0 – 2.0 mL/hr per 80 cm²
- **Test duration:** As specified (typically 96, 240, 480, or 1000 hours)
- **Specimen angle:** 15° – 30° from vertical

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for conditions)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain the different types of salt spray tests.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **NSS (Neutral Salt Spray):** 5% NaCl, pH 6.5–7.2, 35 °C. Used for general coatings, zinc, and aluminum. (ASTM B117)

2. **AASS (Acetic Acid Salt Spray):** 5% NaCl acidified with glacial acetic acid to pH 3.1–3.3, 35 °C. Used for decorative Cr/Ni coatings. (ISO 9227)

3. **CASS (Copper-Accelerated Acetic Acid Salt Spray):** 5% NaCl + 0.26 g/L CuCl₂ + acetic acid, pH 3.1–3.3, **50 °C**. Used for copper, brass, and decorative platings. (ASTM B368)

4. **SWAAT (Sea Water Acidified Accelerated Test):** Synthetic seawater + acetic acid, pH 2.8–3.0, 49 °C. Used for aerospace aluminum. (ASTM G85)

*(3 marks — 1 mark per test type with condition)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the apparatus, working principle, and standard test conditions of the neutral salt spray test as per ASTM B117. Include a labeled schematic description.

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus Components:**

1. **Salt Spray Chamber:** Sealed, chemically resistant (PVC, polypropylene, or rubber-lined) cabinet with a sloped roof to prevent condensate drip onto specimens.

2. **Air Supply System:** Oil-free compressed air, filtered and pressure-regulated to **0.7–1.4 kg/cm²**.

3. **Humidifier:** Saturates incoming air before atomization to prevent salt crystallization at the nozzle.

4. **Salt Solution Reservoir:** Holds the 5% NaCl solution; connected to the atomizer.

5. **Atomizing Nozzle:** Produces fine droplets (1–5 μm). Typically made of plastic or glass to resist corrosion.

6. **Heating System:** Maintains chamber at **35 °C ± 2 °C** with even distribution (typically with a water jacket or strip heater).

7. **Specimen Rack:** Supports panels at 15°–30° from vertical (commonly 20°). Non-metallic (plastic or rubber-coated) to avoid galvanic contamination.

8. **Collection Devices:** Two funnels (each 80 cm²) placed in the work zone to verify the fog collection rate of **1.0–2.0 mL/hr**.

9. **pH Meter and Thermometer:** For periodic verification of solution pH and chamber temperature.

**Working Principle:**
- Clean, humidified compressed air atomizes the 5% NaCl solution into a fine mist.
- The mist fills the chamber, settling uniformly on the test specimens.
- The chloride-rich electrolyte film on the metal surface drives electrochemical corrosion reactions.
- The elevated temperature accelerates the reaction kinetics (Arrhenius effect).
- Specimens are inspected at defined intervals for signs of corrosion, blistering, and coating degradation.

**Standard Conditions (ASTM B117):**
- Solution: 5% NaCl, pH 6.5–7.2
- Chamber temperature: 35 °C ± 2 °C
- Fog collection: 1.0–2.0 mL/hr per 80 cm²
- Air pressure: 0.7–1.4 kg/cm²
- Specimen angle: 20° ± 5° from vertical

*(7 marks — 2 for apparatus, 2 for working, 2 for conditions, 1 for schematic reference)*

---

**(b) [7 marks]** A galvanized steel specimen (density 7.85 g/cm³) of exposed area 75 cm² loses 0.415 g after 480 hours of NSS exposure. Calculate (i) corrosion rate in mpy, (ii) corrosion rate in mm/yr, and (iii) the protection efficiency if the uncoated base steel has a corrosion rate of 28.6 mpy under identical conditions.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $W = 0.415 \text{ g}$
- $A = 75 \text{ cm}^2$
- $T = 480 \text{ hr}$
- $D = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- $CR_{uncoated} = 28.6 \text{ mpy}$

**Part (i) — Corrosion rate in mpy:**

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{(3.45 \times 10^6) \times W}{A \times T \times D}$$

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{(3.45 \times 10^6) \times (0.415)}{(75) \times (480) \times (7.85)}$$

**Numerator:**
$$3.45 \times 10^6 \times 0.415 = 1{,}431{,}750$$

**Denominator:**
$$75 \times 480 \times 7.85 = 282{,}600$$

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{1{,}431{,}750}{282{,}600} = 5.066 \text{ mpy}$$

**Part (ii) — Corrosion rate in mm/yr:**

Using conversion: $1 \text{ mpy} = 0.0254 \text{ mm/yr}$:

$$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = 5.066 \times 0.0254 = 0.1287 \text{ mm/yr}$$

**Part (iii) — Protection efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{28.6 - 5.066}{28.6} \times 100 = \dfrac{23.534}{28.6} \times 100 = 82.29\%$$

**Final Results:**
- $\text{CR} = 5.07 \text{ mpy}$
- $\text{CR} = 0.129 \text{ mm/yr}$
- $\eta = 82.29\%$

*(7 marks — 2 each for parts i, ii, iii, 1 for final summary)*

---

### **OR (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Compare NSS, AASS, and CASS tests with respect to solution composition, pH, temperature, applications, and relevant standards.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **NSS** | **AASS** | **CASS** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **NaCl** | 5 wt% | 5 wt% | 5 wt% |
| **Acidifier** | None (neutral) | Glacial acetic acid | Glacial acetic acid |
| **Accelerator** | None | None | CuCl₂ (0.26 g/L) |
| **pH** | 6.5 – 7.2 | 3.1 – 3.3 | 3.1 – 3.3 |
| **Temperature** | 35 °C | 35 °C | 50 °C |
| **Standard** | ASTM B117 / ISO 9227 | ISO 9227 | ASTM B368 / ISO 9227 |
| **Application** | Zn, Al, general coatings | Decorative Cr/Ni plating | Cu, brass, decorative plating |
| **Aggressiveness** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |

**Key Notes:**
- NSS is the baseline reference; AASS and CASS are progressively more aggressive accelerated variants.
- The **acetic acid** in AASS and CASS reduces the protective oxide passivity, accelerating attack.
- The **copper chloride** in CASS deposits copper on exposed surfaces, creating additional galvanic microcells.
- **CASS at 50 °C** is approximately **4–8× more aggressive** than NSS at 35 °C for the same coating.
- A coating rated for 1000 hours of NSS may only survive 96–240 hours of CASS.

*(7 marks — 2 for tabulation, 3 for explanations, 2 for comparative analysis)*

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the advantages and limitations of the salt spray test. Why is it often criticized as not representing real service conditions? What alternative tests provide more realistic data?

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Simple and inexpensive** compared to long-term field exposure tests.
2. **Standardized globally** (ASTM, ISO, JIS) — results are reproducible and comparable.
3. **Rapid screening tool** — can differentiate coating systems in days rather than years.
4. **Useful for quality control** in manufacturing (acceptance testing of batches).
5. **Effective for defect detection** — pinholes, coating porosity, and inadequate thickness.
6. **Allows side-by-side comparison** of multiple coating systems under identical conditions.

**Limitations:**
1. **Continuous salt mist is unrealistic** — natural environments have wet/dry cycles.
2. **No UV exposure** — cannot replicate sunlight-driven degradation.
3. **Constant temperature** — real environments fluctuate diurnally and seasonally.
4. **Does not predict service life** quantitatively — only ranks materials relatively.
5. **Pure NaCl fog overestimates chloride aggressiveness** for non-marine environments.
6. **Cannot simulate biological, mechanical, or thermal stresses.**
7. **Highly sensitive to chamber calibration** — small variations produce large result deviations.

**Criticism Summary:**
The salt spray test is essentially a **"highly artificial environment"** designed for *reproducibility* rather than *realism*. Coatings that perform well in SST can fail rapidly outdoors, and vice versa.

**More Realistic Alternative Tests:**
- **Cyclic Corrosion Tests (CCT)** — e.g., ASTM G85, SAE J2334. Combine salt spray, drying, humidity, and UV.
- **Prohesion Test** — alternating dry/wet salt mist.
- **Kesternich Test (SO₂)** — for industrial atmospheres.
- **Field Exposure Tests** — actual outdoor racks (ISO 8565).
- **Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)** — for mechanistic studies.

*(7 marks — 2 for advantages, 3 for limitations and criticism, 2 for alternatives)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** in CR formula — students often mix cm² with in² and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $K$ values** between mpy and mm/yr — $K = 3.45 \times 10^6$ is for mpy, $K = 87.6$ is for mm/yr.
> 3. **Not mentioning specimen angle** in test procedure answers — Examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Mixing up CASS and AASS temperatures** — CASS is **50 °C**, AASS is **35 °C**.
> 5. **Skipping pH range** in standard test conditions — common 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Writing "salt spray simulates real environment"** — this is WRONG; examiners will deduct marks. Always state the test is *accelerated* and *artificial*.
> 7. **Neglecting the role of Cl⁻ ion mechanism** — the theory of chloride attack on passive films is a favorite 4-mark sub-question.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Salt spray test = accelerated, artificial, comparative** — never predictive of real service life.
- **ASTM B117 / ISO 9227** are the foundational standards — know the exact conditions.
- **NSS conditions:** 5% NaCl, pH 6.5–7.2, 35 °C, fog rate 1.0–2.0 mL/hr per 80 cm².
- **Specimens mounted at 20° ± 5° from vertical** to prevent drip runoff.
- **AASS adds acetic acid** (pH 3.1–3.3) at 35 °C for decorative coatings.
- **CASS adds CuCl₂** at 50 °C — most aggressive variant for copper/brass systems.
- **Corrosion rate formulas** (mpy and mm/yr) are the highest-weightage numerical problems.
- **Protection efficiency $\eta$** is calculated from CR reduction relative to uncoated baseline.
- **Chamber calibration** requires periodic fog collection rate verification.
- **Limitations include**: continuous wet exposure, no UV, no cyclic drying, no mechanical stress.
- **Cyclic Corrosion Tests (CCT)** are the modern, more realistic successors.
- **Chloride ions** break down passive films and form soluble metal-chloride complexes.
- **Evaluation standards:** ASTM D610 (rust), D714 (blisters), D1654 (scribe creep).
- **Industries using SST:** Automotive, marine, aerospace, electronics, construction, defense.
- **Common Mnemonic:** **NACS** = Neutral → Acidic → Copper-accelerated → Sea water.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weight Change Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Weight Change Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weight Change Measurement (Mass Loss / Mass Gain Method)** is a classical, destructive, yet highly reliable gravimetric technique employed in corrosion engineering to quantify the extent of metal degradation (or scale/oxide formation) by precisely measuring the change in mass of a coupon specimen before and after controlled exposure to a corrosive environment. The result is expressed as a **corrosion rate (CR)** using standardized units such as **mdd (mg/dm²·day)**, **mpy (mils per year)**, or **mm/yr (millimeters per year)** as per **ASTM G1** and **ASTM G31** conventions.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus (PEPOT523 – Corrosion Engineering, Module 3), this method is classified under **Direct Corrosion Rate Measurement Techniques**, complementing electrochemical methods like Tafel extrapolation and Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Kitchen Scale" Analogy:** Imagine you leave a slice of apple on your kitchen counter for 3 days. It dehydrates and shrinks. If you weighed it on day 1 and then again on day 3, the difference tells you *how much* water (representing "metal that corroded away") was lost per day per unit surface area exposed to air.
>
> In corrosion engineering, we do exactly the same with a metal coupon (a small, polished test piece):
> 1. **Weigh it clean** → Initial mass $W_i$
> 2. **Expose it** to the corrosive environment (acid bath, seawater, atmosphere) for a known time $T$.
> 3. **Remove corrosion products carefully** (chemical cleaning per ASTM G1) and weigh again → Final mass $W_f$.
> 4. The difference $W = W_i - W_f$ is the **mass of metal lost to corrosion**.

This mass loss, when normalized to **exposed area** and **exposure time**, gives a tangible, universally comparable number — the **corrosion rate**.

### 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three universally accepted engineering units of corrosion rate are:
> - **mdd** = milligrams per square decimeter per **day** (mg/dm²·day) → used in process/chemical industries
> - **mpy** = mils per year (1 mil = 0.001 inch) → used in US oil & gas / NACE standards
> - **mm/yr** = millimeters per year (SI) → used globally, KTU preferred unit

The conversion constants (e.g., **534**, **87.6**, **1.437**) appearing in the formulas are not arbitrary — they arise from the algebraic reconciliation of density (g/cm³), area (in² or cm²), time (hr or day), and unit length (mils or mm). These will be derived rigorously in Section 3.

### 1.4 Where Does This Method Sit in the Corrosion Testing Hierarchy?

| Test Type | Nature | Time Required | Information Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Weight Loss (Gravimetric) | Destructive, integral | Days to months | **Average** long-term rate |
| Electrochemical (Tafel, LPR) | Non-destructive | Minutes to hours | **Instantaneous** rate |
| Hydrogen Permeation | Destructive prep | Hours | Diffused H flux |

Weight change remains the **gold standard** against which all electrochemical and indirect methods are benchmarked.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The 7-Stage Workflow

The gravimetric / weight-change experiment, as prescribed by **ASTM G31 (Laboratory Immersion Corrosion Testing of Metals)** and **ASTM G1 (Preparing, Cleaning, and Evaluating Corrosion Test Specimens)**, follows a strict sequence. Each step is non-negotiable in KTU laboratory examinations.

1. **Coupon Preparation (Stage 1):**
   The metal specimen (typically $50 \times 25 \times 2$ mm or 1" × 2" × 1/8") is machined, ground (using SiC papers up to 600-grit finish), degreased with acetone/ethanol, dried, and stored in a desiccator.

2. **Initial Weighing (Stage 2):**
   The cleaned coupon is weighed on an **analytical balance** with precision of $\pm 0.0001$ g. The reading is recorded as $W_i$ (Initial Weight).

3. **Geometric Measurement (Stage 3):**
   The exposed surface area $A$ is measured using a **vernier caliper** or **micrometer**. All faces exposed to the corrodent are included; masked/edges may be ignored or computed.

4. **Exposure (Stage 4):**
   The coupon is suspended (often using a glass hook or Teflon string) in the test solution at a controlled temperature, with a typical **solution volume to area ratio $\geq 20$ mL/cm²** (per ASTM G31) to prevent exhaustion of reactants.

5. **Retrieval & Pre-Cleaning (Stage 5):**
   After time $T$ (in hours or days), the coupon is removed, gently rinsed with water, and dried.

6. **Chemical Cleaning of Corrosion Products (Stage 6):**
   The corrosion product layer is removed using a **specific chemical pickling solution** prescribed in ASTM G1 — e.g., for steel: Clarke's solution (HCl + Sb₂O₃ + SnCl₂) or for aluminum: chromic acid + phosphoric acid. A **blank correction** is applied: an uncorroded coupon is cleaned identically, and any mass loss from cleaning is subtracted.

7. **Final Weighing (Stage 7):**
   The cleaned, dried coupon is reweighed to obtain $W_f$. The **weight loss** $W = W_i - W_f$ is computed (in mg after unit conversion from g).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step (Conceptual Reinforcement)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Why weigh to 0.0001 g?** Because a corrosion rate of just 1 mpy on a 25 cm² coupon over 100 days corresponds to a mass change of only ~0.5 mg — well below 0.01 g balance precision.
> - **Why area normalization?** A larger surface loses more metal simply because it has more area. Dividing by $A$ isolates the *intensity* of attack.
> - **Why time normalization?** A 30-day test must be comparable to a 7-day test; dividing by $T$ yields a rate (per day or per year).
> - **Why blank correction?** Even mild acid cleaning dissolves a tiny amount of base metal. Without correction, the apparent weight loss is exaggerated.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all** formulas you will need in the KTU ESE. Memorize the *meaning* of each constant.

| S.No | Corrosion Rate Unit | Formula | Meaning of Symbols |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **mdd** (mg/dm²·day) | $CR = \dfrac{W}{A \times T}$ | $W$ = weight loss (mg), $A$ = area (dm²), $T$ = time (days) |
| 2 | **mpy** (mils/yr) | $CR = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | $W$ = mg, $A$ = in², $T$ = hours, $D$ = density (g/cm³) |
| 3 | **mm/yr** (SI) | $CR = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \times T}$ | $W$ = mg, $A$ = cm², $T$ = hours, $D$ = g/cm³ |
| 4 | Area Conversion | $A_{\text{in}^2} = \dfrac{A_{\text{cm}^2}}{6.4516}$ | 1 in² = 6.4516 cm² |
| 5 | Area Conversion | $A_{\text{dm}^2} = \dfrac{A_{\text{cm}^2}}{100}$ | 1 dm² = 100 cm² |
| 6 | Time Conversion | $T_{\text{yr}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{hr}}}{8760}$ | 1 year = 8760 hours (non-leap) |
| 7 | Penetration Depth from mpy | $d_{\text{mm/yr}} = \text{mpy} \times 0.0254$ | 1 mil = 0.0254 mm |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas (NACE MR0175):** Determines if a candidate alloy qualifies for sour-service (H₂S) environments.
- **Marine / Offshore (DNV-RP-B401):** Cathodic-protection design uses weight-loss coupons to verify anode current capacity.
- **Boiler / Heat Exchanger (ASTM D2688):** Estimates tube thinning rates.
- **Atmospheric Corrosion (ISO 9223):** Establishes corrosivity categories C1–CX from mass loss of standard panels.
- **Pharma / Food (3-A Sanitary Standards):** Confirms stainless-steel passivation efficacy in CIP (Clean-in-Place) cycles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Universal Corrosion Rate Equation

We begin from first principles. The **fundamental physical definition** of corrosion rate is the *linear penetration depth* of attack per unit time:

$$CR = \frac{\text{Penetration Depth}}{\text{Time}}$$

**Step 1 — Express penetration depth from mass loss.**

By definition of density $\rho$ (hereafter denoted $D$):

$$D = \frac{\text{Mass}}{\text{Volume}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V = \frac{W}{D}$$

where $W$ is the mass loss and $D$ is the density of the metal in g/cm³.

**Step 2 — Express volume in terms of area and depth.**

$$V = A \times d$$

where $A$ is the exposed area and $d$ is the linear penetration depth.

**Step 3 — Equate and solve for $d$.**

$$A \times d = \frac{W}{D} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; d = \frac{W}{D \cdot A}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to corrosion rate (depth per unit time).**

$$CR = \frac{d}{T} = \frac{W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$$

This is the **dimensionally consistent master equation**. The "magic" constants like 534 and 87.6 are simply **unit-conversion multipliers** that arise when $W$, $A$, $T$ are measured in non-SI units.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the **mdd** (mg/dm²·day) Formula

> [!NOTE]
> For mdd, we work in: $W$ in mg, $A$ in dm², $T$ in days. The master equation $CR = \dfrac{W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ has units of $\dfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{g/cm}^3 \cdot \text{dm}^2 \cdot \text{day}}$, which is dimensionally inconsistent for "depth per time." Hence, for mdd, we *redefine* the formula without density.

By definition (mdd is a *mass-loss rate per area*):

$$CR_{\text{mdd}} = \frac{\text{Mass Loss (mg)}}{\text{Area (dm}^2) \times \text{Time (days)}}$$

There is **no density** in this formula because mdd is a *mass-based* metric, not a *penetration-based* metric.

$$\boxed{\,CR_{\text{mdd}} = \frac{W\,(\text{mg})}{A\,(\text{dm}^2)\; T\,(\text{days})}\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the **mpy** (mils per year) Formula

> [!NOTE]
> For mpy, we work in: $W$ in **mg**, $A$ in **in²**, $T$ in **hours**, $D$ in **g/cm³**, but the *output* must be in **mils per year** (1 mil = 0.001 inch).

We start with the master penetration equation:

$$d = \frac{W}{D \cdot A}$$

Substitute $W$ in mg $\rightarrow$ convert to g by dividing by 1000:

$$W_{\text{g}} = \frac{W_{\text{mg}}}{1000}$$

Substitute $A$ in in² $\rightarrow$ convert to cm² by multiplying by 6.4516:

$$A_{\text{cm}^2} = 6.4516 \cdot A_{\text{in}^2}$$

So depth in cm:

$$d_{\text{cm}} = \frac{W_{\text{mg}}/1000}{D \cdot (6.4516 \cdot A_{\text{in}^2})}$$

Convert cm to mils: $1 \text{ cm} = 1000 \text{ mil} / 2.54 = 393.7 \text{ mil}$ (since 1 inch = 2.54 cm = 1000 mil, so 1 cm = 1000/2.54 mil):

$$d_{\text{mil}} = d_{\text{cm}} \times \frac{1000}{2.54} = d_{\text{cm}} \times 393.7$$

Now combine:

$$d_{\text{mil}} = \frac{W_{\text{mg}} \times 393.7}{1000 \cdot D \cdot 6.4516 \cdot A_{\text{in}^2}}$$

$$d_{\text{mil}} = \frac{W_{\text{mg}}}{D \cdot A_{\text{in}^2}} \times \frac{393.7}{6451.6} = \frac{W_{\text{mg}}}{D \cdot A_{\text{in}^2}} \times 0.06102$$

For **annual** rate, we need time in years. $T$ is given in hours, so number of years $= T_{\text{hr}} / 8760$:

$$CR_{\text{mpy}} = \frac{d_{\text{mil}}}{T_{\text{yr}}} = \frac{0.06102 \cdot W_{\text{mg}}}{D \cdot A_{\text{in}^2}} \times \frac{8760}{T_{\text{hr}}}$$

$$CR_{\text{mpy}} = \frac{W_{\text{mg}} \times 0.06102 \times 8760}{D \cdot A_{\text{in}^2} \cdot T_{\text{hr}}}$$

$$CR_{\text{mpy}} = \frac{W_{\text{mg}} \times 534.55}{D \cdot A_{\text{in}^2} \cdot T_{\text{hr}}}$$

Rounding to standard textbook form:

$$\boxed{\,CR_{\text{mpy}} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}\,}$$

where $W$ in mg, $A$ in in², $T$ in hours, $D$ in g/cm³.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the **mm/yr** Formula

> [!NOTE]
> For mm/yr: $W$ in mg, $A$ in cm², $T$ in hours, $D$ in g/cm³. Output in mm/yr.

Starting from $d_{\text{cm}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{mg}}/1000}{D \cdot A_{\text{cm}^2}}$:

Convert cm to mm: $d_{\text{mm}} = 10 \cdot d_{\text{cm}}$:

$$d_{\text{mm}} = \frac{10 \cdot W_{\text{mg}}}{1000 \cdot D \cdot A_{\text{cm}^2}} = \frac{W_{\text{mg}}}{100 \cdot D \cdot A_{\text{cm}^2}}$$

Annualize with $T_{\text{hr}}/8760$:

$$CR_{\text{mm/yr}} = \frac{W_{\text{mg}} \times 8760}{100 \cdot D \cdot A_{\text{cm}^2} \cdot T_{\text{hr}}}$$

$$CR_{\text{mm/yr}} = \frac{W_{\text{mg}} \times 87.6}{D \cdot A_{\text{cm}^2} \cdot T_{\text{hr}}}$$

$$\boxed{\,CR_{\text{mm/yr}} = \frac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}\,}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A mild steel coupon of dimensions $5\text{ cm} \times 2\text{ cm} \times 0.2\text{ cm}$ is immersed in $1\text{ M}$ H₂SO₄ for 48 hours. Initial mass = 14.8234 g, final mass after cleaning = 14.5102 g. Density of steel = 7.86 g/cm³. Calculate the corrosion rate in **(a) mdd, (b) mpy, (c) mm/yr**.

**Step 1 — Compute weight loss.**

$$W = W_i - W_f = 14.8234 - 14.5102 = 0.3132 \text{ g} = 313.2 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute exposed area (assuming all 6 faces exposed, but typically both $5\times 2$ faces and the 4 edges; here we use the total surface area).**

$$A = 2(5 \times 2) + 2(5 \times 0.2) + 2(2 \times 0.2) = 20 + 2 + 0.8 = 22.8 \text{ cm}^2$$

For mdd we need dm²: $A = 22.8 / 100 = 0.228 \text{ dm}^2$.

**Step 3 — Compute time in required units.**

$$T_{\text{day}} = 48/24 = 2 \text{ days} \quad;\quad T_{\text{hr}} = 48 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 4 (a) — Corrosion rate in mdd:**

$$CR_{\text{mdd}} = \frac{313.2}{0.228 \times 2} = \frac{313.2}{0.456} = 686.8 \text{ mdd}$$

**Step 4 (b) — Corrosion rate in mpy (need $A$ in in²):**

$$A_{\text{in}^2} = 22.8 / 6.4516 = 3.534 \text{ in}^2$$

$$CR_{\text{mpy}} = \frac{534 \times 313.2}{7.86 \times 3.534 \times 48} = \frac{167{,}249}{1333.2} \approx 125.5 \text{ mpy}$$

**Step 4 (c) — Corrosion rate in mm/yr:**

$$CR_{\text{mm/yr}} = \frac{87.6 \times 313.2}{7.86 \times 22.8 \times 48} = \frac{27{,}436}{8601.5} \approx 3.19 \text{ mm/yr}$$

**Verification:** $125.5 \text{ mpy} \times 0.0254 = 3.19 \text{ mm/yr}$ ✓ (Consistent)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $CR = 686.8 \text{ mdd} = 125.5 \text{ mpy} = 3.19 \text{ mm/yr}$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Constant

| Given Data Units | Output Wanted | Constant to Use | Density Needed? |
|---|---|---|---|
| mg, dm², day | mdd | None (use plain $W/AT$) | ❌ No |
| mg, in², hr | mpy | **534** | ✅ Yes |
| mg, cm², hr | mm/yr | **87.6** | ✅ Yes |
| mg, cm², day | mm/day (rare) | 0.01 | ✅ Yes |
| g, m², hr | mm/yr | 0.0876 | ✅ Yes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall #1:** Students often write $CR = W/(A \cdot T)$ and put units in cm² and hr — but this yields a meaningless unit of $\text{mg}/(\text{cm}^2\cdot\text{hr})$, **not** mdd. Always **convert area to dm²** for mdd or **include the density** for mpy/mm·yr.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall #2:** Forgetting to convert $W$ from grams to milligrams. A factor of **1000** in error instantly tanks the answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Weight-Loss Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Receive Metal Coupon] --> A1[Stage 1 - Coupon Preparation]
    A1 --> A2[Grinding up to 600 grit SiC]
    A2 --> A3[Degrease with Acetone]
    A3 --> A4[Dry in Desiccator 24 hr]
    A4 --> B1[Stage 2 - Initial Weighing]
    B1 --> B2[Analytical Balance precision 0.0001 g]
    B2 --> B3[Record Initial Mass Wi]
    B3 --> C1[Stage 3 - Geometry Measurement]
    C1 --> C2[Measure Length Width Thickness]
    C2 --> C3[Compute Exposed Area A]
    C3 --> D1[Stage 4 - Exposure]
    D1 --> D2[Suspend in Test Solution]
    D2 --> D3[Maintain Temperature and Time T]
    D3 --> E1[Stage 5 - Retrieval]
    E1 --> E2[Rinse and Dry]
    E2 --> F1[Stage 6 - Chemical Cleaning]
    F1 --> F2[Apply ASTM G1 Pickling Solution]
    F2 --> F3[Apply Blank Correction]
    F3 --> G1[Stage 7 - Final Weighing]
    G1 --> G2[Record Final Mass Wf]
    G2 --> H1[Compute Weight Loss Delta W]
    H1 --> I1{Choose Target Unit}
    I1 --> I2[mdd Formula 1]
    I1 --> I3[mpy Formula 2]
    I1 --> I4[mm per yr Formula 3]
    I2 --> J1[Report Corrosion Rate]
    I3 --> J1
    I4 --> J1
    J1 --> K0[End - Report to KTU Examiner]
```

### 4.2 Data Flow Diagram — Unit Conversion Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MassA[Mass in grams] -->|multiply by 1000| MassB[Mass in mg]
    AreaA[Area in cm2] -->|divide by 100| AreaB[Area in dm2]
    AreaA -->|divide by 6.4516| AreaC[Area in in2]
    TimeA[Time in hours] -->|divide by 24| TimeB[Time in days]
    TimeA -->|divide by 8760| TimeC[Time in years]
    MassB --> F1[Formula mdd]
    AreaB --> F1
    TimeB --> F1
    MassB --> F2[Formula mpy]
    AreaC --> F2
    TimeA --> F2
    DensityA[Density in g per cm3] --> F2
    MassB --> F3[Formula mm per yr]
    AreaA --> F3
    TimeA --> F3
    DensityA --> F3
    F1 --> OUT[Final Corrosion Rate Report]
    F2 --> OUT
    F3 --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — When to Apply Blank Correction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Coupon type?} -->|Corroded| P1[Pickle per ASTM G1]
    Q1 -->|Uncorroded Blank| P2[Same Pickling Procedure]
    P1 --> W1[Weigh Wf]
    P2 --> W2[Weigh Wb]
    W1 --> C1[Delta W observed = Wi minus Wf]
    W2 --> C2[Blank loss = Wb blank minus Wf blank]
    C1 --> C3{Corrosion product is?}
    C3 -->|Adherent| C4[Mechanical Scrubbing + Chemical]
    C3 -->|Loose| C5[Brushing + Ultrasonic Bath]
    C4 --> FINAL[Apply True Weight Loss = Observed minus Blank]
    C5 --> FINAL
    C2 --> FINAL
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **blank correction** is one of the most frequently skipped steps in KTU lab reports. Always run an uncorroded control coupon through the identical pickling sequence; the small mass loss it incurs is the *chemical dissolution* of the base metal by the cleaning solution itself, and must be subtracted from the test coupon's apparent mass loss.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the term "corrosion rate" and list any two standard units used to express it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Corrosion rate is the quantity of metal lost per unit area per unit time due to chemical or electrochemical attack by the surrounding environment. It is a quantitative measure of the rate at which a metal deteriorates under corrosive conditions. Two standard units of corrosion rate are:
> 1. **mdd** — milligrams per square decimeter per day (mg/dm²·day)
> 2. **mpy** — mils per year (1 mil = 0.001 inch per year)
>
> A third common unit is **mm/yr** (millimeters per year), used in SI-based systems. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for any two units = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is the significance of performing a "blank correction" in a weight-loss corrosion test?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The blank correction is necessary to compensate for the small amount of base metal that is dissolved by the chemical cleaning (pickling) solution used to remove corrosion products from the test coupon. If uncorrected, the apparent weight loss will be artificially high, leading to an overestimation of the true corrosion rate. A blank coupon (an uncorroded specimen of the same metal) is subjected to the identical cleaning procedure; its mass loss is then subtracted from the test coupon's mass loss. This yields the *true* weight loss due to corrosion alone. *(3 marks — 1 for problem, 1 for procedure, 1 for purpose)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a true KTU ESE, Module 3 questions carry internal choice. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below for practice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]** A rectangular mild-steel coupon of dimensions $4 \text{ cm} \times 2.5 \text{ cm} \times 0.3 \text{ cm}$ is exposed to a $3\%$ NaCl solution for 30 days. Initial mass = $21.4521$ g, final mass after pickling = $21.1806$ g. Blank loss = $0.0020$ g. Density of mild steel = $7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$.

**(a)** Calculate the corrosion rate in **mpy** and **mm/yr**. (7 marks)
**(b)** Discuss the experimental procedure for weight-loss measurement as per ASTM G31, highlighting the role of solution volume to area ratio. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — True weight loss (with blank correction):**

$$W_{\text{true}} = (W_i - W_f) - W_{\text{blank}} = (21.4521 - 21.1806) - 0.0020 = 0.2715 \text{ g} = 271.5 \text{ mg}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Total exposed area:**

$$A = 2(4 \times 2.5) + 2(4 \times 0.3) + 2(2.5 \times 0.3) = 20 + 2.4 + 1.5 = 23.9 \text{ cm}^2$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Convert area to in²:**

$$A_{\text{in}^2} = 23.9 / 6.4516 = 3.7046 \text{ in}^2$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Time in hours:**

$$T = 30 \text{ days} \times 24 = 720 \text{ hours}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Corrosion rate in mpy:**

$$CR_{\text{mpy}} = \frac{534 \times 271.5}{7.85 \times 3.7046 \times 720} = \frac{144{,}981}{20{,}931} \approx 6.93 \text{ mpy}$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 6 — Corrosion rate in mm/yr:**

$$CR_{\text{mm/yr}} = 6.93 \times 0.0254 \approx 0.176 \text{ mm/yr}$$

*(1 mark — final result)*

> **Final Answer for Q.A(a):** $CR = 6.93 \text{ mpy} = 0.176 \text{ mm/yr}$

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

The ASTM G31 procedure is the **standard practice for laboratory immersion corrosion testing of metals**, and consists of the following sequential steps (each worth 1 mark):

1. **Coupon Preparation:** Specimens of standardized geometry (typically $50 \times 25 \times 2$ mm or 1" × 2") are machined, ground sequentially with SiC papers (120, 240, 320, 400, 600 grit) to obtain a uniform surface finish, then degreased with acetone or ethanol, dried, and stored in a desiccator. *(1 mark)*

2. **Initial Weighing and Dimensional Measurement:** The coupon is weighed to a precision of $\pm 0.0001$ g on an analytical balance. Length, width, and thickness are measured with vernier calipers to compute the exposed surface area. *(1 mark)*

3. **Exposure Assembly:** The coupon is suspended in the test vessel using a glass rod, Teflon string, or a specimen holder such that it does not touch the walls or other coupons. **The solution volume to specimen area ratio is maintained at $\geq 20 \text{ mL/cm}^2$** (i.e., 200 mL minimum per 10 cm² of coupon). This is critical because insufficient volume leads to depletion of dissolved oxygen and aggressive ions, which artificially suppresses the corrosion rate. *(2 marks — 1 for procedure, 1 for volume ratio rationale)*

4. **Temperature and Aeration Control:** The test is conducted at the specified temperature (commonly 25°C, 60°C, or 80°C) with controlled aeration (e.g., quiescent, naturally aerated, or deaerated by N₂ sparging). *(1 mark)*

5. **Retrieval, Cleaning, and Final Weighing:** At the end of the exposure period, the coupon is retrieved, rinsed, dried, and the corrosion products are removed by chemical pickling as per ASTM G1. A blank coupon is processed in parallel. The cleaned coupon is reweighed, and the true mass loss is computed. *(1 mark)*

6. **Reporting:** The corrosion rate is reported in mdd, mpy, or mm/yr along with complete information on the metal composition, solution chemistry, temperature, exposure duration, surface finish, and any deviations from standard. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]** Answer the following:

**(a)** With the help of a neat derivation, obtain the expression for corrosion rate in **mm/yr** in terms of weight loss $W$, density $D$, area $A$, and time $T$. State clearly the units of each variable. (7 marks)

**(b)** An aluminum coupon of area $50 \text{ cm}^2$ loses $0.4125$ g in 240 hours of exposure to an alkaline solution. Density of Al = $2.70 \text{ g/cm}^3$. Compute the corrosion rate in **(i) mdd** and **(ii) mm/yr**. Comment on the severity of corrosion. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

The derivation proceeds from the **fundamental penetration depth** definition. Each step is to be written explicitly:

**Step 1 — Penetration depth from mass loss:**

From the definition of density:

$$D = \frac{W}{V} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V = \frac{W}{D}$$

Since $V = A \cdot d$, where $d$ is the linear depth of attack:

$$d = \frac{W}{D \cdot A} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Convert to mm/yr:**

We need $W$ in mg, $A$ in cm², $D$ in g/cm³, $T$ in hours.

Substitute $W_{\text{mg}} = 1000 \cdot W_{\text{g}}$:

$$d_{\text{cm}} = \frac{W_{\text{mg}} / 1000}{D \cdot A_{\text{cm}^2}}$$

Convert cm to mm ($d_{\text{mm}} = 10 \cdot d_{\text{cm}}$):

$$d_{\text{mm}} = \frac{10 \cdot W_{\text{mg}}}{1000 \cdot D \cdot A_{\text{cm}^2}} = \frac{W_{\text{mg}}}{100 \cdot D \cdot A_{\text{cm}^2}} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

*(2 marks)*

Convert hours to years ($T_{\text{yr}} = T_{\text{hr}} / 8760$):

$$CR = \frac{d_{\text{mm}}}{T_{\text{yr}}} = \frac{W_{\text{mg}} \times 8760}{100 \cdot D \cdot A_{\text{cm}^2} \cdot T_{\text{hr}}}$$

$$CR_{\text{mm/yr}} = \frac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Units declaration:**

$W$ in **mg**, $D$ in **g/cm³**, $A$ in **cm²**, $T$ in **hours**. Output in **mm/yr**. *(1 mark)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Convert weight loss to mg:**

$$W = 0.4125 \text{ g} = 412.5 \text{ mg}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Convert area to dm² and time to days:**

$$A = 50 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.50 \text{ dm}^2$$

$$T = 240 \text{ hr} = 10 \text{ days} = 10 \text{ days}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Corrosion rate in mdd:**

$$CR_{\text{mdd}} = \frac{412.5}{0.50 \times 10} = \frac{412.5}{5} = 82.5 \text{ mdd}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Corrosion rate in mm/yr (use $A$ in cm², $T$ in hours):**

$$CR_{\text{mm/yr}} = \frac{87.6 \times 412.5}{2.70 \times 50 \times 240} = \frac{36{,}135}{32{,}400} \approx 1.115 \text{ mm/yr}$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 5 — Severity comment:**

| $CR$ (mm/yr) | Classification |
|---|---|
| < 0.025 | Negligible |
| 0.025 – 0.1 | Low |
| 0.1 – 1.0 | Moderate |
| **> 1.0** | **High / Severe** |

A corrosion rate of **1.115 mm/yr** falls in the **"high / severe"** category, indicating that the alkaline environment is aggressively attacking the aluminum. The aluminum hydroxide/soluble aluminate film is unstable in strong alkali, hence the high rate. *(2 marks — 1 for the rate, 1 for the qualitative comment)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Weight-Change problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit mix-up in the formula** — Using the **534** constant when the area is in cm² (instead of in²), or using the **87.6** constant when area is in dm². This single error forfeits 3–4 marks in one stroke.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert grams → mg** — The 534 and 87.6 constants *demand* $W$ in mg. A factor of 1000 error is unforgivable.
>
> 3. **Omitting the blank correction** — When given in the problem, blank loss must be subtracted *before* applying any formula. Many students skip this entirely.
>
> 4. **Time in wrong units** — mdd requires **days**, mpy and mm/yr require **hours**. Substituting 30 days directly into the mpy formula (instead of 720 hours) gives a wildly wrong answer.
>
> 5. **Not specifying units in the final answer** — Always write "mpy" or "mm/yr" explicitly. A bare number is treated as incomplete.
>
> 6. **Confusing area:** The exposed area must be computed for *only* the surfaces in contact with the corrodent. Mounted coupons with a single face exposed are a common variant in KTU questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Exam:**
>
> - **Definition:** Weight change = measure mass before & after exposure, compute $\Delta W$, normalize to area & time.
> - **Three units:** mdd (mass-based, no density), mpy (penetration-based with density), mm/yr (SI penetration-based).
> - **Two key formulas:**
>   - $CR_{\text{mdd}} = W / (A \times T)$ — $W$ in mg, $A$ in dm², $T$ in days
>   - $CR_{\text{mm/yr}} = 87.6 \, W / (D \cdot A \cdot T)$ — $W$ in mg, $A$ in cm², $T$ in hr, $D$ in g/cm³
>   - $CR_{\text{mpy}} = 534 \, W / (D \cdot A \cdot T)$ — $W$ in mg, $A$ in in², $T$ in hr, $D$ in g/cm³
> - **Master equation:** $CR = W / (D \cdot A \cdot T)$ is the dimensionally consistent penetration-rate base; all unit variants stem from it.
> - **ASTM standards:** **G1** (cleaning) and **G31** (immersion testing) are the governing procedures.
> - **Solution-to-area ratio:** $\geq 20 \text{ mL/cm}^2$ per ASTM G31 — critical for valid tests.
> - **Blank correction:** Always subtract the blank's weight loss to remove cleaning-induced error.
> - **Constants to remember:** 534 (mpy), 87.6 (mm/yr), 1 in² = 6.4516 cm², 1 dm² = 100 cm², 1 year = 8760 hr, 1 mil = 0.0254 mm.
> - **Severity scale:** < 0.025 mm/yr negligible, 0.025–0.1 low, 0.1–1.0 moderate, > 1.0 mm/yr severe.
> - **Advantages of method:** Highly reliable, average rate over long exposure, no instrumentation calibration issues.
> - **Limitations:** Time-consuming (days–months), destructive, gives only *average* rate (cannot detect transient behavior), does not identify mechanism.
> - **Comparison point:** Tafel extrapolation (electrochemical) gives instantaneous rate, while weight loss gives integral rate — they should agree within a factor of 2–3× for valid tests.
> - **Cleaning agents (ASTM G1 examples):** Steel — Clarke's solution (HCl + Sb₂O₃ + SnCl₂); Aluminum — HNO₃ + chromic acid; Copper — H₂SO₄; Zinc — saturated ammonium acetate.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — Weight Change Measurements.** Master the three formulas, the unit conversions, and the derivation logic. In the KTU ESE, this is among the highest-scoring topics because the questions are **predictable and formulaic** — practice 5–7 numerical problems and you can secure full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Coatings: Inhibitors and Surface Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design and Coatings: Inhibitors and Surface Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Corrosion Inhibitors — Formal Definition

A **corrosion inhibitor** is a chemical substance that, when added in small concentrations (typically **0.1 % to 1 % by mass**, or in the **ppm range**) to a corrosive environment, effectively reduces the rate of attack on a metal without significantly altering the concentration of the aggressive species (Cl⁻, O₂, H⁺, SO₄²⁻, etc.) in the bulk medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEPOT523 / M4):**
> A corrosion inhibitor is a substance which, when added in small concentration to an environment, effectively decreases the corrosion rate of a metal exposed to that environment. Inhibitors function by adsorption, film formation, or by altering the electrochemical characteristics of the anodic and/or cathodic reactions.

## 1.2 Surface Engineering & Protective Coatings — Formal Definition

**Surface Engineering** refers to the systematic modification of the outer boundary of a metallic component to develop a protective, functional, or decorative surface layer. **Coatings** are engineered surface layers—metallic, inorganic, or organic—that isolate the substrate from the corrosive environment and may additionally provide wear resistance, thermal stability, or aesthetic properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEPOT523 / M4):**
> Design considerations for corrosion prevention include the proper selection of materials, the application of protective coatings (metallic, inorganic, and organic), and the use of corrosion inhibitors. Surface engineering involves modifying the surface of a material to improve its corrosion resistance, wear resistance, and other functional properties.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The "Sunscreen + Raincoat" Model 🧴🧥**
> - **Inhibitors** behave like **sunscreen lotion**: invisible molecules added to the electrolyte (the "skin surface") that absorb, neutralize, or deflect aggressive species (UV rays → corrosive ions) at the molecular interface. You don't see the protection, but it's there.
> - **Surface coatings** behave like a **raincoat**: a physical barrier of finite thickness that completely isolates the metallic substrate from the hostile environment. Different "raincoat materials" exist—zinc (galvanizing), paint (organic), chromium (electroplating)—each suited to different climates (environments).
> - Together, inhibitors + coatings = a **complete protection system**, exactly as sunscreen + raincoat together protect you on a tropical beach.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

- It is a **direct 14-mark ESE question** favorite (one full Part B question).
- The numerical sections (adsorption isotherms, inhibition efficiency, coating thickness) are **guaranteed Part A (3-mark)** derivations.
- It carries strong **CO2 (Analyze corrosion mechanisms and apply control methods)** mapping under NEP 2020 OBE.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Corrosion Inhibitors

### 2.1.1 Based on Electrochemical Mechanism

- **Anodic Inhibitors (Passivators)** — suppress the **metal dissolution (anodic) reaction** by promoting the formation of a thin, adherent, and protective **passive oxide film** (typically **2–10 nm** thick).
  - Examples: **Chromates (CrO₄²⁻), Nitrites (NO₂⁻), Molybdates (MoO₄²⁻), Tungstates (WO₄²⁻), Phosphates**.
  - **Critical Pitfall:** Anodic inhibitors are **"dangerous inhibitors"**—if dosed below the critical concentration, they cause *localized pitting corrosion* instead of general protection.

- **Cathodic Inhibitors** — suppress the **cathodic reaction** (H⁺ reduction, O₂ reduction, or H₂O reduction) by forming insoluble precipitates on cathodic sites, blocking active cathodic areas.
  - Examples: **Zinc salts (ZnSO₄), Polyphosphates, Calcium bicarbonate, Sulfites (O₂ scavenger), Arsenates, Benzotriazole (for Cu)**.
  - Generally **"safe inhibitors"**—underdosing only causes *partial protection*, never localized attack.

- **Mixed Inhibitors** — suppress *both* anodic and cathodic reactions simultaneously, typically by adsorbing uniformly over the entire metal surface.
  - Examples: **Amines, Amides, Imidazolines, Schiff bases, Quaternary ammonium salts, Thiourea derivatives**.

- **Vapor Phase Inhibitors (VPI) / Volatile Corrosion Inhibitors (VCI)** — volatile organic compounds that sublime and reach the metal surface via the vapour phase, where they hydrolyze to release protective anions.
  - Examples: **Cyclohexylamine carbonate, Dicyclohexylamine nitrite, Benzotriazole vapour**.

### 2.1.2 Based on Chemical Nature

- **Inorganic inhibitors** — chromates, nitrites, phosphates, molybdates, silicates, arsenates.
- **Organic inhibitors** — amines, imidazolines, benzotriazole, tolyltriazole, Schiff bases, polymers.

### 2.1.3 Based on Mode of Action at the Interface

- **Adsorption-type inhibitors** — physical (physisorption, weak van der Waals) or chemical (chemisorption, coordinate bond) attachment to the surface.
- **Film-forming inhibitors** — create a thick, visible film (e.g., phosphate conversion coatings).
- **Interfacial (interphase) inhibitors** — modify the dielectric constant of the double layer.

## 2.2 The Electrochemical Role of Inhibitors

For the generic corrosion reaction:

$$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^{-} \quad \text{(anodic, suppressed by anodic inhibitor)}$$

$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-} \quad \text{(cathodic in neutral aerated solution, suppressed by cathodic inhibitor)}$$

An inhibitor shifts the **mixed potential** to a more noble (anodic inhibitor at high dose) or less noble (cathodic inhibitor) direction, while reducing the **corrosion current density $i_{corr}$** measured at the new mixed potential.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Examiner Favorite):**
> The primary measurable effect of an inhibitor is the **reduction of $i_{corr}$** as seen on an **Evans diagram** (also called a *polarization diagram*). The **secondary effect** is the shift in $E_{corr}$.

## 2.3 Adsorption Isotherms — The Mathematical Backbone

The efficiency of an adsorption-type inhibitor is determined by fitting the experimental **surface coverage (θ)** data to standard isotherm models.

### 2.3.1 Langmuir Isotherm

The **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** assumes:
1. **Monolayer** coverage only.
2. **Fixed number of identical adsorption sites.**
3. **No lateral interaction** between adsorbed molecules.
4. Each site holds **one and only one** molecule.

$$\theta = \frac{K C}{1 + K C} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{C}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K} + C$$

where:
- $C$ = inhibitor concentration (mol/L or ppm)
- $\theta$ = fractional surface coverage
- $K$ = equilibrium adsorption constant (L/mol)
- $K$ is related to the **standard free energy of adsorption** $\Delta G_{ads}^{0}$ by:

$$K = \frac{1}{C_{solvent}} \exp\left(\frac{-\Delta G_{ads}^{0}}{RT}\right)$$

where $C_{solvent}$ is the **molar concentration of water** ≈ **55.5 mol/L**.

### 2.3.2 Freundlich Isotherm

Assumes heterogeneous surface with non-uniform site energies and multilayer adsorption.

$$\theta = K C^{1/n} \quad \text{or} \quad \log \theta = \log K + \frac{1}{n} \log C$$

where $n > 1$ for favorable adsorption.

### 2.3.3 Temkin Isotherm

Accounts for **indirect adsorbate–adsorbate interactions** (uniformly distributed binding energies up to some maximum).

$$\theta = \frac{RT}{b} \ln(K C) \quad \text{or} \quad \theta = \frac{2.303 RT}{b} \log K + \frac{2.303 RT}{b} \log C$$

where $b$ is the **Temkin constant** (related to adsorbate interaction energy).

## 2.4 Inhibition Efficiency (IE%)

The **Inhibition Efficiency (IE)** is the single most reported metric for an inhibitor's performance:

$$IE\,(\%) = \frac{CR_{0} - CR_{i}}{CR_{0}} \times 100$$

where:
- $CR_{0}$ = corrosion rate without inhibitor (control)
- $CR_{i}$ = corrosion rate with inhibitor at concentration $C_{i}$

Equivalently, using polarization data:

$$IE\,(\%) = \frac{i_{corr,0} - i_{corr,i}}{i_{corr,0}} \times 100$$

And from weight-loss data:

$$IE\,(\%) = \frac{W_{0} - W_{i}}{W_{0}} \times 100$$

The **surface coverage θ** is numerically equal to the **fractional inhibition efficiency**:

$$\theta = \frac{IE\,(\%)}{100}$$

## 2.5 Protective Coatings — Classification

### 2.5.1 Metallic Coatings

| Type | Mechanism | Examples | Coating Thickness | Substrate Examples |
|------|-----------|----------|------------------|--------------------|
| **Anodic (Sacrificial)** | Sacrificial anode — coating corrodes *instead* of substrate. | Hot-dip **Galvanizing (Zn)**, **Aluminizing (Al)**, **Zinc-rich primers** | **50–150 µm** | Steel fasteners, structural sections, automotive body |
| **Cathodic (Noble)** | Acts as a *physical barrier only*; if breached, accelerates substrate corrosion. | **Electroplated Cr, Ni, Sn, Ag, Au, Cu** on steel | **5–50 µm** | Decorative trim, food cans (tin), hydraulic cylinders (Cr) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** A cracked **tin coating on steel** is far more dangerous than no coating at all. Tin is **cathodic** to iron (E°(Sn²⁺/Sn) ≈ –0.14 V vs SHE; E°(Fe²⁺/Fe) ≈ –0.44 V), so a coating defect causes **rapid galvanic pitting of the exposed steel**.

### 2.5.2 Inorganic (Ceramic/Conversion) Coatings

- **Anodizing (Al, Ti, Mg)** — electrochemical oxidation to form a thick, hard Al₂O₃ layer (**5–25 µm**).
- **Chromate Conversion Coatings (Alodine, Iridite)** — Al, Zn, Mg substrates; Cr⁶⁺ → Cr³⁺ reduction yields a Cr₂O₃-rich film (**0.5–2 µm**).
- **Phosphate Conversion Coatings (Parkerizing, Bonderizing)** — Fe, Zn, Mn phosphate crystals (**5–20 µm**).
- **Enamel coatings** — vitreous glass fused to steel (**100–500 µm**).

### 2.5.3 Organic Coatings

- **Paints** — binder (alkyd, epoxy, polyurethane, acrylic) + pigment + solvent + additives.
- **Powder coatings** — electrostatically applied, then heat-cured (**50–100 µm**).
- **Polymer linings** — PVC, PE, PTFE, PP, rubber, fiberglass-reinforced plastics (FRP) for tanks and pipes.
- **Bituminous coatings** — coal-tar enamel, asphalt, used for buried pipelines.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU 2024 ESE-exam-relevant**. Memorize the variable definitions and units.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $IE\% = \frac{CR_{0} - CR_{i}}{CR_{0}} \times 100$ | Inhibition efficiency (weight loss or polarization method) | dimensionless % |
| 2 | $\theta = \frac{IE\%}{100}$ | Surface coverage (fractional) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\frac{C}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K} + C$ | Langmuir linear form | C in mol/L or ppm |
| 4 | $\log \theta = \log K + \frac{1}{n} \log C$ | Freundlich linear form | log-log plot |
| 5 | $\theta = \frac{2.303\,RT}{b} \log K + \frac{2.303\,RT}{b} \log C$ | Temkin linear form | – |
| 6 | $K = \frac{1}{55.5} \exp\left(\frac{-\Delta G_{ads}^{0}}{RT}\right)$ | Adsorption equilibrium constant relation | L/mol, J/mol |
| 7 | $\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -RT \ln(55.5 K)$ | Standard free energy of adsorption | J/mol or kJ/mol |
| 8 | $DFT = WFT \times V_{s}$ | Dry film thickness from wet film thickness | µm |
| 9 | $A_{cov} = \frac{V_{s} \times WFT \times 10}{DFT}$ | Theoretical coverage (m²/L) | m²/L |
| 10 | $i_{c} = \frac{W_{corr}}{A \cdot t}$ | Corrosion current density from weight loss | g/(m²·h) or mpy |
| 11 | $\text{mpy} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Corrosion rate in mils per year (imperial) | mils/year |
| 12 | $\text{mm/yr} = \frac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Corrosion rate in mm/year (SI) | mm/yr |
| 13 | $I = A \cdot i_{corr}$ | Cathodic protection current requirement | A |
| 14 | $P = I \cdot V$ | Power for impressed-current CP system | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention (KTU Board Exam):**
> - W = weight loss (mg)
> - D = density of metal (g/cm³)
> - A = exposed area (cm²)
> - t = exposure time (hours)
> - 534 and 87.6 are the standard conversion constants (derived from unit conversions involving density, area, time, and Faraday's laws).

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Inhibitors** are the most economical single-corrosion-control method in **oil & gas pipelines, cooling water circuits, pickling baths, automotive coolants, and concrete reinforcement**.
- **Galvanized steel** accounts for > **50 % of the world's coated steel production**, used in construction, automotive, and appliances.
- **Chromate conversion coatings** are still the **gold standard** for aerospace aluminum alloys (replaced in some applications by trivalent chromium or zirconium-based "green" alternatives).
- **Epoxy + polyurethane duplex systems** are the industry standard for offshore structures and bridges.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm

The Langmuir isotherm can be derived rigorously from **kinetic considerations** at equilibrium.

### Step 1 — Define the elementary surface reaction

Adsorption of inhibitor molecule $I$ from solution onto an empty surface site $S$:

$$I_{(soln)} + S \xrightleftharpoons[k_{d}]{k_{a}} I \cdot S$$

### Step 2 — Write the rate of adsorption (forward)

$$r_{a} = k_{a} \cdot C \cdot (1 - \theta)$$

where:
- $C$ = inhibitor concentration in solution
- $(1 - \theta)$ = fraction of vacant surface sites
- $k_{a}$ = rate constant for adsorption

### Step 3 — Write the rate of desorption (backward)

$$r_{d} = k_{d} \cdot \theta$$

where:
- $\theta$ = fraction of occupied sites
- $k_{d}$ = rate constant for desorption

### Step 4 — Apply equilibrium condition

At equilibrium, $r_{a} = r_{d}$:

$$k_{a} \cdot C \cdot (1 - \theta) = k_{d} \cdot \theta$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $\theta$

$$k_{a} C - k_{a} C \theta = k_{d} \theta$$

Rearranging:

$$k_{a} C = \theta \, (k_{d} + k_{a} C)$$

$$\theta = \frac{k_{a} C}{k_{d} + k_{a} C} = \frac{(k_{a}/k_{d}) C}{1 + (k_{a}/k_{d}) C}$$

### Step 6 — Define the equilibrium constant K

Let $K = k_{a}/k_{d}$. The final form is the **Langmuir isotherm**:

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{K C}{1 + K C}}$$

### Step 7 — Invert to the linear form for plotting

$$\frac{1}{\theta} = \frac{1 + K C}{K C} = \frac{1}{K C} + 1$$

Multiplying through by $C$:

$$\frac{C}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K} + C$$

> [!NOTE]
> A plot of $C/\theta$ vs $C$ yields a straight line with:
> - **Slope = 1**
> - **Intercept = $1/K$** (hence $K$ is determined)
> - Correlation coefficient $R^{2} > 0.99$ confirms Langmuir-type monolayer adsorption.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Calculating Inhibition Efficiency and Surface Coverage

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A mild steel specimen exhibits a corrosion rate of **0.85 mm/yr** in 1 M H₂SO₄ at 25 °C. After adding **2 × 10⁻³ M** of an organic inhibitor, the corrosion rate drops to **0.11 mm/yr**.
> **(a)** Calculate the inhibition efficiency (IE%).
> **(b)** Calculate the surface coverage $\theta$.
> **(c)** Using the Langmuir isotherm, determine the equilibrium constant $K$ and the standard free energy of adsorption $\Delta G_{ads}^{0}$ at 25 °C.

### Part (a) — Inhibition Efficiency

Using $IE\% = \frac{CR_{0} - CR_{i}}{CR_{0}} \times 100$:

$$IE\% = \frac{0.85 - 0.11}{0.85} \times 100 = \frac{0.74}{0.85} \times 100$$

$$IE\% = 87.06\,\%$$

### Part (b) — Surface Coverage

$$\theta = \frac{IE\%}{100} = \frac{87.06}{100} = 0.8706$$

### Part (c) — Equilibrium Constant K (Langmuir)

From the Langmuir isotherm: $\theta = \frac{K C}{1 + K C}$

Rearranging for $K$:

$$K = \frac{\theta}{C(1 - \theta)}$$

Substitute $\theta = 0.8706$ and $C = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L:

$$K = \frac{0.8706}{2 \times 10^{-3} \times (1 - 0.8706)}$$

$$K = \frac{0.8706}{2 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1294} = \frac{0.8706}{2.588 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$K = 3363.99 \approx 3.36 \times 10^{3} \text{ L/mol}$$

### Computing $\Delta G_{ads}^{0}$

Using the relation $K = \frac{1}{55.5} \exp\left(\frac{-\Delta G_{ads}^{0}}{RT}\right)$:

Rearranging:

$$\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -RT \ln(55.5 K)$$

Substitute $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T = 298$ K, $K = 3363.99$:

$$55.5 \times K = 55.5 \times 3363.99 = 186701.5$$

$$\ln(186701.5) = 12.137$$

$$\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -8.314 \times 298 \times 12.137$$

$$\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -30069.5 \text{ J/mol} = -30.07 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:**
> - $\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -30.07$ kJ/mol is **more negative than –20 kJ/mol but less negative than –40 kJ/mol**, indicating **mixed-mode adsorption**: a combination of **physisorption (electrostatic)** and **chemisorption (charge sharing/transfer)**.
> - $K = 3.36 \times 10^3$ L/mol indicates **strong, spontaneous adsorption** with high surface affinity.

| $\Delta G_{ads}^{0}$ range | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| $\Delta G_{ads}^{0} > -20$ kJ/mol | Pure **physisorption** (electrostatic, weak) |
| $-40 \leq \Delta G_{ads}^{0} \leq -20$ kJ/mol | **Mixed physisorption + chemisorption** |
| $\Delta G_{ads}^{0} < -40$ kJ/mol | Pure **chemisorption** (coordinate covalent bond) |

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Coating Coverage Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A maintenance engineer applies a wet epoxy coating of **WFT = 200 µm** on a steel tank surface. The coating has **60 % volume solids** ($V_s = 0.60$).
> **(a)** Determine the dry film thickness (DFT).
> **(b)** The coating is sold in 5 L cans. The tank's external surface area is **75 m²**, and three coats are required. Determine how many cans are needed (neglecting losses).

### Part (a) — DFT

$$DFT = WFT \times V_s = 200 \times 0.60 = 120 \text{ µm}$$

### Part (b) — Cans Required

Theoretical coverage:

$$A_{cov} = \frac{V_s \times WFT \times 10}{DFT} = \frac{0.60 \times 200 \times 10}{120} = 10 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}$$

(where the factor of 10 converts µm·%/100 = µm·decimal fraction; for WFT and DFT both in µm, the formula simplifies to $A_{cov} = 10\,V_s \times$ (WFT/DFT) m²/L. With WFT = DFT/$V_s$, this gives $A_{cov} = 10/V_s \cdot (V_s) = 10/V_s \cdot V_s$ — let us re-derive carefully below.)

**Clean re-derivation of coverage:**

Total volume of liquid paint required per m² = WFT × 1 m² (treating WFT as a layer thickness). Volume of solids in that paint = WFT × $V_s$ = DFT. The **solids coverage** is:

$$A_{cov} = \frac{V_s \times 10}{DFT_{µm}} \text{ (m}^2/\text{L)}$$

$$A_{cov} = \frac{0.60 \times 10}{120} = 0.05 \text{ m}^2/\text{L (per coat)}$$

Wait — the standard SSPC formula is $A_{cov} = \frac{V_s \times 10}{DFT}$ in m²/L when DFT is in **mils** (1 mil = 25.4 µm). Let us use the universally correct version:

$$A_{cov}\left(\frac{m^2}{L}\right) = \frac{10 \times V_s}{DFT\,(\text{mils})}$$

Converting DFT = 120 µm to mils: $DFT = 120/25.4 = 4.724$ mils.

$$A_{cov} = \frac{10 \times 0.60}{4.724} = \frac{6.0}{4.724} = 1.270 \text{ m}^2/\text{L per coat}$$

Total paint per coat for 75 m²:

$$V_{coat} = \frac{75}{1.270} = 59.06 \text{ L per coat}$$

For 3 coats:

$$V_{total} = 3 \times 59.06 = 177.18 \text{ L}$$

Number of 5-L cans (round up to nearest integer):

$$N = \lceil 177.18 / 5 \rceil = \lceil 35.44 \rceil = 36 \text{ cans}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering rule of thumb:** Always add **10–20 % wastage factor** in real projects → recommended = 36 × 1.15 ≈ 42 cans.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Inhibition Mechanisms

| Inhibitor Type | Site of Action | Polarization Behavior | Risk of Pitting at Low Dose | Typical Examples | Common Applications |
|----------------|----------------|----------------------|----------------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Anodic (passivator)** | Anodic sites | Shifts $E_{corr}$ **nobler** | **YES — high risk** | NaNO₂, Na₂CrO₄, Na₂MoO₄ | Cooling water, rebar concrete |
| **Cathodic** | Cathodic sites | Shifts $E_{corr}$ **less noble** | **NO — safe** | ZnSO₄, polyphosphates, Ca(HCO₃)₂ | Boiler water, neutral solutions |
| **Mixed** | Both | $E_{corr}$ shift minimal, **$i_{corr}$ reduced** | **Low** | Amines, imidazolines, BTA | Acid pickling, oil & gas |
| **Vapor Phase (VCI)** | Whole surface (via vapor) | Anodic + barrier | Low | Cyclohexylamine carbonate | Export packaging, storage |
| **Film-forming** | Anodic + cathodic | Strong $i_{corr}$ drop | Low | Phosphate + silicate blends | Pre-treatment primers |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Coating Type

| Service Environment | Recommended Coating | Reason | Expected Life |
|---------------------|--------------------|----|---------------|
| **Marine atmospheric** | Galvanizing + epoxy + PU | Zn sacrificially protects; epoxy barrier | 20–30 yr |
| **Buried soil** | FBE (Fusion Bond Epoxy) + cathodic protection | Resist soil chemicals; CP compensates for holidays | 50+ yr |
| **Hot acid (HCl, H₂SO₄)** | Rubber lining / FRP / PTFE | Chemical resistance of polymer | 10–20 yr |
| **High-temperature exhaust** | Aluminizing / thermal-spray Al-Si | Stable oxide at > 500 °C | 5–15 yr |
| **Food contact** | Electroless Ni-P / tin | Non-toxic, sanitary | 10–25 yr |
| **Aerospace Al alloys** | Chromate conversion + primer + topcoat | Proven corrosion + adhesion system | 5–10 yr |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Corrosion Inhibitor Classification & Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Corrosion Inhibitors] --> B[Anodic Inhibitors]
    A --> C[Cathodic Inhibitors]
    A --> D[Mixed Inhibitors]
    A --> E[Vapor Phase Inhibitors VCI]

    B --> B1[Examples: Chromates, Nitrites, Molybdates, Phosphates]
    B --> B2[Mechanism: Promote passive oxide film 2-10 nm]
    B --> B3[Risk: Pitting at under-dose]

    C --> C1[Examples: Zn salts, Polyphosphates, Sulfites]
    C --> C2[Mechanism: Form insoluble precipitate at cathodic sites]
    C --> C3[Risk: Safe at under-dose]

    D --> D1[Examples: Amines, Imidazolines, BTA, Schiff bases]
    D --> D2[Mechanism: Adsorb as monolayer on entire surface]
    D --> D3[Risk: Low]

    E --> E1[Examples: Cyclohexylamine carbonate, Dicyclohexylamine nitrite]
    E --> E2[Mechanism: Sublime and protect via vapour]
    E --> E3[Application: Export packaging storage]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Surface Engineering Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Substrate Metal e.g. Mild Steel] --> P[Surface Preparation]
    P --> P1[Degreasing]
    P --> P2[Pickling Acid]
    P --> P3[Blasting Sa 2.5]

    P3 --> C1[Conversion Coating]
    P3 --> C2[Metallic Coating]
    P3 --> C3[Organic Coating]

    C1 --> C1a[Phosphate]
    C1 --> C1b[Chromate]
    C1 --> C1c[Anodizing]

    C2 --> C2a[Anodic Sacrificial Zn Al]
    C2 --> C2b[Cathodic Noble Cr Ni Sn]

    C3 --> C3a[Primer Epoxy Zn rich]
    C3 --> C3b[Midcoat Epoxy]
    C3 --> C3c[Topcoat Polyurethane]

    C1a --> F[Final Corrosion Resistant System]
    C1b --> F
    C1c --> F
    C2a --> F
    C2b --> F
    C3a --> F
    C3b --> F
    C3c --> F
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Polarization Behaviour of Inhibitor Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Polar[Polarization Behaviour of Inhibitors] --> An[Anodic Inhibitor]
    Polar --> Cath[Cathodic Inhibitor]
    Polar --> Mix[Mixed Inhibitor]

    An --> An1[E corr shifts noble direction]
    An --> An2[Tafel slope for anodic reaction increases]
    An --> An3[i corr drastically reduced]
    An --> An4[Risk of pitting if under-dosed]

    Cath --> Cath1[E corr shifts active direction]
    Cath --> Cath2[Tafel slope for cathodic reaction increases]
    Cath --> Cath3[i corr moderately reduced]
    Cath --> Cath4[Safe even at under-dose]

    Mix --> Mix1[E corr nearly unchanged]
    Mix --> Mix2[Both Tafel slopes increase]
    Mix --> Mix3[i corr strongly reduced]
    Mix --> Mix4[Preferred for acid pickling]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Cathodic vs Anodic Metallic Coating Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Metallic Coating] --> Q1{Is substrate more active than coating metal?}

    Q1 -->|Yes e.g. Steel with Zn| Anodic[Anodic Sacrificial Coating]
    Q1 -->|No e.g. Steel with Cr| Cath[Cathodic Noble Coating]

    Anodic --> A1[Coating corrodes preferentially]
    Anodic --> A2[Provides galvanic protection at scratches]
    Anodic --> A3[Examples Galvanizing Aluminizing]

    Cath --> C1[Coating is inert barrier only]
    Cath --> C2[Coating defect causes severe localized corrosion]
    Cath --> C3[Examples Tin on steel decorative Cr]
```
