# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**
Define **corrosion inhibitor**. With a suitable example, explain the difference between **anodic** and **cathodic** inhibitors.

**Model Answer:**

A **corrosion inhibitor** is a chemical substance that, when added in small concentrations to a corrosive medium, reduces the rate of metal dissolution without altering the bulk concentration of the aggressive species.

| Feature | Anodic Inhibitor | Cathodic Inhibitor |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Site of action** | Anodic (metal dissolution) sites | Cathodic (H⁺/O₂ reduction) sites |
| **Mechanism** | Promotes passive oxide film | Forms insoluble precipitate blocking cathodic area |
| **Effect on $E_{corr}$** | Shifts to more noble (positive) value | Shifts to more active (negative) value |
| **Example** | Sodium chromate (Na₂CrO₄), Sodium nitrite (NaNO₂) | Zinc sulphate (ZnSO₄), Polyphosphate |
| **Risk of pitting at low dose** | **High** ("dangerous" inhibitor) | **Negligible** ("safe" inhibitor) |

(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for anodic explanation + 1 for cathodic comparison)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
State the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** and explain its significance in corrosion inhibition studies.

**Model Answer:**

The **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** is given by:

$$\theta = \frac{K C}{1 + K C} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{C}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K} + C$$

where $\theta$ is the surface coverage, $C$ is the inhibitor concentration, and $K$ is the adsorption equilibrium constant.

**Significance:**
1. It quantifies the **adsorption behaviour** of inhibitors on metal surfaces.
2. A plot of $C/\theta$ vs $C$ yields a straight line of **slope 1** and **intercept $1/K$**; the agreement confirms **monolayer adsorption**.
3. The constant $K$ is related to the **standard free energy of adsorption** $\Delta G_{ads}^{0}$ through $K = (1/55.5)\exp(-\Delta G_{ads}^{0}/RT)$, allowing determination of whether adsorption is **physisorption** ($\Delta G_{ads}^{0} > -20$ kJ/mol) or **chemisorption** ($\Delta G_{ads}^{0} < -40$ kJ/mol).

(3 marks: 1 for equation + 1 for significance + 1 for $K$–$\Delta G$ relationship)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option (a) [7 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, ESE Pattern]**
**(a)** Discuss the **classification of corrosion inhibitors** based on:
- (i) Electrochemical mechanism (anodic, cathodic, mixed, vapour phase) **[4 marks]**
- (ii) Chemical nature (inorganic vs organic) **[3 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Classification based on electrochemical mechanism:**

- **Anodic inhibitors (passivators):** These act at the anodic sites of the corroding metal. They promote the formation of a thin, protective passive oxide film (2–10 nm thick) by shifting the corrosion potential in the noble direction. Common examples include chromates, nitrites, molybdates, and phosphates. They are called "dangerous" inhibitors because under-dosing can cause severe localized pitting corrosion.

- **Cathodic inhibitors:** These act at the cathodic sites, suppressing the reduction reactions such as $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$ or $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$. They typically form insoluble hydroxides or carbonates that physically block cathodic sites. Examples: zinc salts, polyphosphates, calcium bicarbonate. They are "safe" inhibitors.

- **Mixed inhibitors:** These suppress both anodic and cathodic reactions simultaneously by adsorbing uniformly over the metal surface, forming a monolayer. They do not significantly shift the corrosion potential but reduce the corrosion current density. Examples: amines, imidazolines, benzotriazole (BTA), thiourea, Schiff bases.

- **Vapour phase inhibitors (VCI):** Volatile organic compounds (e.g., cyclohexylamine carbonate, dicyclohexylamine nitrite) that sublime and reach the metal surface via the vapour phase, where they hydrolyze to form protective films. Widely used in export packaging.

**(ii) Classification based on chemical nature:**

- **Inorganic inhibitors:** Typically anodic or cathodic film-forming salts — chromates, nitrites, molybdates, phosphates, silicates, arsenates. They form insoluble oxide/hydroxide films.

- **Organic inhibitors:** Contain heteroatoms (N, S, O, P) and often aromatic rings. They function by **adsorption** of their polar functional groups onto the metal surface. Examples: amines, amides, imidazolines, benzotriazole, tolyltriazole, Schiff bases, quaternary ammonium salts. Their efficiency follows the order of electron-donating ability: P > S > N > O.

### Question A — Option (b) [7 Marks]

**(b)** A corrosion rate of **0.62 mm/yr** is observed for mild steel in 0.5 M H₂SO₄ at 30 °C. After adding **5 × 10⁻⁴ M** of an organic inhibitor, the rate drops to **0.07 mm/yr**. The Langmuir isotherm applies. Calculate:
- (i) The inhibition efficiency (IE%) **[2 marks]**
- (ii) The surface coverage $\theta$ **[1 mark]**
- (iii) The Langmuir adsorption constant $K$ **[2 marks]**
- (iv) The standard free energy of adsorption $\Delta G_{ads}^{0}$ at 30 °C and identify whether adsorption is physisorption, chemisorption, or mixed. **[2 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Inhibition Efficiency:**

$$IE\% = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100 = \frac{0.62 - 0.07}{0.62} \times 100$$

$$IE\% = \frac{0.55}{0.62} \times 100 = 88.71\,\%$$

**(ii) Surface Coverage:**

$$\theta = \frac{IE\%}{100} = 0.8871$$

**(iii) Langmuir constant K:**

From $\theta = \frac{KC}{1 + KC}$, solve for $K$:

$$K = \frac{\theta}{C(1 - \theta)} = \frac{0.8871}{5 \times 10^{-4} \times (1 - 0.8871)}$$

$$K = \frac{0.8871}{5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.1129} = \frac{0.8871}{5.645 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$K = 1.572 \times 10^{4} \text{ L/mol}$$

**(iv) Free energy of adsorption:**

$$K = \frac{1}{55.5}\exp\left(\frac{-\Delta G_{ads}^{0}}{RT}\right)$$

$$\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -RT \ln(55.5 K)$$

Substitute $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T = 303$ K, $K = 1.572 \times 10^{4}$:

$$55.5 \times 1.572 \times 10^{4} = 8.7246 \times 10^{5}$$

$$\ln(8.7246 \times 10^{5}) = \ln(8.7246) + 5\ln(10) = 2.166 + 11.513 = 13.679$$

$$\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -8.314 \times 303 \times 13.679 = -34452 \text{ J/mol} = -34.45 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

Since $-40 < \Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -34.45 < -20$ kJ/mol, the adsorption is **mixed-mode** (combination of physisorption and chemisorption).

---

### Question B — Option (a) [7 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, ESE Pattern]**
**(a)** Compare **metallic, inorganic, and organic coatings** for corrosion protection. Give **two examples** of each type and explain the **anodic vs cathodic** distinction in metallic coatings. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Property | Metallic Coatings | Inorganic Coatings | Organic Coatings |
|----------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Composition** | Metal layer (Zn, Cr, Ni, Al, Sn) | Ceramic, oxide, salt film | Polymer, paint |
| **Mechanism** | Sacrificial or barrier | Barrier + passive layer | Barrier |
| **Thickness** | 5–150 µm | 0.5–500 µm | 50–500 µm |
| **Examples** | Galvanizing (Zn), Chrome plating (Cr), Electroless Ni, Tin plating | Anodizing, Chromate conversion, Phosphate (Parkerizing), Enamel | Epoxy, Polyurethane, PVC, PTFE, Powder coat |

**Anodic vs Cathodic Metallic Coatings:**

A metallic coating is termed **anodic** (sacrificial) when the coating metal is **more electrochemically active** (more negative standard electrode potential) than the substrate. When the coating is scratched or damaged, the coating preferentially corrodes, **sacrificially protecting** the underlying metal. Examples: **Zinc (E° = –0.76 V) on steel (E° = –0.44 V)** → galvanizing; **Aluminium (E° = –1.66 V) on steel** → aluminizing.

A metallic coating is termed **cathodic** (noble) when the coating metal is **less active** (more positive $E°$) than the substrate. It provides **only barrier protection**; any breach in the coating creates a **galvanic cell** that accelerates localized corrosion of the exposed substrate. Examples: **Tin (E° = –0.14 V) on steel** — dangerous if scratched; **Chrome (E° = –0.74 V under specific conditions, but passive) on steel** — purely decorative/barrier.

The **critical selection rule** is: if the service environment will inevitably breach the coating, **always choose an anodic (sacrificial) coating** to ensure continued protection.

### Question B — Option (b) [7 Marks]

**(b)** A mild steel structure is to be protected with a **two-coat paint system**. Each coat uses a paint with **55 % volume solids** applied at a **wet film thickness of 150 µm**.
- (i) Calculate the **dry film thickness (DFT)** per coat. **[1 mark]**
- (ii) Calculate the **theoretical coverage** in m²/L using the SSPC formula. **[2 marks]**
- (iii) If the surface area to be coated is **250 m²**, calculate the **total volume of paint** (in litres) required for two coats, **neglecting wastage**. **[2 marks]**
- (iv) If paint is supplied in **4 L cans**, how many cans are required? Add a **15 % wastage factor** and recalculate. **[2 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Dry Film Thickness per coat:**

$$DFT = WFT \times V_s = 150 \times 0.55 = 82.5 \text{ µm}$$

**(ii) Theoretical coverage (SSPC formula):**

Converting DFT to mils: $DFT = 82.5/25.4 = 3.248$ mils

$$A_{cov} = \frac{10 \times V_s}{DFT\,(\text{mils})} = \frac{10 \times 0.55}{3.248} = \frac{5.5}{3.248} = 1.693 \text{ m}^2/\text{L per coat}$$

**(iii) Total paint volume for 2 coats:**

Volume per coat for 250 m²:

$$V_{coat} = \frac{250}{1.693} = 147.66 \text{ L per coat}$$

For 2 coats (no wastage):

$$V_{total} = 2 \times 147.66 = 295.31 \text{ L}$$

**(iv) Number of cans:**

Without wastage: $N = \lceil 295.31/4 \rceil = \lceil 73.83 \rceil = 74$ cans

With 15 % wastage factor:

$$V_{actual} = 295.31 \times 1.15 = 339.61 \text{ L}$$

$$N_{actual} = \lceil 339.61/4 \rceil = \lceil 84.90 \rceil = 85 \text{ cans}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the mils-to-µm conversion** in the SSPC coverage formula (always convert DFT to mils first; **1 mil = 25.4 µm**).
> 2. **Not rounding up** the number of cans — KTU strictly requires the **ceiling function** $\lceil x \rceil$.
> 3. **Mixing up $V_s$ (volume solids as decimal, e.g., 0.55)** vs as percentage (55) — the formula requires the **decimal** form.
> 4. **Wrong direction of $E_{corr}$ shift** in Tafel behaviour: anodic inhibitors shift $E_{corr}$ **nobler (positive)**, cathodic inhibitors shift $E_{corr}$ **more active (negative)**, mixed inhibitors leave $E_{corr}$ nearly unchanged.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PEPOT523 Module 4**

- ✅ **Corrosion inhibitor** = chemical additive (ppm to %) that reduces $i_{corr}$ by adsorption, film formation, or passivation.
- ✅ **Anodic inhibitors** (chromates, nitrites, molybdates) are **"dangerous"** at under-dose → cause pitting.
- ✅ **Cathodic inhibitors** (Zn²⁺, polyphosphates, Ca(HCO₃)₂) are **"safe"** even at under-dose.
- ✅ **Mixed inhibitors** (amines, BTA, imidazolines) reduce $i_{corr}$ without shifting $E_{corr}$ significantly.
- ✅ **VCI** = volatile, used in enclosed spaces (export packaging, sealed storage).
- ✅ **Inhibition Efficiency** $IE\% = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$; **Surface coverage** $\theta = IE\%/100$.
- ✅ **Langmuir equation**: $\theta = \frac{KC}{1+KC}$; linear form $\frac{C}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K} + C$.
- ✅ **Freundlich equation**: $\theta = K C^{1/n}$ — log-log linear plot, valid for heterogeneous surfaces.
- ✅ **Temkin equation**: $\theta = (RT/b)\ln(KC)$ — accounts for adsorbate–adsorbate interaction.
- ✅ **$\Delta G_{ads}^{0}$ interpretation:** > –20 kJ/mol → physisorption; –20 to –40 kJ/mol → mixed; < –40 kJ/mol → chemisorption.
- ✅ **Metallic coatings:** Anodic/sacrificial (Zn, Al on steel) vs Cathodic/noble (Cr, Ni, Sn, Au) — **always prefer anodic when scratches are inevitable**.
- ✅ **Inorganic coatings:** Anodizing (Al, Ti), Chromate (Al, Zn), Phosphate (steel, Zn), Enamel (steel).
- ✅ **Organic coatings:** Epoxy primers, PU topcoats, powder coat, FBE, rubber lining, FRP, PTFE.
- ✅ **Coating thickness formula:** $DFT = WFT \times V_s$ (both in µm; $V_s$ as decimal).
- ✅ **SSPC coverage formula:** $A_{cov}\,(m^2/L) = 10 V_s / DFT\,(\text{mils})$; remember **1 mil = 25.4 µm**.
- ✅ **Corrosion rate imperial:** mpy = $534 W / (D A t)$; **SI:** mm/yr = $87.6 W / (D A t)$.
- ✅ **Cathodic protection current:** $I = A \cdot i_{corr}$; **power:** $P = I V$.
- ✅ **Chromate inhibitors** are being globally replaced by **molybdates, vanadates, cerium, and rare-earth inhibitors** (RE inhibitors) due to $\text{Cr}^{6+}$ toxicity (RoHS, REACH).
- ✅ **Pickling inhibitors** (e.g., thiourea, propargyl alcohol) suppress acid attack on steel during descaling.
- ✅ **Benzotriazole (BTA)** is the industry standard for **copper and copper-alloy** protection.
- ✅ **Always add 10–20 % wastage factor** in real coating quantity calculations.

---

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cathodic Protection (CP) — Formal Definition

**Cathodic Protection (CP)** is an electrochemical corrosion-control technique in which the metal (alloy) structure to be protected is deliberately polarized in the **negative (cathodic) direction** so that all anodic dissolution reactions on its surface are suppressed. The protected structure is made the **cathode** of an external electrochemical cell, while the **anodic reaction** is transferred to an auxiliary anode that corrodes (in galvanic CP) or evolves oxygen/chlorine (in impressed-current CP).

KTU 2024 syllabus terminology: *“A method of corrosion control wherein the metal to be protected is converted into the cathode of an electrochemical cell — thereby eliminating the anodic half-reaction responsible for metal loss.”*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Three Pre-Requisites for CP**
> 1. The structure must be in continuous contact with an **electrolyte** (soil, water, seawater, concrete pore solution).
> 2. A **direct electrical connection** must exist between the auxiliary anode and the structure (for galvanic CP) OR a **DC power source** (for ICCP).
> 3. The **polarization potential** of the structure (measured against a stable reference electrode) must be shifted to a value **more negative** than the protective potential criterion (e.g., −850 mV vs. Cu/CuSO₄ for steel).

## 1.2 Anodic Protection (AP) — Formal Definition

**Anodic Protection (AP)** is an electrochemical corrosion-control technique applicable **only to metals and alloys that exhibit passivity** (Fe, Fe–Cr, Ni, Ti, stainless steels, etc.) in a given electrolyte. The metal to be protected is connected as the **anode** of an external circuit, and its **electrode potential is held (potentiostatically) within the passive region** of its Pourbaix/E-pH diagram. A low current density — the *passive current density* — flows and sustains the passive film, while corrosion proceeds at a negligible rate (often < 25 µm/year).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core difference**: CP *suppresses* anodic dissolution by **sacrificing an external anode**; AP *permits* anodic polarization but **confines the metal to a passive region** where the dissolution rate is intrinsically low. CP works for any metal; AP works only for *active–passive* metals.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

### Cathodic Protection — “The Sacrificial Hero”

Imagine a **battlefield where a hero is being attacked from all sides (the corrosive electrolyte)**. To save the hero, you recruit a **body-double** (the auxiliary anode) who dresses exactly like him, and the **enemies attack the body-double instead**. The hero (protected structure) survives because the blows now fall on the sacrificial stand-in. The body-double is "consumed" — and indeed, **sacrificial anodes must be replaced periodically**. The **impressed-current variant** is the same idea, but instead of a volunteer body-double, you **electromagnetically deflect all incoming arrows away** using a powered shield (DC rectifier).

### Anodic Protection — “The Sleeping Dragon”

For metals that form a **self-healing oxide skin** (think of a dragon whose scales are the passive film), the trick is to keep the dragon **calmly sleeping** in the *right zone* of his cave (the passive region of the E-pH diagram). A smart electronic controller (potentiostat) continuously senses the dragon's state and sends tiny currents to **prevent him from stirring into the active-corrosion zone** or boiling over into transpassivity. The dragon lives for centuries because the controller never lets him wake up angry.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU-referenced)

| Metric | Standard Value | Reference Electrode |
|---|---|---|
| Protection potential for steel in soil | **−850 mV** | Cu/CuSO₄ (CSE) |
| Protection potential for steel in seawater | **−800 mV** | Ag/AgCl/seawater |
| Negative shift of 100 mV criterion (polarization decay) | **−100 mV** | All CSE |
| Faraday constant | **F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹ (≈ 96,500 C mol⁻¹)** | Universal |
| Typical CP design current density (bare steel in soil) | **10–20 mA/m²** | — |
| Typical CP design current density (coated steel) | **0.1–1 mA/m²** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Specific Terminology to Remember**
> - **CP** = Cathodic Protection (most widely used globally; ~ $30 billion installed base).
> - **SACP** = Sacrificial Anode CP (also called *Galvanic Anode CP*).
> - **ICCP** = Impressed Current CP (requires rectifier + power supply).
> - **AP** = Anodic Protection (limited but extremely effective in H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄).
> - **CSE** = Copper/Copper Sulphate Reference Electrode (most common for buried/underwater CP).

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cathodic Protection — Operating Principles

CP works by **shifting the open-circuit corrosion potential (E_corr)** of the protected structure to a value more negative than the **equilibrium potential of the anodic reaction (E_M/M^n+)**. When the entire structure's surface lies in the cathodic region of its Evans diagram, **local anodes cannot form**, and uniform cathodic reactions (2H₂O + 2e⁻ → H₂ + 2OH⁻ or O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻) occur instead.

### 2.1.1 Galvanic (Sacrificial-Anode) Cathodic Protection — Operating Logic

1. Two dissimilar metals are electrically bonded and immersed in a common electrolyte.
2. The **more active metal (anode)** preferentially dissolves (e.g., Mg, Zn, Al).
3. The **protected metal (cathode)** receives electrons and is shielded.
4. No external power source is required.
5. The driving voltage equals the **galvanic potential difference** between anode and cathode.
6. Current is limited by the **galvanic voltage** (typically 0.2–0.5 V), so SACP is suited to **low-resistivity electrolytes** (seawater, moist clay) and **moderately sized structures**.

### 2.1.2 Impressed-Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) — Operating Logic

1. An **inert (or slowly consumed) auxiliary anode** is connected to the **positive terminal** of a DC rectifier.
2. The **protected structure** is connected to the **negative terminal**.
3. The rectifier **forces DC current** from anode → electrolyte → protected structure → back to rectifier.
4. Voltage can be adjusted (typically 5–50 V DC) to overcome high-resistivity electrolytes.
5. Anode materials are **inert or semi-inert**: high-silicon cast iron, graphite, platinized titanium (Pt/Ti), mixed metal oxide (MMO/Ti), scrap steel.
6. ICCP is used for **large structures in high-resistivity media**: long pipelines, water tanks, marine platforms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does CP reduce corrosion rate to near zero?**
> When the structure's polarized potential lies at the **protective potential**, the **net anodic current density on the structure = 0**. By the **Mixed Potential Theory (Evans Diagram)**, the **corrosion current density i_corr → 0** because the cathodic polarization curve is forced to intersect the anodic curve at the most negative point of the active region (or below the equilibrium potential of M/M^n+).

## 2.2 Cathodic Protection Design — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Determine the total surface area (A_cathode)** to be protected (m²).
2. **Select a design current density (i_cd)** based on coating condition and environment (mA/m²).
3. **Calculate the total current required**: I_total = A_cathode × i_cd.
4. **Estimate the electrolyte resistivity (ρ)** in Ω·m.
5. **Compute the anode-to-cathode circuit resistance (R)**.
6. **For ICCP**: select rectifier voltage (V_R) and size the anode (number, length, spacing).
7. **For SACP**: select anode mass such that **anode life ≥ service life**.
8. **Verify protection potential** at the most distant (electrically remote) point using the **100-mV polarization-decay criterion**.

## 2.3 Cathodic Protection — Anode Materials (SACP)

| Anode | Open-Circuit Potential (V vs. CSE) | Theoretical Capacity (A·h/kg) | Practical Efficiency (%) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Magnesium (Mg-6Al-3Zn) | −1.55 | 2,200 | 50 | High driving voltage; dry soils |
| Zinc (high-purity) | −1.10 | 820 | 90 | Seawater, low-resistivity soils |
| Aluminium (Al-Zn-In) | −1.05 | 2,980 | 85–95 | Seawater; cheaper than Zn |

## 2.4 Cathodic Protection — Anode Backfill (for buried SACP)

Backfill (a low-resistivity mixture placed around the buried anode) typically contains:
- **Gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O)** — 75% — for ionic conductivity.
- **Bentonite clay** — 20% — to retain moisture and swell.
- **Sodium sulphate (Na₂SO₄)** — 5% — to maintain low resistivity.

## 2.5 Anodic Protection — Operating Principles

AP is conceptually the **opposite of CP** in some senses:
- The **protected structure is the anode**.
- A **potentiostat** holds the structure's potential in the **passive region** of the anodic polarization curve.
- The cathodic reaction is split: part occurs on the **auxiliary cathode** (e.g., stainless steel, Pt, nickel), part is **oxygen reduction** at the protected surface.
- A **reference electrode** continuously measures the structure's potential; the potentiostat adjusts the current to keep it within the passive window.

### 2.5.1 The Three Critical Current Densities

- **Critical Passivation Current Density (i_cc)**: the current density at the **Flade potential (E_F)** above which the metal is fully passivated. Often **1000× the passive current density** — this is the *start-up* surge.
- **Passive Current Density (i_p)**: the small, steady current density required to **maintain** the passive film — typically 1–10 µA/cm².
- **Transpassive Current Density (i_t)**: current rises again above the **transpassive potential (E_T)** due to pitting, oxygen evolution, or Cr⁶⁺ formation. The operating potential must stay **below E_T**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake**: Students often confuse the **cathodic** and **anodic** current densities. In AP, the *passive* state is achieved **only after** crossing the active peak, so during start-up a large current burst (i_cc) is required. Once passive, the current collapses to i_p.

## 2.6 Anodic Protection — Equipment

| Component | Function | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|
| Reference electrode | Senses E of structure | Ag/AgCl, Hg/HgSO₄, Pt/air, Pb/PbO₂ (in H₂SO₄) |
| Potentiostat | Controls potential | Electronic three-terminal controller |
| Auxiliary cathode | Completes the circuit | Stainless steel (304/316), nickel, platinum |
| Power supply | Provides DC | Low-voltage DC, current-limited |

## 2.7 Anodic Protection — Industrial Applications

| Industry | Equipment | Environment | Why AP? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sulphuric acid storage | Mild steel tanks | 93–98% H₂SO₄ at 25–150 °C | CP cannot protect the *inside*; AP is ideal |
| Phosphoric acid | Reactors, tanks | 30–100% H₃PO₄ | Stainless steel would pit; mild steel passivates |
| Aluminium hydroxide | Digesters | Caustic solutions (NaOH) | Conventional steel cannot be used |
| Ammonium nitrate | Tanks | 80–95% NH₄NO₃ | Mild steel passivates at controlled potential |

## 2.8 Advantages & Limitations — CP vs. AP

| Criterion | Cathodic Protection (CP) | Anodic Protection (AP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Metals applicable** | All metals | Only active–passive metals (Fe, Cr, Ni, Ti, SS) |
| **Electrolyte** | Any conductive medium | Strong acids, salt solutions |
| **Current source** | Galvanic or rectifier | Potentiostat mandatory |
| **Control type** | Constant current or constant potential | Constant potential (mandatory) |
| **Cost** | Moderate to high | High (electronic control) |
| **Maintenance** | Anode replacement | Sensor/electronics replacement |
| **Risk** | Hydrogen embrittlement, overprotection | Transpassive pitting, acid burns |
| **Typical current** | 10–100 mA/m² | 0.001–1 mA/m² (passive) |

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas in the table are required for KTU 2024 ESE Numerical Problems on Module 4.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M_w}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's law of mass loss (anode consumption) | kg |
| 2 | $I_{total} = A_{cathode} \cdot i_{cd}$ | Total CP current required | A |
| 3 | $i_{cd} = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | Current density | A/m² |
| 4 | $A_{cathode} \cdot i_{cathode} = A_{anode} \cdot i_{anode}$ | Charge-balance: equal charge in/out of electrolyte | A |
| 5 | $N_{anodes} = \dfrac{I_{total}}{I_{per \, anode}}$ | Number of anodes | dimensionless |
| 6 | $L_{life} = \dfrac{m_{anode} \cdot u}{8760 \cdot I_{per \, anode}}$ | Anode life in years (u = utilization factor, dimensionless) | years |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ | Sunde's equation — single vertical anode resistance to remote earth | Ω |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{L}{h}\right) - 2 + \dfrac{2h}{L}\right]$ | Dwight's equation — single horizontal anode | Ω |
| 9 | $\rho = 2\pi a \cdot R_{measured}$ | Soil resistivity (Wenner 4-pin method) | Ω·m |
| 10 | $\Delta E = \dfrac{\rho \cdot I \cdot L}{2\pi d}$ | Voltage drop per unit length in pipeline (Ohm's law on cylindrical geometry) | V |
| 11 | $P_{rectifier} = V_{DC} \cdot I_{DC}$ | Rectifier DC power output | W |
| 12 | $V_{DC} = I \cdot (R_{anode} + R_{cathode} + R_{cable})$ | Kirchhoff's voltage law around the CP loop | V |
| 13 | $E_{protected} \le E_{Fe/Fe^{2+}} + \Delta E_{polarization}$ | Schutz's criterion (steel) — protective potential must be more negative | V |
| 14 | $\eta = \dfrac{i_{cc}}{i_p}$ | AP passivation ratio (must be high for safety) | dimensionless |
| 15 | $P_{AP} = I_{passive} \cdot (E_{anode} - E_{cathode})$ | AP power consumption (very small) | W |

> **Constants**: $F = 96\,485$ C mol⁻¹, $M_{Mg} = 24.3$ g mol⁻¹, $M_{Zn} = 65.4$ g mol⁻¹, $M_{Al} = 27.0$ g mol⁻¹, $M_{Fe} = 55.85$ g mol⁻¹.

## 2.10 Real-World Utility

- **Offshore platforms** (e.g., Mumbai High, North Sea platforms) — ICCP protects the submerged hull, risers, and jacket structures.
- **Cross-country pipelines** (e.g., Hazira–Vijaipur–Jagdishpur gas pipeline) — ICCP at 25–50 km intervals.
- **Ships and boats** — SACP with Al-Zn-In anodes bolted to the hull.
- **Sulphuric acid plants** (e.g., Hindalco, Sterlite Cu smelters) — AP on 93% H₂SO₄ storage tanks.
- **Buried water mains** — ICCP with MMO/Ti anodes in coke-asphalt backfill.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cathode Area / Anode Area Current Balance

**Statement**: In any electrochemical protection circuit, the **total current entering the cathode = total current leaving the anode** (Kirchhoff's Current Law applied to the electrolyte). This is a fundamental charge-conservation principle.

$$
A_{cathode} \cdot i_{cathode} = A_{anode} \cdot i_{anode}
$$

where:
- $A_{cathode}$ = area of the protected structure (m²)
- $i_{cathode}$ = cathodic current density flowing into the protected structure (A/m²)
- $A_{anode}$ = area of the auxiliary anode (m²)
- $i_{anode}$ = anodic current density at the auxiliary anode (A/m²)

**Step 1 — Express current densities from Faraday's law:**
For the cathode (oxygen reduction, n = 4):
$$
i_{cathode} = \dfrac{n_{cathode} \cdot F \cdot r_{O_2}}{M_{O_2}}
$$
For the anode (metal dissolution, n = 2 for most divalent metals):
$$
i_{anode} = \dfrac{n_{anode} \cdot F \cdot r_{M}}{M_M}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply charge conservation (no accumulation of charge in electrolyte):**
$$
I_{cathode} = I_{anode}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute current as $I = A \cdot i$:**
$$
A_{cathode} \cdot i_{cathode} = A_{anode} \cdot i_{anode}
$$

**Step 4 — Express $A_{anode}$ in terms of $A_{cathode}$:**
$$
A_{anode} = \dfrac{A_{cathode} \cdot i_{cathode}}{i_{anode}}
$$

**Real-world use**: This is the **first design step** in any CP system. For a 1000 m² pipeline ($A_{cathode}$) requiring $i_{cathode} = 10$ mA/m², with a Zn anode rated at $i_{anode} = 100$ mA/m², the **required anode area = 100 m²**, which translates to **N anodes** of selected geometry.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Anode Life (Sacrificial Anode)

**Statement**: The service life of a sacrificial anode (in years) is the ratio of its **usable metal mass** to its **mass loss rate**, with appropriate unit conversion.

$$
L_{life} = \dfrac{m_{anode} \cdot u}{8760 \cdot I_{per\,anode}}
$$

where:
- $L_{life}$ = anode life (years)
- $m_{anode}$ = mass of the anode (kg)
- $u$ = utilization factor (typically 0.85 for Zn, 0.50 for Mg; dimensionless)
- $8760$ = hours per year (h/year)
- $I_{per\,anode}$ = current output of one anode (A)

**Step 1 — Faraday's law for the mass dissolved per second:**
From Faraday's law, the mass of metal dissolved per unit time is:
$$
\dfrac{dm}{dt} = \dfrac{I \cdot M_w}{n \cdot F}
$$

**Step 2 — For zinc (anodic reaction Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻), $M_w = 65.4$ g mol⁻¹, n = 2:**
$$
\dfrac{dm}{dt} = \dfrac{I \cdot 65.4}{2 \cdot 96\,485} = \dfrac{I \cdot 65.4}{192\,970} \quad [\text{g/s}]
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mass loss per hour:**
$$
\dfrac{dm}{dt} = \dfrac{I \cdot 65.4 \cdot 3600}{192\,970} = I \cdot 1.220 \quad [\text{g/h}]
$$

**Step 4 — Account for utilization factor u (not all anode mass is consumable):**
$$
\text{Usable mass} = m_{anode} \cdot u
$$

**Step 5 — Time to consume usable mass:**
$$
t = \dfrac{m_{anode} \cdot u}{I \cdot 1.220} \quad [\text{h}]
$$

**Step 6 — Convert hours to years (divide by 8760):**
$$
L_{life} = \dfrac{m_{anode} \cdot u}{8760 \cdot I \cdot 1.220} \quad [\text{years}]
$$

**Step 7 — Generalize for any metal of atomic mass $M_w$ and valence n:**
$$
L_{life} = \dfrac{m_{anode} \cdot u \cdot n \cdot F}{8760 \cdot I \cdot M_w}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style)**:
A 5 kg Zn anode (u = 0.85) supplies 0.5 A in seawater. Calculate life.
- Usable mass = $5 \times 0.85 = 4.25$ kg = 4250 g
- Mass loss per hour = $0.5 \times 1.220 = 0.610$ g/h
- Life = $4250 / 0.610 = 6967$ h = **0.795 years ≈ 290 days**

> ⚠️ **Common Error**: Forgetting to multiply by u, or omitting the **per-anode** current, or mixing kg vs g. KTU marks are awarded for full unit tracking.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sunde's Equation (Vertical Anode Ground Resistance)

**Statement**: The electrical resistance between a single vertical cylindrical anode and remote earth is a key input to the ICCP rectifier sizing. Sunde's equation is derived from the field of a line current source in a uniform infinite medium.

$$
R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]
$$

where:
- $R$ = resistance to remote earth (Ω)
- $\rho$ = soil resistivity (Ω·m)
- $L$ = anode length (m)
- $d$ = anode diameter (m)

**Step 1 — Begin with the electric field of a line current source in a homogeneous medium of resistivity $\rho$:**
For a current $I$ entering at a point, the potential at distance $r$ is:
$$
V(r) = \dfrac{\rho \cdot I}{4\pi r}
$$

**Step 2 — For a finite-length vertical line anode, integrate along the line:**
$$
V_{anode} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot I}{4\pi L} \int_{0}^{L} \dfrac{dz}{r(z)}
$$

**Step 3 — Approximate for $L \gg d$ (long thin anode):**
$$
V_{anode} \approx \dfrac{\rho \cdot I}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]
$$

**Step 4 — By definition of resistance $R = V/I$:**
$$
R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Dimensional check**: $[\rho] = \Omega\cdot m$, $[L] = m$, $[d] = m$, so $R = \Omega\cdot m / m \cdot \ln(\text{dimensionless}) = \Omega$. ✓

**Step 6 — Parallel resistance for N identical anodes:**
$$
R_{N} = \dfrac{R}{N} \quad \text{(if anodes are far apart)}
$$

**Numerical Example**: Soil resistivity 50 Ω·m, $L = 3$ m, $d = 0.05$ m, N = 10 anodes.
- $R_{single} = 50 / (2\pi \cdot 3) \times [\ln(8 \times 3 / 0.05) - 1] = 2.653 \times [\ln(480) - 1] = 2.653 \times [6.173 - 1] = 2.653 \times 5.173 = 13.72$ Ω
- $R_{10} = 13.72 / 10 = 1.372$ Ω
- Required voltage for I = 5 A: $V = I \cdot R = 5 \times 1.372 = 6.86$ V

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Soil Resistivity from Wenner 4-Pin Method

**Statement**: Soil resistivity is measured in the field by driving **four equally-spaced electrodes** in a straight line and passing current between the outer two while measuring voltage across the inner two.

$$
\rho = 2\pi a \cdot R_{measured}
$$

**Step 1 — Configuration**: four pins at equal spacing $a$ (m). Current $I$ flows through outer pins (C1, C2). Voltage $V$ measured across inner pins (P1, P2).

**Step 2 — From current-source theory in a homogeneous half-space, the potential difference between two surface points at distance $a$ from a current source $I$ is:**
$$
V_{P1} = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi a}, \quad V_{P2} = -\dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi a}
$$

(The factor 2 in denominator arises because of the **image** of the current source about the air-soil interface.)

**Step 3 — Voltage difference between P1 and P2:**
$$
\Delta V = V_{P1} - V_{P2} = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi a} - \left(-\dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi a}\right) = \dfrac{\rho I}{\pi a}
$$

**Step 4 — Measured resistance:**
$$
R_{measured} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{I} = \dfrac{\rho}{\pi a}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\rho$:**
$$
\rho = \pi a \cdot R_{measured} \cdot 2 = 2\pi a \cdot R_{measured}
$$

**Step 6 — Note**: The spacing $a$ is **proportional to the depth of investigation** — larger $a$ measures deeper soil layers. This is how the soil-resistivity *profile* vs. depth is obtained.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Anodic Protection Power Consumption

**Statement**: The DC power consumed by an anodic protection system equals the product of passive current and cell voltage.

$$
P_{AP} = I_{passive} \cdot (E_{anode} - E_{cathode})
$$

**Step 1 — Define passive current for a protected area $A$:**
$$
I_{passive} = A \cdot i_{p}
$$

where $i_{p}$ is the passive current density (typically 1–10 µA/cm² for stainless steel in H₂SO₄).

**Step 2 — Define cell voltage:**
The potentiostat must supply voltage equal to the difference between the **anode (structure) potential** and the **auxiliary cathode potential** in the electrolyte, plus the **ohmic drop** in the electrolyte.

$$
E_{cell} = (E_{anode} - E_{cathode}) + I \cdot R_{electrolyte}
$$

**Step 3 — Power product:**
$$
P_{AP} = I_{passive} \cdot E_{cell} \approx I_{passive} \cdot (E_{anode} - E_{cathode})
$$

(Ohmic drop is usually small because the structure's surface area is large.)

**Numerical Example (KTU-style)**: A 50 m² mild steel tank containing 93% H₂SO₄ is anodically protected. The passive current density is 5 µA/cm² (= 50 mA/m²). The reference/cathode potential is −0.5 V vs. SHE; the structure is held at +0.5 V vs. SHE.

- $I_{passive} = 50 \times 0.050 = 2.5$ A
- $E_{cell} = 0.5 - (-0.5) = 1.0$ V
- $P_{AP} = 2.5 \times 1.0 = 2.5$ W

Compare this to **CP power** of similar scope: 100 W–10 kW. **AP is overwhelmingly more energy-efficient**, which justifies its high electronic-control cost.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Selecting CP vs. AP

| Question | If YES → | If NO → |
|---|---|---|
| Is the structure a buried/underwater Fe-based system? | **CP** | Consider other techniques |
| Is the environment highly resistive (>100 Ω·m)? | **ICCP** (CP) | SACP may suffice |
| Is the structure an active–passive metal (Fe, Cr, Ni, Ti, SS) in concentrated acid/alkali? | **AP** is feasible | AP is not applicable |
| Is the electrolyte highly conductive (e.g., 93% H₂SO₄, 30% NaOH)? | **AP** preferred (low i_p) | CP may be more economic |
| Does the structure have an internal surface that cannot be easily coated? | **AP** is ideal | CP via impressed current |
| Is electronic instrumentation (potentiostat) acceptable? | **AP** is feasible | Stick to **CP** |
| Is the structure very large (km of pipeline, ship hull)? | **ICCP (CP)** | SACP or AP |

---

## 3.7 Worked Example — Full SACP Design (KTU-style 14-mark problem)

**Problem**: A buried 10 km long, 0.5 m diameter steel pipeline is to be protected by SACP. The pipe is bare steel in soil of resistivity 30 Ω·m. Required CP current density is 20 mA/m². Each Mg sacrificial anode (50 kg, u = 0.5) supplies a current of 0.4 A. Calculate:
(a) the total current required,
(b) the number of anodes,
(c) the anode life.

**Solution**:

**Step 1 (a) — Total current required**:

Surface area of pipe: $A = \pi \cdot D \cdot L = \pi \cdot 0.5 \cdot 10\,000 = 15\,708$ m².

$$
I_{total} = A \cdot i_{cd} = 15\,708 \times 0.020 = 314.16 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2 (b) — Number of anodes**:

$$
N = \dfrac{I_{total}}{I_{per\,anode}} = \dfrac{314.16}{0.4} = 785.4 \approx 786 \text{ anodes}
$$

**Step 3 (c) — Anode life**:

Using $L_{life} = m_{anode} \cdot u \cdot n \cdot F / (8760 \cdot I \cdot M_w)$ for Mg ($M_w = 24.3$ g/mol, n = 2):

$$
L_{life} = \dfrac{50 \times 0.5 \times 2 \times 96\,485}{8760 \times 0.4 \times 24.3} = \dfrac{4\,824\,250}{85\,140} = 56.66 \text{ years}
$$

Alternatively, using the simpler form (with Mg's practical capacity ~1100 A·h/kg and u = 0.5):

$$
\text{Effective capacity} = 50 \times 1100 \times 0.5 = 27\,500 \text{ A·h}
$$

$$
L_{life} = \dfrac{27\,500}{8760 \times 0.4} = \dfrac{27\,500}{3504} = 7.85 \text{ years}
$$

> ⚠️ **Discrepancy note**: The two methods differ because the **practical capacity** of Mg (~1100 A·h/kg with u) already includes the utilization factor. KTU expects students to **state which assumption** they use. For Mg, KTU commonly uses **1100 A·h/kg (effective)** for life calculation.

**Result**: With 786 anodes along 10 km, the design life is **≈ 7.85 years**.

## 4.2 Sacrificial Anode CP (SACP) — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MgAnode["Mg Sacrificial Anode (-1.55 V vs CSE)"] -->|Dissolves| Electrolyte1["Soil Electrolyte (low resistivity)"]
    Electrolyte1 -->|Electron flow through wire| SteelPipe["Steel Pipeline (Cathode, protected)"]
    SteelPipe -->|Cathodic reaction 2H2O + 2e minus gives H2 + 2OH minus| Electrolyte1
    Electrolyte1 -->|Completes ionic circuit| MgAnode
    ProtectionCheck["Protection Potential -850 mV vs CSE"] -.Monitor.-> SteelPipe
    RefElectrode["Reference Electrode Cu CuSO4"] -.Senses.-> SteelPipe
```

## 4.3 Impressed Current CP (ICCP) — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ACSource["AC Mains 230V"] --> Rectifier["DC RECTIFIER 0-50V, 0-50A"]
    Rectifier -->|Positive terminal via cable| MMOAnode["MMO / Ti Impressed Anode (inert)"]
    MMOAnode -->|Forces DC current through| Electrolyte2["High Resistivity Soil or Water"]
    Electrolyte2 --> SteelStructure["Steel Structure (Cathode, polarized negative)"]
    SteelStructure -->|Returns via cable| Rectifier
    RefElectrode2["Permanent Reference Electrode (Cu CuSO4)"] -->|Senses E of structure| Controller["Controller adjusts Rectifier V"]
    Controller -->|Feedback| Rectifier
```

## 4.4 Anodic Protection — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PowerSupply["DC Power Supply"] -->|Positive terminal| Structure["Protected Steel Structure (Anode)"]
    Structure -->|Held in Passive Region by potentiostat| AcidTank["Concentrated Acid H2SO4 or H3PO4"]
    AcidTank -->|Ionic conduction| AuxCathode["Auxiliary Cathode (SS 304 or Ni)"]
    AuxCathode -->|Negative terminal| PowerSupply
    RefElectrode3["Reference Electrode (Ag AgCl or Pb PbO2)"] -->|Senses E| Structure
    Potentiostat["Potentiostat (3-terminal controller)"] -->|Compares E ref to E setpoint| PowerSupply
    PowerSupply -->|Adjusts I to maintain E| Structure
    PassiveFilm["Passive Film Fe2O3 or Cr2O3 on surface"] -.Forms and is sustained.-> Structure
```

## 4.5 Anodic Polarization Curve — Decision Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Active Region: E less than E corr, i rises steeply"] --> B["Active Peak: i = i cc, critical current density"]
    B --> C["Active to Passive Transition: Flade Potential E F"]
    C --> D["Passive Region: E F less than E less than E T, i collapses to i p"]
    D --> E["Transpassive Region: E greater than E T, i rises due to pitting or O2 evolution"]
    A -.Anodic Protection operates here.-> D
    A -.Corrosion damage here.-> B
    E -.Avoid this region in AP.-> D
```

## 4.6 CP Monitoring & Survey Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PipeLine["Buried Steel Pipeline (Cathode)"] -->|Test Station at every 1-2 km| TestStation1["Test Station 1"]
    PipeLine --> TestStation2["Test Station 2"]
    PipeLine --> TestStation3["Test Station 3"]
    TestStation1 -->|On potential measurement| CSE1["Cu CuSO4 Reference (placed on soil above pipe)"]
    TestStation2 --> CSE2["Cu CuSO4 Reference"]
    TestStation3 --> CSE3["Cu CuSO4 Reference"]
    CSE1 --> Surveyor["Corrosion Engineer (Annual Survey)"]
    CSE2 --> Surveyor
    CSE3 --> Surveyor
    Surveyor -->|If E less than minus 850 mV vs CSE| Pass["Protection Adequate"]
    Surveyor -->|If E greater than minus 850 mV vs CSE| RectifierCheck["Check Rectifier Output or Anode Mass"]
```

## 4.7 Comparison Matrix — CP vs. AP Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPCell["CATHODIC PROTECTION (CP)"]
        direction TB
        cp_a[Protected metal = CATHODE]
        cp_b[Auxiliary = ANODE dissolves]
        cp_c[CP works on ALL metals]
        cp_d[Galvanic or Rectifier]
        cp_e[Earth / Water / Seawater]
    end

    subgraph APCell["ANODIC PROTECTION (AP)"]
        direction TB
        ap_a[Protected metal = ANODE]
        ap_b[Auxiliary = CATHODE inert]
        ap_c[AP works only on ACTIVE PASSIVE metals]
        ap_d[MUST be Potentiostat]
        ap_e[Strong Acids or Caustic only]
    end

    CPCell --- vs["VERSUS"] --- APCell
    vs --> Decision[Select CP for buried, AP for acid tanks]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip**: Always include a **labelled schematic** in your 14-mark answers. A well-drawn circuit with anode, electrolyte, cathode, rectifier/potentiostat, and reference electrode (with all potentials labelled) carries **2–3 easy marks** in ESE.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define Cathodic Protection. List the two main methods of CP with one example each.

**Model Answer**:

**Definition**: Cathodic Protection is an electrochemical corrosion control technique in which the metal to be protected is made the **cathode** of an external electrochemical cell, so that the anodic (metal-dissolution) reaction on the protected surface is suppressed.

**Two methods**:
1. **Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)** — uses a more active metal (e.g., **Zn, Mg, or Al**) that preferentially corrodes. *Example*: Zn anodes bolted to the hull of a ship.
2. **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** — uses an external DC power source (rectifier) connected to an inert auxiliary anode. *Example*: MMO/Ti anodes with rectifier on a buried pipeline.

> ⚠️ 3-mark answer: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming both methods, 1 mark for example.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is Anodic Protection? Why is it applicable only to active–passive metals?

**Model Answer**:

**Definition**: Anodic Protection is an electrochemical corrosion-control technique in which the metal to be protected is connected as the **anode** of an external circuit and held at a constant potential **within the passive region** of its anodic polarization curve using a **potentiostat**. The passive film (oxide layer) formed on the surface drastically reduces the corrosion rate to a few micrometres per year.

**Why only active–passive metals?**
- AP relies on the existence of a **stable passive film** on the metal surface. Metals like **Fe, Cr, Ni, Ti, and stainless steels** exhibit this passivity in specific electrolytes (concentrated H₂SO₄, HNO₃, NaOH).
- Active metals (e.g., **Mg, Zn**) do **not passivate**; if polarized anodically, they simply corrode faster — AP would be useless and dangerous.
- The **Flade potential** and the **passive range** (E_F to E_T) are the operational window. Without an active peak and a stable passive plateau, no such window exists.

> ⚠️ 3-mark answer: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the role of passive film, 1 mark for the active–passive requirement.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — KTU Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Module 4]** *(a) With neat schematic, explain the principle and design of Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) for a buried pipeline. List the anode materials and reference electrodes used. (7 marks)*

*(b) A 5 km long, 30 cm diameter buried steel pipeline is to be protected by ICCP. The required CP current density is 15 mA/m². The soil resistivity is 80 Ω·m. The rectifier is to deliver 30 V. Calculate: (i) total current required, (ii) total anode-to-pipe resistance, (iii) number of vertical silicon cast-iron anodes (each 1.5 m long, 0.08 m diameter) needed. Use Sunde's equation. (7 marks)*

#### Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 marks

**Principle (2 marks)**: In ICCP, an external DC source (rectifier) drives current from an inert auxiliary anode, through the soil electrolyte, onto the protected steel pipeline (made the cathode), and back to the rectifier's negative terminal. The current density at the pipe surface shifts its potential negatively (polarization) until the **−850 mV vs. Cu/CuSO₄** protection criterion is met everywhere on the pipe.

**Schematic (2 marks)** — Describe the block diagram from SECTION 4.3 (AC mains → rectifier → MMO anode → soil → pipeline → return cable). Label all potentials: E_anode, E_pipe, E_ref (CSE).

**Anode materials (1.5 marks)**:
- High-silicon cast iron (14–16% Si)
- Graphite
- Platinized titanium (Pt/Ti)
- Mixed metal oxide coated titanium (MMO/Ti)
- Scrap steel (consumable, used in deep ground beds)

**Reference electrodes (1.5 marks)**:
- **Cu/CuSO₄** — for soil/underground use (most common)
- **Ag/AgCl** — for seawater
- **Saturated calomel (SCE)** — laboratory
- **Zinc** — permanent buried reference
- **Hg/HgSO₄** — for H₂SO₄ environments

#### Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (i) — Total current required (2 marks)**:
Pipeline surface area: $A = \pi \cdot D \cdot L = \pi \cdot 0.30 \cdot 5000 = 4712.4$ m².
$$
I_{total} = A \cdot i_{cd} = 4712.4 \times 0.015 = 70.69 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2 (ii) — Total circuit resistance (1.5 marks)**:
Using Ohm's law for the loop (assuming cable + back-resistance is small):
$$
R_{total} = \dfrac{V_{DC}}{I_{total}} = \dfrac{30}{70.69} = 0.424 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 3 (iii) — Sunde's equation for single anode (1.5 marks)**:
$R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$

For L = 1.5 m, d = 0.08 m, ρ = 80 Ω·m:
$$
R_{single} = \dfrac{80}{2\pi \times 1.5}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8 \times 1.5}{0.08}\right) - 1\right]
$$
$$
= \dfrac{80}{9.425} \left[\ln(150) - 1\right] = 8.488 \times [5.011 - 1] = 8.488 \times 4.011 = 34.05 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of anodes in parallel (2 marks)**:
$$
N = \dfrac{R_{single}}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{34.05}{0.424} = 80.3 \approx 81 \text{ anodes}
$$

**Conclusion**: 81 vertical Si cast-iron anodes, each 1.5 m × 0.08 m, connected in parallel, will provide the required 70.7 A at 30 V in 80 Ω·m soil.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — KTU Choice Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Module 4]** *(a) Explain the principle of Anodic Protection with a suitable E-log i diagram. Mention the equipment used and list three industrial applications. (7 marks)*

*(b) An 80 m² mild steel tank holds 93% H₂SO₄ at 60 °C. Anodic protection is to be applied. The critical passivation current density is 200 µA/cm² and the passive current density is 5 µA/cm². The cell voltage is 1.2 V. Calculate: (i) start-up current required, (ii) steady-state power consumption, (iii) current density ratio η (i_cc/i_p). (7 marks)*

#### Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 marks

**Principle (2 marks)**: The steel structure is connected as the **anode** of an external circuit, and a potentiostat holds its potential in the **passive region** of its anodic polarization curve. A reference electrode continuously monitors the structure's potential; the potentiostat adjusts the auxiliary cathode current to maintain the setpoint.

**E-log i diagram description (2 marks)**: A standard anodic polarization curve has four regions:
- **Active region** (E < E_corr): i rises rapidly with E.
- **Active peak** at i_cc (critical passivation current density).
- **Active–passive transition** at the **Flade potential E_F**.
- **Passive region** (E_F < E < E_T): i collapses to i_p (passive current density) and stays low.
- **Transpassive region** (E > E_T): i rises again (pitting, oxygen evolution, Cr⁶⁺).
- The operating point is chosen in the **middle of the passive plateau**.

**Equipment (1.5 marks)**: Reference electrode, potentiostat (3-terminal electronic controller), auxiliary cathode (Pt, Ni, or SS), DC power supply, connecting cables, instrumentation for log/monitoring.

**Industrial applications (1.5 marks)**:
1. **Sulphuric acid storage tanks** (93–98% H₂SO₄, mild steel).
2. **Phosphoric acid** digesters and storage.
3. **Ammonium nitrate** storage tanks.
4. **Caustic (NaOH) handling** equipment where passivity is exploitable.

#### Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (i) — Start-up current (2 marks)**:
$$
I_{startup} = A \cdot i_{cc} = 80 \times 0.200 = 16 \text{ A}
$$
(Converting i_cc = 200 µA/cm² = 200 × 10⁻⁶ A / 10⁻⁴ m² = 2.0 A/m² × ... wait: 200 µA/cm² = 0.200 A/m²; then $I = 80 \times 0.200 = 16$ A.)

> Recalculation check: $1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$. So $i_{cc} = 200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A} / 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 2 \text{ A/m}^2$. Then $I = 80 \text{ m}^2 \times 2 \text{ A/m}^2 = 160 \text{ A}$.

**Corrected**: $I_{startup} = 80 \times 2.0 = 160$ A (this is the *large* start-up surge).

**Step 2 (ii) — Steady-state power (2.5 marks)**:
Passive current density: $i_p = 5 \text{ µA/cm}^2 = 0.050 \text{ A/m}^2$.
$$
I_{passive} = 80 \times 0.050 = 4.0 \text{ A}
$$
$$
P_{steady} = I_{passive} \times E_{cell} = 4.0 \times 1.2 = 4.8 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 (iii) — Passivation ratio (2.5 marks)**:
$$
\eta = \dfrac{i_{cc}}{i_p} = \dfrac{200 \text{ µA/cm}^2}{5 \text{ µA/cm}^2} = 40
$$

> **Conclusion**: A high η (= 40) is favourable because it means the **start-up surge** (160 A) is only transient; once passive, the system operates at a tiny 4.8 W — a hallmark energy efficiency of AP.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Read Carefully — Marks Lost Here)**
>
> 1. **Confusing units of current density**: KTU problems switch between **mA/m²**, **µA/cm²**, and **A/cm²**. Always convert: 1 mA/m² = 0.1 µA/cm² = 10⁻⁴ A/m².
> 2. **Forgetting utilization factor (u)**: In SACP anode-life problems, the practical capacity already includes u; the Faraday form requires you to multiply by u explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing anodic & cathodic**: In AP, the protected structure is the **anode** — not the cathode. Many students write the opposite.
> 4. **Not labelling the schematic**: An ESE 14-mark answer without a labelled diagram forfeits ~2 marks easily.
> 5. **Mixing electrode potentials**: Always state which **reference electrode** (SHE, CSE, Ag/AgCl, SCE) the potential is measured against. KTU expects a specific reference, not a generic "volts".
> 6. **Selecting wrong anode material for the electrolyte**: Mg in seawater corrodes by *parasitic* hydrogen evolution and gives only 50% efficiency — must use Zn or Al in seawater. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this if you specify Mg for marine SACP.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density KTU Module 4 Revision — Cathodic & Anodic Protection**

- [x] **CP definition**: Protected structure = **cathode**; auxiliary = **anode**. **AP definition**: Protected structure = **anode**; auxiliary = **cathode**. (Opposite of CP!)
- [x] **SACP driving force** = galvanic potential difference (~0.2–0.5 V, self-limiting).
- [x] **ICCP driving force** = rectifier voltage (5–50 V, adjustable, high current).
- [x] **Anode materials — SACP**: **Mg, Zn, Al** (consumable). **ICCP**: **MMO/Ti, Pt/Ti, Si cast iron, graphite** (inert or slow-consumption).
- [x] **Reference electrode for soil CP**: **Cu/CuSO₄ (CSE)**. **For seawater**: **Ag/AgCl**. **For H₂SO₄**: **Pb/PbO₂ or Hg/HgSO₄**.
- [x] **Protection criterion for steel in soil**: **E ≤ −850 mV vs. CSE** (absolute), or **100 mV polarization decay** (negative shift).
- [x] **Anode backfill** (SACP): **75% gypsum + 20% bentonite + 5% Na₂SO₄** by mass.
- [x] **AP applicability**: Only **active–passive metals** (Fe, Ni, Cr, Ti, SS) in **concentrated acids/alkali** where a stable passive film forms.
- [x] **AP requires**: **Potentiostat + reference electrode + inert auxiliary cathode**.
- [x] **AP three current densities**: $i_{cc}$ (critical, large) > $i_{p}$ (passive, small) > $i_{t}$ (transpassive, rising). Operate in the **passive plateau** between E_F (Flade) and E_T (transpassive).
- [x] **Faraday's law**: $m = (I \cdot t \cdot M_w) / (n \cdot F)$, with **F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹**.
- [x] **Anode life formula**: $L_{life} = (m \cdot u \cdot n \cdot F) / (8760 \cdot I \cdot M_w)$ — remember 8760 h/yr.
- [x] **Sunde's equation** (vertical anode): $R = (\rho / 2\pi L)[\ln(8L/d) - 1]$.
- [x] **Wenner 4-pin soil resistivity**: $\rho = 2\pi a \cdot R_{measured}$.
- [x] **AP power**: tiny (few watts for tanks), much smaller than CP power (kW).
- [x] **CP risks**: **Hydrogen embrittlement** (high-strength steel), **overprotection** (coating disbondment, alkaline attack on coatings, carbonate deposition).
- [x] **AP risks**: **Transpassive pitting** (going too positive), **acid burns** during maintenance (electronics in acid zones).
- [x] **KTU numerical favourites**:
  - SACP anode life (Zn/Mg/Al, mass × utilization)
  - ICCP rectifier sizing (Sunde's + I_total + V_DC)
  - AP start-up vs. steady-state current (i_cc vs. i_p)
  - Pipeline attenuation / IR drop in long lines.
- [x] **Standards** (for awareness): **NACE SP0169** (buried pipelines), **ISO 15589-1/2** (CP of pipelines), **NACE SP0108** (CP of reinforced concrete), **API RP 651** (CP of aboveground storage tanks).
- [x] **First successful CP**: Sir Humphry Davy, **1824**, protected the copper sheathing of British Navy wooden ships using Zn anodes.
- [x] **First industrial AP**: **1954**, **Dow Chemical Company** for caustic storage; **Edeleanu** later for H₂SO₄ tanks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion and Its Control in Industries: Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Corrosion and Its Control in Industries: Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Corrosion in the Power Industry** is defined as the irreversible deterioration of metallic components in power generation systems (thermal, nuclear, and hydroelectric plants) caused by electrochemical, chemical, and mechanical interactions with aggressive service environments. These environments include high-temperature steam, combustion gases, cooling water, molten salts, condensed acids, and supercritical fluids.

The most critical degradation modes observed in power-generation hardware are:

1. **High-Temperature Oxidation (HTO)** of boiler tubes, superheaters, and reheaters exposed to hot combustion gases (often > 600 °C).
2. **Steam-Side Oxidation** of ferritic and austenitic steels producing exfoliating magnetite/hematite scales inside water-wall tubes, risers, and turbine blades.
3. **Flow-Accelerated Corrosion (FAC)** in single-phase feedwater and two-phase wet-steam piping.
4. **Caustic Embrittlement** and **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** in riveted/bolted boiler drums and austenitic superheater tubes.
5. **Hot Corrosion** (Type I: 700–950 °C sulfate melts; Type II: 600–750 °C) on gas-turbine blades by fused Na₂SO₄–V₂O₅–NaCl deposits.
6. **Cold-End / Dew-Point Corrosion** in air preheaters, economizers, and flue-gas ducts.
7. **Pitting and Crevice Corrosion** in condensers (titanium, cupronickel, stainless-steel tubes) handling saline cooling water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** For Module-4 industry studies, the examiner expects students to correlate **environment → mechanism → affected alloy → control measure**. Memorising just the *name* of the corrosion type (e.g., "FAC") without the *counter-measure* will cost marks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a power plant as a **human body's circulatory system**:

| Body Part | Power-Plant Equivalent | What Corrosion Does |
|---|---|---|
| Heart | Boiler / Steam generator | Pumping action weakens due to tube thinning |
| Arteries | Steam piping, superheater | "Arterial plaque" (oxide scale) builds up and flakes off |
| Lungs | Condenser + cooling tower | Heat exchange is choked by biofouling and pitting |
| Bones | Turbine blades (Ni-superalloys) | Structural fatigue from corrosion-fatigue |
| Stomach acid | Combustion-gas condensate (H₂SO₄ dew) | Stomach ulcers → dew-point corrosion |

Just as a slow arterial blockage eventually causes a heart attack, **progressive oxide-scale growth on a superheater tube leads to a catastrophic boiler-tube failure** — a single tube rupture can trip a 500 MW unit, causing losses of **several crore rupees per day**.

## 1.3 Critical Operating Parameters and Constants

The following numerical thresholds govern the design life of power-plant components and are routinely tested in KTU examinations:

- **Maximum allowable tube-wall temperature in coal-fired superheaters:** **600–650 °C** (ferritic steels) and **650–700 °C** (austenitic TP347H).
- **Dew-point of sulphuric acid vapour from sulphur-bearing fuels:** **130–160 °C** — below this, cold-end corrosion becomes severe.
- **Recommended feedwater dissolved oxygen:** **< 7 µg/L (ppb)** for once-through boilers, **< 5 µg/L** for sub-critical drum boilers.
- **Recommended boiler-water pH at 25 °C:** **8.8 – 9.6** (coordinated phosphate) or **9.2 – 9.6** (caustic).
- **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** threshold for protective oxide: **1 < PBR < 2**.

> [!NOTE]
> A PBR **less than 1** (e.g., alkali and alkaline-earth metals) produces porous, non-protective oxide — the metal *cannot* form a passive layer.
> A PBR **greater than 2** (e.g., iron at high T) causes the scale to spall under compressive stress.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of Corrosion Damage in a Coal-Fired Power Plant

A modern **subcritical 500 MW unit** has the following corrosion-prone zones (from air intake to chimney):

**Air Side → Coal Pulveriser → Furnace → Water-Wall Tubes → Superheater → Reheater → HP/IP/LP Turbines → Condenser → Feedwater Heaters → Economiser → Deaerator → BFP → Boiler (drum).**

Each zone has a *characteristic* corrosion mechanism, summarised in structured steps below.

### Step 1 — Furnace and Water-Wall Tubes (1 200 – 1 500 °C gas / 350 °C metal)
- **Mechanism:** External oxidation of carbon-steel tubes by H₂O, CO₂, SO₂ from coal combustion.
- **Why:** Outer surface is exposed to reducing flue gas containing H₂S (from sulphur in coal), producing iron sulphide *beneath* a porous Fe₃O₄ layer.
- **Net reaction (simplified):**
$$\text{Fe} + \text{H}_2\text{S} \rightarrow \text{FeS} + \text{H}_2$$
- **How it progresses:** FeS is *non-protective* (PBR ≈ 1.6 but porous), so oxidation continues at linear → parabolic kinetics.

### Step 2 — Superheater and Reheater Tubes (600 – 700 °C steam)
- **Mechanism:** Steam-side oxidation producing **double-layered scale**:
  - Inner layer: fine-grained, adherent **Fe–Cr spinel** (protective).
  - Outer layer: columnar, porous **magnetite (Fe₃O₄)** — this is the *exfoliation* layer.
- **Why it spalls:** Differential thermal expansion between steel (≈ 12 × 10⁻⁶ /K) and Fe₃O₄ (≈ 14–15 × 10⁻⁶ /K) creates shear stress at every start-up/shut-down cycle.
- **Consequence:** Spalled oxide is carried by steam → **solid-particle erosion** of turbine blades, particularly the first-stage nozzle vanes.

### Step 3 — High-Pressure / Intermediate-Pressure Turbines (HP/IP: 565 °C / 565 °C steam)
- **Mechanism:** **Stress-Assisted Corrosion Oxidation (SACO)** + **Hot Corrosion Type II** (Na₂SO₄ deposits melt at ~ 620 °C and dissolve the protective Cr₂O₃ layer).
- **Key chemistry:**
$$\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + \frac{1}{2}\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3 + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CrO}_4 + \text{SO}_3$$
- The chromate formation *destroys* the otherwise passive Cr₂O₃ film.

### Step 4 — LP (Low-Pressure) Turbines and Last-Stage Blades
- **Mechanism:** **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** in wet steam; **corrosion fatigue** in long LSBs (typical length 1 m, tip speed ≈ 700 m/s).
- **Why:** Chloride / sulphate deposits from carry-over concentrate in the last stages where steam is wet (moisture > 12 %).

### Step 5 — Condenser
- **Mechanism:** **Pitting, crevice, and erosion–corrosion** of titanium (Ti-Grade 2), aluminium brass, or **Cu-Ni 90/10 / 70/30** tubes.
- **Aggressives:** Cl⁻ up to **1 900 ppm** in estuarine cooling water, dissolved oxygen 6–8 ppm, sand/silt causing erosion.

### Step 6 — Economiser and Air-Preheater (Cold End)
- **Mechanism:** **Dew-point corrosion** when metal temperature falls below the **acid dew point (~ 130 °C)**. The condensed film is essentially **50–70 % H₂SO₄** by mass.
- **Net reaction:**
$$\text{Fe} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{FeSO}_4 + \text{H}_2\uparrow$$

### Step 7 — ID / FD Fans and Ductwork
- **Mechanism:** **Erosion–corrosion** by fly-ash particles (hardness ≈ 6–7 Mohs) + atmospheric oxidation.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Domain | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta m^2 = k_p \, t$  (Wagner's parabolic law) | High-T oxidation mass gain vs. time | mg² cm⁻⁴ ; s |
| 2 | $k_p = k_0 \, \exp\!\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius dependence of parabolic rate constant | g² cm⁻⁴ s⁻¹ ; kJ mol⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\text{PBR} = \dfrac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal}}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{ox}} \, \rho_{\text{m}}}{n \, M_{\text{m}} \, \rho_{\text{ox}}}$ | Pilling–Bedworth ratio (protective scale if 1 < PBR < 2) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\dot{r} = \dfrac{k_p}{2 \Delta m}$ | Instantaneous parabolic rate of thickness growth | cm s⁻¹ |
| 5 | $t_f = \dfrac{\delta^2}{k_p}$ | Time for scale to reach critical thickness δ (spallation) | s |
| 6 | $V_{\text{linear}} = k_\ell$ (mg cm⁻² h⁻¹) | Linear kinetics for porous / non-protective scales | mg cm⁻² h⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\log k_p = A - \dfrac{Q}{2.303\,R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Linearised Arrhenius form for plotting $\log k_p$ vs. $1/T$ | — |
| 8 | $v_{\text{FAC}} = k_{\text{FAC}}\, C^{n} \, v^{m}$ | FAC rate (carbon steel) — C = DO, v = velocity, n, m ≈ 0.4–1 | mm yr⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\text{CCL} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{A \, t}$ | Corrosion-current-like mass-loss indicator | g m⁻² h⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\text{IPY} = \dfrac{534 \, W}{D\,A\,t}$ | Iron penetration / year in mils (1 mil = 25.4 µm) | mils yr⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU problems often require substituting the **PBR formula** in the form shown above. Note that $M_{\text{ox}}$ is the molar mass of the *whole* oxide molecule (e.g., 159.7 g mol⁻¹ for Fe₂O₃, not 53.2 g mol⁻¹ for one Fe atom), $n$ is the number of metal atoms in the oxide formula, and $\rho$ values are in **g cm⁻³**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of superheater alloys** uses Wagner's parabolic equation to predict the scale thickness after a 30-year design life (≈ 2.5 × 10⁸ s).
- **Risk-Based Inspection (RBI)** in power plants uses predicted remaining-life values from these equations to schedule tube replacement.
- **Online corrosion monitoring** (e.g., Electrical Resistance probes, LPR probes) feeds real-time mass-loss data back to the DCS for **chemistry trim**.
- **Failure Analysis (FA)** of a ruptured boiler tube is performed by cross-referencing the oxide morphology (magnetite vs. hematite vs. FeS) against the **expected mechanism table** for the zone in which it operated.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Parabolic Oxidation Law (Wagner's Theory, 1933)

**Statement to prove:** The mass gain $\Delta m$ per unit area during high-temperature oxidation of a metal M forming a dense, adherent oxide scale follows
$$(\Delta m)^2 = k_p \, t$$
where $k_p$ is the parabolic rate constant in **mg² cm⁻⁴ s⁻¹**.

### Step-by-Step Logical Build

**Step 1 — Identify the rate-controlling process.**

At high temperature, the oxide scale grows by **counter-current diffusion** of:
- metal cations $\text{M}^{n+}$ moving **outward** through the scale, and
- electrons / oxygen anions $\text{O}^{2-}$ moving **inward** through the scale.

The slower of the two ionic fluxes controls the rate. In most engineering scales (Fe₃O₄, Cr₂O₃, Al₂O₃) the **cation vacancy diffusion** is rate-limiting.

**Step 2 — Apply Fick's first law across the scale.**

Let the scale thickness be $x$ at time $t$. The cation-vacancy concentration gradient between the *outer surface* (vacancy-rich, at oxygen partial pressure $p_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{out}}$) and the *inner interface* (vacancy-poor, at $p_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{in}}$) is

$$\frac{dC_v}{dx} = \frac{C_v^{\text{out}} - C_v^{\text{in}}}{x}$$

The flux of cations (per unit area) is therefore

$$J_{\text{M}^{n+}} = -D_v \frac{dC_v}{dx} = \frac{D_v \left(C_v^{\text{in}} - C_v^{\text{out}}\right)}{x}$$

**Step 3 — Relate mass gain to cation flux.**

For every $n$ moles of electrons per mole of metal oxidised, the mass gain per unit area is
$$\frac{d(\Delta m)}{dt} = M_{\text{ox-equivalent}} \, J_{\text{M}^{n+}}$$
where $M_{\text{ox-equivalent}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{oxide}}}{n}$ converts the ionic flux to an equivalent oxide mass.

**Step 4 — Substitute the geometric relation between $\Delta m$ and $x$.**

If the scale is dense and stoichiometric, the oxide mass per unit area is also related to scale thickness through the oxide density:
$$\Delta m = \rho_{\text{ox}} \, x \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = \frac{\Delta m}{\rho_{\text{ox}}}$$

**Step 5 — Combine and integrate.**

Substituting $x$ from Step 4 into the flux equation in Step 3:
$$\frac{d(\Delta m)}{dt} = \frac{M_{\text{ox-eq}} \, D_v \, \rho_{\text{ox}} \left(C_v^{\text{in}} - C_v^{\text{out}}\right)}{\Delta m}$$

Define the constant
$$k_p^{\prime} = 2 \, M_{\text{ox-eq}} \, D_v \, \rho_{\text{ox}} \left(C_v^{\text{in}} - C_v^{\text{out}}\right)$$

Then
$$\Delta m \, d(\Delta m) = \frac{k_p^{\prime}}{2} \, dt$$

Integrating from $t = 0$ (where $\Delta m = 0$) to time $t$:
$$\int_0^{\Delta m} \Delta m \, d(\Delta m) = \frac{k_p^{\prime}}{2} \int_0^{t} dt$$

$$\frac{(\Delta m)^2}{2} = \frac{k_p^{\prime}}{2} \, t$$

$$(\Delta m)^2 = k_p^{\prime} \, t$$

**Hence proved** — parabolic rate law. $\quad \blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The integration constant in Step 5 vanishes only because at $t = 0$ there is no pre-existing scale. In practical engineering, plants start with a *thin air-formed oxide* of thickness $x_0$, and the integrated form becomes
> $$x^2 - x_0^2 = k_p \, t$$
> This is the form used in remaining-life calculations for superheater tubes.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Pilling–Bedworth Ratio for Fe₃O₄ on Iron

**Given:**  
- Molar mass of Fe₃O₄, $M_{\text{ox}} = 231.5$ g mol⁻¹  
- Molar mass of Fe, $M_{\text{m}} = 55.85$ g mol⁻¹  
- Number of Fe atoms in formula, $n = 3$  
- Density of Fe₃O₄, $\rho_{\text{ox}} = 5.18$ g cm⁻³  
- Density of Fe, $\rho_{\text{m}} = 7.87$ g cm⁻³  

**Required:** Calculate the Pilling–Bedworth Ratio and comment on the protectiveness of the scale.

**Solution:**

Using the formula
$$\text{PBR} = \frac{M_{\text{ox}} \, \rho_{\text{m}}}{n \, M_{\text{m}} \, \rho_{\text{ox}}}$$

Substituting:
$$\text{PBR} = \frac{231.5 \times 7.87}{3 \times 55.85 \times 5.18}$$

Compute the numerator:
$$231.5 \times 7.87 = 1821.9 \text{ g}^2 \text{ cm}^{-3} \text{ mol}^{-1}$$

Compute the denominator:
$$3 \times 55.85 \times 5.18 = 3 \times 289.3 = 867.9 \text{ g}^2 \text{ cm}^{-3} \text{ mol}^{-1}$$

Therefore:
$$\text{PBR} = \frac{1821.9}{867.9} = 2.10$$

**Inference:** PBR = 2.10 **lies just above the upper protective limit of 2**, indicating that the Fe₃O₄ scale is **marginally protective** at low temperatures but becomes **non-protective at high temperatures** (≥ 570 °C) because of compressive spallation. This is why austenitic stainless steels (PBR of Cr₂O₃ ≈ 1.99) are used above 600 °C.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Remaining Life of a Superheater Tube

**Given:** A TP347H austenitic superheater tube operates at 650 °C in a 500 MW boiler. The parabolic rate constant at 650 °C is $k_p = 1.2 \times 10^{-10}$ g² cm⁻⁴ s⁻¹. The critical oxide thickness for spallation is $\delta = 80$ µm. The tube has already operated for 8 years (= 2.52 × 10⁸ s). Initial oxide thickness at start of service: $x_0 = 5$ µm.

**Required:** Estimate the remaining life of the tube.

**Solution:**

Convert $k_p$ to µm² s⁻¹. Since $1 \text{ g cm}^{-2} = 10^4$ mg cm⁻² and $1 \text{ mg} = 10^{-3}$ g, also $1 \text{ cm} = 10^4$ µm, the mass ↔ thickness conversion through oxide density $\rho_{\text{ox}} = 5.18$ g cm⁻³ (≈ Cr-rich spinel) gives:

$$k_p \text{ [µm}^2\text{s}^{-1}\text{]} = \frac{k_p \text{ [g}^2\text{cm}^{-4}\text{s}^{-1}\text{]}}{\rho_{\text{ox}}^2} \times 10^8$$

$$k_p = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-10}}{(5.18)^2} \times 10^8 = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-10}}{26.83} \times 10^8$$

$$k_p = 4.47 \times 10^{-4} \text{ µm}^2 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Critical thickness squared: $\delta^2 = 80^2 = 6\,400$ µm².

Using the integrated form $x^2 - x_0^2 = k_p \, t$:

$$t_{\text{critical}} = \frac{\delta^2 - x_0^2}{k_p} = \frac{6400 - 25}{4.47 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$t_{\text{critical}} = \frac{6375}{4.47 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.426 \times 10^7 \text{ s} = 0.45 \text{ years}$$

This is the *additional* time to reach $\delta$ from $x_0$ **at 650 °C** — but in reality, scale growth decelerates as $x$ increases, and the tube can be expected to operate for another **~ 6 – 8 years** in practice, since most of the operating duty is at partial load (lower T).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU problems of this type frequently expect the candidate to use the simplified **Booth-Mindlin** form of Wagner's equation, which gives
> $$t_{\text{life}} = \frac{2 \delta^2}{k_p}$$
> for design purposes (factor of 2 safety). The 0.45-year value above is therefore an *absolute minimum*.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Corrosion Mechanisms in Different Plant Zones

| Plant Zone | Metal Temperature (°C) | Aggressive Species | Corrosion Type | Critical Alloy Selection | Primary Control Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Furnace water-wall | 300 – 450 | H₂S, SO₂, Cl⁻ | Sulphidation, hot corrosion | T11 / T22 (1–2 % Cr-Mo) | Soot-blower optimisation, low-S coal |
| Superheater | 540 – 700 | Steam, H₂ | Steam-side oxidation | TP304H, TP347H, HR3C | Shot-peening, surface grinding |
| Reheater | 540 – 700 | Steam, H₂ | Steam-side oxidation | TP321H, HR160 | Use of Ni-based alloys (Inconel 740) |
| HP/IP turbine (Stage 1) | 565 – 600 | Na₂SO₄, V₂O₅ | Hot corrosion Type II | Inconel 738 LC, GTD-111 | TBC coating, fuel additive (MgO) |
| LP turbine (Last stage) | 60 – 120 | Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, moisture | SCC, corrosion fatigue | 17-4 PH, 12 Cr | Stress relief, water chemistry |
| Condenser tubes | 30 – 60 | Cl⁻, O₂, sand | Pitting, erosion-corrosion | Ti-Grade 2, Cu-Ni 90/10 | Cathodic protection, sponge-ball cleaning |
| Economiser | 200 – 350 | H₂SO₄ (condensate) | Dew-point corrosion | Corten, ND steel | Recirculation, alloy cladding |
| Air preheater (cold end) | 100 – 180 | H₂SO₄ + fly ash | Dew-point + erosion | Enamel-coated carbon steel | Hot-end basket replacement |
| Steam piping (carbon steel) | 250 – 350 | Dissolved O₂ | FAC, grooving | A 106 Gr.B | AVT chemistry, O₂ scavenging |

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Oxide-Scale Growth and Spallation Mechanism (Superheater Tube)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Steam as High Pressure Steam
    participant Scale as Oxide Scale Fe3O4
    participant Steel as TP347H Tube Wall
    participant Turbine as First Stage Nozzle

    Note over Steam,Steel: Stage 1 - Initial Exposure (t = 0)
    Steam->>Scale: Fe ions diffuse outward through scale
    Steel->>Scale: O anions diffuse inward to metal-scale interface
    Scale->>Scale: Growth by counter-current diffusion

    Note over Scale,Steel: Stage 2 - Steady State (t = 1 to 8 years)
    Scale->>Scale: Parabolic growth law dm^2 = kp times t
    Note over Scale: Protective Cr-rich spinel forms at inner interface
    Note over Scale: Outer layer is porous columnar Fe3O4

    Note over Scale,Steel: Stage 3 - Load Variation
    Steam->>Scale: Thermal cycling 540 C to 600 C
    Scale->>Steel: Shear stress due to CTE mismatch
    Scale->>Scale: Micro-cracks initiate at grain boundaries

    Note over Scale,Turbine: Stage 4 - Spallation Event
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** Define *hot corrosion*. Distinguish between **Type I** and **Type II** hot corrosion in terms of temperature range and dominant salt deposit. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (target ≈ 3 marks, 60–80 words):**

Hot corrosion is the accelerated oxidation of metallic surfaces caused by the presence of fused salt deposits — predominantly **sodium sulphate (Na₂SO₄)** — that flux and dissolve the otherwise protective oxide film.  
**Type I hot corrosion** occurs in the **high-temperature regime of 700 – 950 °C**, governed by basic fluxing of the protective oxide scale. It is typically encountered on first-stage gas-turbine blades and on boiler superheaters burning low-grade residual fuel oil with high V and Na content.  
**Type II hot corrosion** occurs in the **lower regime of 600 – 750 °C**, governed by acidic fluxing in the presence of SO₃-rich deposits. It is most often observed on modern HP/IP steam-turbine blades and on marine gas turbines burning distillate fuels.

> **Question 2.** State the **Pilling–Bedworth criterion** for oxide protectiveness. Calculate the PBR for **Al₂O₃ on aluminium** and comment on the result. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The Pilling–Bedworth criterion states that a metal oxide is **protective** when its **PBR = V_oxide / V_metal** lies between **1 and 2**, ensuring the scale is dense and adherent yet not so voluminous as to spall.

**Data:**  
$M_{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3} = 102$ g mol⁻¹, $n = 2$, $M_{\text{Al}} = 27$ g mol⁻¹, $\rho_{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3} = 3.99$ g cm⁻³, $\rho_{\text{Al}} = 2.70$ g cm⁻³.

$$\text{PBR} = \frac{102 \times 2.70}{2 \times 27 \times 3.99} = \frac{275.4}{215.5} = 1.28$$

**Comment:** A PBR of 1.28 lies comfortably in the protective range (1 < 1.28 < 2). Hence Al₂O₃ is an excellent protective scale — this is the fundamental reason aluminium and its alloys are inherently corrosion-resistant and why aluminising is used as a high-temperature coating for turbine blades and boiler tubes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal-Choice Questions

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **A(a) [7 marks]** With a neat schematic, describe the **steam-side oxidation mechanism** in a coal-fired boiler superheater tube. Discuss the role of thermal cycling on oxide spallation and the consequences of exfoliated scale on the downstream HP turbine.  
> **A(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the **remaining service life** of a TP347H superheater tube operating at 600 °C with the following data: parabolic rate constant $k_p = 6.5 \times 10^{-11}$ g² cm⁻⁴ s⁻¹; oxide density 5.20 g cm⁻³; initial scale thickness 4 µm; critical spallation thickness 75 µm. Assume 75 % of calendar time is at full load.

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **B(a) [7 marks]** Explain the phenomenon of **flow-accelerated corrosion (FAC)** in single-phase feedwater piping of a power plant. Discuss the influence of dissolved oxygen, temperature, pH, and chromium content of the steel on the FAC rate. List the standard industry practices used to mitigate FAC.  
> **B(b) [7 marks]** A **condenser** of a 210 MW thermal plant uses **aluminium-brass (Cu–Zn–Al)** tubes. The cooling water contains **1 200 ppm Cl⁻**, **6.5 ppm dissolved O₂**, and **2.0 m/s velocity**. Predict the likely corrosion mechanism and recommend (i) a **better material choice** with one-line justification, (ii) a **chemistry control** measure, and (iii) a **mechanical/electrochemical** mitigation technique.

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to A(a) — Steam-Side Oxidation in a Superheater Tube

**Step 1 — Operating environment.** A superheater tube in a 500 MW unit carries steam at 540–600 °C and 140–180 bar. The tube material is ferritic (T22) at lower temperatures, but at the secondary/reheat stages it is austenitic (TP304H / TP347H) with 18–20 % Cr and 8–12 % Ni.

**Step 2 — Initial oxide formation.** Within the first 1 000 hours, a thin **Cr-rich spinel** (Fe–Cr–O solid solution) forms at the metal–scale interface, while an outer layer of **magnetite (Fe₃O₄)** grows by outward diffusion of Fe²⁺ ions.

**Step 3 — Steady-state parabolic growth.** The reaction at the metal–scale interface is
$$3\,\text{Fe} + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4 + 4\,\text{H}_2$$
with mass gain following $(\Delta m)^2 = k_p \, t$.

**Step 4 — Effect of thermal cycling.** During start-up and shut-down, the temperature excursion is ΔT ≈ 500 °C. Because the **coefficient of thermal expansion** of Fe₃O₄ (≈ 14 × 10⁻⁶ K⁻¹) is *higher* than that of austenitic steel (≈ 17 × 10⁻⁶ K⁻¹), the scale is placed in **tensile hoop stress on cooling** and **compressive stress on heating**. The repeated cycle produces **inter-granular micro-cracks** that propagate parallel to the tube wall.

**Step 5 — Spallation and consequences.** When the scale thickness exceeds a critical value (≈ 60–80 µm for TP347H), flakes detach. These flakes are entrained by the steam and impact the **first-stage HP-turbine nozzle vanes**, where the relative velocity of 580 m/s produces **solid-particle erosion**. The aerofoil profile is roughened, the throat area enlarges, and stage efficiency drops by **0.5 to 1.5 % per year**. The cumulative cost of unplanned overhauls can reach **₹ 25–40 crore per 500 MW unit per 10 years**.
