**Step 6 — Control measures.** (i) Maintain feedwater **dissolved O₂ < 5 ppb** with hydrazine or carbohydrazide; (ii) apply **shot peening** to introduce a residual compressive stress that opposes spallation; (iii) use **higher-Cr austenitic grades (HR3C with 25 Cr, 20 Ni)** in the highest-temperature stages; (iv) control chloride carry-over below **100 ppb** in HP steam.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write *"oxide scale grows on the outside of the tube"* in superheaters. The high-temperature steam **is inside** the tube (single-pass once-through design) or **inside the tubes of radiant/reheat sections**; the flue gas is on the *outside*. Conflating these is a guaranteed mark-loser.

---

### Solution to A(b) — Remaining-Life Calculation

**Step 1 — Convert $k_p$ to µm² s⁻¹.**

Using oxide density $\rho_{\text{ox}} = 5.20$ g cm⁻³, the conversion factor is
$$k_p \text{ [µm}^2\text{s}^{-1}\text{]} = \frac{k_p \text{ [g}^2\text{cm}^{-4}\text{s}^{-1}\text{]}}{\rho_{\text{ox}}^2} \times 10^8$$

$$k_p = \frac{6.5 \times 10^{-11}}{27.04} \times 10^8 = 2.405 \times 10^{-4} \text{ µm}^2 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the integrated Wagner equation.**

$$t_{\text{critical}} = \frac{\delta^2 - x_0^2}{k_p} = \frac{(75)^2 - (4)^2}{2.405 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$t_{\text{critical}} = \frac{5625 - 16}{2.405 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{5609}{2.405 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.331 \times 10^7 \text{ s}$$

Convert to years:
$$t_{\text{critical}} = \frac{2.331 \times 10^7}{3.154 \times 10^7} = 0.739 \text{ years (continuous at full load)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 75 % load factor.**

Effective calendar time = 0.739 / 0.75 = **0.985 years ≈ 1 year**.

**Step 4 — Engineering safety factor.**

Apply a **design factor of 2** (Booth-Mindlin), giving a recommended retirement after ≈ **2 years from now** or at next scheduled overhaul, whichever is earlier.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to *convert* $k_p$ from g² cm⁻⁴ s⁻¹ to µm² s⁻¹ before substituting into the $\delta^2$ form. This produces answers off by a factor of ~ $10^4$ and is the most common numerical error in this question type.

---

### Solution to B(a) — Flow-Accelerated Corrosion (FAC)

**Step 1 — Definition.** FAC is the *convective mass-transfer-accelerated* dissolution of the protective magnetite (Fe₃O₄) layer on carbon-steel piping in single-phase feedwater, producing characteristic **"tiger-stripe" or scalloped** wall-thinning patterns downstream of bends, orifice plates, and tee-junctions.

**Step 2 — Chemistry.** The protective magnetite dissolves under reducing, slightly alkaline conditions by the reaction
$$\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4 + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow 3\,\text{FeO}^{*} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
where FeO* is a soluble hydrated ferrous species $\text{Fe(OH)}_2$ (or $\text{Fe}^{2+}_{\text{aq}}$).

**Step 3 — Variables.**

| Variable | Effect on FAC rate |
|---|---|
| Dissolved O₂ | **0.4 < DO < 50 ppb** accelerates FAC; > 200 ppb forms a new, denser Fe₂O₃ that *reduces* FAC |
| Temperature | Maximum FAC at **120 – 180 °C**; falls sharply above 250 °C |
| pH | Minimum at pH 25 °C ≈ **8.0 – 8.5**; rising pH to 9.2–9.6 sharply reduces FAC |
| Cr content of steel | Adding **≥ 1.25 % Cr** reduces FAC rate by ~ 90 %; 2.25 % Cr gives an order of magnitude reduction |
| Local velocity | Rate ∝ $v^{0.7}$ to $v^{1.5}$ for v between 1 and 6 m/s |

**Step 4 — Industry practices for mitigation.** (i) **AVT-O chemistry** with DO maintained at **< 5 ppb** and pH at 9.2–9.6 using morpholine or ammonia; (ii) **material upgrade** from A 106 Gr.B to **T22 (2.25 Cr-1 Mo)** or **T91 (9 Cr-1 Mo)** in the high-FAC-risk zones; (iii) **redesign of piping** to reduce local velocity below 3 m/s and avoid sharp bends; (iv) **CHECWORKS-type predictive software** for periodic screening; (v) **non-intrusive UT scanning** of high-risk fittings every 4 years.

---

### Solution to B(b) — Aluminium-Brass Condenser Tube Analysis

**Step 1 — Diagnosis of mechanism.** Aluminium brass is susceptible to:
- **Pitting corrosion** when Cl⁻ exceeds ~ 500 ppm (here 1 200 ppm — well above safe limit).
- **Impingement attack / erosion-corrosion** when velocity exceeds ~ 2.4 m/s (here 2.0 m/s — borderline).
- **Ammonia attack** if cooling water contains > 1 ppm NH₃.

The combination of high Cl⁻, near-saturation dissolved O₂, and elevated velocity makes **erosion-facilitated pitting** the dominant failure mode.

**Step 2 — Recommendations.**

| Measure | Choice | One-line justification |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Material | **Titanium Grade 2** (UNS R50400) | Immune to Cl⁻ pitting up to 70 °C; erosion limit 30 m/s — orders of magnitude better than Cu-alloy |
| (ii) Chemistry | **Continuous ferrous sulphate (FeSO₄) dosing** at 0.5–1.0 ppm to form a protective Fe(OH)₃ film on the brass | Forms a sacrificial but self-healing ferric-oxide layer that suppresses pitting nucleation |
| (iii) Mechanical / Electrochemical | **Impressed-current cathodic protection** with Ti/MMO anodes and 50 mA/m² current density, plus **sponge-ball cleaning** every 8 h | Cathodic polarisation shifts the tube surface ~ 200 mV cathodic, preventing pit initiation; sponge balls remove biofouling and silt that exacerbate under-deposit corrosion |

**Step 3 — Bonus note.** If capital cost of Ti upgrade is prohibitive, **Cu-Ni 90/10** tubes with **0.6 % Fe + 1.0 % Mn** and continuous FeSO₄ dosing is the next-best option, with an *expected* life of 12–15 years versus 4–5 years for aluminium brass under these conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students recommend *stainless steel* as a replacement for condenser tubes. **Austenitic SS 304/316 is unsuitable for saline cooling water** because of chloride-induced SCC and pitting. KTU board examiners will *specifically* deduct marks for this recommendation; the correct answer is **titanium Grade 2** or **Cu-Ni 90/10 with Fe addition**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise the following points before sitting the KTU Module-4 paper on "Corrosion and Its Control in Industries: Power":**

- ☐ **Seven key corrosion zones** in a thermal power plant: water-wall (sulphidation), superheater (steam-side oxidation), HP turbine (hot corrosion Type II), LP turbine (SCC + corrosion fatigue), condenser (pitting + erosion), economiser (dew-point corrosion), air preheater (acid dew + fly-ash erosion).
- ☐ **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio:** 1 < PBR < 2 → protective; PBR < 1 → porous (alkali metals); PBR > 2 → spalling (Fe₃O₄ at high T).
- ☐ **Parabolic law:** $(\Delta m)^2 = k_p \, t$; $k_p$ follows Arrhenius behaviour with $Q$ between 120 and 220 kJ mol⁻¹ for most engineering alloys.
- ☐ **Steam-side oxide** is a *double layer*: inner Cr-spinel (protective) + outer columnar Fe₃O₄ (exfoliating).
- ☐ **Hot Corrosion Type I:** 700–950 °C, basic fluxing, oil-fired boilers, Na₂SO₄. **Type II:** 600–750 °C, acidic fluxing, modern turbines, SO₃-rich deposits.
- ☐ **Dew-point corrosion** of H₂SO₄ occurs when metal temperature falls below **130–160 °C**; remedy is *recirculation* of warm flue gas to keep metal above dew point.
- ☐ **FAC** maximum rate at 120–180 °C; mitigated by AVT-O chemistry (DO < 5 ppb, pH 9.2–9.6) and ≥ 1.25 % Cr in steel.
- ☐ **Condenser tube** choice: Ti-Grade 2 > Cu-Ni 90/10 (with FeSO₄ dosing) > aluminium brass (marginal) > austenitic SS (avoid in saline water).
- ☐ **Numerical skills to master:** PBR formula; $k_p$ conversion between units; remaining-life calculation from Wagner equation with load factor.
- ☐ **Always pair** mechanism ↔ alloy ↔ chemistry control ↔ inspection technique in any descriptive answer.
- ☐ **Vocabulary for the examiner:** *pig tail, scalloping, tiger stripe, exfoliation, basic fluxing, acidic fluxing, shot peening, AVT-O, coordinated phosphate, dew point* — using these terms correctly elevates your answer from "average" to "board-exam standard".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Process" in Corrosion Engineering?

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Corrosion Engineering (Module 4), a **Corrosion Protection Process** refers to a controlled, engineered sequence of chemical, electrochemical, or mechanical operations applied to a metal substrate to either:

1. **Remove existing corrosion products** (e.g., pickling, descaling), or
2. **Form a protective layer** (passive, conversion, or anodic film) that drastically reduces the rate of further degradation, or
3. **Shift the electrochemical potential** of the metal into a region of thermodynamic immunity or kinetic passivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner Standard):**
> A corrosion protection process is a *deliberate surface-engineering intervention*—chemical, electrochemical, or mechanical—that alters the surface condition of a metallic component to achieve a measurable reduction in its corrosion rate (typically expressed in **mm/year** or **mpy**—mils per year).

The five classical "processes" expected from Module 4 are:

| # | Process | Primary Mechanism |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Pickling | Acid dissolution of oxide scale |
| 2 | Passivation | Spontaneous/reinforced Cr₂O₃-rich oxide growth |
| 3 | Phosphating | Chemical conversion to crystalline metal phosphate |
| 4 | Chromating | Chemical conversion to Cr(III)/Cr(VI) mixed oxide |
| 5 | Anodizing | Electrolytic growth of thick barrier + porous oxide |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Raincoat" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy for first-time learners:**
> Imagine a freshly built iron gate left in the open. It rusts in weeks. Now imagine applying a transparent **raincoat** (paint) — the rain no longer touches the metal. Corrosion protection processes are essentially **chemistry-based "raincoats" formed *by* the metal itself** rather than painted on.
> - **Pickling** is like scrubbing off the old, flaky rust patch before painting.
> - **Passivation** is letting a thin invisible skin of chromium oxide form on stainless steel — like the way aluminum forms its own protective skin in air.
> - **Phosphating & Chromating** are like giving the surface a "rough, absorbent primer" that helps paint stick tightly.
> - **Anodizing** is like growing the natural skin of aluminum into a thick, controllable oxide layer — the same way a pearl forms thicker nacre when an irritant is left in an oyster.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Module 4

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key units you MUST memorize for KTU Board Exams:**
> - Corrosion rate: **mm/year** (SI) and **mpy** (US customary, 1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/year)
> - Coating weight: **g/m²** or **mg/ft²**
> - Current density: **A/m²** or **A/dm²**
> - Film thickness: **µm** (micrometers)

## 1.4 Why is Module 4 critical for the KTU examiner?

> [!WARNING]
> Module 4 carries the highest *applied* weightage. KTU 2024 examiners expect students to:
> (a) Write balanced chemical equations for **every** process,
> (b) State **operating parameters** (temperature, concentration, time, current density),
> (c) Justify the **industrial application** with at least one real component (e.g., anodized aluminium window frames, phosphated automotive bodies, chromated aircraft skins).
> Skipping any one of (a), (b), or (c) results in a direct 3–4 mark deduction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pickling Process — Detailed Step-by-Step Logic

Pickling is the **chemical removal of oxide scale, rust, and welding slags** from a ferrous or non-ferrous metal using aqueous acid solutions.

### Why pickling is needed
- Hot-rolled steel forms a tightly adherent **mill scale** (FeO, Fe₃O₄, Fe₂O₃) which:
  - Causes poor paint adhesion
  - Leads to under-film corrosion
  - Interferes with subsequent plating/coating

### The Operational Logic (Bulleted Flow)
- **Step 1 — Pre-cleaning:** Degreasing with hot alkaline solution (NaOH, Na₂CO₃, Na₃PO₄) at 60–90 °C for 10–20 min.
- **Step 2 — Acid pickling bath:** Immersion in 5–15 % **H₂SO₄** at 60–80 °C, OR 5–10 % **HCl** at room temperature, for 5–30 min.
- **Step 3 — Inhibitor addition:** 0.1–0.5 % organic inhibitor (e.g., **rodine**, **picklette**, dibutylthiourea) — suppresses acid attack on *base metal* but does not stop scale dissolution (a critical KTU favourite question).
- **Step 4 — Rinsing:** Double water rinse to remove residual acid.
- **Step 5 — Neutralization & Passivation:** Dip in dilute Na₂CO₃ or NaOH; for stainless steel, final **HNO₃ passivation** restores the Cr-rich oxide.

### Critical Chemistry (H₂SO₄ on Iron)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FeO} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 &\longrightarrow \text{FeSO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 &\longrightarrow \text{Fe}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{Fe} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 &\longrightarrow \text{FeSO}_4 + \text{H}_2\uparrow
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why HCl is preferred for stainless steel pickling:**
> Stainless steel contains Cr-rich oxides that are insoluble in H₂SO₄. **HCl + HNO₃ mixtures** dissolve the Cr₂O₃ layer, while HF is added for silicate scale removal.

## 2.2 Passivation Process

Passivation is the formation of a **thin, adherent, passive oxide film** (typically 1–10 nm) on a metal surface that brings the metal into the **passive region** of its E vs log i diagram.

### The Operational Logic
- **Step 1 — Surface cleaning:** Mechanical + alkaline degreasing.
- **Step 2 — Acid activation:** Pickling in HNO₃-HF (for SS) to remove iron contamination and enrich surface Cr.
- **Step 3 — Oxidizing acid treatment:** 20–50 % **HNO₃** at 50–60 °C for 30 min — chromium oxidizes preferentially to form Cr₂O₃.
- **Step 4 — Rinse and dry.**

### Critical Chemistry (Stainless Steel — 18-8 Type)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cr} + \text{HNO}_3 &\longrightarrow \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3 \;\;(\text{thin, adherent, self-healing}) \\
2\,\text{Cr} + 6\,\text{HNO}_3 &\longrightarrow \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3 + 6\,\text{NO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **ASTM A967 standard** specifies passivation: 5–25 % HNO₃ at 20–55 °C for 30 min minimum. Citric acid (4–10 %) is a modern safer alternative (ASTM A967).

## 2.3 Phosphating Process

Phosphating (or **Parkerizing** — a trademark of Henkel) is the chemical conversion of a metal surface to a layer of **insoluble crystalline metal phosphate**.

### The Operational Logic
- **Step 1 — Surface preparation:** Degrease + pickle to a water-break-free surface.
- **Step 2 — Phosphating bath immersion or spray:**
  - **Zinc phosphating:** ZnO 5–10 g/L, H₃PO₄ 10–20 g/L, NaNO₂ 0.5–1 g/L, temp 50–70 °C, time 5–15 min.
  - **Iron phosphating:** NaH₂PO₄ + accelerator, pH 4–5, room temp.
  - **Manganese phosphating:** for wear resistance + corrosion.
- **Step 3 — Rinsing** → **Drying** → **Post-treatment seal** (chrome rinse, oil, or paint).
- **Step 4 — Coating weight measurement** for quality control.

### Critical Chemistry
$$
\begin{aligned}
3\,\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^- &\longrightarrow \text{Zn}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2\downarrow + 4\,\text{H}^+ \\
\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + \text{Fe} &\longrightarrow \text{FeHPO}_4 + \text{H}_2\uparrow
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Coating weight for **crystalline Zn-phosphating on steel = 10–30 g/m²**; for **Mn-phosphating (anti-wear) = 5–40 g/m²** — these are KTU Board numbers.

## 2.4 Chromating Process (Chromate Conversion Coating, CCC)

Chromating forms a **gel-like film of mixed Cr(III)/Cr(VI) oxides + sulfates** on Zn, Al, Mg, Cd.

### Critical Chemistry
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Zn} + 2\,\text{H}^+ + \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} &\longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2\,\text{Cr}^{3+} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{Cr}^{3+} + \text{CrO}_4^{2-} + \text{H}_2\text{O} &\longrightarrow \text{Cr(OH)}_3 \cdot \text{CrO}_3 \;\;(\text{gel film})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **REACH & RoHS compliance:** Hexavalent chromium is **carcinogenic** (Category 1A). Modern industry has shifted to **trivalent chromium (Cr-III) chromating** (e.g., Iridite NCP) — a very common KTU question on environment-friendly alternatives.

## 2.5 Anodizing Process

Anodizing is an **electrolytic process** that converts the metal surface into a **thick, porous, decorative + protective oxide layer**. Predominantly used for **aluminium** and its alloys.

### The Operational Logic
- **Step 1 — Pre-treatment:** Degreasing + alkaline etching (NaOH 5 %, 50–70 °C) + desmutting (HNO₃).
- **Step 2 — Anodizing cell setup:** Component = **ANODE** (+), lead/stainless steel = **CATHODE** (−), electrolyte = **H₂SO₄ 15–20 %** (Type II, Type IIB), or **CrO₃** (Type I, "hardcoat" precursor).
- **Step 3 — Electrolysis parameters:**
  - Voltage: **12–20 V** (Sulfuric), 30–40 V (Hard anodizing)
  - Current density: **1.0–1.5 A/dm²**
  - Temperature: **18–22 °C** (Sulfuric) or 0–5 °C (Hardcoat)
  - Time: 20–60 min (Type II), 60–120 min (Type III/hardcoat)
- **Step 4 — Coloring** (optional): organic/inorganic dye, electrolytic coloring.
- **Step 5 — Sealing:** Hot water (95–100 °C, 15–30 min) or nickel acetate seal — closes the porous structure via hydration: Al₂O₃ → Al₂O₃·H₂O (boehmite).

### Critical Chemistry (Aluminium Sulfuric Anodizing)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Anode (Al)}: \quad &2\,\text{Al} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 6\,\text{H}^+ + 6\,e^- \\
\text{Cathode (Pb)}: \quad &6\,\text{H}^+ + 6\,e^- \longrightarrow 3\,\text{H}_2\uparrow
\end{aligned}
$$

The film has two layers:
- **Barrier layer** (thin, dense, against the metal) — gives corrosion resistance.
- **Porous layer** (thick, honeycomb) — gives dye absorption + mechanical keying.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** governs the protectiveness of any oxide film:
> $$\text{PBR} = \frac{\text{Volume of oxide produced}}{\text{Volume of metal consumed}}$$
> A PBR between **1 and 2** is protective. For Al₂O₃/Al, PBR ≈ 1.28. For MgO/Mg, PBR ≈ 0.81 (non-protective — magnesium corrodes!). KTU loves this number.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Corrosion rate (SI) | $CR = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | mm/year | Weight-loss Tafel test |
| Corrosion rate (mpy) | $CR_{mpy} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | mpy | ASTM G1 / G31 |
| Pilling–Bedworth Ratio | $\text{PBR} = \dfrac{M_{\text{ox}} \cdot \rho_{\text{m}}}{n \cdot M_{\text{m}} \cdot \rho_{\text{ox}}}$ | dimensionless | Predict oxide protectiveness |
| Coating weight | $C_w = \dfrac{(W_2 - W_1)}{A}$ | g/m² | Phosphating quality control |
| Faraday's law (mass) | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | g | Anodizing rate prediction |
| Anodizing current density | $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | A/dm² | Cell design |
| Anodizing rate | $r = \dfrac{25.7 \cdot J}{n \cdot \rho_{Al}}$ | µm/min | Anodizing thickness |
| Nernst Equation | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0592}{n}\log Q$ | V | Passivation potential determination |
| Tafel slope relation | $\eta = a + b \log i$ | V | Polarization curve interpretation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants you must know (FYI, no need to memorize at KTU board):**
> Faraday constant $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, $\rho_{Al} = 2.70$ g/cm³, $\rho_{Fe} = 7.87$ g/cm³, $M_{Al} = 26.98$ g/mol, $M_{Al_2O_3} = 101.96$ g/mol.

## 2.7 Real-world Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Pickling** → Continuous **pickling lines** in integrated steel plants (e.g., Bokaro, Jamshedpur) for hot-rolled coils before cold rolling.
> - **Passivation** → Pharmaceutical & food-grade stainless steel vessels, surgical instruments.
> - **Phosphating** → Automotive bodies (4-stage zinc phosphating) — almost every Maruti, Hyundai, Tata car has this layer under its paint.
> - **Chromating** → Aerospace aluminium skins (Alclad 2024) — yellow iridite film.
> - **Anodizing** → Architectural aluminium (windows, facades), consumer electronics (iPhone bodies — Type II anodized), defence hardware (Type III hardcoat).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Corrosion Rate from Weight-Loss Data

A standard KTU 14-mark question: *"A steel coupon (area 25 cm², density 7.87 g/cm³) loses 0.42 g after 240 hours in 1 M H₂SO₄. Calculate the corrosion rate in mm/year and mpy."*

**Step 1 — State the formula (ASTM G1 standard):**
$$
CR \;\; (\text{mm/yr}) = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}
$$

where $W$ is weight loss in mg, $D$ is density in g/cm³, $A$ is exposed area in cm², $t$ is time in hours.

**Step 2 — Convert units (text-row explanation):**
The standard KTU conversion logic is: $W$ (g) × 1000 = mg, and we must express area in cm² (NOT m²), and time in hours. The constant 87.6 already incorporates the 8760 h/year and 10 mm/cm scaling factor.

**Step 3 — Plug in the values:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \dfrac{87.6 \times (0.42 \times 1000)}{7.87 \times 25 \times 240} \\
   &= \dfrac{87.6 \times 420}{47{,}220} \\
   &= \dfrac{36{,}792}{47{,}220} \\
   &\approx 0.779 \;\; \text{mm/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mpy:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \;\; \text{mm/yr} &= \dfrac{1}{0.0254} \;\; \text{mpy} = 39.37 \;\; \text{mpy} \\
CR_{mpy} &= 0.779 \times 39.37 \approx 30.7 \;\; \text{mpy}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation (KTU marker loves this):**
> [!NOTE]
> A rate of ~30 mpy is **unacceptably high** for most service environments. The acid environment needs an inhibitor (e.g., thiourea at 0.5 mM) to reduce this to below 5 mpy.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pilling–Bedworth Ratio for Al₂O₃/Al

**Step 1 — State the PBR formula:**
$$
\text{PBR} = \dfrac{M_{\text{ox}} \cdot \rho_{\text{m}}}{n \cdot M_{\text{m}} \cdot \rho_{\text{ox}}}
$$
where $n$ is the number of metal atoms per oxide molecule (for Al₂O₃, $n = 2$).

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**
- $M_{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3} = 101.96$ g/mol
- $M_{\text{Al}} = 26.98$ g/mol
- $\rho_{\text{Al}} = 2.70$ g/cm³
- $\rho_{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3} = 3.95$ g/cm³

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PBR} &= \dfrac{101.96 \times 2.70}{2 \times 26.98 \times 3.95} \\
           &= \dfrac{275.29}{213.14} \\
           &\approx 1.29
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret using the PBR rule:**
- PBR < 1 → oxide is **porous, non-protective** (e.g., Mg, alkali metals).
- 1 < PBR < 2 → oxide is **protective** (Al, Cr, Ti, stainless steels).
- PBR > 2 → oxide **spalls off** under stress (e.g., Fe₂O₃/Fe = 2.15 — rust flakes off, this is why iron rusts freely!).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Anodizing Thickness from Faraday's Law

**Step 1 — Faraday's law of electrolysis:**
$$
m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
$$

**Step 2 — For aluminium anodizing (n = 3 since Al → Al³⁺ + 3e⁻):**
$$
m_{Al} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot 26.98}{3 \times 96{,}485} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{10{,}729} \;\; \text{(in grams, if I in A, t in s)}
$$

**Step 3 — Thickness from mass consumed:**
The Al converted to oxide is the *anodized depth*. Volume of Al = $m / \rho$, and thickness = volume / area.
$$
\delta_{Al} = \dfrac{m_{Al}}{\rho_{Al} \cdot A}
$$

**Step 4 — Combined expression for thickness per unit time at current density J = I/A:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{Al} &= \dfrac{J \cdot t \cdot M_{Al}}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho_{Al}} \\
            &= \dfrac{1.5 \;\; \text{A/dm}^2 \times 1800 \;\; \text{s} \times 26.98}{3 \times 96{,}485 \times 2.70} \\
            &= \dfrac{72{,}846}{781{,}728} \;\; \text{cm} \\
            &\approx 0.0932 \;\; \text{cm} = 932 \;\; \mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Anodizing rate ≈ **0.4–0.5 µm/min** at J = 1.5 A/dm². A 20-µm Type II coating needs ≈ 40–50 min. A 50-µm Type III hardcoat needs ≈ 100–130 min. KTU Board examiners quote these numbers in viva.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Phosphating Coating Weight (Quality Control)

A panel 10 cm × 15 cm is phosphated. Mass before = 124.578 g, mass after stripping the phosphate layer = 124.523 g. Calculate coating weight.

**Step 1 — Mass of coating:** $\Delta W = 124.578 - 124.523 = 0.055$ g = 55 mg.

**Step 2 — Area of panel:** $A = 10 \times 15 = 150$ cm² = 0.015 m².

**Step 3 — Coating weight:**
$$
C_w = \dfrac{55 \;\; \text{mg}}{0.015 \;\; \text{m}^2} \approx 3667 \;\; \text{mg/m}^2 = 3.67 \;\; \text{g/m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Spec interpretation:** This is **light crystalline Zn-phosphating** (1.5–4.5 g/m²). Heavy anti-wear Mn-phosphating would be 5–40 g/m².

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Five Processes

| Property | Pickling | Passivation | Phosphating | Chromating | Anodizing |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Surface change | Removes oxide | Builds Cr-rich film | Builds Zn/Mn phosphate | Builds Cr(III/VI) gel | Builds Al₂O₃ |
| Layer thickness | 0 (dissolution) | 1–10 nm | 5–50 µm | 0.05–1 µm | 5–150 µm |
| Process type | Chemical | Chemical (oxidizing) | Chemical conversion | Chemical conversion | Electrolytic |
| Temp | 60–80 °C | 20–60 °C | 25–95 °C | 20–40 °C | 0–22 °C |
| Duration | 5–30 min | 20–60 min | 1–15 min | 0.5–5 min | 20–120 min |
| Main substrate | Steel, SS, Cu | SS, Ti, Al | Steel, Zn, Al | Zn, Al, Mg, Cd | Al, Ti, Mg, Ta |
| PBR relationship | N/A (removal) | ≈ 1.3 (protective) | ≈ 1.6 (Zn₃(PO₄)₂) | ≈ 2.0 (gel) | ≈ 1.29 (Al₂O₃) |
| Industrial spec | ASTM A380 | ASTM A967 | ASTM B633 / TT-C-490 | MIL-DTL-5541 | MIL-A-8625 |
| Toxicity | Acid fumes | HNO₃ fumes | Low | High (Cr-VI) | Low (H₂SO₄) |
| Cost (relative) | Low | Low | Low | Medium | Medium-High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow: Process Decision Tree for a Steel Component

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Steel Component] --> B[Incoming Inspection]
    B --> C{Scale or Rust Present?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Alkaline Degrease]
    D --> E[Pickling in H2SO4 or HCl]
    E --> F[Inhibitor-dosed Acid Bath]
    F --> G[Water Rinse]
    G --> H{Needs Paint or Lubricant?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Phosphating Process]
    H -- No --> J{Needs Passivity?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Passivation in HNO3]
    J -- No --> L[Ship]
    I --> M[Drying + Paint or Oil]
    M --> L
    K --> L
    L --> N[Final QA]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Anodizing Cell Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Power Supply +] --> B[Aluminium Anode]
    A2[DC Power Supply -] --> C[Lead Cathode]
    B --> D[Sulphuric Acid 15-20 percent Bath 18-22 C]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Barrier Layer grows first 0.01 um]
    E --> F[Porous Layer grows 5-150 um]
    F --> G[Rinse and Dye bath optional]
    G --> H[Hot Water Seal 95-100 C]
    H --> I[Anodized Component]
```

## 4.3 Phosphating Bath Chemistry — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J[Bath Feed Tank] --> K[ZnO plus H3PO4 Solution]
    K --> L[Accelerator NaNO2 or H2O2]
    L --> M[Working Tank 50-70 C]
    M --> N[Steel Substrate Immersion]
    N --> O[Pickling Reaction Stage - Etching of Fe]
    O --> P[Deposition Stage - Crystal Growth]
    P --> Q[Rinse Stage]
    Q --> R[Drying Stage]
    R --> S[Sealing Chrome Rinse or Oil]
```

## 4.4 PBR Decision Logic for Oxide Protectiveness

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Calculate PBR for Metal Oxide] --> T{PBR less than 1.0}
    T -- Yes --> U[Non-protective Porous Oxide e.g. MgO]
    T -- No --> V{PBR between 1 and 2}
    V -- Yes --> W[Protective Adherent e.g. Al2O3 Cr2O3]
    V -- No --> X{PBR greater than 2}
    X -- Yes --> Y[Spalling and Cracking e.g. Fe2O3]
    X -- No --> Z[Boundary Case Check stoichiometry]
```

## 4.5 Functional Process-Parameter Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Setpoint Bath Temperature] --> B1[PT100 Sensor in Tank]
    B1 --> C1{PID Controller}
    C1 -- Heat --> D1[Heater ON]
    C1 -- Cool --> E1[Chiller ON]
    D1 --> F1[Tank]
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> B1
    F1 --> G1[Quality Check Coating Weight Thickness Adhesion]
    G1 -- OK --> H1[Pass to Next Process]
    G1 -- NOT OK --> I1[Adjust Setpoint and Re-run]
    I1 --> A1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**"Differentiate between pickling and passivation of steel with one example each."**

**Model Answer (Board-standard 3-mark format):**

| Feature | Pickling | Passivation |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Removes oxide scale and rust | Forms a thin, protective oxide film |
| **Mechanism** | Acid dissolution of oxides | Chemical oxidation to a stable passive oxide |
| **Reagent** | 5–15 % H₂SO₄ or HCl | 20–50 % HNO₃ (oxidizing acid) |
| **Layer formed** | None — surface is *cleaned* | 1–10 nm of Cr₂O₃ on stainless steel |
| **Example** | Hot-rolled steel coil descaling at Jamshedpur plant | Surgical-grade 316L SS instrument dipped in HNO₃ per ASTM A967 |

> [!NOTE]
> Award **1 mark** for purpose, **1 mark** for mechanism/reagent, **1 mark** for a valid example.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**"State Pilling–Bedworth ratio. Give one example each of a protective and a non-protective oxide using PBR."**

**Model Answer:**
The **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** is the ratio of the molar volume of an oxide to the molar volume of the metal from which it is formed:
$$
\text{PBR} = \dfrac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal}}}
$$
- **Protective oxide:** $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3/\text{Al}$, PBR = 1.28 → adherent, self-healing.
- **Non-protective oxide:** $\text{MgO}/\text{Mg}$, PBR = 0.81 → porous, flakes off.
- (Optional 3rd example for high marks: $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3/\text{Fe}$ PBR = 2.15 → cracks and spalls.)

> [!NOTE]
> 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for protective example, 1 mark for non-protective example.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — Internal Choice

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style]`

**(a)** With neat flow diagram and chemical equations, describe the **process of sulphuric acid anodizing of aluminium**. State the parameters of operation and explain the role of the **sealing operation** at the end of the process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** Anodizing is an electrolytic process that converts the Al surface to a controlled, thick Al₂O₃ coating by making the component the **anode** of an electrolytic cell.

2. **Process Flow (3 marks):**
   - **Pre-treatment:** Degreasing (5 % NaOH, 60 °C) → pickling (HNO₃ desmutting) → water rinse.
   - **Anodizing cell:** Component (anode, +) and lead cathode (−) immersed in 15–20 % H₂SO₄ electrolyte at 18–22 °C.
   - **Electrolysis parameters:** Voltage 12–20 V, current density 1.0–1.5 A/dm², time 20–60 min for Type II coatings.
   - **Chemistry:**
   $$
   2\,\text{Al} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 6\,\text{H}^+ + 6\,e^-
   $$
   At cathode: $2\,\text{H}^+ + 2\,e^- \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\uparrow$
   - **Post-treatment:** Optional dye, then sealing.

3. **Role of Sealing (2 marks):** Sealing in **boiling deionized water (95–100 °C, 15–30 min)** or **nickel acetate solution** converts the porous anodic film:
   $$
   \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\;(\text{boehmite})
   $$
   This **closes the pores**, traps dye molecules, dramatically improves corrosion resistance and prevents dye leaching.

4. **Why each parameter matters (1 mark):** Higher J → faster but rougher film; lower T → higher density film (Type III hardcoat at 0 °C, 30 V, J = 3 A/dm²).

**(b)** Explain the **phosphating process** with chemical equations. Discuss the **applications** and **quality control** of phosphating in industry. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** Phosphating is a chemical conversion treatment in which a steel surface is reacted with a dilute solution of **phosphoric acid + metal phosphate** to form a crystalline, adherent layer of insoluble metal phosphate (typically Zn₃(PO₄)₂ · 4H₂O — *hopeite*).

2. **Process Steps (2 marks):**
   - Degrease → Rinse → Pickle → Rinse → Phosphating bath (immersion/spray) → Rinse → Drying → Post-treatment.
   - Bath composition: ZnO 5–10 g/L, H₃PO₄ 10–20 g/L, accelerator NaNO₂ 0.5 g/L; T = 50–70 °C; t = 5–15 min.

3. **Chemistry (2 marks):**
   $$
   \begin{aligned}
   3\,\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^- &\longrightarrow \text{Zn}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2\downarrow + 4\,\text{H}^+ \\
   \text{Fe} + 2\,\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 &\longrightarrow \text{Fe}(\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 + \text{H}_2\uparrow \;\;\text{(etching of substrate)} \\
   \text{Fe}(\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 &\longrightarrow \text{FeHPO}_4 + \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4
   \end{aligned}
   $$
   The acid attack raises pH at the surface, precipitating insoluble Zn₃(PO₄)₂ and FeHPO₄.

4. **Applications (1 mark):** Automotive body panels (4-stage Zn-phosphating before ED-coat), appliance housings, fasteners, transformer laminations.

5. **Quality control (1 mark):** Coating weight measurement (g/m²), salt spray test (ASTM B117 ≥ 96 h to white rust on phosphated steel), thickness 5–50 µm, XRF for coating composition.

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style Alternative]`

**(a)** With a labelled diagram, describe the **chromate conversion coating process** on aluminium. Discuss the **environmental concern** associated with Cr(VI) and the **modern Cr(III) alternatives**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** Chromate conversion coating (CCC) is a chemical treatment that forms a thin (0.05–1 µm) gel-like film of mixed chromium oxides and sulfates on Al, Zn, Mg, or Cd substrates.

2. **Process (2 marks):**
   - Pre-clean → Rinse → CCC bath (immersion 0.5–5 min at RT) → Rinse → Hot DI rinse → Dry.
   - Bath: CrO₃ 5–10 g/L + H₂SO₄ 0.5–2 g/L + accelerator (Na₂SiF₆).

3. **Chemistry (2 marks):**
   $$
   \begin{aligned}
   3\,\text{Zn} + 7\,\text{CrO}_3 + 7\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 &\longrightarrow 3\,\text{ZnSO}_4 + \text{Cr}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 3\,\text{Cr}(\text{OH})_3 + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
   \text{Final film:} \;\;\text{Cr(OH)}_3 \cdot \text{xCrO}_3 \cdot \text{yH}_2\text{O}
   \end{aligned} $$

4. **Environmental concern (1 mark):** Cr(VI) is a **Category 1A carcinogen** per IARC, regulated under **REACH (Annex XIV)**, **RoHS 3** (max 0.1 % w/w in electronics), and **OSHA PEL 5 µg/m³**. Wastewater must be reduced to Cr(III) and precipitated.

5. **Modern Cr(III) alternatives (1 mark):** Iridite NCP, SurTec 650, Henkel Alodine 5900. These are **trivalent chromium + zirconium/titanium** systems giving equivalent salt-spray performance for non-aerospace applications.

**(b)** A steel coupon of **exposed area 30 cm²** and **density 7.85 g/cm³** loses **0.56 g** after **300 hours** in a corrosive medium. Calculate the **corrosion rate in mm/year and mpy**. Comment on the suitability of the medium for long-term service. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**
1. **Formula (1 mark):**
   $$
   CR = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}
   $$

2. **Substitution (2 marks):**
   $$
   CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times (0.56 \times 1000)}{7.85 \times 30 \times 300} = \dfrac{49{,}056}{70{,}650}
   $$

3. **Result in mm/year (1 mark):**
   $$
   CR \approx 0.694 \;\; \text{mm/year}
   $$

4. **Conversion to mpy (1 mark):**
   $$
   CR_{mpy} = 0.694 \times \dfrac{1000}{25.4} \approx 27.3 \;\; \text{mpy}
   $$

5. **Engineering Comment (2 marks):**
   - For a typical structural steel in atmospheric service, the **acceptable corrosion rate is < 5 mpy** (≈ 0.13 mm/year).
   - A rate of **27.3 mpy** is **excessive** — corresponds to ~700 µm/year metal loss, equivalent to a 5-mm-thick structural plate failing in ~7 years.
   - Recommendations: add a corrosion inhibitor (e.g., 0.1 % sodium benzoate), use a coating system (phosphating + epoxy + polyurethane), or upgrade the alloy to a weathering steel (e.g., ASTM A588) or a 304 SS.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing pickling with passivation** (1–2 marks lost) — pickling *removes* oxide; passivation *creates* oxide.
> 2. **Forgetting the cathode half-reaction** in anodizing (2 marks lost) — examiners always want both anode and cathode reactions.
> 3. **Not mentioning the inhibitor** in pickling (1 mark lost) — the inhibitor is *not optional*; it is the reason pickling doesn't over-etch.
> 4. **Writing "Cr plating" instead of "Cr conversion coating"** (1 mark lost) — chromating ≠ chrome plating (which is electrodeposited).
> 5. **Skipping the sealing step** in anodizing answers (2 marks lost) — sealing is what makes the coating actually protective.
> 6. **Wrong units in corrosion rate** (1 mark lost) — mm/yr vs mpy conversion factor 39.37, not 25.4 (the 25.4 is for mm ↔ in, not mm/yr ↔ mpy).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for Module 4 — "Process":**
> - Pickling uses **HCl or H₂SO₄** + organic inhibitor; removes mill scale, not base metal.
> - Passivation uses **HNO₃**; builds thin Cr-rich oxide on SS — ASTM A967.
> - Phosphating produces **Zn₃(PO₄)₂ · 4H₂O** (hopeite) on steel; standard for automotive pretreatment.
> - Chromating produces mixed **Cr(III)/Cr(VI) gel film**; Cr-VI is being phased out per **REACH/RoHS**.
> - Anodizing is **electrolytic**; component is **anode**; produces barrier + porous Al₂O₃; **must be sealed** in hot water or nickel acetate.
> - **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio:** 1 < PBR < 2 → protective; < 1 porous; > 2 spalls.
> - **Faraday's law** governs anodizing thickness; rate ≈ 0.4–0.5 µm/min at 1.5 A/dm².
> - **Corrosion rate formula:** CR (mm/yr) = 87.6 W / (D A t); CR (mpy) = 534 W / (D A t) — both with W in mg, A in cm², t in h, D in g/cm³.
> - **Conversion factor:** 1 mm/yr = 39.37 mpy.
> - **Industrial specifications to remember:** ASTM A380 (pickling), ASTM A967 (passivation), ASTM B117 (salt spray), MIL-A-8625 (anodizing), MIL-DTL-5541 (chromating).
> - Always write **balanced chemical equations** with state symbols (s, l, g, aq) in the KTU Board exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Petrochemical
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Petrochemical

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Petrochemical Corrosion Engineering** is the specialized branch of corrosion science that studies the electrochemical and chemical degradation of metallic materials (and to a lesser extent, non-metallic materials) used in the construction, processing, storage, and transportation equipment of petroleum refineries, natural gas processing plants, and petrochemical complexes. It deals with damage mechanisms arising from crude oils, hydrocarbon streams, sour (H₂S-containing) and sweet (CO₂-containing) services, naphthenic acids, high-temperature hydrogen, and various catalytic process environments.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, this module focuses on identifying the dominant **damage mechanisms** (a standardized term defined by **API 571 – "Mechanisms Influencing Fixed Equipment in the Refining Industry"**), the metallurgical and environmental factors controlling them, and the **materials selection / mitigation strategies** used to ensure mechanical integrity and asset reliability.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy – The "Cooking Vessel" Model:**
> Imagine your kitchen steel pressure cooker. If you only ever cook plain water, it lasts decades. But the moment you start cooking acidic tomato curry, salty sambar, or sugary desserts, the inner surface begins to pit, discolour, and eventually leak. Now scale this up by **10⁶ times** in size, **100 times** in temperature, and **50 times** in pressure, and feed it with crude oil that contains sulfur, salt water, organic acids, dissolved CO₂, H₂S, and chlorides — and you have the petrochemical corrosion challenge.
>
> The "ingredients" (process fluid) and the "cooking conditions" (T, P, velocity) are the two big levers that decide *how fast* the metal "cooker" (reactor/distillation tower/transfer line) will deteriorate.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and units recur throughout petrochemical corrosion calculations:

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday's constant | F | **96,485 C/mol** | Links current to mass loss in electrochemical corrosion |
| Gas constant | R | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | Used in Arrhenius-type temperature dependence |
| Corrosion rate (engineering) | CR | **mm/yr** or **mpy** (mils per year) | Most common engineering metric |
| Density of carbon steel | ρ_Fe | **7.85 g/cm³** | Used in weight-loss → thickness loss conversion |
| Equivalent weight of Fe | EW | **27.92 g/eq** | For Faraday's-law corrosion calculations |
| 1 mil | — | **25.4 µm** | Unit conversion for mpy → mm/yr |
| NACE standard CO₂ partial pressure (sweet service) | pCO₂ | **> 0.0007 MPa** | Threshold for "sour" classification per NACE MR0175 |
| NACE standard H₂S partial pressure (sour service) | pH₂S | **> 0.0003 MPa** | Threshold for "sour" classification per NACE MR0175 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to (a) classify petrochemical corrosion mechanisms, (b) identify process variables that drive each mechanism, and (c) recommend appropriate alloys / inhibitors / cathodic protection for a given service. This is directly tested in ESE questions and is the core of Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Petrochemical Corrosion Mechanisms

The petrochemical industry recognizes **~70 distinct damage mechanisms** per API 571. For KTU Module 4, the most examinable ones are:

#### 2.1.1 Low-Temperature Aqueous Corrosion (< 250 °C)

1. **Sweet Corrosion (CO₂ Corrosion)**
   - **Mechanism:** Dissolved CO₂ + H₂O → carbonic acid (H₂CO₃) → H⁺ + HCO₃⁻. The H⁺ ions depolarize the cathodic reaction, accelerating anodic Fe dissolution.
   - **Anodic:** Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻
   - **Cathodic:** 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂ (or 2H₂CO₃ + 2e⁻ → H₂ + 2HCO₃⁻)
   - **Affected units:** Crude overhead systems, gas-processing absorbers, CO₂ injection lines.
   - **Controlling variables:** Partial pressure of CO₂ (pCO₂), temperature, water cut, velocity, chloride content.

2. **Sour Corrosion (H₂S-Related)**
   - **Mechanism:** H₂S dissociates in water to give HS⁻ and S²⁻. These act as cathodic stimulators (depolarizers).
   - **Special concern:** Hydrogen-Induced Cracking (HIC), Sulfide Stress Cracking (SSC), and Stress-Oriented Hydrogen-Induced Cracking (SOHIC) — all governed by **NACE MR0175 / ISO 15156**.
   - **Threshold:** pH₂S > 0.0003 MPa (0.05 psi) classifies the service as "sour".

3. **Naphthenic Acid Corrosion (NAC)**
   - **Mechanism:** High-molecular-weight carboxylic acids (R-COOH) in crude oil react directly with Fe at high temperatures (> 220 °C) to form soluble iron naphthenates.
   - **Formula:** Fe + 2RCOOH → Fe(RCOO)₂ + H₂
   - **Affected units:** Atmospheric and vacuum distillation columns, transfer lines, pumps, heat exchangers processing high-TAN (Total Acid Number) crudes (TAN > 0.5 mg KOH/g).
   - **Marker:** **"Groove-like” or “narrow scalloped”** metal loss at high-velocity zones.

4. **Ammonium Bisulfide Corrosion**
   - **Mechanism:** NH₃ + H₂S → NH₄HS. This salt deposits in overhead systems and creates localized under-deposit corrosion.
   - **Concern:** NH₄HS concentration > 2 wt% is a red-flag threshold for fouling and aggressive localized attack.

5. **Pitting and Crevice Corrosion (Cl⁻ induced)**
   - Driven by stagnant chloride-rich brines in dead legs and beneath gaskets/deposits.

#### 2.1.2 High-Temperature Corrosion (> 250 °C) — No Aqueous Phase

1. **Sulfidation / High-Temperature H₂S/H₂ Attack**
   - **Mechanism:** Fe + H₂S → FeS + H₂. Scale growth follows **parabolic kinetics** (Wagner's theory).
   - **Governed by:** **Nelson curves** (API 571 / API 939) – graphical limits of H₂ partial pressure vs. temperature above which a given steel grade is unsuitable.

2. **High-Temperature Oxidation**
   - **Mechanism:** Formation of Fe₂O₃ / Fe₃O₄ / FeO scales; rate follows parabolic or linear kinetics depending on temperature.
   - **Typical limit:** Carbon steel usable up to ~560 °C; 9Cr-1Mo (P9) up to ~620 °C; austenitic SS (304H) up to ~800 °C.

3. **Carburization**
   - **Mechanism:** Carbon from CO/CH₄/H₂CO diffuses into the alloy matrix, forming carbides that degrade mechanical and corrosion properties.
   - **Affected units:** Ethylene cracking furnaces, reformer tubes.

4. **Metal Dusting**
   - **Mechanism:** Catastrophic disintegration of alloys into a "dust" of metal particles + carbon, occurring in carburizing atmospheres at 400–800 °C.
   - **Mitigation:** Use high-Cr / Ni / Al alloys (e.g., Alloy 800H, 601).

5. **Hot Corrosion (Sulfate-Induced)**
   - **Mechanism:** Molten Na₂SO₄ + V₂O₅ deposits react with protective oxide scales, leading to accelerated fluxing attack.
   - **Affected units:** Gas turbine blades, fired heater tubes fired with residual fuel oil.

#### 2.1.3 Environment-Assisted Cracking (EAC) Family

- **SSC** (Sulfide Stress Cracking) – sour, hard steels.
- **HIC / SOHIC** – sour, low-strength pipeline steels.
- **Cl-SCC** (Chloride Stress Corrosion Cracking) – 300-series austenitic SS above 60 °C.
- **Caustic Embrittlement** – boiler tubes, FCC main fractionators.
- **Hydrogen Embrittlement** – from cathodic protection over-protection or HF alkylation units.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 7 formulas — they cover >85% of numerical questions in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula | Application / Context |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Weight-loss corrosion rate in mils per year |
| 2 | $\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Same, but SI/metric (W in mg, D in g/cm³, A in cm², t in hr) |
| 3 | $\text{mpy} \times 0.0254 = \text{mm/yr}$ | Unit conversion (1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr) |
| 4 | $\text{Thickness loss} = \dfrac{\text{CR} \times \text{Service years}}{1000}$ (mm) | Used in remaining-life calculations |
| 5 | $\log_{10}\text{CR} = \dfrac{-E_a}{2.303 R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\right) + \log_{10}A$ | Arrhenius equation for temperature dependence of corrosion/oxidation rate |
| 6 | $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$ | Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number — alloy selection tool |
| 7 | $\text{Acid number effect: NAC rate} \propto (\text{TAN})^{n} \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Empirical correlation for naphthenic acid attack |
| 8 | $\text{Polarization resistance: } R_p = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\Delta i}$ | LPR probe measurement; $CR \propto 1/R_p$ |
| 9 | $\text{Faraday's mass loss: } m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Electrochemical mass loss from measured current |
| 10 | $\text{Nelson limit for H}_2\text{:} \log_{10}P_{H_2} = f(T)$ | Graphical — read directly from Nelson curve for chosen alloy |

> Where:
> - W = weight loss (mg)
> - D = metal density (g/cm³)
> - A = exposed area (cm²)
> - t = exposure time (hr)
> - Eₐ = activation energy (J/mol)
> - I = corrosion current (A)
> - M = atomic/molar mass of metal
> - n = number of electrons transferred
> - F = Faraday constant = 96,485 C/mol

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The petrochemical sector loses **>US$ 10 billion / year** to corrosion (NACE IMPACT study). For a B.Tech graduate entering the workforce:

- **Process engineer:** Must select the correct carbon-steel grade vs. alloy upgrade vs. inhibitor injection.
- **Inspection engineer:** Must know which NDE method (UT, RT, MFL, eddy current) is suitable for which damage mechanism.
- **Reliability / corrosion engineer:** Must interpret LPR, ER, and weight-loss coupon data to schedule turnarounds.
- **Pipeline engineer:** Must apply NACE MR0175 / ISO 15156 for sour service qualification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Weight-Loss Corrosion Rate (mm/yr)

The fundamental engineering relationship for expressing metal loss as a uniform rate:

**Step 1 – Volume loss from mass loss:**

Divide the measured weight loss W (in mg) by the metal density D (g/cm³) to get the volume lost.

$$
V = \dfrac{W}{D} \quad [\text{cm}^3]
$$

**Step 2 – Average thickness loss:**

Divide the volume by the exposed surface area A (cm²).

$$
h = \dfrac{V}{A} = \dfrac{W}{D \cdot A} \quad [\text{cm}]
$$

**Step 3 – Convert cm to mils (1 cm = 393.7 mil):**

$$
h_{mils} = \dfrac{W}{D \cdot A} \times 393.7
$$

**Step 4 – Divide by exposure time (hr) and multiply by hours-per-year (8760):**

$$
\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{393.7 \cdot W \cdot 8760}{D \cdot A \cdot t} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}
$$

**Step 5 – SI version (mm/yr):**

Using 1 cm = 10 mm and 8760 hr/yr, and noting the constant 393.7·8760/1000 = 3448 and the SI unit conversions give:

$$
\boxed{\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}}
$$

where W is in mg, D in g/cm³, A in cm², and t in hours.

> The constant **87.6** comes from: $\dfrac{10\,(\text{mm/cm}) \times 24 \times 365}{1000} = 87.6$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Arrhenius Temperature Dependence

Corrosion and oxidation rates are temperature-activated processes, following:

**Step 1 – Empirical observation:**

Experimentally, ln(CR) is a linear function of 1/T.

$$
\ln(\text{CR}) = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R \cdot T}
$$

**Step 2 – Linearize with log₁₀:**

Using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$
\log_{10}(\text{CR}) = \log_{10} A - \dfrac{E_a}{2.303 R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\right)
$$

**Step 3 – Rate ratio at two temperatures:**

Taking the difference between two states (T₁, T₂) eliminates the pre-exponential A:

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{CR}_2}{\text{CR}_1}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{2.303 R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 – Convert temperature differences into engineering impact:**

For a typical activation energy Eₐ ≈ 50 kJ/mol for sulfidation:

$$
\dfrac{\text{CR}_2}{\text{CR}_1} = 10^{\left(\dfrac{50000}{2.303 \times 8.314}\right)\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)} \approx 10^{2611 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)}
$$

This is the basis of the rule of thumb: **every 10 °C rise in temperature roughly doubles the high-temperature corrosion rate** (valid in the 200–600 °C range for sulfidation/oxidation).

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Sour-Service Coupon in Overhead)

> A carbon-steel corrosion coupon (density 7.85 g/cm³, area 28.0 cm²) is exposed for **120 days** in a refinery overhead system. The weight loss is **1180 mg**. Calculate:
> (a) Corrosion rate in **mpy**.
> (b) Corrosion rate in **mm/yr**.
> (c) Expected **thickness loss after 5 years** of continuous service.
> (d) Comment on whether the result is acceptable (NACE typical acceptable limit: < 5 mpy for CS overheads).

**Solution:**

**(a) mpy:**
- t = 120 days × 24 hr/day = 2880 hr

$$
\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{534 \times 1180}{7.85 \times 28.0 \times 2880} = \dfrac{630120}{632832} \approx 0.996 \text{ mpy}
$$

**(b) mm/yr:**

$$
\text{CR (mm/yr)} = 0.996 \times 0.0254 \approx 0.0253 \text{ mm/yr}
$$

**(c) 5-year thickness loss:**

$$
\text{Loss} = 0.0253 \times 5 = 0.1265 \text{ mm} \;\;(\approx 127 \text{ µm})
$$

**(d) Comment:** CR ≈ 1 mpy is well within the **NACE acceptable limit (< 5 mpy)**. If TAN > 0.5, however, naphthenic acid attack would be suspected and an upgrade to **5Cr-0.5Mo** or **9Cr-1Mo** would be evaluated.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Damage Mechanism vs. Process Variable

| Process Variable | Sulfidation ↑ | NAC ↑ | SSC/HIC ↑ | Cl-SCC ↑ | Pitting ↑ | Oxidation ↑ |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Temperature | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | – | ↑ | – | ↑↑ |
| H₂S partial pressure | ↑↑ | – | ↑↑↑ | – | – | – |
| pCO₂ | – | – | – | ↑ | ↑ | – |
| TAN (acid number) | – | ↑↑↑ | – | – | – | – |
| Chloride content | – | – | – | ↑↑↑ | ↑↑ | – |
| Velocity | – | ↑↑ | – | – | – | – |
| Hardness (steel) | – | – | ↑↑↑ | – | – | – |
| Water cut | – | (↓) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | – |

> Legend: ↑ mild increase, ↑↑ strong, ↑↑↑ dominant driver, – negligible.

### 3.5 Worked Example – Alloys Selection via PREN

Choose between **Type 316L SS (16 Cr, 2 Mo, 0.05 N)** and **Alloy 825 (42 Cr, 3 Mo, 0.15 N)** for a chloride-containing overhead system. Chloride content = 50,000 ppm, T = 90 °C.

$$
\text{PREN}_{316L} = 16 + 3.3(2) + 16(0.05) = 16 + 6.6 + 0.8 = 23.4
$$

$$
\text{PREN}_{825} = 42 + 3.3(3) + 16(0.15) = 42 + 9.9 + 2.4 = 54.3
$$

**Threshold:** For seawater / high-Cl service, **PREN > 40** is required to resist pitting. Hence:
- **316L → NOT acceptable** (PREN = 23.4)
- **Alloy 825 → Acceptable** (PREN = 54.3)

> A more economical alternative is **Alloy 20 (Sanicro 28 type, PREN ≈ 36)** with inhibitor injection — but the KTU exam expects the student to know the PREN-based decision logic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Petrochemical Corrosion Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Process Unit and Service] --> B{Operating Temperature}
    B -->|T less than 250 deg C| C{Low-Temp Aqueous}
    B -->|T greater than 250 deg C| D{High-Temp Dry}

    C --> E{Contains H2S above 0.0003 MPa}
    E -->|Yes| F[Sour Service]
    E -->|No| G{Contains CO2 above 0.7 kPa}
    G -->|Yes| H[Sweet Service]
    G -->|No| I{Contains Naphthenic Acids TAN greater than 0.5}
    I -->|Yes| J[NAC Service]
    I -->|No| K{Chloride greater than 1000 ppm}
    K -->|Yes| L[Cl Pitting and SCC]
    K -->|No| M[General Corrosion or EAC]

    F --> N[Materials per NACE MR0175]
    H --> O[Carbon Steel plus Inhibitor or 5Cr 0.5Mo]
    J --> P[Upgrade to 316 SS or 317 SS]
    L --> Q[Use PREN greater than 40 alloys]

    D --> R{Sulfidizing Atmosphere}
    R -->|Yes| S[Check Nelson Curves for H2]
    R -->|No| T{Carburizing Atmosphere}
    T -->|Yes| U[Use Alloy 800H or 601]
    T -->|No| V[Standard High-Temp Oxidation]
    S --> W[Apply 9Cr 1Mo or 304H SS]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow with Corrosion Hot-Spots – Atmospheric and Vacuum Distillation Unit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crude Desalter] --> B[Atmospheric Heater]
    B --> C[Atmospheric Column]
    C --> D[Overhead Receiver]
    C --> E[Vacuum Heater]
    E --> F[Vacuum Column]
    F --> G[HVGO and Residue]

    subgraph Z1[High-Temperature Corrosion Zone]
    B
    E
    F
    end

    subgraph Z2[Low-Temperature Aqueous Corrosion Zone]
    A
    C
    D
    end

    Z1 --> NACor[Sulfidation plus NAC above 220 deg C]
    Z2 --> NACE[Sour plus Cl plus NH4HS at Top of Column]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every block in the **Z1** region is a candidate for **high-temperature sulfidation / NAC**; every block in the **Z2** region is a candidate for **sour / chloride / under-deposit attack**. Materials selection decisions must follow the corresponding NACE / API document.

### 4.3 Architecture of an Integrated Corrosion Management Programme

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Process Data and Operating Conditions] --> R1[Risk Based Inspection per API 580]
    R1 --> R2[Damage Mechanism Identification per API 571]
    R2 --> R3[Materials Selection per NACE and ASME]
    R2 --> R4[Corrosion Monitoring via LPR plus ER plus Coupon]
    R2 --> R5[Chemical Injection Program]
    R4 --> R6[Data Trending and KPI]
    R5 --> R6
    R6 --> R7[Turnaround Inspection Planning]
    R7 --> P1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**
*"Distinguish between 'sweet' and 'sour' service in the petrochemical industry. State the threshold partial pressure values and the governing standard."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Sweet service:** Process fluid contains CO₂ and water but no significant H₂S. pH₂S < 0.0003 MPa (0.05 psi). Corrosion product is typically FeCO₃ (siderite).
- **Sour service:** Process fluid contains H₂S above the NACE MR0175 threshold. **Threshold: pH₂S > 0.0003 MPa** OR H₂S concentration > 50 ppm in the gas phase. Corrosion products are iron sulfides (mackinawite, troilite, pyrrhotite). Sour service also demands qualification per **NACE MR0175 / ISO 15156** to prevent **SSC, HIC, and SOHIC**.
- **Governing standard:** **NACE MR0175 / ISO 15156 – "Petroleum and natural gas industries – Materials for use in H₂S-containing environments."**
- **Why it matters:** Material selection, hardness limits (typically HRC < 22 for sour service), and welding procedures all change dramatically across the sweet-sour boundary.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
*"Define Total Acid Number (TAN) and explain its significance in naphthenic acid corrosion of crude distillation units."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **TAN (Total Acid Number):** The mass of KOH (in mg) required to neutralize 1 g of crude oil or distillate — a measure of the total acidic constituents, dominated by **naphthenic acids** in crude oil.
- **Significance:**
  - When **TAN > 0.5 mg KOH/g** and operating temperature is **> 220 °C**, naphthenic acid corrosion (NAC) becomes a credible damage mechanism.
  - NAC rate is roughly proportional to **(TAN)^(0.6 – 1.0)** and increases sharply with velocity — hence localized attack at elbows, pump impellers, and return bends.
  - Mitigation: blend to lower TAN, inject **phosphate-ester or imidazoline inhibitors**, upgrade to **5Cr, 9Cr, 12Cr, or 316 SS** in high-risk zones.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
> (a) *(7 marks)* Classify the major corrosion damage mechanisms encountered in a petroleum refinery. For each, state the typical process location, controlling variables, and the recommended alloy family.
> (b) *(7 marks)* A carbon-steel coupon (density 7.85 g/cm³, exposed area 30 cm²) was installed in the overhead line of a crude distillation unit for 90 days. The weight loss was 850 mg. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mm/yr and mpy**, and determine whether the corrosion rate is acceptable per NACE guidelines (acceptable limit ≤ 5 mpy for CS in overhead). If unacceptable, suggest **two** alloy upgrades for a chloride-containing overhead stream.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Refinery Corrosion Damage Mechanisms (7 marks):**

Present in tabular form (4 marks for correct classification, 3 marks for the rest):

| Mechanism | Typical Location | Key Variables | Recommended Material |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sour corrosion (H₂S) | Overhead, hydrotreater | pH₂S, T, pH | CS + inhibitor; SS if severe |
| Naphthenic acid | Atm/Vac column, transfer lines | TAN, T, V | 5Cr-0.5Mo, 9Cr-1Mo, 316 SS |
| Sulfidation (high-T) | Heater tubes, reactor outlets | T, pH₂, pH₂S | Per Nelson curves |
| Cl-SCC | Overhead exchangers | Cl⁻, T, stress | Upgrade to duplex 2205 or Alloy 825 |
| Ammonium bisulfide | Overhead receivers | NH₄HS % | Wash water + inhibitor |
| Carburization | Ethylene cracker | T, pCO, activity C | Alloy 800H, 601 |
| Oxidation | Fired heater, FCC | T | 9Cr-1Mo, SS, high-Ni alloys |

(Examiner's note: 1 mark for correctly identifying at least 6 mechanisms; 1 mark for process locations; 1 mark for variables; 2 marks for material selection; 2 marks for engineering justification.)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 – Time conversion:** t = 90 × 24 = **2160 hr** (1 mark)

**Step 2 – Corrosion rate (mpy):** (1 mark)

$$
\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{534 \times 850}{7.85 \times 30 \times 2160} = \dfrac{453900}{508680} = 0.892 \text{ mpy}
$$

**Step 3 – Conversion to mm/yr:** (1 mark)

$$
\text{CR (mm/yr)} = 0.892 \times 0.0254 = 0.02266 \text{ mm/yr}
$$

**Step 4 – Acceptability check:** (1 mark)

0.892 mpy < 5 mpy → **ACCEPTABLE** for carbon steel under current conditions.

**Step 5 – Hypothetical upgrade analysis (assuming an increase to > 5 mpy):** (2 marks)

- **Option 1: Austenitic SS Type 316L (PREN ≈ 25)** — handles up to ~200 °C with Cl⁻ up to 1000 ppm.
- **Option 2: Duplex SS 2205 (PREN ≈ 35)** — better Cl-SCC resistance up to 60 °C above chloride SCC threshold, higher strength.
- **Option 3 (Bonus):** Alloy 825 (PREN ≈ 54) for high chloride / high temperature service.

**Step 6 – Concluding remark on inhibitor + monitoring (1 mark):** Pair alloy upgrade with **filming-amine inhibitor** injection and **LPR probe** monitoring.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
> (a) *(7 marks)* With the aid of the **Nelson curve** concept, explain how high-temperature H₂ / H₂S attack is evaluated in a hydrocracker reactor circuit. Discuss the metallurgical response of carbon steel, 1.25Cr-0.5Mo, and Type 321 SS to increasing temperature and H₂ partial pressure.
> (b) *(7 marks)* Define **Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)**. For the following alloys, calculate PREN and recommend suitability for a process stream containing 8% Cl⁻ at 95 °C and pH 4:
> - Alloy A: 18 Cr, 11 Ni, 2 Mo, 0.05 N (Type 316L)
> - Alloy B: 25 Cr, 7 Ni, 4 Mo, 0.3 N (Super-duplex 2507)
> - Alloy C: 20 Cr, 35 Ni, 4 Mo, 0.15 N (Alloy 825)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Nelson Curves and High-Temp H₂/H₂S Attack (7 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Nelson curves (originally by G.A. Nelson, 1949; now in API 571 / API 939) are **empirical plots** of allowable H₂ partial pressure (MPa) versus temperature (°C) for various alloy families. Above a given curve for a chosen material, the alloy is unsafe for that service.
- **Mechanism of attack (1 mark):** H₂ diffuses into steel at high T; H₂S reacts with Fe to form FeS scale. Above ~250 °C, **hydrogen attack** decarburizes the steel by forming CH₄ bubbles at grain boundaries, leading to loss of strength and fissuring.
- **Behavior of materials (3 marks):**
  - **Carbon steel (C-0.5Mo):** Decarburizes rapidly above ~250 °C; not recommended for hydrocracker reactors where H₂ partial pressure > 5 MPa and T > 350 °C.
  - **1.25Cr-0.5Mo (P11):** Better creep and H₂ resistance; safe up to ~400 °C at moderate H₂ pressure.
  - **Type 321 SS (Ti-stabilized 18-8):** Excellent resistance; can be used up to ~600 °C in H₂ service. Ti-stabilization prevents chromium-carbide sensitization.
- **Practical use (2 marks):** In hydrocracker design, the engineer plots the operating point (T, P_H₂) and selects the **lowest-cost alloy whose Nelson curve lies above that point**. Modern reactor shells are typically 2.25Cr-1Mo or 3Cr-1Mo; internals use SS 321/347.

**(b) PREN Calculations and Recommendation (7 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):**

$$
\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \cdot \%\text{Mo} + 16 \cdot \%\text{N}
$$

- **Alloy A — 316L (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{PREN}_A = 18 + 3.3(2) + 16(0.05) = 18 + 6.6 + 0.8 = 25.4
$$

- **Alloy B — Super-duplex 2507 (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{PREN}_B = 25 + 3.3(4) + 16(0.3) = 25 + 13.2 + 4.8 = 43.0
$$

- **Alloy C — Alloy 825 (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{PREN}_C = 20 + 3.3(4) + 16(0.15) = 20 + 13.2 + 2.4 = 35.6
$$

- **Suitability (1.5 marks):** For 8% Cl⁻ at 95 °C and pH 4, the rule of thumb is **PREN ≥ 40** for safe pitting resistance.
  - **316L (25.4) → NOT suitable** (severe pitting and Cl-SCC risk)
  - **Alloy 825 (35.6) → Marginal** (acceptable only with inhibitor and lower Cl⁻ or T)
  - **Super-duplex 2507 (43.0) → Recommended** — high Cr/Mo/N gives both pitting and SCC resistance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here):**
> 1. **Wrong units:** Mixing SI (mm/yr) and FPS (mpy) without proper conversion costs full marks. Always state the formula being used.
> 2. **Forgetting the time conversion** in weight-loss problems (days → hours). This alone can halve your marks.
> 3. **Confusing "sour" with "sweet":** Many students write "sour = CO₂" and lose a clear-cut mark.
> 4. **Skipping the engineering interpretation:** Just giving a number without saying "this is acceptable / not acceptable" loses 1–2 marks in Part A and Part B.
> 5. **Forgetting NACE / API standards:** Always name the standard (NACE MR0175, API 571, API 939, API 580, etc.) — examiners specifically reward this.
> 6. **In PREN problems:** Watch the factor — Mo is multiplied by 3.3, N by 16 (or 30 for highly alloyed grades). Mixing them up is the #1 trap.
> 7. **In Nelson-curve questions:** Don't describe it as a "theoretical limit" — it is **empirical**, based on long-term plant operating experience.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Petrochemical Corrosion (Module 4):**

- **The "Big Four" low-T mechanisms to know cold:** sweet (CO₂), sour (H₂S), NAC (TAN), Cl⁻ pitting/SCC.
- **Sour threshold:** pH₂S > **0.0003 MPa** OR > 50 ppm in gas → triggers NACE MR0175.
- **Sweet threshold:** pCO₂ > 0.0007 MPa → consider CO₂ corrosion.
- **NAC threshold:** TAN > 0.5 mg KOH/g AND T > 220 °C → high-velocity attack in atm/vacuum.
- **Nelson curves** govern high-T H₂ / H₂S attack; never use CS above its curve.
- **PREN ≥ 40** = required for high-Cl⁻ / pitting service; remember the factors **3.3 × %Mo and 16 × %N**.
- **Arrhenius rule of thumb:** every 10 °C rise roughly doubles the rate in the 200–600 °C range.
- **Always state engineering acceptability** (e.g., NACE ≤ 5 mpy for CS overhead) in numerical answers.
- **Cite standards:** NACE MR0175, API 571, API 580, API 939, ASME B31.3 — examiners reward this.
- **Mitigation toolkit:** materials upgrade, inhibitor injection (imidazoline, phosphate ester, filming amine), cathodic protection, process control (desalting, blending, water washing), corrosion monitoring (LPR, ER, UT, MFL, coupon).
- **For sour service:** hardness limit **HRC ≤ 22** for carbon and low-alloy steels; **NACE MR0175** defines qualified materials.
- **Metal dusting** is the most aggressive form of carburization attack — counter with high-Cr/Ni/Al alloys.
- **Naphthenic acid** is the only corrosion mechanism that attacks **without** an aqueous phase (oil-soluble).
- **NACE IMPACT estimate:** global corrosion cost in oil & gas ≈ **US$ 60–80 billion/year** — context to drop in a 14-mark answer.
- **Final tip:** A perfect KTU answer links mechanism → variable → monitoring → mitigation in a closed loop (the "Corrosion Management Loop" shown in Section 4.3).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ship Building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ship Building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Marine Corrosion in Ship Building** is defined as the electrochemical degradation of metallic hull structures, superstructures, propellers, ballast tanks, and auxiliary components of a marine vessel, occurring due to prolonged exposure to seawater, marine atmosphere, splash zones, and seabed sediments. It encompasses the synergistic action of **chloride-induced pitting**, **galvanic corrosion** between dissimilar metals, **cavitation erosion-corrosion**, **microbiologically influenced corrosion (MIC)**, and **stress corrosion cracking (SCC)**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, ship building is treated as a **case study application** of integrated corrosion engineering, where the student must demonstrate competence in selecting materials, designing cathodic protection systems, specifying coating schedules, and evaluating failure modes across multiple corrosion zones.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Terminology to Memorize:**
> - **IMO** — International Maritime Organization (regulatory body)
> - **PSPC** — Performance Standard for Protective Coatings (IMO Resolution MSC.215(82))
> - **ICCP** — Impressed Current Cathodic Protection
> - **SACP** — Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection
> - **HSC** — High Solids Coating
> - **NACE** — National Association of Corrosion Engineers
> - **TBT** — Tributyltin (now banned antifouling agent)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Multi-Zone Lab"

Think of a ship as a **floating corrosion laboratory with five distinct climatic zones stacked vertically and horizontally on the same steel body**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Ship as a Five-Story Building in Different Climates**
> 
> Imagine a building where:
> - The **basement** is permanently soaked in saltwater (similar to a flooded cellar)
> - The **ground floor** is hit by ocean waves every few seconds (like a waterfall)
> - The **middle floors** face salty, humid air (like living in a coastal city)
> - The **top floor** has cargo tanks that alternately hold seawater and then air (humidity swings 0–100%)
> - The **underground pipes** are filled with bacteria-laden mud
> 
> Every "floor" requires a **completely different corrosion protection strategy** because the environment is unique. A paint that works in dry air dissolves instantly in the splash zone, and a zinc anode that protects steel in still seawater is useless in the atmospheric zone.

## 1.3 Why Ship Building is a Critical Corrosion Engineering Topic

The global annual cost of marine corrosion is estimated at **$50–80 billion USD**, with shipping accounting for a major share. A single bulk carrier can lose **0.1–0.5 mm/year** of hull thickness without protection, leading to catastrophic failure. KTU 2024 emphasizes this topic because:

1. It integrates **every previous module** (mechanisms, materials, coatings, CP, inhibitors).
2. It is a **high-weightage ESE question** (typically 14-mark full question).
3. It tests the student's ability to **apply theory to a real multi-variable system**.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Ship Corrosion (Memorize Bold Values):**
> - Seawater resistivity: **20–30 Ω·cm**
> - Typical hull steel: **AH36 / DH36 grade (yield strength 355 MPa)**
> - Design life of a commercial vessel: **25–30 years**
> - Coating system expected life: **10–15 years** (with maintenance)
> - Cathodic protection design potential: **−0.85 V vs Ag/AgCl/seawater reference**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Corrosion Zones of a Ship — A Structured Breakdown

A merchant ship is conventionally divided into **five corrosion zones**, each with distinct degradation kinetics:

### Zone 1: Bottom Plate (Permanently Immersed Zone)
- **Environment:** Fully submerged in seawater (0–100% immersion).
- **Primary mechanisms:** Uniform corrosion, pitting (especially under deposits), cathodic blistering of coatings, MIC from sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB).
- **Corrosion rate of unprotected mild steel:** **0.10–0.15 mm/year**.
- **Protection strategy:** Epoxy primer + antifouling topcoat + ICCP/SACP.

### Zone 2: Boot Top / Heel / Topsides (Splash and Tidal Zone)
- **Environment:** Alternate wet/dry cycling, UV exposure, mechanical impact from waves, debris.
- **Primary mechanisms:** **Most severe corrosion zone** — accelerated low-water corrosion (ALWC) due to differential aeration, erosion-corrosion, coating breakdown.
- **Corrosion rate of unprotected mild steel:** **0.30–0.50 mm/year** (highest of all zones).
- **Protection strategy:** Thicker epoxy systems (≥ 600 μm DFT), sometimes combined with **sacrificial aluminum anodes** or **monel sheathing** on naval vessels.

### Zone 3: External Superstructure (Atmospheric Marine Zone)
- **Environment:** Salt-laden air, high humidity, UV radiation, rain washing.
- **Primary mechanisms:** Cosmetic corrosion, filiform corrosion under defective paint, bolt/rivet crevice corrosion.
- **Corrosion rate of unprotected mild steel:** **0.05–0.10 mm/year**.
- **Protection strategy:** Epoxy primer + polyurethane (PU) topcoat for color retention.

### Zone 4: Ballast Tanks (Hidden Critical Zone)
- **Environment:** Seawater ballast (alternate filling/emptying), high humidity, oil/water interface, sediment accumulation.
- **Primary mechanisms:** **Severe pitting** (up to 1 mm/year locally), MIC in sediments, coating blistering at edges, severe corrosion at structural hot spots.
- **Protection strategy:** **PSPC-compliant epoxy coating (≥ 320 μm DFT)**, often combined with **SACP** zinc anodes.

### Zone 5: Internal Compartments and Void Spaces
- **Environment:** Condensation, stagnant humid air, possible cargo residues.
- **Protection strategy:** Epoxy coating, dehumidifiers, controlled atmosphere.

## 2.2 Why Each Zone Differs — The Underlying Electrochemistry

The variation in corrosion rate across zones is governed by **oxygen availability**, **electrolyte conductivity**, **wet-dry cycling**, and **biological activity**.

- **Permanently immersed:** Oxygen diffusion is slow → low corrosion rate, but **cathodic protection is highly effective**.
- **Splash zone:** Continuous oxygen replenishment + electrolyte film = **diffusion-limited cathodic reaction** → maximum corrosion rate (oxygen reduction is rate-determining).
- **Atmospheric:** Thin electrolyte film → corrosion rate depends on **time of wetness (TOW)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Oxygen Reduction Cathodic Reaction (Rate-Limiting in Splash Zone):**
> 
> $$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$$
> 
> In the splash zone, this reaction is **fast** because oxygen is continuously supplied by breaking waves, but the anodic dissolution ($Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$) is limited by the thin film. The decoupling between cathodic and anodic areas leads to severe pitting.

## 2.3 Cathodic Protection Design in Ship Hulls

Two systems are deployed:

### A. Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)
- **Anode materials:** Zinc, aluminum (Zn–In–Cd or Al–Zn–In alloys), magnesium (rarely used in seawater).
- **Design potential:** **−0.85 V vs Ag/AgCl** (for bare steel) or **−0.95 V vs Ag/AgCl** (for coated steel, more negative to protect coating defects).
- **Anode output (typical):** Zinc anode ≈ **780 A·h/kg**; Aluminum anode ≈ **2700 A·h/kg**.

### B. Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)
- **Anode materials:** Platinized titanium, mixed metal oxide (MMO) titanium.
- **Reference electrode:** Ag/AgCl/seawater or zinc/seawater.
- **Power source:** Transformer-rectifier (TR) unit, 5–50 V DC, 50–500 A typical.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table — every formula has been asked in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cathodic current density (bare steel in seawater) | $i_c = 10 \text{ to } 30$ | mA/m² | Hull CP design |
| 2 | Cathodic current density (coated steel) | $i_c = 1 \text{ to } 5$ | mA/m² | After coating |
| 3 | Total cathodic current required | $I_{total} = i_c \times A_{wetted}$ | A | CP sizing |
| 4 | Anode mass required (SACP) | $m = \dfrac{I_{total} \times T \times 8760}{C \times U}$ | kg | Anode lifetime design |
| 5 | Anode current capacity (C) | Zn: 780, Al: 2700 | A·h/kg | SACP |
| 6 | Anode utilization factor (U) | 0.85 (typical) | — | Anode design |
| 7 | Design life (T) | 5, 10, 15, 25 years | years | Project life |
| 8 | Hull wetted area (rough estimate) | $A_w = L \times (B + 2D) \times C_f$ | m² | Empirical formula |
| 9 | Protection potential (bare steel) | $E_{prot} = -0.85$ | V vs Ag/AgCl | KTU standard |
| 10 | Protection potential (coated steel) | $E_{prot} = -0.95$ | V vs Ag/AgCl | KTU standard |
| 11 | Stern tube / propeller (more negative) | $E_{prot} = -1.05$ | V vs Ag/AgCl | Bronze propeller protection |
| 12 | Polarization decay (overpotential) | $\eta = E_{applied} - E_{corr}$ | V | CP efficiency |

**Where:**
- $L$ = Length Between Perpendiculars (m)
- $B$ = Breadth of ship (m)
- $D$ = Design Draft (m)
- $C_f$ = Form coefficient (0.85–0.95)
- $A_w$ = Wetted hull area (m²)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Classification societies** (Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, Bureau Veritas) require CP system design approval before commissioning.
- **Newbuilding specification** typically calls for **dual system** (SACP + ICCP) for redundancy.
- **Dry-docking interval:** Every **2.5–5 years**, with full anode replacement, hull coating inspection, and potential survey.
- **Failure to comply** with PSPC standards (effective from 2008) makes the vessel **un-insurable** and un-registerable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example: SACP Design for a Bulk Carrier Hull

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style 14-Mark Question):**
> 
> Design a sacrificial anode cathodic protection system for a bulk carrier with the following data:
> - Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP), $L = 200$ m
> - Breadth (molded), $B = 32$ m
> - Design Draft, $D = 12$ m
> - Form coefficient, $C_f = 0.90$
> - Coating breakdown factor (after 5 years), $f_{cb} = 0.20$ (i.e., 20% of area bare)
> - Cathodic current density on bare steel at design life, $i_c = 20 \text{ mA/m}^2$
> - Cathodic current density on coated steel, $i_c' = 2 \text{ mA/m}^2$
> - Anode material: **Aluminum alloy (Al–Zn–In)**, capacity $C = 2700$ A·h/kg
> - Anode utilization factor, $U = 0.85$
> - Design life, $T = 5$ years

### Step 1: Calculate the Wetted Hull Area

The empirical formula for wetted area of a displacement ship is:

$$A_w = L \times (B + 2D) \times C_f$$

Substituting the values:

$$A_w = 200 \times (32 + 2 \times 12) \times 0.90$$

$$A_w = 200 \times (32 + 24) \times 0.90$$

$$A_w = 200 \times 56 \times 0.90$$

$$A_w = 200 \times 50.4$$

$$A_w = 10080 \text{ m}^2$$

**Interpretation:** The underwater hull surface area to be protected is approximately 10,080 m² — equivalent to about 1.5 football fields.

### Step 2: Decompose Wetted Area into Coated and Bare Fractions

After 5 years in service, assuming the coating has deteriorated such that **20% of the area is bare** and **80% retains coating**:

$$A_{bare} = A_w \times f_{cb} = 10080 \times 0.20 = 2016 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{coated} = A_w \times (1 - f_{cb}) = 10080 \times 0.80 = 8064 \text{ m}^2$$

**Conversion logic:** The fraction 0.20 represents the "coating breakdown factor" which accounts for holidays, mechanical damage, and UV degradation over time.

### Step 3: Calculate Total Cathodic Current Required

The total current demand is the sum of contributions from bare and coated areas, but using the respective current densities:

$$I_{bare} = A_{bare} \times i_c = 2016 \text{ m}^2 \times 20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A/m}^2 = 40.32 \text{ A}$$

$$I_{coated} = A_{coated} \times i_c' = 8064 \text{ m}^2 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A/m}^2 = 16.128 \text{ A}$$

$$I_{total} = I_{bare} + I_{coated} = 40.32 + 16.128 = 56.448 \text{ A}$$

**Engineering rounding:** Use $I_{total} \approx 57$ A for design purposes (always round up for safety margin).

### Step 4: Calculate Total Charge Required Over Design Life

The design life in hours:

$$T_{hours} = T \times 365 \times 24 = 5 \times 365 \times 24 = 43800 \text{ h}$$

(Note: KTU also accepts $T \times 8760$ since 365 × 24 = 8760 hours/year.)

Total charge (coulombs or ampere-hours) required over the design life:
