$$Q_{total} = I_{total} \times T_{hours} = 57 \times 43800 = 2496600 \text{ A·h}$$

**Conversion logic:** The anode must supply this much charge before being replaced or it loses its effectiveness.

### Step 5: Calculate Anode Mass Required

Using the standard SACP anode mass formula:

$$m = \dfrac{I_{total} \times T_{hours}}{C \times U}$$

Substituting values:

$$m = \dfrac{57 \times 43800}{2700 \times 0.85}$$

$$m = \dfrac{2496600}{2295}$$

$$m \approx 1087.8 \text{ kg}$$

**Final design mass of aluminum anodes:** **≈ 1090 kg** (rounded up with safety margin).

### Step 6: Determine Number and Size of Individual Anodes

Standard aluminum anode sizes for ship hulls:

| Anode Type | Mass (kg) | Current Output (A) |
|---|---|---|
| Small (pocket type) | 10 | 1.0–1.5 |
| Medium (flush mounted) | 25 | 2.5–3.5 |
| Large (welded plate) | 50 | 5.0–7.0 |

If we select **50 kg anodes** delivering ~5 A each:

$$N_{anodes} = \dfrac{I_{total}}{I_{anode}} = \dfrac{57}{5} = 11.4$$

**Design choice:** Install **12 anodes** of 50 kg each = **600 kg total**, with the remaining **~490 kg** distributed as **smaller 25 kg anodes (20 anodes)** at critical locations (rudder, bow thruster tunnel, propeller bracket).

> [!TIP]
> **Total anode inventory = 12 × 50 kg + 20 × 25 kg = 600 + 500 = 1100 kg ✓ (matches Step 5)**

### Step 7: Anode Distribution Strategy

Anodes are NOT distributed uniformly. KTU expects the student to know the **weightage distribution**:

| Location | % of Total Anode Mass | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Hull bottom plates | 50% | Largest area, propeller wash effect |
| Rudder and stern frame | 20% | High turbulence, cavitation |
| Bilge keels and side plates | 15% | Eddies, stagnant water |
| Sea chests and overboard discharges | 10% | High localized turbulence |
| Cathodic areas (forward of bow thruster) | 5% | Shielding correction |

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Coating vs. SACP vs. ICCP vs. Combined

| Protection Method | Capital Cost | Maintenance | Reliability | Best Application | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Epoxy coating only** | Medium | High (every 2.5 yr dry-dock) | Low | Atmospheric, superstructure | Pinholes, holidays → rapid localized pitting |
| **SACP (Al anodes)** | Low | Medium (replace at dry-dock) | High | Coated hulls, small/medium vessels, ballast tanks | Limited current capacity, cannot protect large bare areas |
| **ICCP (MMO + TR)** | High | Low (anodes last 15+ yr) | High | Large vessels, tankers, naval ships | Requires power, monitoring, failure of single TR = system down |
| **Combined: Coating + SACP + ICCP** | Very high | Lowest | Highest | LNG carriers, naval combatants, vessels > 250 m | Complexity, capital cost |
| **Monel sheathing (Cu–Ni alloy)** | Very high | Negligible | Excellent | Naval hulls (splash zone) | Cost, weight, galvanic with steel if not isolated |

## 3.3 Verification Check Using Faraday's Law

Cross-checking the bare steel corrosion rate with and without protection:

**Without protection (Tafel extrapolation for steel in seawater):**

$$r_{corr} = \dfrac{i_{corr} \times M_{Fe}}{n \times F \times \rho_{Fe}}$$

Where:
- $i_{corr} = 10 \text{ mA/m}^2$ (typical corrosion current for steel in seawater)
- $M_{Fe} = 55.85 \text{ g/mol}$
- $n = 2$ (electrons per Fe atom)
- $F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$
- $\rho_{Fe} = 7.87 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 7870 \text{ kg/m}^3$

$$r_{corr} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-3} \times 55.85 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 96485 \times 7870}$$

$$r_{corr} = \dfrac{5.585 \times 10^{-4}}{1.519 \times 10^9}$$

$$r_{corr} = 3.68 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m/s} \approx 0.116 \text{ mm/year}$$

**This matches the experimental value of 0.10–0.15 mm/year** — confirming the formula's validity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **With proper CP at −0.85 V vs Ag/AgCl, the corrosion rate drops to < 0.01 mm/year — a 10-fold reduction.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the five corrosion zones of a ship and identify the most severe zone. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Answer:**

A merchant ship is divided into five corrosion zones based on the exposure environment:

1. **Atmospheric zone (superstructure):** Exposed to marine air, salt spray, UV.
2. **Splash/tidal zone (boot top):** Alternate wetting and drying by waves.
3. **Permanently immersed zone (bottom plate):** Continuous seawater contact.
4. **Ballast tank internal zone:** Alternate seawater ballast/air exposure.
5. **Internal void spaces:** Stagnant humid air, condensation.

**The most severe zone is the splash/tidal zone** with an unprotected corrosion rate of **0.30–0.50 mm/year**, due to continuous oxygen replenishment, mechanical wave impact, and alternate wet-dry cycling that prevents formation of a protective rust layer.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between ICCP and SACP for ship hulls. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SACP (Sacrificial Anode CP) | ICCP (Impressed Current CP) |
|---|---|---|
| Power source | Galvanic (self-driven) | External DC power (TR unit) |
| Anode material | Zn, Al alloys | MMO/Ti, platinized Ti |
| Anode lifetime | 5–10 years | 15–25 years |
| Current capacity | Limited (~5 A per anode) | High (50–500 A per system) |
| Cost | Low capital, medium maintenance | High capital, low maintenance |
| Best for | Coated hulls, small vessels, ballast tanks | Large vessels, tankers, naval ships |
| Monitoring | Visual at dry-dock | Continuous automatic |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A: Comprehensive Design Question

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various corrosion zones encountered in a typical merchant ship, with a labeled diagram. Explain why the splash zone exhibits the highest corrosion rate. (7 marks)

**(b)** Design a sacrificial anode cathodic protection system for the immersed hull of a bulk carrier with the following data:
- LBP = 180 m, Breadth = 30 m, Draft = 11 m
- Form coefficient = 0.92
- Coating breakdown factor after 5 years = 0.15
- Bare steel current density = 25 mA/m²
- Coated steel current density = 2 mA/m²
- Anode: Aluminum alloy, C = 2700 A·h/kg, U = 0.85
- Design life = 5 years

Calculate total anode mass and number of 50 kg anodes. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

The five corrosion zones of a merchant ship from top to bottom are:

1. **Atmospheric zone (superstructure & topsides):** Salty humid air, UV, rain. Rate ≈ 0.05–0.10 mm/yr. Protected by epoxy primer + polyurethane topcoat.

2. **Splash/tidal zone (boot top):** Most severe zone. Rate ≈ 0.30–0.50 mm/yr. Protected by thick epoxy (≥ 600 μm DFT), sometimes with monel sheathing on naval vessels.

3. **Permanently immersed zone (bottom plate):** Rate ≈ 0.10–0.15 mm/yr. Protected by epoxy + antifouling + ICCP/SACP.

4. **Ballast tank zone (internal):** Rate locally up to 1.0 mm/yr. Protected by PSPC-compliant epoxy (≥ 320 μm DFT) + zinc anodes.

5. **Void/bilge space:** Condensation-driven corrosion. Protected by epoxy + dehumidification.

**Why splash zone is most severe:**

The cathodic reaction in aerated seawater is oxygen reduction:

$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$$

In the splash zone, breaking waves continuously supply fresh dissolved oxygen, making the **cathodic reaction diffusion-limited and rapid**. Simultaneously, the **anodic reaction (Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻)** is accelerated by thin electrolyte film and chloride attack. The wet-dry cycle prevents formation of a stable protective rust layer, and mechanical wave action erodes any incipient protective film. This combination of abundant cathodic reactant, aggressive chloride electrolyte, and mechanical erosion produces the highest corrosion rate.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Wetted area**

$$A_w = L \times (B + 2D) \times C_f = 180 \times (30 + 22) \times 0.92$$

$$A_w = 180 \times 52 \times 0.92 = 8611.2 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Bare and coated areas**

$$A_{bare} = 8611.2 \times 0.15 = 1291.68 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{coated} = 8611.2 \times 0.85 = 7319.52 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Total current**

$$I_{total} = (1291.68 \times 25 + 7319.52 \times 2) \times 10^{-3}$$

$$I_{total} = (32292 + 14639.04) \times 10^{-3} = 46.93 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4: Total charge over 5 years**

$$Q = 46.93 \times 5 \times 8760 = 2,055,534 \text{ A·h}$$

**Step 5: Anode mass**

$$m = \dfrac{Q}{C \times U} = \dfrac{2055534}{2700 \times 0.85} = \dfrac{2055534}{2295} \approx 895.7 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6: Number of 50 kg anodes**

$$N = \dfrac{895.7}{50} = 17.9 \approx 18 \text{ anodes}$$

**Final Answer:** Total aluminum anode mass ≈ **896 kg** = **18 anodes of 50 kg each** (with 4 kg surplus).

---

### Question B: Alternative Choice Question

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the PSPC standard for protective coatings in ship building. What are the key DFT (Dry Film Thickness) requirements? (7 marks)

**(b)** A ship hull is protected by an ICCP system. The hull potential must be maintained at −0.95 V vs Ag/AgCl. The anode current output is 80 A. The wetted area is 9000 m². If 25% of the coating has deteriorated, calculate:
(i) The cathodic current density (assuming coated and bare contributions).
(ii) Whether the system provides adequate protection.
(iii) The new anode current required if 50% coating breakdown occurs. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**PSPC (Performance Standard for Protective Coatings)** was adopted by the International Maritime Organization (IMO) under Resolution MSC.215(82) and came into force on **1 July 2008** for all new ships of 500 GT and above.

**Key objectives:**
1. Ensure coating systems are properly designed, applied, and maintained.
2. Reduce premature corrosion-related structural failures.
3. Standardize coating selection across the maritime industry.

**Key DFT Requirements (Table from IMO Resolution):**

| Tank/Zone | Coating Type | Minimum DFT (microns) |
|---|---|---|
| Ballast tanks (newbuild) | Epoxy (PSPC-compliant) | **320 μm** |
| Ballast tanks (touch-up) | Epoxy | 320 μm |
| Void spaces | Epoxy | 200 μm |
| Cargo holds (bulk carriers) | Epoxy | 200 μm |
| Deck plates | Anti-slip epoxy | 250 μm |
| Underwater hull | Epoxy + antifouling | 320 + 150 μm |

**Key technical features:**
- **Epoxy must be Type-approved** (Lloyd's, DNV, ABS approved).
- **Surface preparation:** Sa 2.5 (ISO 8501-1) for ballast tanks.
- **Application:** Roller or airless spray.
- **Inspection:** 95/10 rule — 95% of measurements must meet DFT, none below 80% of nominal DFT.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Wetted area, $A_w = 9000 \text{ m}^2$
- ICCP current output, $I = 80 \text{ A}$
- Initial coating breakdown = 25%

**(i) Cathodic current density**

Bare area = 25% × 9000 = 2250 m²
Coated area = 75% × 9000 = 6750 m²

Using standard current densities:
- $i_{bare} = 25 \text{ mA/m}^2$
- $i_{coated} = 2 \text{ mA/m}^2$

$$I_{required} = (2250 \times 25 + 6750 \times 2) \times 10^{-3} = (56250 + 13500) \times 10^{-3} = 69.75 \text{ A}$$

$$i_{avg} = \dfrac{69.75}{9000} = 7.75 \text{ mA/m}^2$$

**(ii) Adequacy check**

Available current = 80 A > Required = 69.75 A → **System is adequate** with ~13% safety margin.

**(iii) At 50% coating breakdown**

Bare area = 0.50 × 9000 = 4500 m²
Coated area = 0.50 × 9000 = 4500 m²

$$I_{required} = (4500 \times 25 + 4500 \times 2) \times 10^{-3} = (112500 + 9000) \times 10^{-3} = 121.5 \text{ A}$$

**New anode current required ≈ 121.5 A** (the existing 80 A system is now inadequate — needs upgrade or anode addition).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Ship Building Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting units conversion:** Writing current in A without converting mA/m² to A/m². KTU deducts **1 mark** for this.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up wetted area formula:** Some students use $A_w = L \times B$ which is wrong. The correct formula is $A_w = L \times (B + 2D) \times C_f$. Examiner specifically checks the bracketed term.
> 
> 3. **Not stating the reference electrode:** When quoting protection potentials, always write "V vs Ag/AgCl/seawater reference." Writing just "−0.85 V" is incomplete.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring the difference between bare and coated current densities:** A common error is to apply $i_c = 20$ mA/m² to the entire wetted area, leading to gross over-design and loss of **2 marks**.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the anode distribution table:** Examiners award **1–2 marks** for a distribution table (bottom 50%, rudder 20%, etc.). Many students skip this.
> 
> 6. **Confusing PSPC with PSC:** PSPC = Performance Standard for Protective Coatings. PSC = Port State Control. These are completely different things.
> 
> 7. **Not including safety margin:** Industry practice is 1.2× to 1.5× safety factor on anode mass. KTU expects at least an explicit statement of safety margin.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ship Building (Module 4):**
> 
> - [ ] Five corrosion zones: **Atmospheric, Splash, Immersed, Ballast, Void** — splash is most severe (0.3–0.5 mm/yr).
> - [ ] Splash zone severity reason: continuous O₂ supply + chloride + wet-dry cycling.
> - [ ] Ballast tanks use **PSPC epoxy ≥ 320 μm DFT** + zinc anodes.
> - [ ] Cathodic protection potential: **−0.85 V vs Ag/AgCl** (bare), **−0.95 V** (coated), **−1.05 V** (propeller).
> - [ ] Wetted area formula: $A_w = L(B + 2D)C_f$.
> - [ ] SACP anode capacities: **Zn = 780 A·h/kg, Al = 2700 A·h/kg**.
> - [ ] Anode utilization factor, $U = 0.85$ (default).
> - [ ] Anode mass formula: $m = \dfrac{I \times T \times 8760}{C \times U}$.
> - [ ] Cathodic current densities: **bare = 10–30 mA/m², coated = 1–5 mA/m²**.
> - [ ] ICCP uses **MMO titanium anodes + Ag/AgCl reference + auto-controlled TR unit**.
> - [ ] Anode distribution: **Hull 50%, Rudder 20%, Sides 15%, Sea chests 10%, Bow 5%**.
> - [ ] **IMO PSPC standard** effective from 1 July 2008 (MSC.215(82)).
> - [ ] Dry-docking interval: **2.5 to 5 years** with full anode survey.
> - [ ] Antifouling: TBT banned since 2008, replaced by **copper-based or biocide-free fouling release coatings**.
> - [ ] Stern tube/shaft grounding required to prevent **shaft-driven galvanic corrosion** of propeller.
> - [ ] **Lloyd's, DNV, ABS, BV** are classification societies that approve CP/coating designs.
> - [ ] Use **Faraday's law** to verify corrosion rate: $r = \dfrac{i_{corr} \times M}{n \times F \times \rho}$.
> - [ ] Cavitation erosion-corrosion occurs on **propeller blades and rudder** in fast vessels.
> - [ ] Microbiologically influenced corrosion (MIC) from **SRB** is a major issue in ballast tank sediments.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marine and Fertilizer Industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT523 - CORROSION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Marine and Fertilizer Industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Marine Industry — Formal Definition

**Marine Corrosion** is defined as the *electrochemical degradation of metallic structures* (ship hulls, offshore platforms, sub-sea pipelines, harbor structures, desalination plants, and ballast tanks) caused by prolonged exposure to **seawater** as the bulk electrolyte, combined with mechanical loads, marine biological activity, and atmospheric marine aerosols.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Seawater is the *largest natural electrolyte reservoir on Earth*. It contains approximately **3.5 wt % dissolved salts**, dominated by NaCl (~2.7%), MgCl₂, MgSO₄, and trace chlorides. Its high electrical conductivity (~**50,000 µS/cm** at 25 °C) makes it one of the most aggressive corrosion media known to engineers.

### Sub-Categorisation of Marine Corrosion (KTU Syllabus)

| Marine Zone | Environment | Dominant Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Atmospheric Zone** | Salt-laden air, wet–dry cycling | Uniform + Crevice |
| **Splash Zone** | Alternate wetting, wave impact | Erosion–Corrosion, Cavitation |
| **Tidal Zone** | Periodic seawater immersion | Differential aeration |
| **Submerged Zone** | Continuous immersion | Uniform, Pitting, Biofouling |
| **Mud Zone** | Anaerobic seabed | MIC (Sulfate Reducing Bacteria) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine your bicycle left on a *Kerala beach* (think Kovalam) for a year. The salty breeze coats the chain with a thin invisible brine film. Add tropical humidity, the occasional monsoon wave splash, and you have a perfect "corrosion accelerator." Now scale this up to a 300,000-tonne crude oil tanker — the same physics applies, but the consequences involve billions of rupees and environmental catastrophe. *That* is marine corrosion in one sentence.

---

## 1.2 Fertilizer Industry — Formal Definition

**Fertilizer Industry Corrosion** refers to the *degradation of process equipment, storage tanks, pipelines, and reactors* in fertilizer manufacturing plants due to the extreme chemical environments encountered in producing **urea, ammonia, ammonium nitrate, phosphoric acid, sulfuric acid, and NPK (Nitrogen-Phosphorus-Potassium) complexes**.

### Major Corrosive Environments Encountered

- **Ammonia synthesis gas** — H₂, N₂, CO, CO₂ at high T & P (Nitriding, hydrogen attack)
- **Ammonium carbamate** — Intermediate in urea manufacture (extremely aggressive)
- **Hot ammonium nitrate solutions** — Causes *intergranular corrosion*
- **Wet-process phosphoric acid (WPPA)** — Contains Cl⁻, F⁻, SiO₂, sulfuric acid impurities
- **Sulfuric acid** (chamber and contact process)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The most examination-relevant case in fertilizer corrosion is the *urea reactor*, where **ammonium carbamate** (NH₂COONH₄) decomposes back into NH₃ and CO₂ — this fluid attacks plain carbon steel at rates exceeding **10 mm/year** unless *urea-grade* stainless steels (e.g., AISI **316L UREA-grade**) are used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Think of a fertilizer plant as a giant industrial "stomach" — it digests sulfur, phosphate rock, and natural gas into plant food. But the "stomach acids" inside (sulfuric acid at 200 °C, molten ammonium carbamate) are so corrosive that *normal kitchen-grade stainless steel dissolves in months*. Engineers must therefore use "super-metals" — special stainless steels, titanium, and exotic nickel alloys — to keep the plant alive. *That* is fertilizer industry corrosion in one sentence.

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Industries?

Both industries rank among the **Top 5 most-corrosion-cost-intensive** sectors globally:

- **Marine:** ~ **$50–80 billion/year** in global corrosion cost (NACE IMPACT study).
- **Fertilizer:** Plant shutdowns due to corrosion cause *production losses of crores of rupees* per day.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU Module-4 specifically groups these two industries because both operate with **highly conductive aqueous electrolytes** (seawater & process acids/condensates) and share *common mitigation strategies* — **cathodic protection, alloy selection, coatings, and inhibitors**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Marine Industry — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Electrochemistry of Seawater Corrosion

The general cathodic reaction in oxygenated seawater is the **oxygen reduction reaction (ORR)**:

$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$$

Because dissolved O₂ in seawater is limited (~**8 mg/L** at 25 °C), the corrosion is **cathodically controlled** — i.e., the rate is governed by O₂ diffusion to the metal surface (concentration polarisation).

### 2.1.2 Forms of Marine Corrosion

1. **Uniform / General Corrosion** — Affects ship hulls, sheet piles; mitigated by coatings + CP.
2. **Pitting Corrosion** — Chloride ions penetrate passive films on stainless steel → deep, narrow pits. **Most dangerous** in marine service.
3. **Crevice Corrosion** — Occurs in gasket joints, lap welds, riveted connections; differential aeration cell.
4. **Galvanic Corrosion** — Bronze propellers + steel hulls (driven by ~0.3 V potential difference).
5. **Erosion–Corrosion** — Impingement of sand-laden water on condenser tubes, pump impellers.
6. **Cavitation Damage** — Bubble collapse near propeller blades.
7. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** — Especially *chloride SCC* in austenitic SS heat exchanger tubes.
8. **Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC)** — Sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB) in marine mud → produces H₂S, pitting.
9. **Marine Fouling** — Barnacles, mussels, algae create differential aeration cells under their bases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanism of MIC (SRB):** SRB use sulfate as terminal electron acceptor, producing H₂S, which reacts with Fe²⁺ to form **iron sulfide (FeS)** deposits that accelerate localised corrosion in anaerobic mud zones.

### 2.1.3 Material Selection for Marine Service

| Material | Application | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel (with coating + CP) | Hulls, jackets | Needs continuous protection |
| **Cu-Ni (90/10, 70/30)** | Seawater piping, condensers | Limited to < 5 m/s flow |
| **Aluminium Bronze** | Propellers, pump bodies | Subject to dealuminification |
| **Austenitic SS 316L** | Architectural marine | Pitting in stagnant Cl⁻ |
| **Duplex SS (2205)** | Offshore piping | SCC if > 70 °C |
| **Super-Austenitic (254 SMO, AL-6XN)** | Seawater handling | Cost |
| **Titanium Gr-2** | Heat exchangers, propeller shafts | Very expensive |
| **FRP / GRE composites** | Piping, gratings | Mechanical limits |

### 2.1.4 Cathodic Protection (CP) of Marine Structures

Two methods are universally applied in KTU-relevant offshore design:

**(a) Sacrificial Anode (Galvanic) CP:**
- Anodes: **Al–Zn–In alloy** (preferred) or **Zn** or **Mg**
- Typical current demand: **100–300 mA/m²** for bare steel in seawater
- Anode life: 10–30 years

**(b) Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP):**
- Inert anodes: **Platinised Ti**, **Mixed Metal Oxide (MMO/Ti)**, **Graphite**, **High-Silicon Cast Iron**
- Power source: Transformer-rectifier, typically **12–24 V DC**, currents up to several hundred amperes
- Reference electrode: Ag/AgCl or Zn

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Criterion:** For offshore structures, the protective potential of steel w.r.t. **Ag/AgCl/seawater** reference must be maintained between **−0.80 V and −1.10 V**. The "−0.80 V" lower bound prevents under-protection, and the "−1.10 V" upper bound prevents hydrogen embrittlement and excessive calcareous deposit.

### 2.1.5 Protective Coatings for Marine Service

A typical **three-coat marine system**:

1. **Primer:** Inorganic zinc-rich (IOZ) — sacrificial protection
2. **Intermediate:** Epoxy with micaceous iron oxide (MIO) — barrier + film reinforcement
3. **Topcoat:** Polyurethane (PU) — UV resistance, gloss retention

> For ballast tank interiors (PSPC — IMO Performance Standard for Protective Coatings), **pure epoxy** systems of 320 µm DFT are mandated.

---

## 2.2 Fertilizer Industry — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Corrosive Streams & Their Nature

| Stream | pH / Concentration | Temperature | Aggressive Species |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ammonium carbamate | ~ pH 9, molten | 180–200 °C | CO₂, NH₃, H₂O |
| Nitric acid (weak) | 50–65% HNO₃ | 80–120 °C | HNO₃, NO₂, HF (if phosphate) |
| Sulfuric acid | 95–98% H₂SO₄ | 40–80 °C | H₂SO₄, SO₃ |
| Wet phosphoric acid | 28–54% P₂O₅ | 60–95 °C | H₃PO₄, F⁻, Cl⁻, H₂SO₄ |
| Urea melt | 99% urea | 135–140 °C | Biuret, NH₃ |
| Ammonium nitrate (AN) | 80–95% AN | 150–200 °C | NH₄NO₃, free HNO₃ |

### 2.2.2 Corrosion Mechanisms in Urea Service

- **Ammonium carbamate corrosion** — caused by the formation of *iron carbamate* complexes (Fe(NH₂COO)₂) that dissolve the protective oxide film.
- **Activation of carbon steel** — when CO₂ partial pressure is high, protective magnetite (Fe₃O₄) breaks down.
- **Intergranular corrosion** — sensitised austenitic SS in ammonium nitrate service (grain-boundary Cr depletion).

### 2.2.3 Material Selection for Fertilizer Plants

| Component | Material |
|---|---|
| Urea reactor liner (high-pressure) | **AISI 316L Urea-grade** (low C, controlled ferrite) |
| Urea stripper tubes | **2RE69 (25 Cr-22 Ni-2 Mo)** or **DP28W** |
| Nitric acid cooler/condenser | **AISI 304L**, or **Lean Duplex** |
| HNO₃ (hot, strong) plant | **Silicon stainless steel (Saramet)** or Ti |
| Sulfuric acid coolers | **Hastelloy B/C**, or **Anode-protected SS** |
| Phosphoric acid evaporator | **Sanicro 28** (27 Cr-31 Ni-3.5 Mo) |
| Pumps, valves for hot NH₄NO₃ | **AISI 316L** (low C ≤ 0.03%) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Focus:** Why *316L* and not *316* in urea service? — Because *sensitisation* (grain-boundary Cr₂₃C₆ precipitation at 450–850 °C) depletes Cr at grain boundaries, causing **intergranular corrosion**. The **"L" grade** restricts C to ≤ **0.03%**, preventing carbide precipitation — a classic KTU 2-mark question.

### 2.2.4 Anodic Protection in Fertilizer Service

For sulfuric acid storage, **anodic protection** (rather than cathodic) is used:

- **Power source:** potentiostat
- **Reference:** Ag/AgCl or Hg/Hg₂SO₄
- **Passivation range of carbon steel in 96% H₂SO₄:** ~+200 mV to +1100 mV (SHE)
- **Passive current density:** as low as **1–5 µA/cm²** (vs. corrosion current of 1 mA/cm² unprotected)

### 2.2.5 Corrosion Inhibitors in Fertilizer Service

- **Ammonium thiocyanate (NH₄SCN)** — classical vapour-phase inhibitor added to urea melt
- **Hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂)** — added to phosphoric acid to oxidise Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺
- **Nitrite + nitrate blends** — passivating inhibitors for carbon steel in alkaline fertiliser solutions
- **Phosphate + zinc blends** — for cooling water circuits

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Numerical Value | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Faraday's Law of Corrosion | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | kg (W), A·s (I·t), g/mol (M) | Metal loss calculation |
| 2 | Corrosion rate (from mass loss) | $CR = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | mm/yr | Universal corrosion rate formula |
| 3 | Penetration rate conversion | $1 \text{ mpy} = 0.0254 \text{ mm/yr}$ | — | Unit conversion |
| 4 | Tafel equation (anodic) | $\eta_a = \beta_a \log \dfrac{i}{i_{corr}}$ | V (η), A/m² (i) | Polarisation curve fitting |
| 5 | Tafel equation (cathodic) | $\eta_c = \beta_c \log \dfrac{i}{i_{corr}}$ | V (η), A/m² (i) | Polarisation curve fitting |
| 6 | Stern–Geary equation | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R_p}$ | A/m² | LPR-based corrosion rate |
| 7 | Galvanic series ordering | More noble to more active in seawater | V vs SHE | Material pairing decisions |
| 8 | CP current demand (offshore) | $i = 100\text{–}300$ | mA/m² | Cathodic protection design |
| 9 | Cathodic protection potential window (steel in seawater) | $-1.10 \leq E_{Ag/AgCl} \leq -0.80$ | V | CP criterion |
| 10 | Urea-grade 316L max carbon content | $C \leq 0.03$ | wt% | Material specification |
| 11 | Seawater conductivity | $\sigma \approx 50{,}000$ | µS/cm | Aggressivity indicator |
| 12 | Pitting Resistance Equiv. Number | $PREN = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N$ | dimensionless | Stainless steel selection |
| 13 | Passive current density (SS in H₂SO₄) | $i_{pass} \approx 1\text{–}5$ | µA/cm² | Anodic protection design |
| 14 | SRB-induced corrosion rate | $0.2\text{–}1.0$ | mm/yr | MIC estimation in mud zone |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Every formula in the table above is *directly applicable* to the design of a *real* marine or fertilizer plant. For instance, Equation 2 is used to verify the actual field corrosion rate against an *allowable corrosion allowance* (typically 3–6 mm in offshore jacket legs), and Equation 12 (PREN) is the *industry standard* for choosing between 316L (PREN ≈ 25), 2205 (PREN ≈ 35), and 254 SMO (PREN ≈ 43) for chloride service.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Corrosion Rate from Mass Loss (Full KTU Derivation)

### Step 1: Faraday's First Law
The mass of metal dissolved by electrolysis is directly proportional to the total charge passed.

$$W \propto Q = I \cdot t$$

### Step 2: Introduce the proportionality constant
For 1 mole of electrons, charge transferred = **Faraday's constant F ≈ 96,485 C/mol**.

For a metal M with valency *n*, the molar mass *M* is dissolved per *n* × F coulombs of charge.

$$W = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} \quad \text{...(3.1)}$$

This is the **fundamental Faraday corrosion equation**.

### Step 3: Convert mass loss to penetration depth
Penetration rate *CR* (mm/yr) is obtained by dividing *W* by:
- Density $\rho$ of metal (g/cm³) → *D* in kg/m³ for SI
- Surface area *A* (m²)
- Time *t* (hours → year conversion factor 87.6 includes 24×365 = 8760)

$$CR = \frac{W}{A \cdot t \cdot \rho} = \frac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t} \quad \text{...(3.2)}$$

The constant **87.6 = (24 × 365 × 10) / 1000** appears because:
- 24 h/day × 365 day/yr = 8760 h/yr
- 10 to convert cm to mm
- 1000 to convert g to kg

### Step 4: Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A carbon steel coupon of area **0.05 m²** and density **7850 kg/m³** loses **2.8 g** in **720 hours** of immersion in seawater. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mm/yr** and in **mpy** (mils per year).

**Solution (Part A):**

$$CR = \frac{87.6 \times 2.8}{7850 \times 0.05 \times 720}$$

$$CR = \frac{245.28}{282{,}600}$$

$$CR = 8.68 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm/yr}$$

That is *very low* for carbon steel — let me re-check the numbers with a *realistic* example.

**Corrected Realistic Example:**

> A steel coupon of area **0.05 m²**, density **7850 kg/m³**, loses **150 g** in **720 h** of exposure.

$$CR = \frac{87.6 \times 150}{7850 \times 0.05 \times 720} = \frac{13{,}140}{282{,}600} = 0.0465 \text{ mm/yr}$$

Convert to mpy:

$$CR_{\text{mpy}} = \frac{0.0465}{0.0254} = 1.83 \text{ mpy}$$

**Interpretation:** This corresponds to *Category "Good" (< 5 mpy)* in NACE corrosion severity classification.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stern–Geary Equation (LPR Method)

### Step 1: Combine the two Tafel equations
The net current at any overpotential η is the sum of anodic and cathodic contributions:

$$i = i_{corr}\left[\exp\left(\frac{2.303\eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{2.303\eta}{\beta_c}\right)\right]$$

### Step 2: Linearise for small η (|η| < 10 mV)
For small polarisation (linear polarisation region near corrosion potential), the exponentials are approximated as 1 + x:

$$i \approx i_{corr} \cdot 2.303 \cdot \eta \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c}\right)$$

### Step 3: Define polarisation resistance
The slope of polarisation curve at η = 0 is the **polarisation resistance**:

$$R_p = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta i} = \frac{\eta}{i}$$

### Step 4: Substitute and solve

$$i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)} \cdot \frac{1}{R_p}$$

This is the **Stern–Geary equation** (1957). The constant is often expressed as the **Stern–Geary coefficient B**:

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

For active corrosion (β_a = β_c = 0.1 V/decade), B ≈ 21.7 mV. For passivating systems, B ≈ 52 mV.

### Step 5: Numerical Application
A LPR probe gives R_p = **5000 Ω·cm²** in a urea reactor. With B = 26 mV:

$$i_{corr} = \frac{0.026}{5000} = 5.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A/cm}^2$$

Convert to mm/yr using Faraday's equation (3.2) for Fe²⁺:

$$CR = \frac{0.00327 \times i_{corr} \times EW}{D} \quad \text{...(3.3)}$$

Where EW = equivalent weight of Fe = 27.92 g/equiv.:

$$CR = \frac{0.00327 \times 5.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 27.92}{7.85} = 6.05 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mm/yr}$$

This is *negligible*, indicating the surface is *passive* — a desired state in urea service.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cathodic Protection Design (Anode Mass Calculation)

### Step 1: Define total current demand
For an offshore jacket leg of surface area A in m²:

$$I_{total} = i_{design} \cdot A \quad \text{(A)}$$

### Step 2: Anode lifetime requirement
Anode life L (years) is given by:

$$m_{anode} = \frac{I_{total} \cdot L \cdot M_{anode}}{n \cdot F} \quad \text{(kg)} \quad \text{...(3.4)}$$

For an Al–Zn–In anode, M = 27 g/mol, n = 3 (Al → Al³⁺), utilisation factor u ≈ 0.85.

### Step 3: Number of anodes
Mass of one anode m_1, then:

$$N = \frac{m_{anode}}{u \cdot m_1}$$

### Step 4: Worked Example

> A jacket leg has surface area **A = 500 m²** below water. Design CP current = **150 mA/m²**. Anode life required = **20 years**. Single anode mass = **150 kg**, u = 0.85, Al anode M = 27 g/mol, n = 3.

$$I_{total} = 0.150 \times 500 = 75 \text{ A}$$

$$m_{anode} = \frac{75 \times (20 \times 365 \times 24 \times 3600) \times 0.027}{3 \times 96485}$$

$$m_{anode} = \frac{75 \times 6.31 \times 10^{8} \times 0.027}{289{,}455}$$

$$m_{anode} = \frac{1.277 \times 10^{9}}{2.89 \times 10^{5}} = 4413 \text{ kg}$$

$$N = \frac{4413}{0.85 \times 150} = \frac{4413}{127.5} = 34.6 \approx 35 \text{ anodes}$$

> **Answer:** 35 anodes are required to protect the jacket leg for 20 years.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Marine vs Fertilizer

| Parameter | Marine Industry | Fertilizer Industry |
|---|---|---|
| **Dominant electrolyte** | NaCl brine | Carbamate, H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄, HNO₃ |
| **Conductivity** | ~50,000 µS/cm | Highly variable (50–200,000 µS/cm) |
| **Temperature** | 5–35 °C (ambient) | 60–250 °C (process) |
| **pH** | 7.8–8.2 | 0.5–11 (extreme range) |
| **Primary corrosion form** | Pitting, Crevice, MIC | Intergranular, SCC, General |
| **Preferred protection** | Cathodic + Coating | Alloying + Inhibitors + Anodic |
| **Material of choice** | Cu-Ni, Duplex SS, Ti | 316L Urea, 2RE69, Sanicro 28 |
| **Key reference electrode** | Ag/AgCl/seawater | Hg/Hg₂SO₄, SCE |
| **Annual cost (global)** | ~$50–80 billion | ~$10–20 billion |
| **Typical inspection method** | ROV, NDT, LPR probes | ER probes, UT, LPR |
| **Impressed current anode** | MMO/Ti, Pt/Ti | Pt, MMO, Si-cast iron |
| **Inhibitor type** | CaCO₃ film, Zn sacrificial | NH₄SCN, H₂O₂, phosphate |

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Pitting Corrosion Mechanism in Marine SS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeP[Passive Film Cr2O3 on 316L] --> nodeQ[Chloride Ion Attack from Seawater]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Local Film Breakdown]
    nodeR --> nodeS[Small Active Pit Anode]
    nodeR --> nodeT[Large Passive Cathode Area]
    nodeS --> nodeU[Autocatalytic Dissolution Fe to Fe2 Plus]
    nodeU --> nodeV[Metal Cations Hydrolyse H Plus]
    nodeV --> nodeW[Local pH drops below 2]
    nodeW --> nodeX[Accelerated Pitting Growth]
    nodeT --> nodeY[Cathodic Reaction O2 Reduction]
    nodeY --> nodeZ[OH minus on surrounding surface]
    nodeX --> nodeAA[Deep Narrow Pit]
    nodeAA --> nodeAB[Structural Failure or Leak]
    nodeZ --> nodeP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Once a pit initiates, the *autocatalytic* mechanism (low pH inside pit, cathodic protection on surrounding surface) makes it self-propagating. This is why **PREN** and **molybdenum content** are critical for marine stainless steel selection.
